$$n = 24, \quad \sum v = 13+15+16+16+19+20+20+21+22+22+25+25+30+33+33+35+35+35+36+40+45+46+52+70$$

$$\sum v = 703$$

$$\mu_A = \frac{703}{24} \approx 29.29$$

**Step 2 — Compute the sample standard deviation.**

$$\sigma_A = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(v_i - \mu_A)^2}{n - 1}}$$

Squared deviations (key terms):

$$
\begin{aligned}
(13 - 29.29)^2 &\approx 264.93 \\
(15 - 29.29)^2 &\approx 203.70 \\
(70 - 29.29)^2 &\approx 1657.06 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing all 24 squared deviations:

$$\sum(v_i - \mu_A)^2 \approx 5589.96$$

$$\sigma_A = \sqrt{\frac{5589.96}{23}} = \sqrt{243.04} \approx 15.59$$

**Step 3 — Apply Z-Score Formula for $v = 35$.**

$$v' = \frac{35 - 29.29}{15.59} = \frac{5.71}{15.59} \approx 0.366$$

**Interpretation:** The age 35 is approximately 0.366 standard deviations **above** the mean age.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Decimal Scaling with Worked Example

### 3.3.1 The Generic Form

$$v' = \frac{v}{10^{j}}$$

### 3.3.2 Determining $j$

Choose the **smallest integer** $j$ such that $\max(\vert v' \vert) < 1$.

For the age dataset where $\max(\vert v \vert) = 70$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
j = 0 &: \max(\vert v' \vert) = 70 \not< 1 \quad \text{(fails)} \\
j = 1 &: \max(\vert v' \vert) = 7 \not< 1 \quad \text{(fails)} \\
j = 2 &: \max(\vert v' \vert) = 0.7 < 1 \quad \text{(passes)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence $j = 2$.

**Normalization for $v = 70$:**

$$v' = \frac{70}{10^2} = \frac{70}{100} = 0.7$$

**Normalization for $v = 13$:**

$$v' = \frac{13}{10^2} = 0.13$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Binning Method (Equal-Frequency, Smoothing by Bin Means)

### 3.4.1 Sorted Data (Age, in ascending order)

$$\left[4, 8, 9, 15, 21, 21, 24, 25, 26, 28, 29, 34, 35, 36, 40, 44, 51, 52, 54, 55, 56, 60, 61, 62, 64, 68, 70, 75, 76, 78, 80, 81, 88, 89, 92, 99\right]$$

Total elements $n = 36$. Using **equal-frequency binning** with **3 bins** → bin depth = 12.

**Bin 1:** $\left[4, 8, 9, 15, 21, 21, 24, 25, 26, 28, 29, 34\right]$

**Bin 2:** $\left[35, 36, 40, 44, 51, 52, 54, 55, 56, 60, 61, 62\right]$

**Bin 3:** $\left[64, 68, 70, 75, 76, 78, 80, 81, 88, 89, 92, 99\right]$

**Step 1 — Compute bin means.**

$$\mu_1 = \frac{4+8+9+15+21+21+24+25+26+28+29+34}{12} = \frac{244}{12} \approx 20.33$$

$$\mu_2 = \frac{35+36+40+44+51+52+54+55+56+60+61+62}{12} = \frac{606}{12} = 50.50$$

$$\mu_3 = \frac{64+68+70+75+76+78+80+81+88+89+92+99}{12} = \frac{960}{12} = 80.00$$

**Step 2 — Smooth by Bin Means (replace each value with its bin mean).**

| Bin | Original Values | Smoothed Values (Bin Mean) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4, 8, 9, 15, 21, 21, 24, 25, 26, 28, 29, 34 | 20.33, 20.33, ..., 20.33 (12 times) |
| 2 | 35, 36, ..., 62 | 50.50 (12 times) |
| 3 | 64, 68, ..., 99 | 80.00 (12 times) |

**Step 3 — Smooth by Bin Boundaries (alternative).**

For Bin 1, boundary values = $\min = 4$, $\max = 34$. Replace each value with the closer boundary.

| Original (Bin 1) | Distance to 4 | Distance to 34 | Closer | Smoothed |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | 0 | 30 | 4 | **4** |
| 8 | 4 | 26 | 4 | **4** |
| 21 | 17 | 13 | 34 | **34** |
| 34 | 30 | 0 | 34 | **34** |

> [!NOTE]
> Binning reduces intra-bin variance and acts as a local smoother. The KTU board often asks: "Demonstrate equal-frequency binning followed by smoothing by bin means." Memorize the mean computation procedure.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Which Normalization to Use?

| Criterion | Min-Max | Z-Score | Decimal Scaling |
|---|---|---|---|
| Known bounds required? | Yes ($\min$, $\max$) | No | No |
| Outlier sensitive? | **Yes — high** | **No — moderate** | **Yes — high** |
| Resulting distribution shape | Preserved | Standardized | Preserved |
| Range of output | $\left[0, 1\right]$ typically | Unbounded | $\left[-1, 1\right]$ |
| Use case | Neural networks, image processing | Distance-based algorithms, PCA | Quick scale-down |

---

## 3.6 Pearson's Correlation — Step-by-Step Worked Example

**Data:** $A = \left[1, 2, 3, 4, 5\right]$, $B = \left[2, 4, 5, 4, 6\right]$, $n = 5$.

**Step 1 — Means.**

$$\bar{A} = 3, \quad \bar{B} = 4.2$$

**Step 2 — Deviations and products.**

| $a_i$ | $b_i$ | $a_i - \bar{A}$ | $b_i - \bar{B}$ | Product |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | -2 | -2.2 | 4.4 |
| 2 | 4 | -1 | -0.2 | 0.2 |
| 3 | 5 | 0 | 0.8 | 0.0 |
| 4 | 4 | 1 | -0.2 | -0.2 |
| 5 | 6 | 2 | 1.8 | 3.6 |

$$\sum (a_i - \bar{A})(b_i - \bar{B}) = 4.4 + 0.2 + 0.0 - 0.2 + 3.6 = 8.0$$

**Step 3 — Standard deviations.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_A &= \sqrt{\frac{(-2)^2 + (-1)^2 + 0^2 + 1^2 + 2^2}{4}} = \sqrt{\frac{10}{4}} = \sqrt{2.5} \approx 1.581 \\
\sigma_B &= \sqrt{\frac{(-2.2)^2 + (-0.2)^2 + 0.8^2 + (-0.2)^2 + 1.8^2}{4}} = \sqrt{\frac{9.76}{4}} = \sqrt{2.44} \approx 1.562 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $r$.**

$$r_{A,B} = \frac{8.0}{4 \cdot 1.581 \cdot 1.562} = \frac{8.0}{9.880} \approx 0.810$$

**Interpretation:** A, B are **strongly positively correlated** ($r \approx 0.81$) → one of them may be **redundant**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Pipeline — KDD Process with Pre-processing Highlighted

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawData[Raw Operational Data Sources] --> warehouse[Data Warehouse]
    warehouse --> selection[Data Selection and Integration]
    selection --> cleaning[Data Cleaning]
    cleaning --> transform[Data Transformation]
    transform --> reduction[Data Reduction]
    reduction --> mining[Data Mining Algorithms]
    mining --> patternExtraction[Pattern Extraction and Evaluation]
    patternExtraction --> knowledge[Knowledge Presentation to User]

    style rawData fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style warehouse fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style cleaning fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style transform fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style reduction fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style mining fill:#f57c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style knowledge fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The **red nodes** (`cleaning`, `transform`, `reduction`) represent the four pillars of pre-processing emphasized in the KTU syllabus. Notice that pre-processing alone spans **three** of the **four** mandatory operations before mining.

---

## 4.2 Data Cleaning Sub-Tasks Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    dirtyData[Dirty Data With Issues] --> missingVal{Missing Values Present}
    dirtyData --> noiseVal{Noisy Data Present}
    dirtyData --> inconsistVal{Inconsistencies Present}

    missingVal -- Yes --> mv1[Ignore Tuple]
    missingVal -- No --> mvPass[Skip to Next Stage]
    mv1 --> mv2[Manual Imputation]
    mv1 --> mv3[Global Constant Fill]
    mv1 --> mv4[Attribute Mean Median or Mode]
    mv1 --> mv5[Most Probable Value Prediction]

    noiseVal -- Yes --> n1[Binning Methods]
    noiseVal -- No --> nPass[Skip to Next Stage]
    n1 --> n2[Equal Width Binning]
    n1 --> n3[Equal Frequency Binning]
    n1 --> n4[Smoothing by Bin Means]
    n1 --> n5[Smoothing by Bin Medians]
    n1 --> n6[Smoothing by Bin Boundaries]
    n1 --> n7[Regression Analysis]
    n1 --> n8[Clustering Based Outlier Detection]

    inconsistVal -- Yes --> i1[Standardization of Units and Codes]
    inconsistVal -- No --> iPass[Skip to Next Stage]

    mv5 --> cleanData
    n8 --> cleanData
    i1 --> cleanData
    cleanData[Cleaned Data Ready for Integration]

    style dirtyData fill:#c62828,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style cleanData fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.3 Data Reduction Taxonomy (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    reductionMaster[Data Reduction Strategies] --> dimRed[Dimensionality Reduction]
    reductionMaster --> numRed[Numerosity Reduction]
    reductionMaster --> compress[Data Compression]

    dimRed --> featSel[Feature Selection]
    dimRed --> pca1[Principal Component Analysis PCA]
    dimRed --> wavelet[Wavelet Transforms]

    numRed --> parametric[Parametric Methods]
    numRed --> nonParametric[Non Parametric Methods]

    parametric --> regression1[Linear and Multiple Regression]
    parametric --> loglin[Log Linear Models]

    nonParametric --> histograms[Histograms]
    nonParametric --> clustering[Clustering Based Sampling]
    nonParamistic2[See Correction]:::hidden
    nonParametric --> sampling1[Sampling Techniques]

    sampling1 --> srs[Simple Random Sampling]
    sampling1 --> strat[Stratified Sampling]
    sampling1 --> clust[Cluster Sampling]

    compress --> lossless[Lossless Compression Huffman and Run Length]
    compress --> lossy[Lossy Compression FFT and Wavelet and DCT]

    classDef hidden display:none
    style reductionMaster fill:#4527a0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style dimRed fill:#1565c0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style numRed fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style compress fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.4 Data Quality Dimensions Wheel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    center[Data Quality Assurance] --> acc[Accuracy]
    center --> comp[Completeness]
    center --> cons[Consistency]
    center --> time[Timeliness]
    center --> believ[Believability]
    center --> valAdd[Value Added]
    center --> interp[Interpretability]
    center --> access[Accessibility]

    style center fill:#1a237e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style acc fill:#004d40,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style comp fill:#004d40,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style cons fill:#004d40,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style time fill:#004d40,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style believ fill:#004d40,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style valAdd fill:#004d40,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style interp fill:#004d40,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style access fill:#004d40,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> **Reading the wheel:** Each leaf node represents a measurable dimension along which a dataset can be evaluated. KTU examiners often frame Part A questions around naming and defining **3 to 4** of these dimensions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
*List any three major tasks in data pre-processing. Briefly explain each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three major tasks in data pre-processing are:

1. **Data Cleaning** — Fills in missing values, smooths noisy data, identifies or removes outliers, and resolves inconsistencies. Example: Replacing missing `salary` with the attribute mean.
2. **Data Integration** — Merges data from multiple heterogeneous sources into a coherent data store. Resolves schema mismatches, redundancy, and value conflicts. Example: Joining customer tables from CRM and ERP systems.
3. **Data Transformation** — Normalizes and aggregates data to make it suitable for mining. Example: Min-max scaling of income values to $\left[0,1\right]$ range.

> [!NOTE]
> Many students lose the **3rd mark** by missing the third task. The KTU board strictly requires **at least three** tasks for full marks.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
*Differentiate between data integration and data transformation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Data Integration | Data Transformation |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Combine multiple data sources into a unified view | Convert data into forms appropriate for mining |
| **Primary Challenge** | Schema mismatch, redundancy, value conflicts | Scale mismatch, type conversion |
| **Example** | Merging sales.db and inventory.db | Normalizing prices to $\left[0,1\right]$ via min-max |
| **Output Nature** | Wider / row-aligned dataset | Rescaled or restructured dataset |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*Consider the following dataset of student marks (sorted):*

$$\left[4, 8, 9, 15, 21, 21, 24, 25, 26, 28, 29, 34, 35, 36, 40, 44, 51, 52, 54, 55, 56, 60, 61, 62\right]$$

*Apply equal-frequency binning with **3 bins** and then smooth the data using **(i) bin means** and **(ii) bin boundaries**. Show all calculations.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Determine bin depth.**

$$n = 24, \quad \text{bin depth} = \frac{24}{3} = 8$$

**Step 2 — Form 3 equal-frequency bins.**

- **Bin 1:** $\left[4, 8, 9, 15, 21, 21, 24, 25\right]$
- **Bin 2:** $\left[26, 28, 29, 34, 35, 36, 40, 44\right]$
- **Bin 3:** $\left[51, 52, 54, 55, 56, 60, 61, 62\right]$

**Step 3 (i) — Smooth by bin means.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_1 &= \frac{4+8+9+15+21+21+24+25}{8} = \frac{127}{8} = 15.875 \\
\mu_2 &= \frac{26+28+29+34+35+36+40+44}{8} = \frac{272}{8} = 34.000 \\
\mu_3 &= \frac{51+52+54+55+56+60+61+62}{8} = \frac{451}{8} = 56.375 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Smoothed sequence: $\left[15.875 \times 8, \ 34.000 \times 8, \ 56.375 \times 8\right]$.

**Step 3 (ii) — Smooth by bin boundaries.**

For Bin 1, $\min = 4$, $\max = 25$:

| Original | Closer Boundary | Smoothed |
|---|---|---|
| 4 | 4 | 4 |
| 8 | 4 | 4 |
| 9 | 4 | 4 |
| 15 | 4 | 4 |
| 21 | 25 | 25 |
| 21 | 25 | 25 |
| 24 | 25 | 25 |
| 25 | 25 | 25 |

**Resulting smoothed sequence (Bin 1):** $\left[4, 4, 4, 4, 25, 25, 25, 25\right]$.

(Repeat analogous process for Bins 2 and 3.)

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*Explain the three methods of data normalization — min-max, z-score, and decimal scaling — with formulas. Apply min-max normalization on the following attribute values to the range $\left[0, 1\right]$:*

$$A = \left[100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600, 700, 800, 900\right]$$

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify formulas.**

- Min-Max: $v' = \frac{v - \min_A}{\max_A - \min_A} \cdot (new\_\max - new\_\min) + new\_\min$
- Z-Score: $v' = \frac{v - \mu_A}{\sigma_A}$
- Decimal Scaling: $v' = \frac{v}{10^{j}}$ where $j$ is the smallest integer with $\max(\vert v' \vert) < 1$.

**Step 2 — Apply min-max normalization.**

$$\min_A = 100, \quad \max_A = 900, \quad new\_\max = 1, \quad new\_\min = 0$$

$$v' = \frac{v - 100}{900 - 100} = \frac{v - 100}{800}$$

| $v$ | $v'$ |
|---|---|
| 100 | 0.000 |
| 200 | 0.125 |
| 300 | 0.250 |
| 400 | 0.375 |
| 500 | 0.500 |
| 600 | 0.625 |
| 700 | 0.750 |
| 800 | 0.875 |
| 900 | 1.000 |

**Comparison with Z-Score:**

$$\mu_A = 500, \quad \sigma_A \approx 258.20$$

$$v'_{z} \text{ for } v = 100: \quad \frac{100-500}{258.20} \approx -1.549$$

**Comparison with Decimal Scaling:**

For $\max = 900$, $j = 3$ → $v' = \frac{v}{1000}$.

$$v'_{d} \text{ for } v = 900: \quad \frac{900}{1000} = 0.9$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2023]**
*Explain the concept of data cleaning. Discuss the various methods to handle missing values and noisy data in a dataset.*

**Model Solution:**

**Data Cleaning Definition (2 marks):** Data cleaning is the process of detecting, correcting, or removing corrupt, inaccurate, or irrelevant records from a dataset. It addresses **missing values, noisy data, and inconsistencies**.

**Methods to Handle Missing Values (3 marks):**

1. **Ignore the tuple** — Suitable when the class label is missing and the dataset is large.
2. **Manual Imputation** — Domain expert fills in the value; tedious and infeasible for large datasets.
3. **Global Constant Fill** — Replace missing value with a constant like "Unknown" or -1.
4. **Attribute-Central Tendency** — Replace with mean (numerical, symmetric), median (numerical, skewed), or mode (categorical).
5. **Most Probable Value** — Use regression, decision tree, or EM algorithm to predict the missing value.

**Methods to Handle Noisy Data (2 marks):**

1. **Binning** — First sort and partition into bins, then smooth by bin means, medians, or boundaries.
2. **Regression** — Fit a regression line/curve to smooth data.
3. **Outlier Analysis via Clustering** — Detect points lying far from cluster centroids.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*With a suitable example, explain how redundancy in data is detected using **correlation analysis** for numerical attributes. State the formula and interpret the value.*

**Model Solution:**

**Concept (2 marks):** A redundancy exists in a dataset when an attribute can be derived from another. For numerical attributes, **Pearson's correlation coefficient** is used to detect such redundancy.

**Formula (2 marks):**

$$r_{A,B} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(a_i - \bar{A})(b_i - \bar{B})}{(n-1)\cdot \sigma_A \cdot \sigma_B}$$

**Interpretation (2 marks):**
- $r_{A,B} \in \left[-1, 1\right]$
- $r > 0$ → positive correlation; $r < 0$ → negative correlation.
- $\vert r \vert$ close to 1 → strong linear relationship → potential redundancy.
- $\vert r \vert$ close to 0 → no linear relationship → both attributes likely needed.

**Example (1 mark):** In a housing dataset, if `house_price` and `tax_amount` yield $r \approx 0.95$, one of them can be safely removed as it is redundant.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (Avoid These Pitfalls):**
> 1. **Confusing equal-width and equal-frequency binning** — equal-width uses range intervals, equal-frequency uses bin depth. Examiners deduct **2 marks** if these are swapped.
> 2. **Forgetting to state the formula for $r_{A,B}$ explicitly** — Always write the full numerator/denominator; partial formulas receive **half** marks.
> 3. **Miscounting bin depth in equal-frequency binning** — Use $n/k$ carefully, especially for non-divisible $n$. The KTU board awards **0 marks** if the bin partition itself is wrong.
> 4. **Reporting only the final transformed value without intermediate steps** — The KTU valuation key requires the **substitution step** to be visible: write $v' = (v - \min)/(\max - \min) \cdot \ldots$ before plugging numbers.
> 5. **Treating outlier detection as removal** — Outliers are *identified* first, then a separate decision to *remove*/*keep/impute* is made. Many students lose **1 mark** by conflating these.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Data pre-processing = cleaning + integration + transformation + reduction.** Memorize this 4-pillar framework in order.
- **Missing value strategies:** Ignore, manual fill, global constant, central tendency, most probable (regression/EM).
- **Noise handling:** Binning (3 smoothings), regression, clustering-based outlier detection.
- **Binning smoothing techniques:** Means, medians, boundaries — *bin boundaries* keeps values as min or max of bin.
- **Equal-width vs Equal-frequency:** Width = range interval; Frequency = bin depth = $n/k$.
- **Redundancy detection:** Pearson's $r$ for numerical, Chi-square for categorical.
- **Three normalization formulas:** Min-max (range-based), Z-score (mean-std), Decimal scaling ($10^j$).
- **Data reduction methods:** Dimensionality (PCA, wavelet), Numerosity (regression, histogram, sampling), Compression (lossless/lossy).
- **Sampling types:** Simple random, stratified, cluster.
- **Quality dimensions:** Accuracy, Completeness, Consistency, Timeliness, Believability, Value Added, Interpretability, Accessibility.
- **GIGO principle:** Bad data → bad models, no matter how good the algorithm.
- **Discretization** generates concept hierarchies (city → state → country).
- **Min-max range is configurable**; default is $\left[0, 1\right]$ but any interval is valid.
- **Decimal scaling** requires the smallest $j$ such that $\max(\vert v' \vert) < 1$ — *not* $\le 1$.
- **Chi-square test** is used for redundancy in **categorical** attributes, not numerical.
- **Wavelet transforms** are lossless and lossy-capable, applicable to high-dimensional data like time series.
- **PCA** projects data onto principal components ordered by variance explained — first $k$ components preserve maximum information.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Efficient and Scalable Frequent Item Set Mining Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT604 - DATA MINING AND WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Efficient and Scalable Frequent Item Set Mining Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frequent Itemset Mining (FIM)** is the foundational task in Association Rule Mining that aims to discover all itemsets (groups of items) whose **support count** meets a user-defined **minimum support threshold (min_sup)**. An itemset is *frequent* if the fraction of transactions in the database that contain it is greater than or equal to `min_sup`.

**Scalable and Efficient Frequent Itemset Mining** refers to algorithmic strategies that overcome the classical limitations of the brute-force and Apriori-style methods — namely the exponential search space ($2^d$ candidate itemsets for $d$ distinct items) and repeated, costly database scans — by employing smarter data structures, partitioning strategies, compression, or vertical storage formats.

The formal problem statement:

Let $\mathcal{D} = \{T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_N\}$ be a transaction database of $N$ transactions over an item universe $\mathcal{I} = \{i_1, i_2, \ldots, i_d\}$. For any itemset $X \subseteq \mathcal{I}$:

$$\text{support}(X) = \frac{\vert \{T_k \in \mathcal{D} : X \subseteq T_k\} \vert}{N}$$

The **Goal** of Efficient & Scalable FIM:

$$\mathcal{F} = \{ X \subseteq \mathcal{I} : \text{support}(X) \geq \text{min\_sup} \}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 1):** Students must be able to *describe, contrast, and justify* scalable alternatives to Apriori, including **Partition, DHP (Direct Hashing & Pruning), DIC (Dynamic Itemset Counting), FP-Growth**, and **vertical-format methods (ECLAT)**, with emphasis on their *I/O cost* and *candidate generation strategies*.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy 🍕

> **Intuition:** Imagine you are the manager of a supermarket and you want to know which items customers frequently buy **together**. You could:
>
> 1. **Brute-Force Method (Apriori):** Walk through every possible *combination* of products in the entire store each day, count them on paper — slow, error-prone, exhausting.
> 2. **Efficient Method (FP-Growth):** Instead of counting combinations from scratch, *compress* all receipts into a single compact tree (a "fingerprint" of buying patterns). Then mine the tree by following paths, not by generating new combinations.
> 3. **Vertical Method (ECLAT):** Flip the receipt book sideways — for each product, just list the *receipt numbers* it appeared on. Finding a pair becomes a simple *intersection* of two such lists.
>
> The "Efficient and Scalable" methods are essentially **better tools for the same job** — they cut down the amount of work and paper-shuffling.

---

## 1.3 The Core Limitation Being Solved

The Apriori algorithm uses the **Apriori Property (anti-monotonicity)**: *"If an itemset is infrequent, all of its supersets are also infrequent."*

> [!WARNING]
> Despite this pruning, Apriori still suffers from:
> - **Costly candidate generation:** Up to $\sum_{k=1}^{d}\binom{d}{k}$ candidates in the worst case.
> - **Repeated full database scans:** At least $k$ scans to find frequent $k$-itemsets.
> - **Bottleneck in support counting:** Comparing every candidate against the database.
>
> Scalable methods attack these problems via *data reduction*, *pattern growth*, or *data layout change*.

---

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Metric | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $N$ | **Number of Transactions** | Total rows in database $\mathcal{D}$ |
| $d$ | **Number of Distinct Items** | Size of item universe $\mathcal{I}$ |
| $W$ | **Average Transaction Width** | Mean number of items per transaction |
| $F_k$ | **Number of Frequent $k$-Itemsets** | Output of level $k$ of FIM |
| $C_k$ | **Number of Candidate $k$-Itemsets** | Generated before support-pruning |
| $\text{min\_sup}$ | **Minimum Support Threshold** | User-defined (often 0.01 – 0.5) |
| $s$ | **Minimum Support Count** | $s = \lceil \text{min\_sup} \cdot N \rceil$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The transition $\text{min\_sup} \rightarrow s$ is critical — most scalable algorithms (FP-Growth, ECLAT) operate on **integer counts** $s$, not fractional support.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Scalable Frequent Itemset Mining Methods

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups these methods into **five strategic families**:

### 🔹 Family 1: Hash-Based Pruning — DHP (Direct Hashing & Pruning)

**Core Idea:** Use a **hash table** to filter out candidate 2-itemsets that cannot possibly be frequent *before* support counting begins.

**Operational Steps:**
1. Scan the database; for every 2-itemset $\{i_a, i_b\}$ generated from a transaction, hash it into a bucket using a hash function $h(\{i_a, i_b\}) = (i_a \cdot \text{const} + i_b) \mod B$ where $B$ is the bucket count.
2. Increment the count of the bucket.
3. After the scan, keep **only** those buckets whose count $\geq s$ (minimum support count). Itemsets in lower-count buckets are pruned.
4. The surviving (item, bucket) pairs reduce the candidate 2-itemset list drastically.

> **Why it works:** Most candidate 2-itemsets occur in only a few transactions. A hash bucket threshold filters out these "noise" candidates.

---

### 🔹 Family 2: Partition-Based Mining

**Core Idea:** Split the database into $p$ partitions that **fit into main memory**. A globally frequent itemset must be *locally frequent in at least one partition*.

**Algorithm (Savasere–Omiecinski–Navathe, 1995):**
- **Phase 1:** For each partition $\mathcal{D}_j$ ($j = 1 \ldots p$), mine all *locally frequent* itemsets using Apriori with $\text{min\_sup}_j = s \cdot \frac{\vert \mathcal{D}_j \vert}{N}$.
- **Phase 2:** Compute the *true support* of each local candidate by scanning the whole database $\mathcal{D}$. Output those with true support $\geq s$.

> **Why it's scalable:** Only **two** full database scans are needed, regardless of how long the longest frequent itemset is.

**Key Theorem:**
$$\text{support}_{\mathcal{D}}(X) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{j=1}^{p} \text{support}_{\mathcal{D}_j}(X) \cdot \vert \mathcal{D}_j \vert$$

---

### 🔹 Family 3: Sampling-Based Mining

**Core Idea:** Mine a random sample $\mathcal{S} \subset \mathcal{D}$ (with $\vert \mathcal{S}\vert \ll N$) in memory using a *lowered* support threshold $s' < s$. Then verify candidates in one full scan of $\mathcal{D}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This trades off *accuracy* (may miss some frequent itemsets) for *speed*. Often used as a *first pass*; verified results are exact after the second pass.

---

### 🔹 Family 4: Dynamic Itemset Counting (DIC)

**Core Idea:** Bracket the database into $M$ equal-sized **transaction blocks** (markers). Instead of generating $C_k$ only after the *entire* $k$-1 scan finishes, **incrementally add new items** to candidate sets as scanning proceeds through the markers.

**Notation:** After seeing the first $c$ transaction blocks (i.e., after processing $|T_{cm}|$ transactions), DIC begins counting $k$-itemsets.

**Why it works:** Earlier-pass counting helps prune dead candidates much earlier — saving memory and CPU.

---

### 🔹 Family 5: Pattern-Growth Methods — **FP-Growth** ⭐

**Core Idea:** Compress the database into a compact **FP-Tree (Frequent Pattern Tree)** and mine it by *recursive conditional pattern fragment growth* — **no candidate generation at all**.

**Two-Phase Operation:**
1. **Build FP-Tree:**
   - Scan $\mathcal{D}$ once → find frequent 1-itemsets $F_1$ (with count $\geq s$). Sort in descending frequency order.
   - Scan $\mathcal{D}$ again → insert each transaction's frequent items (in $F_1$ order) into a tree, sharing common prefixes.
2. **Mine FP-Tree:**
   - For each frequent item $a_i$ in $F_1$ (bottom-up), construct its **conditional pattern base** (set of prefix paths leading to $a_i$).
   - Build a **conditional FP-Tree** from the pattern base.
   - Recursively mine the conditional FP-Tree.

**FP-Tree Size Property:**
$$\text{Size of FP-Tree} \leq \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{(frequent items in } T_i\text{)} \ll N \cdot d$$

> **Why it scales:** The tree is typically *much smaller* than the original database (often by an order of magnitude), and *no candidates are generated*.

---

### 🔹 Family 6: Vertical Data Format — **ECLAT** (Equivalence Class CLAss Transformation)

**Core Idea:** Instead of storing transactions as rows, store each item's **TID-set** (Transaction ID list) as a column.

| Horizontal $\mathcal{D}$ | Vertical $\mathcal{D}^V$ |
|---|---|
| $T_1 = \{A, B, C\}$ | $T(A) = \{1, 3, 5\}$ |
| $T_2 = \{A, C, D\}$ | $T(B) = \{1, 2, 4\}$ |
| $T_3 = \{A, B, C\}$ | $T(C) = \{1, 3, 5\}$ |
| $T_4 = \{B, D\}$ | $T(D) = \{2, 4\}$ |

**Support Counting by Intersection:**
$$\text{support}(\{A, C\}) = \vert T(A) \cap T(C) \vert = \vert \{1, 3, 5\} \cap \{1, 3, 5\} \vert = 3$$

**Algorithm:**
- Start with frequent 1-itemsets (count $T(i)$ length).
- Generate $k$-itemsets by intersecting TID-sets of $(k-1)$-itemsets sharing a common prefix.
- Prune any whose intersection size $< s$.

> **Why it scales:** Intersection is fast, and once TID-sets are loaded, *no further database scans* are needed. Limitations: large itemsets may have huge TID-sets (memory cost).

---

## 2.2 Closed and Maximal Frequent Itemsets — Representation Refinement

As $d$ grows, $\vert \mathcal{F} \vert$ becomes enormous. **Lossless compression** is achieved via:

> [!NOTE]
> **Maximal Frequent Itemset:** A frequent itemset $X$ such that **no proper superset** of $X$ is frequent.  
> **Closed Frequent Itemset:** A frequent itemset $X$ such that **no proper superset** of $X$ has the *same* support as $X$.

**Subset Relationships (always true):**
$$\mathcal{M} \subseteq \mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathcal{F}$$

where $\mathcal{M}$ = maximal set, $\mathcal{C}$ = closed set, $\mathcal{F}$ = full set of frequent itemsets.

| Property | Maximal | Closed | Full Set |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Lossless for support? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Lossless for support count? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Compactness | ⭐⭐⭐ Most compact | ⭐⭐ Compact | ❌ Largest |

**Definition Equations:**
$$X \in \mathcal{M} \iff \text{support}(X) \geq s \ \text{and} \ \nexists Y \supsetneq X \text{ with support}(Y) \geq s$$

$$X \in \mathcal{C} \iff \text{support}(X) \geq s \ \text{and} \ \nexists Y \supsetneq X \text{ with support}(Y) = \text{support}(X)$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **minimum required mathematical toolkit** for any KTU 2024 examination question on this topic. Memorize the form, units, and applicability.

| # | Equation / Rule | Meaning / Use | Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{support}(X) = \frac{\sigma(X)}{N}$ | Fractional support of itemset $X$ | $N$ = # transactions, $\sigma(X)$ = support count |
| 2 | $s = \lceil \text{min\_sup} \cdot N \rceil$ | Convert min_sup to integer count | $s$ = min support count |
| 3 | $\vert \mathcal{L}_k \vert \leq \binom{d}{k}$ | Upper bound on $k$-frequent itemsets | $d$ = total items |
| 4 | $\vert \mathcal{C}_k \vert \leq \sum_{p,q \in \mathcal{L}_{k-1}, p[1]=q[1]} \binom{\vert p \cap q \vert}{k-2}$ | Apriori-gen upper bound | $\mathcal{L}_{k-1}$ = frequent $(k-1)$-itemsets |
| 5 | $\text{support}_{\mathcal{D}}(X) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{j=1}^{p} \sigma_{\mathcal{D}_j}(X) \cdot \vert \mathcal{D}_j \vert$ | Partition support aggregation | $p$ = # partitions |
| 6 | $\sigma(\{A,B\}) = \vert T(A) \cap T(B) \vert$ | ECLAT 2-itemset support | $T(i)$ = TID-set of item $i$ |
| 7 | $\text{Lift}(A \Rightarrow B) = \frac{\text{support}(A \cup B)}{\text{support}(A)\cdot \text{support}(B)}$ | Rule interestingness | $\text{Lift} > 1$: positive correlation |
| 8 | $\text{Confidence}(A \Rightarrow B) = \frac{\text{support}(A \cup B)}{\text{support}(A)}$ | Rule strength | Used after frequent itemsets found |
| 9 | Apriori Property | If $\text{support}(X) < s$ then $\text{support}(X') < s$ for all $X' \supseteq X$ | Monotone decreasing support |
| 10 | $\text{Tree-Node-Count} \leq \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mid T_i \mid_{freq}$ | FP-Tree size bound | $\mid T_i \mid_{freq}$ = # frequent items in $T_i$ |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Scalable FIM |
|---|---|
| **Retail / E-commerce** | Market-basket analysis, recommendation engines |
| **Bioinformatics** | Co-occurring gene mutations, protein interaction patterns |
| **Telecommunications** | Call-detail record (CDR) pattern discovery for fraud |
| **Network Intrusion Detection** | Frequent packet-signature mining in IDS |
| **Healthcare (Clinical Decision Support)** | Co-prescription patterns, adverse drug-event detection |
| **Web Mining** | Clickstream pattern mining, banner-ad placement optimization |
| **Internet of Things (IoT)** | Sensor co-occurrence patterns in smart cities |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often award 2 bonus marks for students who *briefly* map a real-world use case to a specific scalable method. Always include a one-line example in long answers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Numerical Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Apriori Algorithm (Baseline) on Sample Database

Let us first establish the **baseline** (Apriori) so that the efficiency gains of scalable methods can be appreciated.

**Sample Transaction Database $\mathcal{D}$:**

| TID | Items Bought |
|:---:|---|
| $T_1$ | Bread, Butter, Milk |
| $T_2$ | Bread, Butter |
| $T_3$ | Butter, Milk |
| $T_4$ | Bread, Milk |
| $T_5$ | Bread, Butter, Milk |
| $T_6$ | Butter, Milk |

So $N = 6$. Let $\text{min\_sup} = 50\% \Rightarrow s = \lceil 0.5 \cdot 6 \rceil = 3$.

### Step 1: Compute support of 1-itemsets

Scan $\mathcal{D}$ once and tally:

$$\sigma(\{\text{Bread}\}) = 4 \quad \text{(occurs in } T_1, T_2, T_4, T_5\text{)}$$

$$\sigma(\{\text{Butter}\}) = 5 \quad \text{(occurs in } T_1, T_2, T_3, T_5, T_6\text{)}$$

$$\sigma(\{\text{Milk}\}) = 5 \quad \text{(occurs in } T_1, T_3, T_4, T_5, T_6\text{)}$$

All are $\geq s = 3$, so:
$$\mathcal{L}_1 = \{\{\text{Bread}\}, \{\text{Butter}\}, \{\text{Milk}\}\}$$

### Step 2: Generate 2-itemset candidates $C_2$ using Apriori-gen join

Since all 1-itemsets are frequent, $C_2$ has $\binom{3}{2} = 3$ candidates:

$$C_2 = \big\{\{B, Bu\}, \{B, M\}, \{Bu, M\}\big\}$$

### Step 3: Count supports by scanning $\mathcal{D}$ again

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma(\{B, Bu\}) &= \vert\{T_1, T_2, T_5\}\vert = 3 \\
\sigma(\{B, M\}) &= \vert\{T_1, T_4, T_5\}\vert = 3 \\
\sigma(\{Bu, M\}) &= \vert\{T_1, T_3, T_5, T_6\}\vert = 4
\end{aligned}$$

All $\geq 3$, so:
$$\mathcal{L}_2 = \big\{\{B, Bu\}, \{B, M\}, \{Bu, M\}\big\}$$

### Step 4: Generate 3-itemset candidates $C_3$ via self-join on $\mathcal{L}_2$

Join $\{B, Bu\}$ with $\{B, M\}$ (common prefix $B$) → candidate $\{B, Bu, M\}$.  
Similarly $\{B, M\} \bowtie \{Bu, M\}$ (common prefix $M$... wait, common item $M$ for last two, but $Bu$ and $M$ share $M$ as last). Let us re-do:

| Join Pair | Common Prefix | Resulting Candidate |
|---|---|---|
| $\{B, Bu\} \bowtie \{B, M\}$ | $B$ | $\{B, Bu, M\}$ |
| $\{B, Bu\} \bowtie \{Bu, M\}$ | $Bu$ | $\{B, Bu, M\}$ (duplicate) |
| $\{B, M\} \bowtie \{Bu, M\}$ | $M$ | $\{B, Bu, M\}$ (duplicate) |

After Apriori-gen pruning, $C_3 = \{\{B, Bu, M\}\}$ (single candidate).

### Step 5: Prune $C_3$ using Apriori Property

The 2-subsets of $\{B, Bu, M\}$ are $\{B, Bu\}, \{B, M\}, \{Bu, M\}$ — all in $\mathcal{L}_2$. Survives.

### Step 6: Count support of $\{B, Bu, M\}$

$$\sigma(\{B, Bu, M\}) = \vert\{T_1, T_5\}\vert = 2 < 3$$

So:
$$\mathcal{L}_3 = \emptyset \implies \text{Algorithm terminates}$$

### Step 7: Cost summary (for later comparison)

- **Total database scans:** 3 (for $C_1, C_2, C_3$).
- **Candidates generated:** $\vert C_1\vert + \vert C_2\vert + \vert C_3\vert = 3 + 3 + 1 = 7$.
- **Time bottleneck:** Repeated full scans, especially the second scan which checks all 3 candidates against all 6 transactions = 18 itemset-membership checks.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU Examiner's Pitfall: Students often forget that the *number of scans = length of longest frequent itemset + 1*. This becomes a major cost on large databases — which is exactly the motivation for scalable methods.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — DHP (Direct Hashing & Pruning) Reduction

Continuing with the same $\mathcal{D}$ and $s = 3$, suppose we use a hash function with $B = 3$ buckets:
$$h(\{i, j\}) = (i + j) \mod 3$$
(using item-code mapping: B=1, Bu=2, M=3).

### Step 1: Hash all 2-itemsets in transactions

| Transaction | 2-itemsets Generated | Buckets Hit |
|---|---|---|
| $T_1$: {B, Bu, M} | {B,Bu}, {B,M}, {Bu,M} | 0, 1, 2 |
| $T_2$: {B, Bu} | {B,Bu} | 0 |
| $T_3$: {Bu, M} | {Bu,M} | 2 |
| $T_4$: {B, M} | {B,M} | 1 |
| $T_5$: {B, Bu, M} | {B,Bu}, {B,M}, {Bu,M} | 0, 1, 2 |
| $T_6$: {Bu, M} | {Bu,M} | 2 |

### Step 2: Tally bucket counts

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Bucket 0 (hashed } \{B, Bu\}\text{)} &: \{T_1, T_2, T_5\} \Rightarrow \text{count} = 3 \\
\text{Bucket 1 (hashed } \{B, M\}\text{)} &: \{T_1, T_4, T_5\} \Rightarrow \text{count} = 3 \\
\text{Bucket 2 (hashed } \{Bu, M\}\text{)} &: \{T_1, T_3, T_5, T_6\} \Rightarrow \text{count} = 4
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Apply support threshold

All buckets have counts $\geq 3$, so all candidates survive. (In larger databases, low-count buckets cause major pruning.)

> **Observation:** With a 6-transaction database, DHP saves no work here. But imagine a 1M-row database with $\text{min\_sup} = 0.01\%$ — the **bucket threshold** kills 95%+ of candidates *before* the costly count pass.

**Quantitative gain estimate:** For a database of $N = 10^6$ and $\text{min\_sup} = 0.1\%$ ($s = 1000$):
- Naive: $|C_2| \approx \binom{d}{2}$ candidates (e.g., $10^5$ for $d = 500$).
- DHP: After bucket threshold, surviving candidates $\ll 10^5$ (often 10×–100× fewer).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Partition-Based Mining

Let us split $\mathcal{D}$ into $p = 2$ partitions:

| Partition | Transactions | Size |
|---|---|---|
| $\mathcal{D}_1$ | $T_1, T_2, T_3$ | 3 |
| $\mathcal{D}_2$ | $T_4, T_5, T_6$ | 3 |

### Step 1: Compute local min_sup counts

$$s_1 = s \cdot \frac{\vert \mathcal{D}_1\vert}{N} = 3 \cdot \frac{3}{6} = 1.5 \implies \lceil s_1\rceil = 2$$

$$s_2 = s \cdot \frac{\vert \mathcal{D}_2\vert}{N} = 3 \cdot \frac{3}{6} = 1.5 \implies \lceil s_2\rceil = 2$$

### Step 2: Local mining in $\mathcal{D}_1$ (Apriori with threshold 2)

In $\mathcal{D}_1 = \{T_1, T_2, T_3\}$:
- $\sigma_1(\{B\}) = 2$, $\sigma_1(\{Bu\}) = 3$, $\sigma_1(\{M\}) = 2$ → all $\geq 2$ ✓
- $\sigma_1(\{B,Bu\}) = 2$ ✓, $\sigma_1(\{B,M\}) = 1$ ✗, $\sigma_1(\{Bu,M\}) = 2$ ✓
- $\sigma_1(\{B,Bu,M\}) = 1$ ✗

So local frequent in $\mathcal{D}_1$:
$$\mathcal{F}_1 = \big\{\{B\}, \{Bu\}, \{M\}, \{B,Bu\}, \{Bu,M\}\big\}$$

### Step 3: Local mining in $\mathcal{D}_2$ (Apriori with threshold 2)

In $\mathcal{D}_2 = \{T_4, T_5, T_6\}$:
- $\sigma_2(\{B\}) = 2$, $\sigma_2(\{Bu\}) = 2$, $\sigma_2(\{M\}) = 3$ → all $\geq 2$ ✓
- $\sigma_2(\{B,Bu\}) = 1$ ✗, $\sigma_2(\{B,M\}) = 2$ ✓, $\sigma_2(\{Bu,M\}) = 2$ ✓
- $\sigma_2(\{B,Bu,M\}) = 1$ ✗

So local frequent in $\mathcal{D}_2$:
$$\mathcal{F}_2 = \big\{\{B\}, \{Bu\}, \{M\}, \{B,M\}, \{Bu,M\}\big\}$$

### Step 4: Union of local candidates

$$\mathcal{F}_1 \cup \mathcal{F}_2 = \big\{\{B\}, \{Bu\}, \{M\}, \{B,Bu\}, \{Bu,M\}, \{B,M\}\big\}$$

### Step 5: Verify globally with one full scan of $\mathcal{D}$

We already computed the global supports:
- $\{B,Bu\}$: $\sigma = 3$ ✓ frequent
- $\{Bu,M\}$: $\sigma = 4$ ✓ frequent
- $\{B,M\}$: $\sigma = 3$ ✓ frequent

> **Key Takeaway:** Partition-based method used **2 full database scans total** — the same as Apriori for this tiny example, but **constant** regardless of longest pattern length. For 10-itemset patterns, Apriori would need 10 scans; Partition still needs only 2.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — ECLAT (Vertical Format) Mining

Using the vertical representation:

$$\begin{aligned}
T(\{B\}) &= \{T_1, T_2, T_4, T_5\} \\
T(\{Bu\}) &= \{T_1, T_2, T_3, T_5, T_6\} \\
T(\{M\}) &= \{T_1, T_3, T_4, T_5, T_6\}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 1: Frequent 1-itemsets (length $\geq 3$)

All three are frequent (lengths 4, 5, 5).

### Step 2: Generate 2-itemsets by intersection

$$\begin{aligned}
T(\{B,Bu\}) &= T(\{B\}) \cap T(\{Bu\}) = \{T_1, T_2, T_4, T_5\} \cap \{T_1, T_2, T_3, T_5, T_6\} = \{T_1, T_2, T_5\} \\
&\implies \sigma = 3 \geq 3 \text{ ✓ frequent} \\[6pt]
T(\{B,M\}) &= \{T_1, T_2, T_4, T_5\} \cap \{T_1, T_3, T_4, T_5, T_6\} = \{T_1, T_4, T_5\} \\
&\implies \sigma = 3 \geq 3 \text{ ✓ frequent} \\[6pt]
T(\{Bu,M\}) &= \{T_1, T_2, T_3, T_5, T_6\} \cap \{T_1, T_3, T_4, T_5, T_6\} = \{T_1, T_3, T_5, T_6\} \\
&\implies \sigma = 4 \geq 3 \text{ ✓ frequent}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: 3-itemset via deeper intersection

$$T(\{B,Bu,M\}) = T(\{B,Bu\}) \cap T(\{M\}) = \{T_1, T_2, T_5\} \cap \{T_1, T_3, T_4, T_5, T_6\} = \{T_1, T_5\}$$

$\sigma = 2 < 3$, so $\{B,Bu,M\}$ is **not** frequent.

### Step 4: Cost comparison

| Method | Database Scans | Per-candidate work |
|---|---|---|
| Apriori | 3 | Subset check vs. 6 transactions |
| DHP | 2 (count) + 1 (build) | Hash lookup |
| Partition | 2 | Per-partition Apriori + 1 verification |
| **ECLAT** | **1 (build TID-sets)** | **TID-set intersection** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ECLAT's killer feature: **One-time database load** into vertical format, then *all* support counts via fast set intersection. Memory cost can become a bottleneck for very dense or very long patterns.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — FP-Growth Tree Construction

Same $\mathcal{D}$ and $s = 3$. Items sorted by frequency descending: Butter (5), Milk (5), Bread (4). So the global ordering is **Bu > M > B**.

### Step 1: First scan — collect frequent 1-itemsets and sort

$\mathcal{L}_1 = \{\{Bu:5\}, \{M:5\}, \{B:4\}\}$

### Step 2: Second scan — build FP-Tree

For each transaction, keep only frequent items in the order **Bu, M, B**:

| Original $T$ | Filtered & Sorted | Inserted Path |
|---|---|---|
| $T_1$: B, Bu, M | Bu, M, B | Root → Bu:1 → M:1 → B:1 |
| $T_2$: B, Bu | Bu, B | Root → Bu:2 → B:1 |
| $T_3$: Bu, M | Bu, M | Root → Bu:3 → M:2 |
| $T_4$: B, M | M, B | Root → M:3 → B:2 (but M has new branch from root) |
| $T_5$: B, Bu, M | Bu, M, B | Root → Bu:4 → M:3 → B:3 |
| $T_6$: Bu, M | Bu, M | Root → Bu:5 → M:4 |

**Final FP-Tree (after combining shared prefixes):**

```
Root
  |
  +-- Bu:5
  |     |
  |     +-- M:4
  |     |    |
  |     |    +-- B:3
  |
  +-- M:1   (separate branch from T_4 alone)
        |
        +-- B:1
```

**Header Table (pointers to leaves):**

| Item | Count | Node Pointer |
|---|---|---|
| Bu | 5 | → Bu:5 |
| M | 5 | → M:4 → M:1 |
| B | 4 | → B:3 → B:1 |

### Step 3: Mine frequent patterns

**Item B (count 4):**
- Conditional pattern base: all paths ending in B → `{Bu, M:3}, {M:1}`.
- Conditional FP-Tree: from `{Bu, M:3}` and `{M:1}` → tree with M:4, then Bu:3.
- Frequent patterns with B: $\{B\}$, $\{B, M\}$, $\{B, Bu\}$.

**Item M (count 5):**
- Conditional pattern base: `{Bu:4}`.
- Conditional FP-Tree: single node Bu:4.
- Frequent patterns with M: $\{M\}$, $\{M, Bu\}$.

**Item Bu (count 5):**
- Pattern: $\{Bu\}$.

**Consolidated frequent itemsets (no candidates generated!):**
$$\mathcal{F} = \{\{Bu\}, \{M\}, \{B\}, \{M,Bu\}, \{B,M\}, \{B,Bu\}\}$$

> **Key Advantage:** Zero candidate generation; the entire mining is done via *tree traversal*. The compressed tree is typically 10–100× smaller than the original database for sparse, skewed data.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Method vs. Property

| Method | DB Scans | Candidate Generation? | Memory Pattern | Best For |
|---|---|:---:|---|---|
| **Apriori** | $k_{\max}+1$ | Yes (Apriori-gen) | Low (candidates) | Small/medium $d$, educational |
| **DHP** | $k_{\max}+1$ (with 1 hash build) | Yes (filtered) | Low + hash table | Sparse data, lots of low-support 2-itemsets |
| **Partition** | **2 (constant)** | Yes (per partition) | Per-partition | Distributed/large $N$, long patterns |
| **Sampling** | 1 (sample) + 1 (verify) | Yes (on sample) | Sample in memory | Fast approximate answer |
| **DIC** | 1 (interleaved) | Yes (incremental) | $C_k$ with markers | Streaming-like, low latency |
| **FP-Growth** | 2 (build) | **NO** | **FP-Tree** (compact) | Dense, low min_sup, large $N$ |
| **ECLAT** | 1 (vertical load) | Yes (TID intersections) | TID-sets (potentially huge) | Small/medium $d$, dense data |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 14-mark questions often require a *comparative analysis table* like this. Always conclude with a clear "Best for X" column — it shows the examiner you understand *trade-offs*, not just definitions.

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Closed vs. Maximal Frequent Itemsets (Lossless Compression Hierarchy)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FS["Full Frequent Set F - largest"]
    CL["Closed Frequent Set C - subset of F"]
    MX["Maximal Frequent Set M - subset of C"]
    FS -->|"X is in F means support X >= s"| CL
    CL -->|"X is in C means no proper superset has same support as X"| MX
    MX -->|"X is in M means no proper superset is frequent at all"| End["Most compact representation"]

```

> [!NOTE]
> The **closed set** is often preferred in practice because it preserves **support counts** (lossless), whereas the maximal set does not. KTU examiners love to test this distinction.

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Differentiate between frequent itemset, closed frequent itemset, and maximal frequent itemset. Which representation is lossless with respect to support counts? Justify with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Definition | Lossless? |
|---|---|---|
| **Frequent Itemset** | $X$ such that $\text{support}(X) \geq s$ | – (reference) |
| **Closed Frequent Itemset** | A frequent $X$ such that **no proper superset** $Y \supsetneq X$ has $\text{support}(Y) = \text{support}(X)$ | ✅ **Yes** (supports fully recoverable) |
| **Maximal Frequent Itemset** | A frequent $X$ such that **no proper superset** $Y \supsetneq X$ is frequent at all | ❌ No (only know supports $\geq s$, not exact) |

**Example:** Let $s = 3$ and suppose itemsets $\{A, B\}$ and $\{A, B, C\}$ both have support 3.
- $\{A, B\}$ is **closed** because $\{A, B, C\}$ has *different* support (say 2 < 3) — but if it had the *same* support, then $\{A, B\}$ would not be closed.
- If no superset of $\{A, B, C\}$ is frequent, then $\{A, B, C\}$ is **maximal**.

The **closed set** is lossless because every frequent itemset and its support can be derived from the closed set using the property: $\text{support}(X) = \max\{\text{support}(Y) : Y \in \mathcal{C}, Y \subseteq X\}$.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Explain how the FP-Growth algorithm avoids the candidate generation problem of Apriori. What is the role of the FP-Tree header table?**

**Model Answer:**

Apriori generates $C_k$ candidates by self-joining $L_{k-1}$ frequent $(k-1)$-itemsets, which can explode combinatorially. **FP-Growth eliminates candidate generation entirely** by:

1. **Compressing** the database into a compact **FP-Tree** that retains all information necessary for frequent-pattern mining (frequent items, their counts, and co-occurrence order).
2. **Mining recursively** via *conditional pattern bases* — for each frequent item $a_i$, extract the set of prefix paths leading to $a_i$, build a *conditional FP-Tree*, and mine it.
3. **No candidates are ever enumerated** — patterns emerge from concatenating item $a_i$ with patterns found in the conditional FP-Tree.

**Role of the Header Table:**
- A list of all frequent 1-itemsets in frequency-descending order, each with a **head pointer** to its first occurrence in the FP-Tree.
- **Node-links** (dashed arrows in textbook diagrams) connect all nodes for the same item across the tree.
- This allows the algorithm to **traverse all paths ending in $a_i$** efficiently without re-scanning the tree.
- It is the only data structure that connects the *tree* to the *global item ordering*, enabling the divide-and-conquer pattern-growth strategy.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Module 1 FIM):**
> - Students routinely lose **2–3 marks** for not clearly distinguishing *candidate generation* from *candidate pruning* — state both.
> - For numerical examples, you **must** show the **threshold $s$ calculation** at the top, not bury it.
> - In comparison questions, students list methods but forget to give a **quantitative** comparison (e.g., "DB scans: Apriori = 4, FP-Growth = 2"). Always include a comparison table.
> - For ECLAT, students confuse **TID-set** with transaction content — be explicit: *TID-set = list of transaction IDs containing the item*.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Scalable Method Comparison with Numerical Demonstration**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**
>
> **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Consider the following transaction database with $N = 8$ transactions and $\text{min\_sup} = 50\%$. Apply the **ECLAT algorithm** to find all frequent itemsets. Show the TID-set construction and intersection steps explicitly.
>
> | TID | Items |
> |:---:|---|
> | $T_1$ | I1, I2, I5 |
> | $T_2$ | I2, I4 |
> | $T_3$ | I2, I3 |
> | $T_4$ | I1, I2, I4 |
> | $T_5$ | I1, I3 |
> | $T_6$ | I2, I3 |
> | $T_7$ | I1, I3 |
> | $T_8$ | I1, I2, I3, I5 |
>
> **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Compare ECLAT with **FP-Growth** in terms of: (i) data layout, (ii) candidate generation, (iii) memory usage, (iv) database scan count, and (v) suitability for dense vs. sparse data. Justify which is more appropriate for a real-world grocery database with 10 million transactions.

#### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Compute min support count $s$.**
$$s = \lceil \text{min\_sup} \cdot N \rceil = \lceil 0.5 \cdot 8 \rceil = 4$$

**Step 2: Construct TID-sets for each 1-itemset (vertical format).**

| Item | TID-set | Count |
|---|---|---|
| I1 | $\{T_1, T_4, T_5, T_7, T_8\}$ | 5 |
| I2 | $\{T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4, T_6, T_8\}$ | 6 |
| I3 | $\{T_3, T_5, T_6, T_7, T_8\}$ | 5 |
| I4 | $\{T_2, T_4\}$ | 2 |
| I5 | $\{T_1, T_8\}$ | 2 |

**Step 3: Apply threshold $s = 4$ for level-1.**
- I1: 5 ≥ 4 ✓ frequent
- I2: 6 ≥ 4 ✓ frequent
- I3: 5 ≥ 4 ✓ frequent
- I4: 2 < 4 ✗ pruned
- I5: 2 < 4 ✗ pruned

**$\mathcal{L}_1 = \{\{I1\}, \{I2\}, \{I3\}\}$**

**Step 4: Generate 2-itemsets by intersecting TID-sets of $\mathcal{L}_1$ pairs.**

$$T(\{I1, I2\}) = T(I1) \cap T(I2) = \{T_1, T_4, T_5, T_7, T_8\} \cap \{T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4, T_6, T_8\} = \{T_1, T_4, T_8\}$$
$\sigma = 3 < 4$ ✗

$$T(\{I1, I3\}) = \{T_1, T_4, T_5, T_7, T_8\} \cap \{T_3, T_5, T_6, T_7, T_8\} = \{T_5, T_7, T_8\}$$
$\sigma = 3 < 4$ ✗

$$T(\{I2, I3\}) = \{T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4, T_6, T_8\} \cap \{T_3, T_5, T_6, T_7, T_8\} = \{T_3, T_6, T_8\}$$
$\sigma = 3 < 4$ ✗

**$\mathcal{L}_2 = \emptyset$ — algorithm terminates.**

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{\mathcal{F} = \{\{I1\}, \{I2\}, \{I3\}\}}$$

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]

| Criterion | ECLAT | FP-Growth |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Data Layout** | Vertical (TID-sets per item) | Horizontal compressed into FP-Tree |
| **(ii) Candidate Generation** | Yes — by TID-set intersection | **No** — patterns grown from tree |
| **(iii) Memory Usage** | Can be high — TID-sets of frequent items can be very long | FP-Tree size bounded by $\sum \mid T_i \mid_{freq}$; compact for skewed data |
| **(iv) DB Scans** | **1** (single pass to build TID-sets) | **2** (one for $L_1$, one to build tree) |
| **(v) Suitability** | Dense data with short patterns | Sparse data, low min_sup, long patterns |

**Justification for 10M-transaction grocery database:**

For a **real-world grocery database with 10 million transactions**:
- The data is **highly sparse** (typical basket size = 10–50 items out of $d \approx 10{,}000$).
- Frequent itemsets tend to be **short** (3–6 items).
- **FP-Growth** is the better choice because:
  1. Its FP-Tree compression is *extremely* effective on sparse, skewed retail data — the tree size is typically 1–5% of the original database.
  2. It avoids the *memory blow-up* of ECLAT, whose TID-sets for popular items (e.g., "Bread") may contain millions of transaction IDs.
  3. No candidate generation → much faster on large databases with low min_sup.

ECLAT, by contrast, is preferred when the data is **dense** and the number of items $d$ is **small** (e.g., bioinformatics, where patterns are long and itemsets are concentrated).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Partition + Closed Patterns + Engineering Application**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
>
> **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **Partition Algorithm** (Savasere–Omiecinski–Navathe) for mining frequent itemsets. With a suitable example transaction database of 6 transactions, demonstrate that the algorithm requires only **two database scans** to find all frequent itemsets, even if the longest frequent itemset has length 5.
>
> **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Discuss the concept of **Closed Frequent Itemsets** and **Maximal Frequent Itemsets**. Why are they important for scalable mining? Provide a small example with min_sup = 2 on the database: $T_1 = \{A, B, C\}, T_2 = \{A, B\}, T_3 = \{A, B, C, D\}, T_4 = \{A, B, C, D\}$.

#### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Partition Algorithm – Key Idea (S.O.N., 1995):**

Split $\mathcal{D}$ into $p$ disjoint partitions $\mathcal{D}_1, \mathcal{D}_2, \ldots, \mathcal{D}_p$ such that each $\mathcal{D}_j$ fits in main memory.

**Phase 1 — Local Mining:**
For each partition $\mathcal{D}_j$, find all **locally frequent** itemsets with a *relaxed* threshold:
$$s_j = s \cdot \frac{\vert \mathcal{D}_j \vert}{N}$$
Use any in-memory algorithm (e.g., Apriori, FP-Growth) on each partition. This is a *local* count.

**Phase 2 — Global Verification:**
Take the **union** of all local frequent sets across partitions as the *global candidate set* $\mathcal{C}_G$. Make **one full pass** over $\mathcal{D}$ to compute the true support of every $X \in \mathcal{C}_G$. Output those with true support $\geq s$.

**Why only 2 scans?**
- Phase 1 reads each partition once: $p$ sub-scans, but $\sum_j |\mathcal{D}_j| = N$, equivalent to **one logical scan of D** for reading.
- Phase 2 = **one full scan of D**.
- Total = **2 database scans**, *independent* of the longest pattern length.

**Demonstration on a sample 6-transaction database:**

Let $\mathcal{D}$ be partitioned into 3 partitions of 2 transactions each, with $s = 3$ and $N = 6$.

| Partition | TIDs | Local threshold $s_j$ |
|---|---|---|
| $\mathcal{D}_1$ | $T_1, T_2$ | $\lceil 3 \cdot 2/6\rceil = 1$ |
| $\mathcal{D}_2$ | $T_3, T_4$ | $\lceil 3 \cdot 2/6\rceil = 1$ |
| $\mathcal{D}_3$ | $T_5, T_6$ | $\lceil 3 \cdot 2/6\rceil = 1$ |

Suppose $\mathcal{D}_1$ locally yields: $\{A\}, \{B\}, \{A,B\}, \{A,B,C\}$.  
$\mathcal{D}_2$ locally yields: $\{A\}, \{C\}, \{A,C\}, \{A,B,C\}$.  
$\mathcal{D}_3$ locally yields: $\{B\}, \{C\}, \{B,C\}$.

Union of local candidates:
$$\mathcal{C}_G = \{A, B, C, AB, AC, BC, ABC\}$$

**One final scan** of $\mathcal{D}$ confirms which are globally frequent (with true $\sigma \geq 3$). Done.

**Conclusion:** Regardless of whether the longest frequent pattern is 1, 5, or 10 items, **only 2 scans of the full database are needed**.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Definitions:**
- **Closed Frequent Itemset $X$:** A frequent itemset such that there is no proper superset $Y \supsetneq X$ with $\text{support}(Y) = \text{support}(X)$.
- **Maximal Frequent Itemset $X$:** A frequent itemset such that there is no proper superset $Y \supsetneq X$ that is *frequent at all* (regardless of support value).

**Why They Are Important for Scalable Mining:**

1. **Lossless Compression:** The full set $\mathcal{F}$ can have exponentially many itemsets. Closed/maximal sets *summarize* $\mathcal{F}$ without losing essential information.
2. **Closed set is lossless with respect to support counts** — $\mathcal{F}$ and the support of every itemset can be reconstructed from $\mathcal{C}$.
3. **Maximal set is the most compact** but loses exact support counts (only "$\geq s$" is known).
4. **Algorithm Enablers:** Methods like **CHARM**, **CLOSET**, **CLOSET+** mine closed/maximal itemsets directly using FP-Tree variants, achieving orders-of-magnitude speedup over Apriori for dense data.

**Example with min_sup = 2 ($N = 4$):**

Support counts:
- $\sigma(\{A\}) = 4$, $\sigma(\{B\}) = 4$, $\sigma(\{C\}) = 3$, $\sigma(\{D\}) = 2$
- $\sigma(\{A,B\}) = 4$, $\sigma(\{A,C\}) = 3$, $\sigma(\{A,D\}) = 2$, $\sigma(\{B,C\}) = 3$, $\sigma(\{B,D\}) = 2$, $\sigma(\{C,D\}) = 2$
- $\sigma(\{A,B,C\}) = 3$, $\sigma(\{A,B,D\}) = 2$, $\sigma(\{A,C,D\}) = 2$, $\sigma(\{B,C,D\}) = 2$
- $\sigma(\{A,B,C,D\}) = 2$

All itemsets $\geq 2$ are frequent. Now identifying structures:

**Maximal Frequent Itemsets (no frequent superset):**
- $\{A, B, C, D\}$ has no superset → **maximal**.
- This is the **only** maximal itemset.

**Closed Frequent Itemsets (no superset has same support):**
- $\{A, B, C, D\}$ with support 2: check supersets — none. **Closed** ✓
- $\{A, B, C\}$ with support 3: superset $\{A,B,C,D\}$ has support 2 ≠ 3. **Closed** ✓
- $\{A, B, D\}$ with support 2: superset $\{A,B,C,D\}$ has support 2 = 2. ✗ **NOT closed**
- Similarly $\{A,C,D\}$ and $\{B,C,D\}$ are not closed.
- $\{A, B\}$ with support 4: superset $\{A,B,C\}$ has support 3 ≠ 4. **Closed** ✓
- $\{C\}$ with support 3: superset $\{A,C\}$ has support 3 = 3. ✗ NOT closed

**Closed Set:**
$$\mathcal{C} = \big\{\{A,B\}, \{A,B,C\}, \{A,B,C,D\}\big\}$$

**Verification — Lossless Recovery:**
- $\text{support}(\{A,B,D\}) = \text{support}(\{A,B,C,D\}) = 2$ ✓
- $\text{support}(\{A,B\}) = 4$ (from closed set $\{A,B\}$) ✓
- $\text{support}(\{C\}) = \text{support}(\{A,C\}) = 3$ ✓
- Every frequent itemset's support is recoverable — confirming losslessness.

**Insight:** From 15 frequent itemsets, we reduced to **3 closed** or **1 maximal** itemset — a 5×–15× compression.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 1: Efficient & Scalable FIM**

- ✅ **Frequent Itemset:** $X$ with $\sigma(X) \geq s$ where $s = \lceil \text{min\_sup} \cdot N \rceil$.
- ✅ **Apriori Property (anti-monotonicity):** Subsets of frequent sets are frequent; supersets of infrequent sets are infrequent. This is the **foundation** of all candidate-pruning methods.
- ✅ **Apriori's Two Bottlenecks:** (1) Candidate generation, (2) Repeated full DB scans.
- ✅ **DHP:** Uses a **hash table** to prune 2-itemset candidates via bucket-count thresholding.
- ✅ **Partition Algorithm (SON):** Always **2 DB scans** total, regardless of longest pattern length. Phase 1 = local mining in each partition; Phase 2 = global verification.
- ✅ **Sampling (Toivonen):** Mine a small sample with *lowered* support, then verify globally. Risk: **may miss** some frequent itemsets (false negatives only).
- ✅ **DIC:** Adds new items to candidate sets *incrementally* as scans pass markers, achieving earlier pruning.
- ✅ **FP-Growth:** Builds compact **FP-Tree** (no candidate generation). Recursive **conditional pattern growth** on conditional FP-Trees. Best for **sparse, low-min_sup** data.
- ✅ **ECLAT:** Vertical data format; supports computed by **TID-set intersection**. Best for **dense data** with **short patterns**. Memory can be a bottleneck.
- ✅ **Closed Frequent Itemset:** No proper superset with *same support* → **lossless** for support counts.
- ✅ **Maximal Frequent Itemset:** No proper superset is *frequent at all* → most compact, but **lossy** for exact supports.
- ✅ **Hierarchy:** $\mathcal{M} \subseteq \mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathcal{F}$.
- ✅ **Cross-Itemset Support Computation (ECLAT):** $\sigma(\{A, B\}) = \vert T(A) \cap T(B)\vert$.
- ✅ **Association Rule Confidence:** $\text{Conf}(A \Rightarrow B) = \frac{\text{support}(A \cup B)}{\text{support}(A)}$.
- ✅ **Lift Interpretation:** $\text{Lift}(A \Rightarrow B) > 1$ → positive correlation; $= 1$ → independence; $< 1$ → negative correlation.
- ✅ **Engineering Applications to remember:** Market-basket analysis, bioinformatics gene patterns, telecom fraud, web clickstream, IoT sensor co-occurrence, drug-adverse event mining.
- ✅ **Examiner's Pet Trick:** "Which method needs the fewest DB scans?" → **Partition (2 scans, constant)**.
- ✅ **Examiner's Pet Trick 2:** "Which method generates NO candidates?" → **FP-Growth**.
- ✅ **Examiner's Pet Trick 3:** "Which method uses TID-set intersections?" → **ECLAT**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** In 14-mark questions, always conclude with a **comparison table** and a **real-world application sentence**. Examiners explicitly award marks for *trade-off analysis* and *engineering context awareness*, not just definitions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mining Various Kinds of Association Rules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT604 - DATA MINING AND WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mining Various Kinds of Association Rules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mining Various Kinds of Association Rules** refers to the family of extended association analysis techniques that go beyond simple **single-dimensional, single-level, boolean rule mining**. It encompasses techniques for extracting patterns from data that have *hierarchical taxonomies*, *multiple attributes*, *numeric/quantitative dimensions*, and *correlation strength metrics*. According to the KTU 2024 Module-1 syllabus, this includes **Multilevel Associations, Multidimensional Associations, Quantitative Association Rules, Correlation Mining**, and **Constraint-Based Association Mining**.

In the KTU outcome-based framework, the goal is to identify interesting relationships among itemsets where the items themselves may belong to:
- Different **abstraction levels** (e.g., Brand → Category → Sub-category).
- Multiple **dimensions** (e.g., Age, Income, Buys).
- **Continuous/numeric** value ranges rather than just Boolean presence.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a supermarket chain that initially discovers a simple rule:
> *Customers who buy **bread** also buy **butter**.*

Now, real life is messier. What if the manager asks:

1. *"Does the rule still hold across **levels** — say, dairy products instead of butter?"* → **Multilevel**
2. *"Does this depend on **who** buys — age, income, day of week?"* → **Multidimensional**
3. *"What if the customer buys 3-5 loaves vs. 1 loaf — does the pattern change?"* → **Quantitative**
4. *"Is the rule actually **meaningful** or just a coincidence (correlated)?"* → **Correlation Analysis**
5. *"Can I ask only for **expensive items** bought on **weekends**?"* → **Constraint-Based**

This is exactly the landscape of "**various kinds**" of association mining — extending a single Boolean market-basket analysis into a powerful, real-world analytical toolkit.

### 1.3 Why This Matters in KTU 2024 Engineering Context

> [!NOTE]
> The basic Apriori / FP-Growth algorithm you learned in Module 1's first topics assumes *flat*, *boolean*, *single-dimensional* data. **Real transactional, retail, IoT, and biomedical databases are NOT flat.** Hence the KTU 2024 scheme specifically mandates these extended techniques — they bridge the gap between textbook algorithms and **deployable industry-grade mining**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Association Rule Variants

The extended association-rule family can be broken down along **four orthogonal axes**:

| Axis | Variants | Key Idea |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Abstraction Level** | Single-level vs **Multilevel** | Mine at multiple hierarchical resolutions |
| **Dimensionality** | Single-dim vs **Multidimensional** | Use ≥ 2 predicates in rule (Age, Buys) |
| **Data Type** | Boolean vs **Quantitative** | Numeric attributes discretized into ranges |
| **Interestingness** | Support-Confidence vs **Correlation (Lift, χ²)** | Filter rules that are *statistically* meaningful |
| **User Control** | Unconstrained vs **Constraint-Based** | Push user predicates (e.g., price > 100) |

### 2.2 Multilevel Association Rules

A **taxonomy** (is-a hierarchy) over items is given, e.g.,
> *Computer → Laptop → Gaming-Laptop* or *Milk → Dairy-Product → Butter*.

A **multilevel association rule** has the antecedent/consequent drawn from different levels of this hierarchy:

$$\text{buys}(\text{``Computer"}) \Rightarrow \text{buys}(\text{``HP Printer"})$$

**Two Minimum Support Strategies:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Uniform Minimum Support (UMS):** Same `minsup` at every level. Simple, but lower-level items rarely cross the threshold (too specific).
> - **Reduced Minimum Support (RMS):** `minsup` decreases at lower levels. Captures fine-grained rules but may explode combinatorial complexity.

**Two Mining Strategies:**

- **Level-Shared Mining:** First find high-level frequent itemsets, then descend only into their children. *Efficient but may miss cross-level rules.*
- **Level-Cross-Filtering:** Itemsets at level *k+1* must have their parents frequent at level *k*. **Anti-monotone** property on the item taxonomy.

### 2.3 Multidimensional Association Rules

A rule involving **two or more dimensions** (attributes) of the database:

$$\text{age}(X, \text{``30..40"}) \land \text{income}(X, \text{``42K..48K"}) \Rightarrow \text{buys}(X, \text{``Laptop"})$$

- **Inter-dimensional:** No repeated predicates — `age` and `buys`.
- **Intra-dimensional hybrid:** Same dimension repeated — `age(X,30..40) ∧ age(X,41..50)`.

### 2.4 Quantitative Association Rules

Numeric attributes are handled by **discretization** (binning) before mining:

$$\text{income}(X, \text{``40K..50K"}) \Rightarrow \text{buys}(X, \text{``Laptop"})$$

**Discretization Techniques:**

| Technique | Description |
|:---|:---|
| **Static Discretization** | Pre-defined bins (e.g., $40K-$50K, $50K-$60K) using domain knowledge |
| **Dynamic Discretization** | Adjacent bins merged if both pass `minsup`; refined using `minconf` (e.g., Srikant-Agrawal method) |
| **Clustering-based** | Apply 1-D clustering (e.g., equi-depth) on numeric attribute |
| **Statistical Theory** | Use partial completeness (PC) principle to control information loss |

> [!NOTE]
> A common pitfall: naive equi-width binning can miss "**interesting**" rare ranges. KTU board questions often test *why* dynamic discretization is preferred.

### 2.5 Mining Interesting Correlations

Support-Confidence **can be misleading**. A classic example:

> *buys(tea) ⇒ buys(coffee)* has **80% confidence** with **support 20%**.
> But 75% of all customers buy coffee anyway!

The rule is **not interesting** because tea doesn't *increase* the likelihood of coffee.

**Key Metrics Formulas:**

| Metric | Formula | Meaning |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Support** $s(X \Rightarrow Y)$ | $P(X \cup Y)$ | Joint probability |
| **Confidence** $c(X \Rightarrow Y)$ | $\frac{P(X \cup Y)}{P(X)}$ | Conditional probability |
| **Lift** $\text{Lift}(X \Rightarrow Y)$ | $\frac{P(Y \mid X)}{P(Y)} = \frac{c(X \Rightarrow Y)}{s(Y)}$ | Correlation ratio |
| **χ² (Chi-Square)** | $\sum \frac{(O-E)^2}{E}$ | Independence test statistic |
| **All-Confidence** | $\frac{s(X \cup Y)}{\max\{s(X), s(Y)\}}$ | For cross-support patterns |
| **Cosine** | $\frac{s(X \cup Y)}{\sqrt{s(X) \cdot s(Y)}}$ | Geometric similarity |
| **Max-Confidence** | $\min\{c(X \Rightarrow Y), c(Y \Rightarrow X)\}$ | Symmetric confidence |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lift Interpretation:** $\text{Lift} > 1$ → positive correlation, $= 1$ → independence, $< 1$ → negative correlation. This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question**.

### 2.6 Constraint-Based Association Mining

Users express rules of interest via **rule constraints** pushed deep into the mining algorithm.

**Taxonomy of Constraints:**

| Constraint Type | Definition | Example |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Anti-Monotone** | If $S$ violates, so does any superset of $S$ | $\text{sum}(\text{price}) \le 500$ |
| **Monotone** | If $S$ satisfies, so does any superset | $\text{min}(\text{price}) \ge 100$ |
| **Succinct** | Can be enforced by direct selection on data | $\text{type} = \text{``Snack"}$ |
| **Convertible Anti-Monotone** | Anti-monotone after item ordering | $\text{avg}(\text{price}) \le 50$ (ordered by price asc) |
| **Convertible Monotone** | Monotone after item ordering | $\text{avg}(\text{price}) \ge 50$ |
| **Data Anti-Monotone** | Prunable at scan level using data alone | $\text{min}(\text{Qty}) \ge 2$ |

**Real-World Utility:** Used in retail (filter by region), bioinformatics (filter by gene-expression threshold), and telecom (filter by call-duration).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Lift-Based Correlation Mining

Suppose we have a transaction database of **10,000** customers with the following counts:

| Itemset | Count |
|:---|:---|
| Total customers | $N = 10{,}000$ |
| Buys **A** (laptop) | $n(A) = 6{,}000$ |
| Buys **B** (printer) | $n(B) = 7{,}500$ |
| Buys both **A and B** | $n(A \cup B) = 4{,}500$ |

**Step 1 — Compute individual supports (probabilities):**

$$s(A) = \frac{n(A)}{N} = \frac{6000}{10000} = 0.60$$

$$s(B) = \frac{n(B)}{N} = \frac{7500}{10000} = 0.75$$

**Step 2 — Compute joint support:**

$$s(A \cup B) = \frac{n(A \cup B)}{N} = \frac{4500}{10000} = 0.45$$

**Step 3 — Compute confidence of rule $A \Rightarrow B$:**

$$c(A \Rightarrow B) = \frac{s(A \cup B)}{s(A)} = \frac{0.45}{0.60} = 0.75$$

**Step 4 — Compute Lift:**

$$\text{Lift}(A \Rightarrow B) = \frac{c(A \Rightarrow B)}{s(B)} = \frac{0.75}{0.75} = 1.00$$

**Interpretation:**

$$\text{Lift} = 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; A \text{ and } B \text{ are statistically INDEPENDENT}$$

Despite 75% confidence, the rule is **NOT interesting** because B is already bought by 75% of customers — A gives **zero extra predictive power**.

**Step 5 — Re-check using all-confidence (handles cross-support patterns):**

$$\text{all-conf}(A, B) = \frac{s(A \cup B)}{\max\{s(A), s(B)\}} = \frac{0.45}{\max\{0.60, 0.75\}} = \frac{0.45}{0.75} = 0.60$$

If a cross-support threshold $h = 0.40$, then all-conf = 0.60 ≥ 0.40, so the rule **passes** the cross-support filter (no rare-item masking).

### 3.2 Worked Example: Multilevel Association Mining

**Taxonomy:**
> *Milk* → *Dairy* → *Food*

Transaction Table (Level-wise counts):

| Level | Item | Count |
|:---|:---|:---|
| L2 | Food | 8000 |
| L1 | Dairy | 6500 |
| L0 | Milk | 3000 |
| L0 | Butter | 4500 |
| L1 | Bread | 5500 |

Set level-wise `minsup` (RMS strategy):
- L2: 12%
- L1: 7%
- L0: 3%

Compute support % at each level:

$$s_{L_2}(\text{Food}) = \frac{8000}{10000} = 80\% \;(\ge 12\%) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Frequent}$$

$$s_{L_1}(\text{Dairy}) = \frac{6500}{10000} = 65\% \;(\ge 7\%) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Frequent}$$

$$s_{L_1}(\text{Bread}) = \frac{5500}{10000} = 55\% \;(\ge 7\%) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Frequent}$$

Now mine cross-level association:

$$s(\text{Dairy}, \text{Bread}) = \frac{3500}{10000} = 35\% \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Frequent at L1}$$

$$c(\text{Dairy} \Rightarrow \text{Bread}) = \frac{0.35}{0.65} \approx 0.538 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Strong rule}$$

**Multilevel Rule:**

$$\boxed{\text{buys}(\text{Dairy}) \Rightarrow \text{buys}(\text{Bread}) \quad [\,s=35\%,\;c=53.8\%\,]}$$

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Constraint Propagation Example

Given constraint: $\text{sum}(\text{price}) \le 500$ over items {A, B, C, D, E} with prices {50, 80, 120, 200, 300}.

| Step | Itemset | Sum | Status |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | {A} | 50 | ✓ Satisfies |
| 2 | {B} | 80 | ✓ Satisfies |
| 3 | {A,B} | 130 | ✓ Satisfies |
| 4 | {C} | 120 | ✓ Satisfies |
| 5 | {A,B,C} | 250 | ✓ Satisfies |
| 6 | {A,B,C,D} | 450 | ✓ Satisfies |
| 7 | {A,B,C,D,E} | 750 | ✗ Violates (Sum > 500) |
| 8 | {D} | 200 | ✓ |
| 9 | {A,D} | 250 | ✓ |
| 10 | {E} | 300 | ✓ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Once an itemset violates `sum ≤ 500` (an **anti-monotone** constraint), **all its supersets are automatically pruned** — no need to check them. This is the power of constraint-push into Apriori-style mining.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Approaches

| Criterion | Multilevel | Multidim. | Quantitative | Correlation | Constraint-Based |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Complexity** | High | Medium | High | Low | Variable |
| **Data Prep** | Taxonomy | Attribute Schema | Discretization | None | Predicate Index |
| **Rule Quality** | Hierarchical | Rich Context | Numeric | Statistically Valid | User-Centric |
| **Computation Cost** | High (multiple passes) | Medium | High (binning) | Low (post-filter) | Low (pruning) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Taxonomy Diagram — Mining Various Association Rules

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Association Rule Mining"] --> B["Single-Level Boolean"]
    A --> C["Various Kinds - Extended"]
    C --> D["Multilevel Mining"]
    C --> E["Multidimensional Mining"]
    C --> F["Quantitative Mining"]
    C --> G["Correlation Analysis"]
    C --> H["Constraint Based Mining"]
    D --> D1["Uniform Min Support"]
    D --> D2["Reduced Min Support"]
    D --> D3["Level Cross Filtering"]
    E --> E1["Inter Dimensional"]
    E --> E2["Intra Dimensional Hybrid"]
    F --> F1["Static Discretization"]
    F --> F2["Dynamic Discretization"]
    F --> F3["Clustering Based"]
    G --> G1["Lift Metric"]
    G --> G2["Chi Square Test"]
    G --> G3["Cosine All Conf"]
    H --> H1["Anti Monotone"]
    H --> H2["Monotone Succinct"]
    H --> H3["Convertible Constraints"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Constraint-Pushed Mining Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1[Raw Transactional DB] --> step2[Constraint Specification Module]
    step2 --> step3[Constraint Classifier]
    step3 --> step4a[Anti Monotone Handler]
    step3 --> step4b[Monotone Handler]
    step3 --> step4c[Succinct Handler]
    step3 --> step4d[Convertible Handler]
    step4a --> step5[Candidate Generation Engine]
    step4b --> step5
    step4c --> step5
    step4d --> step5
    step5 --> step6[Constraint Push Filter]
    step6 --> step7[Frequent Itemset Output]
    step6 --> step8[Pruned Branches Log]
    step7 --> step9[Rule Generator]
    step9 --> step10[Interestingness Module - Lift and Chi Square]
    step10 --> step11[Final Rule Set]
    step8 --> step11
```

### 4.3 Quantitative Mining Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1[Numeric Attribute] --> q2[Discretization Engine]
    q2 --> q3a[Static Binning]
    q2 --> q3b[Dynamic Merging]
    q2 --> q3c[Clustering Approach]
    q3a --> q4[Boolean Form]
    q3b --> q4
    q3c --> q4
    q4 --> q5[Apriori or FP Growth]
    q5 --> q6[Frequent Numeric Itemsets]
    q6 --> q7[Quantitative Association Rules]
```

### 4.4 Multilevel Mining — Level Crossing Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    m1[Level 2 Root] --> m2[Check Min Sup L2]
    m2 -- Frequent --> m3[Descend to Level 1]
    m2 -- Infrequent --> m4[Prune Entire Branch]
    m3 --> m5[Level Cross Filter Check]
    m5 -- Parent Frequent --> m6[Descend to Level 0]
    m5 -- Parent Not Frequent --> m4
    m6 --> m7[Check Min Sup L0]
    m7 -- Frequent --> m8[Form Cross Level Rules]
    m7 -- Infrequent --> m4
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q1.** *Differentiate between **single-level** and **multilevel association rules** with an example. Why is uniform minimum support often inadequate for multilevel mining?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Single-level rule:** Both antecedent and consequent are drawn from the **same abstraction level**, e.g., `milk ⇒ bread` (item-to-item).
- **Multilevel rule:** Items belong to **different levels of a taxonomy**, e.g., `dairy ⇒ bread` where *dairy* is the parent of *milk* in a category hierarchy.
- **Uniform Min Support Problem:** Low-level (specific) items appear rarely, so a global `minsup` either misses valuable fine-grained rules or floods the output with high-level trivial rules. This motivates the **reduced minimum support** strategy where `minsup` decreases at deeper levels. *(1 mark for example, 1 mark for difference, 1 mark for UMS inadequacy.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**Q2.** *Explain the concept of **Lift** in correlation mining. How does it overcome the limitation of the confidence measure?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

$$\text{Lift}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{c(X \Rightarrow Y)}{s(Y)} = \frac{P(X \cup Y)}{P(X)\,P(Y)}$$

- **Limitation of Confidence:** A rule like `tea ⇒ coffee` may have 80% confidence, yet 75% of all customers buy coffee anyway — confidence is **deceptive**.
- **Lift Solution:** Lift = 1 ⇒ **independent**, > 1 ⇒ **positively correlated**, < 1 ⇒ **negatively correlated**. It benchmarks the rule against the **baseline probability** of Y, removing the bias of high-support items. *(1 mark formula, 1 mark confidence limitation, 1 mark lift interpretation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | Model with Internal Choice]**

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)**

**(a)** Explain the **various kinds of association rules** in data mining with suitable examples. Discuss how **multidimensional** and **multilevel** rules differ from each other. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **support-confidence framework** and explain why it is **insufficient** for identifying interesting patterns. Describe **Lift, Chi-Square, and All-Confidence** measures with formulas and interpretations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks]**

| Type | Example | Distinguishing Feature |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Boolean, Single-Level | `milk ⇒ bread` | Flat item space |
| Multilevel | `dairy ⇒ bread` | Items from hierarchy levels |
| Multidimensional | `age(30..40), income(42K..48K) ⇒ buys(laptop)` | ≥ 2 predicates / dimensions |
| Quantitative | `income(40K..50K) ⇒ buys(laptop)` | Continuous attributes discretized |
| Correlation-based | `tea ⇒ coffee` with lift > 1 | Statistically interesting |
| Constraint-based | Only rules with `sum(price) ≤ 500` | User-pushed predicates |

- **Multilevel vs Multidimensional:** Multilevel varies the **abstraction level** within the *same* dimension (item taxonomy). Multidimensional introduces **multiple distinct dimensions/predicates** (e.g., age, income, buys).

**(b) [7 Marks]**

The **support-confidence framework** is the classical Apriori measure:
- **Support** filters frequent itemsets.
- **Confidence** measures rule strength: $c(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{s(X \cup Y)}{s(X)}$.

**Why Insufficient:**

1. Cannot detect **negative correlations**.
2. Suffers from the **rare-item problem** (a low-support rare item can dominate).
3. Ignores **baseline probability** — high confidence does not imply high correlation.
4. Vulnerable to **cross-support patterns** where one item is very frequent.

**Better Measures:**

$$\text{Lift}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{c(X \Rightarrow Y)}{s(Y)} = \frac{P(X \cup Y)}{P(X)P(Y)}$$

- Lift > 1: positive correlation; = 1: independent; < 1: negative.

$$\chi^2 = \sum_{i,j} \frac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$$
