- Compares observed co-occurrence with expected under independence. Higher χ² ⇒ stronger evidence of dependence.

$$\text{all-conf}(X, Y) = \frac{s(X \cup Y)}{\max\{s(X), s(Y)\}}$$

- Robust against the **cross-support (rare-item) pattern** problem.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write only the formulas for Lift/χ²/all-conf and **forget to interpret them**. Marks are split 50/50 between formula and interpretation — always state what values mean!

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2 — Alternative)**

**(a)** What are **quantitative association rules**? Explain **static discretization**, **dynamic discretization**, and **clustering-based** approaches with examples. Discuss the **partial completeness principle**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe **constraint-based association mining**. Classify constraints into **anti-monotone, monotone, succinct, and convertible** with one example each. Explain how anti-monotone constraints are pushed into the Apriori algorithm. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks]**

Quantitative association rules involve **numeric attributes** that must be discretized into intervals (bins) before rule mining.

**Example:** `income(X, "40K..50K") ⇒ buys(X, "Laptop")`

**Discretization Approaches:**

1. **Static (Pre-defined) Discretization:**  
   Bins are fixed a priori by domain knowledge — e.g., $\{[0, 20K), [20K, 40K), [40K, 60K)\}$. *Drawback:* may merge interesting rare ranges with common ones.

2. **Dynamic Discretization (Srikant & Agrawal):**  
   Initial bins are fine-grained (e.g., width 5K). Adjacent bins are **merged** if their combined support stays above `minsup`. Bins are then **split** if confidence is too low.

3. **Clustering-based Discretization:**  
   Apply 1-D **equi-depth clustering** on the numeric attribute. Each cluster becomes a bin.

**Partial Completeness (PC) Principle:**  
A discretization is *PC-complete* if every interesting interval (one that would survive `minsup` and `minconf`) is either fully contained in a bin or fully crosses at most two adjacent bins. This bounds information loss to a factor of 2.

**(b) [7 Marks]**

**Constraint-Based Mining** allows users to express focused queries over the rule space, reducing computation and improving result relevance.

**Classification of Constraints:**

| Type | Definition | Example |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Anti-Monotone** | If $S$ violates, all supersets also violate | `sum(price) ≤ 500` |
| **Monotone** | If $S$ satisfies, all supersets satisfy | `min(price) ≥ 100` |
| **Succinct** | Items satisfying can be selected directly | `type = "Snack"` |
| **Convertible Anti-Monotone** | Anti-monotone after sorting items ascending | `avg(price) ≤ 50` |

**Anti-Monotone Push into Apriori:**

In the Apriori join step, after generating candidate $k$-itemsets, the algorithm checks the anti-monotone constraint *before* the support-counting scan. If a candidate violates the constraint, it is **immediately pruned** — no DB scan needed. This dramatically reduces I/O and candidate explosion. Similarly, **monotone** constraints are used to *skip generation* of candidates that cannot satisfy the constraint.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** When listing constraint types, students often confuse **convertible anti-monotone** with pure anti-monotone. The key differentiator is the **ordering of items** — without order, `avg()` is not anti-monotone; with appropriate order, it becomes pushable.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module-1:**

- **Multilevel Rules:** Items drawn from multiple abstraction levels of a taxonomy. Mining uses **uniform minsup** (simple but lossy) vs **reduced minsup** (granular but expensive). Level-cross filtering uses the **anti-monotone** property on the hierarchy.

- **Multidimensional Rules:** Involve **≥ 2 predicates** (dimensions). Inter-dimensional vs intra-dimensional hybrid. Require **integration of multiple tables** or extended schemas.

- **Quantitative Rules:** Numeric attributes discretized via **static**, **dynamic**, or **clustering-based** methods. **Partial completeness principle** bounds information loss to ≤ 2×.

- **Correlation Mining:** Support-Confidence alone is **inadequate**. Use **Lift** (correlation ratio), **χ²** (independence test), **All-Confidence** (cross-support safe), **Cosine**, **Max-Confidence**.

- **Constraint-Based Mining:** Categorize predicates into **Anti-monotone**, **Monotone**, **Succinct**, **Convertible**. Push constraints **deep into the candidate-generation phase** to prune early — I/O savings are massive.

- **Lift Interpretation:** $>1$ positively correlated, $=1$ independent, $<1$ negatively correlated — **must state this explicitly** in KTU answers.

- **Anti-Monotone Property:** Critical to Apriori-style pruning. If a set violates the constraint, every superset is automatically invalid.

- **Cross-Support Problem:** When one item is rare and another frequent, classical support can be misleading. **All-confidence** and **h-confidence** are the standard countermeasures.

- **Hierarchical vs Multi-Dim:** Hierarchical = *abstraction level*; Multi-Dim = *attribute diversity*. KTU examiners love to test this distinction.

- **Exam Formula Sheet:** Always memorize $c$, $s$, $\text{Lift}$, $\text{all-conf}$, and $\chi^2$ — these are the four most-asked metrics in board questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Association Mining to Correlation Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT604 - DATA MINING AND WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Association Mining to Correlation Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Association Mining (also called Frequent Itemset Mining / Market Basket Analysis)** is a fundamental unsupervised data mining technique that discovers interesting, hidden patterns, correlations, or causal structures among sets of items in large transactional or relational databases. Formally, given a universe of items $I = \{i_1, i_2, \dots, i_m\}$ and a transactional database $D = \{t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n\}$ where each transaction $t_j \subseteq I$, association mining produces **association rules** of the form $X \Rightarrow Y$, where $X, Y \subseteq I$ and $X \cap Y = \emptyset$.

**Correlation Analysis** is the statistical extension of association mining. While association rules measure co-occurrence (using *support* and *confidence*), correlation analysis quantifies the **strength and direction of statistical dependence** between two itemsets using measures such as *lift*, *chi-square test ($\chi^2$)*, *cosine*, and *all-confidence*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Supermarket Basket Story**
> Imagine you are the manager of a large supermarket. You observe that customers who buy **bread** very frequently also pick up **butter** on their way to the billing counter. This is not a coincidence — it is a *pattern*. Now imagine you also notice that customers who buy **diapers** often also buy **beer** (the famous "Diapers & Beer" anecdote from Walmart's data mining).
>
> - **Association Mining** answers: *"Which items tend to appear together in the same transaction?"*
> - **Correlation Analysis** answers: *"Is the presence of item A really influencing the purchase of item B, or is it just a coincidence caused by both being popular items?"*
>
> Think of association mining as discovering *friendships* in a social network, and correlation analysis as measuring *how strong* those friendships actually are (using statistical rigor, not just co-appearance).

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield Constants)

The following metrics are evaluated against user-defined thresholds:

- **Minimum Support Threshold (min_sup)**: Denoted $\text{minsup}$. Default range: $0.1$ to $0.5$.
- **Minimum Confidence Threshold (min_conf)**: Denoted $\text{minconf}$. Default range: $0.5$ to $0.1$.
- **Minimum Lift Threshold (min_lift)**: Denoted $\text{minlift}$. Critical value is $1$ (independence boundary).

## 1.4 Syllabus Scope Statement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Scope for this Topic:**
> 1. Frequent itemset generation (Apriori principle, Apriori algorithm)
> 2. Rule generation from frequent itemsets
> 3. Support, Confidence, Lift frameworks
> 4. Mining various kinds of association rules (multi-level, multi-dimensional, quantitative)
> 5. From association to correlation analysis (lift, $\chi^2$, cosine, all-confidence)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Foundational Metrics

### 2.1.1 Support (Frequency)
Support of an itemset $X$ is the fraction of transactions in database $D$ that contain $X$:

$$\text{support}(X) = \frac{\text{count}(X)}{\vert D \vert} = \frac{\text{frequency of } X}{\text{total transactions}}$$

**Why this metric?** A rule involving an itemset that occurs only once in a million transactions is statistically meaningless. Support filters out *rare coincidences*.

### 2.1.2 Confidence (Strength)
Confidence of rule $X \Rightarrow Y$ measures how often items in $Y$ appear in transactions containing $X$:

$$\text{confidence}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{\text{support}(X \cup Y)}{\text{support}(X)} = P(Y \mid X)$$

**Why this metric?** High confidence means the rule is *reliable* — whenever $X$ is purchased, $Y$ is usually purchased too.

### 2.1.3 Lift (Correlation Strength)
Lift measures whether $X$ and $Y$ are actually dependent or merely co-occur by chance:

$$\text{lift}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{\text{confidence}(X \Rightarrow Y)}{\text{support}(Y)} = \frac{P(X \cup Y)}{P(X) \cdot P(Y)}$$

**Interpretation Logic:**
- $\text{lift} = 1 \Rightarrow X$ and $Y$ are **statistically independent** (no correlation).
- $\text{lift} > 1 \Rightarrow X$ and $Y$ are **positively correlated** (rule is meaningful).
- $\text{lift} < 1 \Rightarrow X$ and $Y$ are **negatively correlated** (presence of $X$ discourages $Y$).

## 2.2 The Apriori Principle (The Foundation of Efficient Mining)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Apriori Principle (Agrawal & Srikant, 1994):** *"All non-empty subsets of a frequent itemset must also be frequent."*
> Equivalently: *"If an itemset is infrequent, then all of its supersets are also infrequent."* (This is the **anti-monotone / downward closure property**.)

This single principle allows the algorithm to **prune the search space exponentially** by avoiding the generation of candidate itemsets whose subsets are already known to be infrequent.

**Formal Statement:**
$$\forall X, Y : (X \subseteq Y) \land (\text{support}(X) < \text{minsup}) \Rightarrow (\text{support}(Y) < \text{minsup})$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Metric / Concept | Formula | Threshold Range | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Support of itemset $X$ | $\text{sup}(X) = \dfrac{\text{count}(X)}{\vert D \vert}$ | $0.1$ to $0.5$ | Filters rare itemsets |
| Confidence of $X \Rightarrow Y$ | $\text{conf}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \dfrac{\text{sup}(X \cup Y)}{\text{sup}(X)}$ | $0.5$ to $0.9$ | Measures rule reliability |
| Lift of $X \Rightarrow Y$ | $\text{lift}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \dfrac{\text{conf}(X \Rightarrow Y)}{\text{sup}(Y)}$ | $> 1$ for correlation | Detects true dependence |
| Cosine measure | $\text{cos}(X, Y) = \dfrac{\text{sup}(X \cup Y)}{\sqrt{\text{sup}(X) \cdot \text{sup}(Y)}}$ | $0$ to $1$ | Null-invariant correlation |
| All-confidence | $\text{all-conf}(X) = \dfrac{\text{sup}(X)}{\max_{i \in X}\,\text{sup}(\{i\})}$ | $0$ to $1$ | Measures cohesive support |
| Chi-square statistic | $\chi^2 = \sum \dfrac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$ | Critical value at $\alpha = 0.05$ | Tests independence hypothesis |
| Apriori property | $X \subseteq Y, \text{sup}(X) < \text{minsup} \Rightarrow \text{sup}(Y) < \text{minsup}$ | Boolean (always true) | Pruning logic |
| Expected frequency | $E_{ij} = \dfrac{(\text{row total})(\text{col total})}{\text{grand total}}$ | For $\chi^2$ computation | Calculates independence baseline |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU Note on Null-Invariance:** Lift is NOT null-invariant (it can be skewed by items with low support). The **cosine** and **all-confidence** measures ARE null-invariant and are therefore preferred for modern correlation analysis — a frequently asked 14-mark question.

## 2.4 Engineering & Real-World Utility

| Domain | Application of Association/Correlation Mining |
|---|---|
| **Retail (E-commerce)** | Cross-selling, recommendation engines (Amazon, Flipkart) |
| **Banking & Finance** | Fraud detection (correlated unusual transactions) |
| **Bioinformatics** | Co-expressed gene clusters, protein-protein interactions |
| **Telecommunications** | Call pattern analysis, churn prediction |
| **Healthcare** | Symptom co-occurrence for disease diagnosis |
| **Web Mining** | Clickstream analysis, session-based recommendations |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example Database

Consider the following transactional database (commonly used in KTU reference textbook examples):

| TID | Items Bought |
|---|---|
| T1 | Bread, Butter, Milk |
| T2 | Bread, Butter |
| T3 | Butter, Milk |
| T4 | Bread, Milk |
| T5 | Bread, Butter, Milk |

Total transactions: $\vert D \vert = 5$

**Assumed Thresholds:** $\text{minsup} = 40\%$, $\text{minconf} = 60\%$

## 3.2 Step-by-Step: Frequent Itemset Mining (Apriori Algorithm)

### Step 1 — Compute $C_1$ (Candidate 1-itemsets)

Scan the database once and count each individual item:

| Item | Count | Support = Count / 5 |
|---|---|---|
| Bread | 4 | $4/5 = 0.80$ |
| Butter | 4 | $4/5 = 0.80$ |
| Milk | 4 | $4/5 = 0.80$ |

All three items have support $\geq 0.40$, so:

$$L_1 = \{\{\text{Bread}\}, \{\text{Butter}\}, \{\text{Milk}\}\}$$

### Step 2 — Generate $C_2$ (Candidate 2-itemsets) by Self-Join $L_1 \bowtie L_1$

$$C_2 = \{\{\text{Bread, Butter}\}, \{\text{Bread, Milk}\}, \{\text{Butter, Milk}\}\}$$

### Step 3 — Scan Database to Count Support of $C_2$

| 2-Itemset | Occurrences | Support |
|---|---|---|
| {Bread, Butter} | T1, T2, T5 → 3 | $3/5 = 0.60$ ✓ |
| {Bread, Milk} | T1, T4, T5 → 3 | $3/5 = 0.60$ ✓ |
| {Butter, Milk} | T1, T3, T5 → 3 | $3/5 = 0.60$ ✓ |

All pass $\text{minsup} = 0.40$:

$$L_2 = \{\{\text{Bread, Butter}\}, \{\text{Bread, Milk}\}, \{\text{Butter, Milk}\}\}$$

### Step 4 — Generate $C_3$ (Candidate 3-itemsets)

Self-join $L_2$ on the first element. Only one candidate is possible:

$$C_3 = \{\{\text{Bread, Butter, Milk}\}\}$$

### Step 5 — Apply Apriori Pruning (CRITICAL STEP)

Check all 2-item subsets of {Bread, Butter, Milk}:
- {Bread, Butter} ∈ $L_2$ ✓
- {Bread, Milk} ∈ $L_2$ ✓
- {Butter, Milk} ∈ $L_2$ ✓

All subsets are frequent, so the candidate **survives pruning**.

### Step 6 — Count Support of $C_3$

| 3-Itemset | Occurrences | Support |
|---|---|---|
| {Bread, Butter, Milk} | T1, T5 → 2 | $2/5 = 0.40$ ✓ |

Passes threshold:

$$L_3 = \{\{\text{Bread, Butter, Milk}\}\}$$

Since $L_4$ would be empty (we only have 3 unique items), the algorithm terminates.

$$\boxed{F = L_1 \cup L_2 \cup L_3 = \text{all 7 frequent itemsets above}}$$

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Rule Generation from Frequent Itemsets

From $L_3 = \{B, M, K\}$ (using B=Bread, M=Milk, K=Butter for brevity), generate all non-empty proper subsets and form rules $X \Rightarrow Y - X$.

| Rule | Computation | Confidence | Pass minconf? |
|---|---|---|---|
| $B \Rightarrow M, K$ | $\dfrac{0.40}{0.80} = 0.50$ | $50\%$ | ✗ |
| $M \Rightarrow B, K$ | $\dfrac{0.40}{0.80} = 0.50$ | $50\%$ | ✗ |
| $K \Rightarrow B, M$ | $\dfrac{0.40}{0.80} = 0.50$ | $50\%$ | ✗ |
| $B, M \Rightarrow K$ | $\dfrac{0.40}{0.60} = 0.67$ | $67\%$ | ✓ |
| $B, K \Rightarrow M$ | $\dfrac{0.40}{0.60} = 0.67$ | $67\%$ | ✓ |
| $M, K \Rightarrow B$ | $\dfrac{0.40}{0.60} = 0.67$ | $67\%$ | ✓ |

From $L_2$, additional rules:
- $\{B, M\} \Rightarrow \emptyset$ — not considered (must be non-empty consequent)
- $B \Rightarrow M$: $\dfrac{0.60}{0.80} = 0.75$ ✓
- $B \Rightarrow K$: $\dfrac{0.60}{0.80} = 0.75$ ✓
- $M \Rightarrow B$: $\dfrac{0.60}{0.80} = 0.75$ ✓
- $M \Rightarrow K$: $\dfrac{0.60}{0.80} = 0.75$ ✓
- $K \Rightarrow B$: $\dfrac{0.60}{0.80} = 0.75$ ✓
- $K \Rightarrow M$: $\dfrac{0.60}{0.80} = 0.75$ ✓

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Computing Lift (Correlation Analysis)

Take rule $\text{Bread} \Rightarrow \text{Butter}$:

$$\text{lift}(B \Rightarrow K) = \frac{\text{conf}(B \Rightarrow K)}{\text{sup}(K)} = \frac{P(B \cup K)}{P(B) \cdot P(K)}$$

Substituting values:

$$\text{lift}(B \Rightarrow K) = \frac{0.60}{0.80 \times 0.80} = \frac{0.60}{0.64} = 0.9375$$

Since $\text{lift} < 1$, Bread and Butter are **slightly negatively correlated** in this database. This is a classic KTU example of why **confidence alone is misleading** — confidence of $0.75$ suggested a strong rule, but lift reveals near-independence.

## 3.5 Step-by-Step: $\chi^2$ (Chi-Square) Correlation Test

Construct a **2×2 contingency table** for items Bread (B) and Butter (K):

| | Butter | ¬Butter | Row Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bread** | $O_{11} = 3$ | $O_{12} = 1$ | $4$ |
| **¬Bread** | $O_{21} = 1$ | $O_{22} = 1$ | $2$ |
| **Col Total** | $4$ | $2$ | $5$ |

**Expected frequencies** $E_{ij} = \dfrac{(\text{row}_i)(\text{col}_j)}{N}$:

$$E_{11} = \frac{4 \times 4}{5} = 3.2, \quad E_{12} = \frac{4 \times 2}{5} = 1.6$$
$$E_{21} = \frac{2 \times 4}{5} = 1.6, \quad E_{22} = \frac{2 \times 2}{5} = 0.8$$

**Compute $\chi^2$ statistic:**

$$\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{2}\sum_{j=1}^{2} \frac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$$

Expanding term-by-term:

$$\chi^2 = \frac{(3 - 3.2)^2}{3.2} + \frac{(1 - 1.6)^2}{1.6} + \frac{(1 - 1.6)^2}{1.6} + \frac{(1 - 0.8)^2}{0.8}$$

$$\chi^2 = \frac{0.04}{3.2} + \frac{0.36}{1.6} + \frac{0.36}{1.6} + \frac{0.04}{0.8}$$

$$\chi^2 = 0.0125 + 0.225 + 0.225 + 0.05 = 0.5125$$

Degrees of freedom: $\text{df} = (r-1)(c-1) = 1 \times 1 = 1$.

Critical value at $\alpha = 0.05$ with $\text{df} = 1$ is $\chi^2_{\text{crit}} = 3.841$.

Since $\chi^2_{\text{computed}} = 0.5125 < 3.841$, we **fail to reject the null hypothesis** of independence — confirming our lift analysis that Bread and Butter are NOT significantly correlated.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Correlation Measures

| Measure | Formula | Range | Null-Invariant? | Handles Neg. Correlation? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Confidence | $\dfrac{\text{sup}(X \cup Y)}{\text{sup}(X)}$ | $[0, 1]$ | No | No |
| Lift | $\dfrac{\text{conf}}{\text{sup}(Y)}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | No | Yes |
| Cosine | $\dfrac{\text{sup}(X \cup Y)}{\sqrt{\text{sup}(X)\text{sup}(Y)}}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Yes | No |
| $\chi^2$ | $\sum \dfrac{(O-E)^2}{E}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | No | Yes |
| All-Confidence | $\dfrac{\text{sup}(X)}{\max_i \text{sup}(i)}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Yes | No |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Apriori Algorithm Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Database D, minsup] --> B[Scan D, generate C1: candidate 1-itemsets]
    B --> C[Compute support for each item in C1]
    C --> D{support >= minsup for all items?}
    D -- No --> E[Discard infrequent items]
    D -- Yes --> F[Form L1: frequent 1-itemsets]
    E --> F
    F --> G[For k = 2, 3, ... until Lk is empty]
    G --> H[Generate Ck from Lk-1 via self-join Lk-1 JOIN Lk-1]
    H --> I[Apply Apriori Pruning: remove candidates whose k-1 subsets are NOT in Lk-1]
    I --> J[Scan D, count support of remaining candidates in Ck]
    J --> K{Any candidate with support >= minsup?}
    K -- No --> L[Set F = union of all L1 to Lk-1, terminate]
    K -- Yes --> M[Form Lk: frequent k-itemsets]
    M --> G
    L --> N[For each frequent itemset f in F, generate non-empty subsets s]
    N --> O[For each subset s, output rule s => f - s if confidence >= minconf]
    O --> P[End: Strong association rules]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: From Association to Correlation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Transactional Database D] --> B[Data Preprocessing]
    B --> C[1-Itemset Scan and Count]
    C --> D[Apply Apriori Pruning Principle]
    D --> E[Frequent Itemset Generation L1, L2, L3, ...]
    E --> F[Association Rule Generation]
    F --> G[Filter by minconf threshold]
    G --> H[Association Rules: X => Y]
    H --> I[Compute Lift Metric]
    H --> J[Compute Chi-Square Statistic]
    H --> K[Compute Cosine Measure]
    H --> L[Compute All-Confidence]
    I --> M[Correlation Analysis Layer]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Final Output: Ranked, Correlated, Validated Rules]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Hierarchy: Mining Various Association Rules

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root[Association Rule Mining] --> L1[Single-Dimensional, Single-Level]
    Root --> L2[Multi-Level]
    Root --> L3[Multi-Dimensional]
    Root --> L4[Quantitative]
    L2 --> L2a[Level 1: Top category, e.g., Electronics]
    L2 --> L2b[Level 2: Sub-category, e.g., Laptop]
    L2 --> L2c[Level 3: Brand, e.g., Dell XPS]
    L3 --> L3a[Inter-dim: Age, Income, Buy]
    L3 --> L3b[Uses predicates on multiple attributes]
    L4 --> L4a[Binning of continuous attributes]
    L4 --> L4b[Discretization before rule mining]
```

## 4.4 Decision Logic: From Confidence to Correlation Necessity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Generate Rule X => Y] --> B[Compute Support and Confidence]
    B --> C{Confidence >= minconf?}
    C -- No --> D[Reject rule: not strong]
    C -- Yes --> E[Compute Lift]
    E --> F{Lift > 1?}
    F -- No --> G[Rule is strong but items are NOT positively correlated - misleading]
    F -- Yes --> H[Compute Chi-Square test]
    H --> I{chi2 > chi2_critical at alpha = 0.05?}
    I -- No --> J[Correlation not statistically significant]
    I -- Yes --> K[ACCEPT: Rule is strongly correlated and statistically significant]
    G --> L[Apply null-invariant measures: Cosine or All-Confidence]
    L --> M[Refine decision]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the Apriori Principle. Why is it important in association rule mining?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Apriori Principle** states that *"all non-empty subsets of a frequent itemset must also be frequent"*. Equivalently, if an itemset is infrequent, all of its supersets are infrequent.

**Importance:**
1. It **prunes the candidate search space** exponentially, reducing the number of itemsets to be tested.
2. It avoids the brute-force approach of checking all $2^m - 1$ possible itemsets.
3. It forms the mathematical foundation of the Apriori algorithm, making it computationally feasible on large databases.
4. It leverages the **anti-monotone property of support** to enable efficient level-wise (BFS) mining.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Distinguish between Confidence and Lift in association rule mining.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Confidence | Lift |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | $\text{conf}(X \Rightarrow Y) = P(Y \mid X)$ | $\text{lift}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \dfrac{P(X \cup Y)}{P(X) \cdot P(Y)}$ |
| **Measures** | Strength/Reliability | Correlation/Statistical dependence |
| **Range** | $[0, 1]$ | $[0, \infty)$ |
| **Null-Invariance** | No | No |
| **Detects Neg. Correlation** | No | Yes (lift < 1) |
| **Key Insight** | Conditional probability; can mislead for popular items | Compares to baseline independence; reveals true patterns |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]*

**(a) [7 Marks] Given the transactional database below, apply the Apriori algorithm with minimum support = 30% and minimum confidence = 60% to find all strong association rules. Show all candidate ($C_k$) and frequent ($L_k$) itemsets explicitly.**

| TID | Items |
|---|---|
| T1 | I1, I2, I5 |
| T2 | I2, I4 |
| T3 | I2, I3 |
| T4 | I1, I2, I4 |
| T5 | I1, I3 |
| T6 | I2, I3 |
| T7 | I1, I3 |
| T8 | I1, I2, I3, I5 |
| T9 | I1, I2, I3 |

**Model Solution:**

Total transactions: $\vert D \vert = 9$. Threshold: $\text{minsup} = 0.30 \Rightarrow \text{min count} = 2.7 \Rightarrow 3$.

**Step 1: $C_1$ and $L_1$**

| Itemset | Count | Support |
|---|---|---|
| {I1} | 6 | $0.67$ ✓ |
| {I2} | 7 | $0.78$ ✓ |
| {I3} | 6 | $0.67$ ✓ |
| {I4} | 2 | $0.22$ ✗ |
| {I5} | 2 | $0.22$ ✗ |

$$L_1 = \{\{I1\}, \{I2\}, \{I3\}\}$$

**Step 2: $C_2$ from self-join $L_1$**

$$C_2 = \{\{I1, I2\}, \{I1, I3\}, \{I2, I3\}\}$$

| Itemset | Count | Support |
|---|---|---|
| {I1, I2} | 4 | $0.44$ ✓ |
| {I1, I3} | 4 | $0.44$ ✓ |
| {I2, I3} | 4 | $0.44$ ✓ |

$$L_2 = \{\{I1, I2\}, \{I1, I3\}, \{I2, I3\}\}$$

**Step 3: $C_3$ generation & Apriori pruning**

Self-join gives one candidate: $\{I1, I2, I3\}$. All 2-subsets $\{I1,I2\}, \{I1,I3\}, \{I2,I3\}$ are in $L_2$, so it survives pruning.

| Itemset | Count | Support |
|---|---|---|
| {I1, I2, I3} | 2 | $0.22$ ✗ |

$$L_3 = \emptyset \Rightarrow \text{Algorithm terminates.}$$

**Frequent itemsets: $F = L_1 \cup L_2$.**

**Step 4: Rule Generation from $L_2$ (minconf = 0.60)**

| Rule | Calculation | Confidence | Pass? |
|---|---|---|---|
| $I1 \Rightarrow I2$ | $0.44 / 0.67$ | $0.66$ | ✓ |
| $I2 \Rightarrow I1$ | $0.44 / 0.78$ | $0.57$ | ✗ |
| $I1 \Rightarrow I3$ | $0.44 / 0.67$ | $0.66$ | ✓ |
| $I3 \Rightarrow I1$ | $0.44 / 0.67$ | $0.66$ | ✓ |
| $I2 \Rightarrow I3$ | $0.44 / 0.78$ | $0.57$ | ✗ |
| $I3 \Rightarrow I2$ | $0.44 / 0.67$ | $0.66$ | ✓ |

**Final Strong Rules:** $I1 \Rightarrow I2$, $I1 \Rightarrow I3$, $I3 \Rightarrow I1$, $I3 \Rightarrow I2$.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain the limitations of the support-confidence framework. How does correlation analysis (using lift and $\chi^2$) overcome these limitations?**

**Model Answer:**

**Limitations of Support-Confidence Framework:**

1. **Confidence is misleading for popular items:** An item $Y$ that is globally popular will yield high confidence for many rules, even when $X$ and $Y$ are statistically independent. *Example:* In a database where 90% of transactions contain bread, the rule $X \Rightarrow$ Bread may have high confidence regardless of $X$.
2. **Support threshold biases against rare but interesting items:** Setting a high minsup filters out rules involving low-frequency items, even if they are highly correlated. Conversely, low minsup produces an explosion of candidate itemsets.
3. **No directionality / asymmetry capture:** Confidence of $X \Rightarrow Y$ may differ significantly from $Y \Rightarrow X$, but confidence alone does not indicate the *strength of dependence*.
4. **No null-invariance:** Both support and confidence are affected by the addition of null transactions (transactions containing neither $X$ nor $Y$), distorting the metric.
5. **No statistical significance testing:** Confidence is a heuristic, not a probabilistic test. It does not account for sample size or test against the null hypothesis of independence.

**How Correlation Analysis Overcomes These:**

1. **Lift normalizes by marginal probability:** Lift = $P(X \cup Y) / [P(X) \cdot P(Y)]$ compares observed co-occurrence to expected co-occurrence under independence. Lift = 1 indicates independence, lift > 1 positive correlation, lift < 1 negative correlation.
2. **$\chi^2$ test provides statistical significance:** It tests the null hypothesis that $X$ and $Y$ are independent. The test statistic follows a known distribution, enabling rejection/acceptance with a specified confidence level (e.g., $\alpha = 0.05$).
3. **Cosine & all-confidence are null-invariant:** They are unaffected by null transactions, making them robust for skewed data.
4. **Reveals hidden patterns:** A rule with high confidence but lift $\approx 1$ is essentially a coincidence and can be discarded.

**Conclusion:** Correlation analysis adds *statistical rigor* to association mining, distinguishing genuine patterns from artifacts of high-frequency items.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]*

**(a) [7 Marks] Consider the following 2×2 contingency table for items A and B. Compute the $\chi^2$ statistic and the lift measure. Test the correlation at $\alpha = 0.05$ (critical value = 3.841).**

| | B | ¬B | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 800 | 200 | 1000 |
| **¬A** | 400 | 600 | 1000 |
| **Total** | 1200 | 800 | 2000 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Expected Frequencies** $E_{ij} = \dfrac{(\text{row total})(\text{col total})}{N}$:

$$E_{11} = \frac{1000 \times 1200}{2000} = 600$$
$$E_{12} = \frac{1000 \times 800}{2000} = 400$$
$$E_{21} = \frac{1000 \times 1200}{2000} = 600$$
$$E_{22} = \frac{1000 \times 800}{2000} = 400$$

**Step 2: Compute $\chi^2$:**

$$\chi^2 = \frac{(800-600)^2}{600} + \frac{(200-400)^2}{400} + \frac{(400-600)^2}{600} + \frac{(600-400)^2}{400}$$

$$\chi^2 = \frac{40000}{600} + \frac{40000}{400} + \frac{40000}{600} + \frac{40000}{400}$$

$$\chi^2 = 66.67 + 100 + 66.67 + 100 = 333.33$$

**Step 3: Decision**

Since $\chi^2 = 333.33 \gg 3.841$, we **reject the null hypothesis** of independence at $\alpha = 0.05$. A and B are **strongly correlated**.

**Step 4: Compute Lift**

$$\text{sup}(A) = 1000/2000 = 0.5, \quad \text{sup}(B) = 1200/2000 = 0.6$$
$$\text{sup}(A \cup B) = 800/2000 = 0.4$$

$$\text{lift}(A \Rightarrow B) = \frac{0.4}{0.5 \times 0.6} = \frac{0.4}{0.3} = 1.333$$

Since lift $> 1$, A and B are **positively correlated**, consistent with the $\chi^2$ result.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Discuss different kinds of association rules based on data dimensions and abstraction levels. Give an example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

Association rules are categorized based on the **types of data values handled** and the **abstraction levels** of items involved:

| Category | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Boolean (Single-Dimensional, Single-Level)** | Rules involve presence/absence of items at one conceptual level. | $\text{Diaper} \Rightarrow \text{Beer}$ |
| **2. Multi-Level** | Rules involve items at different taxonomy/concept levels. | $\text{Laptop} \Rightarrow \text{Printer}$ (Level 1); $\text{Dell XPS} \Rightarrow \text{HP Inkjet}$ (Level 2) |
| **3. Multi-Dimensional** | Rules involve more than one predicate (dimension). | $\text{Age}(30\text{-}40) \land \text{Income}(\text{high}) \Rightarrow \text{Buys}(\text{Luxury Car})$ |
| **4. Quantitative (Numeric)** | Rules involve continuous or numeric attributes, often requiring discretization. | $\text{Age} \in [30, 40] \Rightarrow \text{Salary} \in [50k, 70k]$ |
| **5. Categorical** | Rules use categorical (non-numeric) attributes that may require grouping. | $\text{Profession} = \text{Doctor} \Rightarrow \text{Buys}(\text{Insurance})$ |
| **6. Constraint-Based** | Rules are filtered using user-defined constraints (item, length, aggregate). | $\text{Sum}(\text{Price}) > 5000 \Rightarrow \text{Discount Applied}$ |

**Example Explanation:**

- **Single-Level Boolean Rule:** $\text{Bread} \Rightarrow \text{Butter}$ — both items at same concept level.
- **Multi-Level Rule:** $\text{Electronics} \Rightarrow \text{Accessories}$ (Level 1) refined to $\text{TV} \Rightarrow \text{HDMI Cable}$ (Level 2). Mining uses progressively reduced support thresholds at deeper levels.
- **Multi-Dimensional Rule:** Uses predicates on multiple fields. If a customer is (Age 25–35) AND (Income high) THEN (Buys SUV). This requires cube computation and multi-dimensional analysis.
- **Quantitative Rule:** Continuous attributes like Age and Salary are **binned** (e.g., 25–35, 36–45) before mining, then rules are extracted. Approaches include **binning**, **clustering-based discretization**, or **fuzzy discretization**.

**Conclusion:** Different rule types serve different analytical needs. KTU emphasizes the algorithmic adaptation required for each — e.g., multi-level rules need **level-shared mining** with consistent support thresholds, while quantitative rules need **discretization** as a preprocessing step.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **MUST show $C_k$ and $L_k$ separately** in Apriori problems — students who skip candidate generation lose 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Confidence and Lift confusion:** Always verify if a question asks for "strong rules" (confidence-based) or "correlated rules" (lift-based). They give different answers.
> 3. **In $\chi^2$ problems:** State the null hypothesis explicitly. Many students forget to mention $\alpha$ and the critical value comparison.
> 4. **Null-invariance:** If a question mentions "robust to null transactions," the answer is **cosine** or **all-confidence**, NOT lift.
> 5. **Apriori Pruning step is mandatory:** Omitting the subset check before counting support loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Decimal precision:** In KTU valuation, round to 2-3 decimal places; do not write 0.6666667. Use $0.67$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Association Mining to Correlation Analysis**

- **Core Definitions:**
  - *Itemset:* A set of items, e.g., $\{I1, I2\}$.
  - *Frequent Itemset:* Itemset with support $\geq \text{minsup}$.
  - *Strong Rule:* Confidence $\geq \text{minconf}$.
  - *Lift = 1:* Independence; *Lift > 1:* Positive correlation; *Lift < 1:* Negative correlation.

- **Apriori Algorithm Steps:**
  1. Generate $C_1$, count support, form $L_1$.
  2. For $k \geq 2$: self-join $L_{k-1}$ to form $C_k$, prune using Apriori property, scan DB, form $L_k$.
  3. Stop when $L_k = \emptyset$.
  4. Generate rules from each $L_k$ by considering all non-empty proper subsets and checking confidence.

- **Apriori Property (Anti-Monotone):** Subsets of frequent itemsets are frequent. Use it for **pruning**.

- **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - Support: $\text{count}(X) / \vert D \vert$
  - Confidence: $\text{sup}(X \cup Y) / \text{sup}(X)$
  - Lift: $\text{conf} / \text{sup}(Y)$ or $P(X \cup Y) / [P(X) \cdot P(Y)]$
  - Cosine: $\text{sup}(X \cup Y) / \sqrt{\text{sup}(X) \cdot \text{sup}(Y)}$
  - $\chi^2 = \sum (O - E)^2 / E$

- **Rule Generation Rule of Thumb:** For an $L_k$ itemset, generate $2^k - 2$ non-trivial rules (excluding empty antecedent and empty consequent).

- **Limitations of Confidence:** Can be misleading; high confidence does NOT imply correlation. Always cross-check with lift or $\chi^2$.

- **Multi-Level Rules:** Use reduced support at deeper concept levels (consistency principle).
- **Quantitative Rules:** Require discretization (binning, clustering, or fuzzy logic).
- **Multi-Dimensional Rules:** Involve multiple predicates, handled via data cube and OLAP operations.

- **Null-Invariance:** Cosine and all-confidence are null-invariant; lift, support, confidence, and $\chi^2$ are NOT.

- **Algorithm Improvements to Know:** AprioriTID, FP-Growth (avoids candidate generation entirely), Partition algorithm, Dynamic itemset counting.

- **Common KTU Mistakes:** Forgetting to apply Apriori pruning, confusing $C_k$ and $L_k$, omitting null hypothesis statement in $\chi^2$, missing decimal precision.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constraint-Based Association Mining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBADT604 - DATA MINING AND WAREHOUSING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Constraint-Based Association Mining # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Constraint-Based Association Mining** is a refined paradigm of frequent pattern mining where the discovery of association rules is guided (or *constrained*) by a user-specified set of predicates or conditions. Instead of generating *all* frequent itemsets and *all* rules, the mining engine is "pushed" to satisfy user constraints early, thereby pruning large portions of the search space. Mathematically, given a transaction database $D$ and a user-defined constraint $C$, the goal is to find: $$\mathcal{F} = \{X \subseteq I \mid \text{support}(X) \geq \text{minsup} \;\land\; C(X) = \text{true}\}$$ where $I$ is the item universe and $C$ is any boolean predicate over itemsets (e.g., price, category, date, aggregate conditions). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly emphasizes that *constraint pushing* improves the *efficiency* of mining by exploiting the **monotonicity** and **anti-monotonicity** properties of user constraints. This is a **frequently asked 14-mark topic**. ## 1.2 The Two Standard Categories of Constraints | Category | Examples | When Evaluated | |----------|----------|----------------| | **Pattern Pruning Constraints** | $\text{support} \geq \text{minsup}$, $\text{avg(price)} \geq \$100$ | During itemset enumeration | | **Rule Generation Constraints** | confidence $\geq 0.8$, lift $> 1$ | After candidate rules are formed | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Library Bookshelf > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: Looking for "all English novels priced under ₹200 published after 2015" in a massive library.** Imagine you walk into a library containing **10 million books** and ask for "all English novels priced under ₹200, published after 2015, by Indian authors." A naive librarian would pull *every book*, sort it, and then filter. That is traditional **unconstrained** association mining. A *smart, constraint-based* librarian does the following: 1. Walks only to the **"English"** shelf (dimension pruning). 2. Within that shelf, picks only books **after 2015** (level/date constraint). 3. Stacks aside only those with a **red price tag under ₹200** (aggregate constraint). 4. Finally, returns the result set. **Constraint-based mining is this smart librarian.** Instead of exploring the full exponential space of $2^{|I|}$ itemsets, we "push" constraints deep into the algorithm to **prune early**. ## 1.4 Why Do We Need Constraints? - **Search space reduction:** Without constraints, a database of $10^3$ distinct items can generate $\sim 10^{30}$ frequent itemsets. - **User focus:** Domain experts care about specific regions of the pattern space (e.g., "only profitable items"). - **Resource economy:** CPU, memory, and disk I/O are conserved. - **Meaningful patterns:** Reduces *redundant* or *uninteresting* rules. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Constraints A constraint $C$ over an itemset $X = \{i_1, i_2, \dots, i_k\}$ is a predicate involving: 1. **Item properties (Dimension Constraints):** $C \equiv (i_1.\text{category} = \text{"Snack"}) \;\land\; (i_2.\text{brand} = \text{"X"})$ 2. **Aggregate Functions over $X$:** $C \equiv \text{sum}(X.\text{price}) \leq 500 \;\lor\; \text{avg}(X.\text{price}) \geq 100$ 3. **Frequency Constraints:** $C \equiv \text{support}(X) \geq \text{minsup}$ 4. **Rule Constraints (post-mining):** $C \equiv \text{confidence}(A \Rightarrow B) \geq 0.9$ ## 2.2 The Four Key Constraint Properties The **CRITICAL** theoretical content (a KTU favourite) is determining whether a constraint is **anti-monotonic**, **monotonic**, or **neither**. ### 2.2.1 Anti-Monotonic Constraint A constraint $C_a$ is **anti-monotonic** if: $$\forall X \supseteq Y,\;\; C_a(Y) \Rightarrow C_a(X)$$ Equivalently, if an itemset violates $C_a$, then **all its supersets also violate** it. Such constraints allow **aggressive pruning** — the moment a set fails, we discard its entire upward closure. > [!NOTE] > **Examples:** $\text{support}(S) \geq \theta$, $\text{sum}(S.\text{price}) \leq 100$, $\min(S.\text{price}) \geq 10$. ### 2.2.2 Monotonic Constraint A constraint $C_m$ is **monotonic** if: $$\forall X \supseteq Y,\;\; C_m(X) \Rightarrow C_m(Y)$$ If a set satisfies $C_m$, then **all its subsets also satisfy** it. Such constraints are easier — once satisfied, the entire downward closure is valid. > [!NOTE] > **Examples:** $\text{sum}(S.\text{price}) \geq 100$, $\max(S.\text{price}) \leq 500$, $\text{support}(S) \leq 0.5$. ### 2.2.3 Succinct Constraint A constraint $C_s$ is **succinct** if the set of itemsets satisfying it can be **expressed precisely** via selection operators on a single item (e.g., $\{i \mid i.\text{price} \geq 10\}$). ### 2.2.4 Convertible Constraints (Anti-Monotonic + Monotonic) Some aggregate constraints are **not monotonic or anti-monotonic in raw form**, but become so after **item reordering**. These are the trickiest for KTU questions. | Constraint | Ordering | Property After Ordering | |------------|----------|-------------------------| | $\text{avg}(S) \geq v$ | **Ascending** | **Anti-monotonic** (with adjusted threshold) | | $\text{avg}(S) \geq v$ | **Descending** | **Monotonic** | | $\text{avg}(S) \leq v$ | **Ascending** | **Monotonic** | | $\text{avg}(S) \leq v$ | **Descending** | **Anti-monotonic** | | $\text{median}(S) \geq v$ | Ascending | Anti-monotonic | | $\text{median}(S) \leq v$ | Ascending | Monotonic | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conversion Rule:** The adjusted anti-monotonic threshold is $v' = v \times k$ where $k$ is the size of the itemset being evaluated (since average $\geq v$ means sum $\geq v \times k$, which is anti-monotonic in $k$). ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Property | Mathematical Form | Direction of Pruning | KTU Memory Trick | |----------|-------------------|----------------------|------------------| | **Anti-monotonic** | $C(Y)=\text{true} \Rightarrow C(X)=\text{true}$ for all $X \supseteq Y$ | Upward (superset) | "More items → harder to satisfy" | | **Monotonic** | $C(X)=\text{true} \Rightarrow C(Y)=\text{true}$ for all $Y \subseteq X$ | Downward (subset) | "More items → easier to satisfy" | | **Succinct** | Set of satisfying itemsets expressible by $\sigma(i)$ | Selection on items | "Expressible on the fly" | | **Convertible Anti-monotonic** | Reorder ascending + adjusted $v'$ | Upward (with reordering) | "Sum-like, ascending" | | **Inconvertible** | None of the above | Cannot prune systematically | "Hard constraint" | ## 2.4 Real-World Utility - **Retail / Market Basket:** "Find rules where the total price of items in the antecedent ≤ \$50 and confidence > 0.7" → instantly focus on budget patterns. - **Bioinformatics:** Mine gene co-expression patterns constrained by chromosome region. - **Web Usage Mining:** Find session patterns where users spent > 5 minutes on pages tagged 'Sports'. - **Telecom:** Detect fraudulent call patterns where call-duration sum > threshold. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples ## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Classifying a Given Constraint **Problem:** Classify the following constraints as **monotonic**, **anti-monotonic**, or **neither**: 1. $C_1: \text{sum}(S.\text{price}) \geq 100$ 2. $C_2: \text{min}(S.\text{price}) \geq 10$ 3. $C_3: \text{avg}(S.\text{price}) \geq 50$ **Step-by-step Solution:** **For $C_1$:** Suppose $S = \{a, b, c\}$ satisfies sum $\geq 100$. Consider a subset $S' \subset S$. Removing an item can only **decrease** the sum, so $\text{sum}(S') \leq \text{sum}(S)$. Thus, the subset *may or may not* satisfy $C_1$. Hence $C_1$ is **not anti-monotonic**. Conversely, $S$ satisfies $\Rightarrow$ subset satisfies? No. So it is also not strictly monotonic in the standard sense — but observe: $S$ satisfies means sum is high; larger set can have even higher sum, so $S \subseteq X \Rightarrow \text{sum}(X) \geq \text{sum}(S) \geq 100$. So $C_1$ **IS monotonic**. ✅ **For $C_2$:** Suppose $S$ has $\min \geq 10$. Any subset of $S$ has elements drawn from $S$, so its $\min$ is also $\geq 10$. Hence $C_2$ is **anti-monotonic**. ✅ **For $C_3$:** The average of a set is **not monotonic** under subset relation. Example: $S = \{50, 100\}$, avg = 75 (satisfies $\geq 50$). Subset $\{100\}$, avg = 100 (still satisfies). But another subset $\{50\}$, avg = 50 (boundary). The problem is that the *threshold itself depends on the size* $k$ of the itemset. So in raw form, $C_3$ is **neither** — but it is **convertible**. ## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Converting an Average Constraint **Problem:** Convert $C: \text{avg}(S.\text{price}) \geq 50$ into an anti-monotonic constraint. **Derivation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{avg}(S.\text{price}) &\geq 50 \\ \frac{\text{sum}(S.\text{price})}{|S|} &\geq 50 \\ \text{sum}(S.\text{price}) &\geq 50 \cdot |S| \end{aligned} $$ If we fix the itemset size to $k$, then $C' : \text{sum}(S.\text{price}) \geq 50k$ is **anti-monotonic** for that fixed $k$ (larger sets have higher or equal sum, so they are more likely to satisfy). **Reordering step:** Sort items by price in **ascending order** $i_1 \leq i_2 \leq \dots \leq i_n$. Now growing the set by appending the next item only **increases** the sum. So the adjusted anti-monotonic constraint becomes: $$C'_{k}: \text{sum}(S_k) \geq 50k$$ where $S_k$ denotes the first $k$ items in ascending order. As $k$ increases, the threshold $50k$ increases, but the sum also increases. The key property: $$\text{If } S \text{ violates } C'_k,\; \text{then any } S' \supseteq S \text{ with } |S'| \geq |S| \text{ in the same order also violates} \Rightarrow \text{anti-monotonic in the ordered sense}.$$ ## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Anti-Monotonic Pruning on a Transaction Database **Database $D$:** (Transactions over items $\{A, B, C, D, E\}$) | TID | Items | |-----|-------| | T1 | A, B, C | | T2 | A, B, D | | T3 | A, C | | T4 | B, D | | T5 | A, B, C, D | **Constraint:** $C \equiv \text{sum}(S.\text{price}) \leq 100$, with prices $A=40, B=30, C=50, D=20, E=60$. **Step 1 — Compute single-item sums (always satisfy if price $\leq 100$):** - $A$: 40 ✅ - $B$: 30 ✅ - $C$: 50 ✅ - $D$: 20 ✅ - $E$: 60 ✅ **Step 2 — Test 2-itemsets (sum $\leq 100$):** - $\{A, B\}$: 70 ✅ - $\{A, C\}$: 90 ✅ - $\{A, D\}$: 60 ✅ - $\{A, E\}$: 100 ✅ - $\{B, C\}$: 80 ✅ - $\{B, D\}$: 50 ✅ - $\{B, E\}$: 90 ✅ - $\{C, D\}$: 70 ✅ - $\{C, E\}$: 110 ❌ → **PRUNE all supersets containing both C and E** - $\{D, E\}$: 80 ✅ **Step 3 — Test 3-itemsets (only those with all 2-subsets surviving):** Since $\{C, E\}$ failed, we **skip all supersets of $\{C, E\}$**: $\{A, C, E\}, \{B, C, E\}, \{C, D, E\}, \{A, B, C, E\}, \dots$ — that's $\binom{3}{2} = 3$ triples + all higher sets, saving significant work. This is the **power of anti-monotonicity**: a single violation prunes an entire upward closure (called the *concept lattice* in formal concept analysis). ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Constraint-Property Lookup | Constraint Type | Anti-Mono? | Mono? | Succinct? | Convertible? | |-----------------|------------|-------|-----------|--------------| | $\text{sum} \leq v$ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | — | | $\text{sum} \geq v$ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | — | | $\text{min} \geq v$ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | — | | $\text{max} \leq v$ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | — | | $\text{count} \leq k$ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | — | | $\text{avg} \geq v$ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ (ascending → anti-mono) | | $\text{avg} \leq v$ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ (descending → anti-mono) | | $\text{support} \geq \theta$ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | — | | $\text{support} \leq \theta$ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | — | | $i.\text{category} = \text{"Snack"}$ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | — | ## 3.5 Algorithm Sketch: Apriori with Constraint Pushing The KTU module typically asks to **modify Apriori** to incorporate constraints. The enhanced algorithm proceeds as: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{1. } L_1 \leftarrow \{ \text{frequent 1-itemsets satisfying } C \} \\ &\text{2. } \textbf{for } (k = 2;\; L_{k-1} \neq \emptyset;\; k{+}{+}) \text{ \textbf{do}} \\ &\text{3. } \quad C_k \leftarrow \text{apriori\_gen}(L_{k-1}) \quad \text{(candidate generation)} \\ &\text{4. } \quad \text{\textbf{for each}} \text{ transaction } t \in D \text{ \textbf{do}} \\ &\text{5. } \quad\quad C_t \leftarrow \text{subset}(C_k, t) \quad \text{(identify candidates in } t\text{)} \\ &\text{6. } \quad\quad \text{\textbf{for each}} \text{ candidate } c \in C_t \text{ \textbf{do}} \\ &\text{7. } \quad\quad\quad c.\text{count} \leftarrow c.\text{count} + 1 \\ &\text{8. } \quad L_k \leftarrow \{ c \in C_k \mid c.\text{count} \geq \text{minsup} \;\land\; C(c) = \text{true} \} \\ &\text{9. } \text{Answer} \leftarrow \bigcup_k L_k \end{aligned} $$ The key change over classical Apriori is the additional boolean check $C(c) = \text{true}$ in Step 8, plus **pre-pruning** of candidates whose subsets violate anti-monotonic constraints (Step 3's apriori\_gen is augmented with a constraint-pruning routine). ## 3.6 Worked Example 4: Mining with Multiple Constraints **Combined Constraint:** $C_1 \equiv \text{support}(S) \geq 30\%$ $\land$ $C_2 \equiv \text{sum}(S.\text{price}) \leq 150$ $\land$ $C_3 \equiv \text{avg}(S.\text{rating}) \geq 4.0$. - $C_1$ is **anti-monotonic** (standard minimum support). - $C_2$ is **anti-monotonic** (sum upper bound). - $C_3$ is **convertible** (average). **Effective Strategy:** - Use $C_1$ and $C_2$ to prune the lattice early. - For $C_3$, reorder items by rating in ascending order, then apply the converted anti-monotonic constraint $\text{sum}(\text{ratings}) \geq 4k$ during $k$-itemset generation. - The conjunction of constraints is also anti-monotonic if **all** components are — this is the key lemma: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Lemma (Conjunctive Anti-Monotonicity):** If $C_1, C_2, \dots, C_m$ are all anti-monotonic, then $C = C_1 \land C_2 \land \dots \land C_m$ is also anti-monotonic. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Taxonomy of Constraint Properties ```mermaid graph TD A[User Constraint C] --> B{Is C expressible
on individual items?} B -- Yes --> C[Succinct Constraint] B -- No --> D{Does C depend
on superset ordering?} D -- No --> E{Monotonicity Check} E -- Violated supersets exist --> F[Anti-Monotonic] E -- Satisfied subsets exist --> G[Monotonic] D -- Yes --> H[Reorder Items
by value] H --> I[Convertible Constraint] I --> J[Convertible Anti-Monotonic] I --> K[Convertible Monotonic] F --> L[Prune Upward Closure] G --> M[Prune Downward Closure] J --> N[Prune with Adjusted Threshold] K --> O[Prune with Adjusted Threshold] C --> P[Use Selection Operator
on Items] style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style J fill:#f8c471,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Constraint-Pushing Architecture (Apriori + Constraints) ```mermaid graph TD RawDB[Raw Transaction DB] --> Scan1[Pass 1: Scan DB] Scan1 --> Freq1[Frequent 1-Itemsets L1] Freq1 --> ConstraintFilter1{Apply
Anti-Monotonic C} ConstraintFilter1 -- Pruned --> DropSet1[Discard Violators] ConstraintFilter1 -- Pass --> CandGen2[Apriori-Gen L2] DropSet1 --> NextIter[Move to Next Iteration] CandGen2 --> Scan2[Pass 2: Scan DB] Scan2 --> ConstraintFilter2{Apply C
+ Prune Subsets} ConstraintFilter2 -- Pruned --> DropSet2[Discard] ConstraintFilter2 -- Pass --> Lk[Frequent k-Itemsets Lk] Lk --> ResultSet[Final Constrained Frequent Set] NextIter --> CandGen2 style RawDB fill:#d5dbdb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style Lk fill:#abebc6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style ResultSet fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style DropSet1 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style DropSet2 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Pruning Power: Anti-Monotonic vs. Monotonic ```mermaid graph LR AM[Anti-Monotonic C] --> P1[If X violates C
then ALL supersets of X
also violate C] P1 --> A1[Prune entire
upward subtree] M[Monotonic C] --> P2[If X satisfies C
then ALL subsets of X
also satisfy C] P2 --> A2[Accept entire
downward subtree] style AM fill:#f1948a,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style M fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.4 Convertible Constraint Reordering Flow ```mermaid graph TD Start[Original Constraint
e.g., avg S greater than v] --> Convert[Convert to Sum Form
sum S greater than v times k] Convert --> Sort{Choose
Ordering} Sort -- Ascending --> Anti[Becomes Anti-Monotonic
after reordering] Sort -- Descending --> Mono[Becomes Monotonic
after reordering] Anti --> Apply1[Prune Upward Closure
with adjusted threshold] Mono --> Apply2[Accept Downward Closure
with adjusted threshold] style Start fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style Anti fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style Mono fill:#abebc6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Definition & Classification **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** > **Q1.** Define **constraint-based association mining**. List any **four categories** of constraints that can be pushed into the mining process. **Model Answer (3 marks):** Constraint-based association mining is the process of mining frequent itemsets and association rules where the search is restricted by **user-specified predicates or conditions** to focus on interesting patterns and improve efficiency. Four categories of constraints: 1. **Knowledge-type constraints** — specify what kind of knowledge to mine. 2. **Data constraints** — restrict the data to be mined (e.g., specific region, time). 3. **Dimension/level constraints** — restrict dimensions or abstraction levels. 4. **Interestingness constraints** — threshold on support, confidence, correlation, etc. *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for categories — 0.5 each)* --- ### Question 2: Anti-Monotonicity **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** > **Q2.** State and explain the **anti-monotonicity** property of a constraint with a suitable example. **Model Answer (3 marks):** A constraint $C_a$ is **anti-monotonic** if for any itemset $X$ and its subset $Y$: $$Y \subseteq X \;\land\; C_a(Y) = \text{false} \Rightarrow C_a(X) = \text{false}$$ In other words, **if a set violates the constraint, all its supersets also violate it.** This enables early pruning of the search space. **Example:** $C_a \equiv \text{sum}(S.\text{price}) \leq 100$. If $\{C, E\}$ has sum = 110, then any superset (like $\{A, C, E\}$, $\{A, B, C, E\}$) will have sum $\geq 110$ and also violate the constraint. Hence the entire upward closure can be pruned. *(1 mark statement, 1 mark explanation, 1 mark example)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### 🔹 Question A — Anti-Monotonic + Convertible Constraints **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`** > **Q (a).** Explain the different **types of constraints** that can be used in constraint-based association mining. Classify them with **examples** in a tabular form. **(7 marks)** > > **Q (b).** What are **convertible constraints**? Show with a **suitable example** how an average constraint can be converted into an anti-monotonic constraint using item reordering. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Q (a): The constraints used in constraint-based mining are classified as follows: | Constraint Class | Mathematical Form | Example | Property | |------------------|-------------------|---------|----------| | **Data Constraint** | $D' = \sigma_{\text{condition}}(D)$ | Sales in 2024 only | Filters tuples | | **Dimension Constraint** | $\text{level}(i) = l$ | Items at category level | Anti-monotonic | | **Aggregate Constraint (Sum)** | $\text{sum}(S) \theta v$ | $\text{sum}(\text{price}) \leq 500$ | Anti-mono if $\theta$ is $\leq$; Mono if $\geq$ | | **Aggregate Constraint (Avg)** | $\text{avg}(S) \theta v$ | $\text{avg}(\text{price}) \geq 100$ | Convertible | | **Frequency Constraint** | $\text{support}(S) \geq \text{minsup}$ | Min support 5% | Anti-monotonic | | **Interestingness (Rule)** | $\text{confidence}(X \Rightarrow Y) \geq 0.8$ | High-confidence rules | Post-mining filter | *(Full tabular explanation with examples — 7 marks; table structure 2 marks, examples 3 marks, explanation 2 marks)* #### Model Solution to Q (b): **Convertible Constraints:** Some aggregate constraints like `avg` are neither monotonic nor anti-monotonic in their raw form because the threshold depends on the **cardinality** $k$ of the itemset. Such constraints are called **convertible** — they can be made monotonic or anti-monotonic by **reordering items by value**. **Example: Converting $\text{avg}(S.\text{price}) \geq 50$ to anti-monotonic form.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{avg}(S.\text{price}) &\geq 50 \\ \frac{\sum_{i \in S} i.\text{price}}{|S|} &\geq 50 \\ \sum_{i \in S} i.\text{price} &\geq 50 \cdot |S| \end{aligned} $$ **Step 1 — Reorder items in ascending order of price:** $$i_1.\text{price} \leq i_2.\text{price} \leq i_3.\text{price} \leq \dots \leq i_n.\text{price}$$ **Step 2 — Define the adjusted constraint for $k$-itemsets:** $$C'_k : \text{sum of first } k \text{ prices} \geq 50k$$ **Step 3 — Show anti-monotonicity under this ordering:** If $S_k$ is the set of the first $k$ items in ascending order and $C'_k$ is violated (i.e., sum $< 50k$), then any superset $S_{k+1} = S_k \cup \{i_{k+1}\}$ has sum increased by at most the largest price. But because items are sorted **ascending**, the growth of sum is bounded. Specifically, for the constraint to remain violated at level $k+1$, we need $\text{sum}(S_k) + i_{k+1}.\text{price} < 50(k+1)$. Since items are sorted ascending, $i_{k+1}.\text{price} \geq i_j.\text{price}$ for all $j \leq k$, so the sum grows at least as fast as the threshold — meaning the violation **does not propagate** upward in general; rather, by **carefully tracking the threshold**, the algorithm can prune candidate supersets whose prefix sum is too small. In practice, the algorithm prunes a candidate $S_k$ if $\text{sum}(S_k) < 50k$, and since larger $k$ requires a higher sum (50k), violations at level $k$ **may still allow some supersets at level $k+1$** — but the technique is still **anti-monotonic within the ordered enumeration** and prunes a large fraction of the search space. *(Definition 2 marks, conversion derivation 2 marks, reordering explanation 2 marks, anti-monotonicity verification 1 mark)* --- ### 🔹 Question B — Lattice Pruning via Anti-Monotonicity **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** > **Q (a).** With a **suitable example**, demonstrate how the **anti-monotonicity property** of a constraint can be used to **prune the itemset lattice** during frequent itemset mining. **(7 marks)** > > **Q (b).** Differentiate between **monotonic** and **anti-monotonic** constraints. Explain with examples how each property is used in **optimizing the mining process**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Q (a): Consider the item universe $I = \{A, B, C, D, E\}$ and the constraint: $$C \equiv \text{sum}(S.\text{price}) \leq 100$$ with prices $A=40$, $B=30$, $C=50$, $D=20$, $E=60$. **Itemset Lattice (Bottom-Up):** Level 1: $\{A\}, \{B\}, \{C\}, \{D\}, \{E\}$ Level 2: $\{A,B\}, \{A,C\}, \{A,D\}, \{A,E\}, \{B,C\}, \{B,D\}, \{B,E\}, \{C,D\}, \{C,E\}, \{D,E\}$ Level 3 and beyond are formed by combinations. **Lattice with constraint values:** | Itemset | Sum | Violates C? | Action | |---------|-----|-------------|--------| | $\{A\}$ | 40 | No | Keep | | $\{B\}$ | 30 | No | Keep | | $\{C\}$ | 50 | No | Keep | | $\{D\}$ | 20 | No | Keep | | $\{E\}$ | 60 | No | Keep | | $\{A,B\}$ | 70 | No | Keep | | $\{A,C\}$ | 90 | No | Keep | | $\{A,D\}$ | 60 | No | Keep | | $\{A,E\}$ | 100 | No | Keep | | $\{B,C\}$ | 80 | No | Keep | | $\{B,D\}$ | 50 | No | Keep | | $\{B,E\}$ | 90 | No | Keep | | $\{C,D\}$ | 70 | No | Keep | | **$\{C,E\}$** | **110** | **Yes** | **PRUNE — and prune all supersets!** | | $\{D,E\}$ | 80 | No | Keep | | $\{A,B,C\}$ | 120 | Yes | Skipped (superset of $\{C,E\}$) | | $\{A,C,E\}$ | 150 | Yes | Skipped | | $\{B,C,E\}$ | 140 | Yes | Skipped | | $\{C,D,E\}$ | 130 | Yes | Skipped | | $\{A,B,C,E\}$ | 180 | Yes | Skipped | | ... | ... | ... | ... | **Pruning Power:** The single violation at $\{C, E\}$ prunes **7 itemsets** at level 3+ and exponentially more at higher levels. This demonstrates the immense savings of anti-monotonic constraint pushing. *(Setup 2 marks, table 3 marks, explanation of pruning 2 marks)* #### Model Solution to Q (b): **Difference Table:** | Aspect | Anti-Monotonic | Monotonic | |--------|----------------|-----------| | **Direction** | $C(Y) \Rightarrow C(X)$ for $X \supseteq Y$ | $C(X) \Rightarrow C(Y)$ for $Y \subseteq X$ | | **Violation Propagation** | Violation propagates **upward** (to supersets) | Violation propagates **downward** (to subsets) | | **Satisfaction Propagation** | Satisfaction propagates **downward** (to subsets) | Satisfaction propagates **upward** (to supersets) | | **Pruning Strategy** | Discard entire upward subtree on violation | Discard entire downward subtree on violation | | **Example** | $\text{support} \geq \text{minsup}$, $\text{sum} \leq 100$ | $\text{sum} \geq 100$, $\text{support} \leq 0.5$ | | **KTU Memory Trick** | "More items → harder to satisfy" | "More items → easier to satisfy" | **Mining Optimization:** For **anti-monotonic** constraints, we apply a **bottom-up traversal** of the lattice (like Apriori). When a set fails, we skip all its supersets. This is the principle behind Apriori's pruning. For **monotonic** constraints, we apply a **top-down traversal**. When a set satisfies, we accept all its subsets without testing. Combined with anti-monotonic constraints (e.g., min support), this dual approach (e.g., **FIUT — Frequent Itemset Ultrametric Tree**) can drastically shrink the search. *(Comparison table 3 marks, optimization explanation 4 marks)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing monotonicity direction.** Many students write "anti-monotonic means if a set satisfies, all subsets also satisfy." This is **WRONG** — that is the definition of **monotonic**. Anti-monotonic: violation propagates upward, satisfaction propagates downward. > > 2. **Forgetting to convert average constraints.** A constraint like $\text{avg} \geq v$ cannot be used directly. You **must convert** to $\text{sum} \geq v \cdot k$ and apply item reordering. Students often skip this step, losing 2-3 marks. > > 3. **Missing the size-dependence in average constraints.** The threshold $v' = v \times k$ depends on the level $k$ in the lattice. Forgetting this loses 1-2 marks. > > 4. **Not mentioning succinct constraints.** KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists **succinct** constraints as a category. Omitting them costs 1 mark in 14-mark questions. > > 5. **Failing to show the lattice-pruning diagram.** In 7-mark sub-questions, a diagram of the pruned lattice earns 2 extra marks. Always draw it. > > 6. **Misclassifying `count ≤ k` as monotonic.** It is **anti-monotonic** (more items → harder to keep count low). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** Constraint-based mining = guided frequent pattern mining with user-specified predicates to prune search space. - **Four Pillars of Constraints:** *Data, Dimension (Level), Knowledge-type, Interestingness.* - **Anti-monotonic constraint:** Violation propagates **upward** to supersets → enables upward subtree pruning. Examples: min support, sum $\leq v$, min $\geq v$, count $\leq k$. - **Monotonic constraint:** Violation propagates **downward** to subsets → enables downward subtree pruning. Examples: sum $\geq v$, max $\leq v$, support $\leq \theta$. - **Succinct constraint:** Can be expressed as a selection $\sigma$ on individual items (e.g., category = "Snack"). Highest pruning power — items are either kept or dropped upfront. - **Convertible constraints:** Aggregate constraints like `avg` that are neither mono nor anti-mono in raw form. They are converted via **item reordering** (ascending/descending) and **threshold adjustment** ($v' = v \cdot k$). - **Conjunction Lemma:** AND of multiple anti-monotonic constraints remains anti-monotonic. The same does **NOT** hold for monotonic in general. - **Algorithm Impact:** Constraint pushing reduces the exponential search from $O(2^{|I|})$ to a much smaller effective subspace. - **Real-world use:** Retail, bioinformatics, web mining, telecom fraud detection. - **KTU-Favourite 14-Mark Structure:** "Explain constraint types (7 marks) + Convertible/anti-monotonic example with lattice pruning (7 marks)." > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Examiner Tip:** When asked to "classify a constraint," always show the **subset/superset argument** explicitly. Don't just state the answer — demonstrate the proof in 2-3 lines. This is what separates an 8-mark answer from a 14-mark answer.

topics.title: Cluster Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT604 - DATA MINING AND WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cluster Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cluster Analysis** (also called **Clustering** or **Unsupervised Classification**) is the process of partitioning a set of data objects (or observations) into subsets (called **clusters**) such that objects in the same cluster are highly similar to each other, while objects in different clusters are highly dissimilar. Unlike classification, clustering operates in an **unsupervised** manner — there is no predefined class label.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Cluster analysis groups data objects based only on information found in the data that describes the objects and their relationships. The goal is that objects within a cluster be similar (high intra-class similarity) and objects in different clusters be dissimilar (low inter-class similarity).*

Formally, given a dataset $D = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ where each $x_i$ is a multi-dimensional feature vector in $\mathbb{R}^d$, clustering seeks to find $k$ partitions $\{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_k\}$ such that:

$$C_1 \cup C_2 \cup \ldots \cup C_k = D \quad \text{and} \quad C_i \cap C_j = \emptyset \text{ for } i \neq j$$

The **intra-cluster similarity** is maximized and **inter-cluster similarity** is minimized.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Library Analogy**
>
> Imagine you walk into a massive, completely disorganized library with thousands of unsorted books dumped into one giant pile. You have no catalog, no labels, no Dewey Decimal system. Your task: group the books so that similar books sit together.
>
> - Books on Physics go on one shelf.
> - Books on Cooking go on another shelf.
> - Romance novels form a third cluster.
>
> You, the librarian, are performing **cluster analysis**. You never *knew* these categories in advance — you *discovered* them by reading the books and finding natural groupings. This is exactly what clustering algorithms do with data: they discover hidden structure without being told what the structure is.

A second analogy: think of a **grocery store**. Items like bread, butter, and milk are kept close because customers who buy one often buy the others. Clustering in **market-basket analysis** discovers these natural purchase groups.

## 1.3 Why Cluster Analysis Matters in KTU 2024

Clustering is foundational to:
- **Customer segmentation** in business intelligence
- **Document/topic discovery** in text mining
- **Image compression** (vector quantization)
- **Anomaly/outlier detection** (noise points in DBSCAN)
- **Bioinformatics** (gene expression clustering)
- **Social network analysis** (community detection)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Tested):**
> - **Classification** = Supervised (class label known in advance)
> - **Clustering** = Unsupervised (class label unknown, must be discovered)

## 1.4 General Requirements of Clustering (Quality Criteria)

According to the standard KTU reference framework, good clustering must satisfy:

1. **Scalability** — efficient for large datasets
2. **Ability to handle different data types** — numerical, categorical, binary, ordinal
3. **Discovery of clusters with arbitrary shape** — not just spherical
4. **Minimal domain knowledge requirement** — few input parameters
5. **Robustness to noise and outliers**
6. **Insensitivity to order of input records**
7. **High dimensionality capability**
8. **Interpretability and usability** of results
9. **Constraint-based clustering** support

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Clustering Methods

Clustering methods are broadly classified into the following categories (a high-yield topic for 14-mark questions):

| Category | Core Idea | Representative Algorithms | Shape of Clusters |
|----------|-----------|---------------------------|-------------------|
| **Partitioning** | Divide data into k non-overlapping subsets | K-Means, K-Medoids (PAM), CLARA | Spherical / Convex |
| **Hierarchical** | Create a tree of clusters (dendrogram) | AGNES, DIANA, BIRCH, Chameleon | Arbitrary |
| **Density-Based** | Grow clusters where data is dense | DBSCAN, OPTICS, DENCLUE | Arbitrary |
| **Grid-Based** | Quantize space into cells, then cluster | STING, CLIQUE, Wave-Cluster | Arbitrary |
| **Model-Based** | Assume a statistical model per cluster | EM, COBWEB, SOM (Neural) | Statistical |
| **Constraint-Based** | User-specified constraints | COD (Clustering with Obstructed Distance) | User-defined |

## 2.2 Types of Data Matrices in Clustering

The input to a clustering algorithm is typically represented as a **data matrix** (objects × features) or a **dissimilarity matrix** (object × object).

**Data Matrix:** $n$ objects each described by $p$ attributes.

$$\begin{bmatrix} x_{11} & x_{12} & \cdots & x_{1p} \\ x_{21} & x_{22} & \cdots & x_{2p} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ x_{n1} & x_{n2} & \cdots & x_{np} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Dissimilarity Matrix** $\Delta$ is symmetric with zeros on the diagonal:

$$\Delta = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & \delta_{12} & \cdots & \delta_{1n} \\ \delta_{21} & 0 & \cdots & \delta_{2n} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \delta_{n1} & \delta_{n2} & \cdots & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet: Proximity / Distance Measures

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FOR KTU EXAMS:** Distance metric questions appear every semester. Master these formulas.

### 2.3.1 Minkowski Distance (General Form)

For two $p$-dimensional points $X = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_p)$ and $Y = (y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_p)$:

$$d(X, Y) = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{p} \vert x_i - y_i \rvert^{r} \right)^{1/r}$$

### 2.3.2 Special Cases of Minkowski

| Distance | Formula | Value of $r$ | Use Case |
|----------|---------|--------------|----------|
| **Manhattan / City-Block ($L_1$)** | $d = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \vert x_i - y_i \vert$ | $r = 1$ | Grid-like movement |
| **Euclidean ($L_2$)** | $d = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{p} (x_i - y_i)^2}$ | $r = 2$ | Most common, default for K-Means |
| **Chebyshev / Supremum ($L_\infty$)** | $d = \max_{i} \vert x_i - y_i \vert$ | $r \to \infty$ | King-move in chess |
| **Mahalanobis** | $d = \sqrt{(X-Y)^T \Sigma^{-1} (X-Y)}$ | Uses covariance $\Sigma$ | Scale-invariant, correlated features |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU answer sheets, the vertical pipe $\vert$ is a frequent typo-trap. Use $\lvert$ and $\rvert$ for typographic correctness, or simply write "abs(...)".

### 2.3.3 Cosine Similarity (Critical for Text Mining)

$$\text{sim}(X, Y) = \cos(\theta) = \frac{X \cdot Y}{\lVert X \rVert \, \lVert Y \rVert} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{p} x_i y_i}{\sqrt{\sum x_i^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum y_i^2}}$$

Cosine **distance** = $1 - \cos(\theta)$. Values range from $-1$ to $1$ (similarity) or $0$ to $2$ (distance).

### 2.3.4 Jaccard Coefficient (For Asymmetric Binary / Set Data)

$$J(A, B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert} = \frac{M_{11}}{M_{11} + M_{10} + M_{01}}$$

where $M_{11}$ is the number of attributes where both objects are 1, etc.

## 2.4 Major Clustering Methods — Algorithmic Details

### 2.4.1 K-Means Partitioning Algorithm

**Objective Function** (Sum of Squared Errors / Within-Cluster Variation):

$$J = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_j} \lVert x - \mu_j \rVert^2$$

where $\mu_j$ is the **centroid** (mean) of cluster $C_j$:

$$\mu_j = \frac{1}{\vert C_j \vert} \sum_{x \in C_j} x$$

**Algorithmic Steps:**

1. Randomly choose $k$ objects as initial centroids
2. **(Assign):** Assign each object to the cluster with the **nearest centroid** (by Euclidean distance)
3. **(Update):** Recompute the centroid of each cluster as the mean of all objects assigned to it
4. Repeat Steps 2–3 until **convergence** (centroids no longer change, or SSE change is below threshold)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convergence:** K-Means is guaranteed to converge (because SSE monotonically decreases), but it may converge to a **local optimum**, not the global optimum. KTU 14-mark questions often test this nuance.

### 2.4.2 K-Medoids (PAM — Partitioning Around Medoids)

K-Medoids replaces the centroid (which is a *virtual* point) with a **medoid** (an *actual* data point that minimizes the sum of distances to other points in the cluster).

**Why K-Medoids?**
- Robust to **outliers** and **noise** (K-Means is highly sensitive)
- Works for non-numeric data (any dissimilarity matrix)
- More expensive: $O(k(n-k)^2)$ per iteration

### 2.4.3 Hierarchical Clustering

Produces a **dendrogram** — a tree-like structure showing nested cluster relationships.

| Approach | Direction | Algorithm | Strategy |
|----------|-----------|-----------|----------|
| **Agglomerative** | Bottom-up | AGNES | Start with $n$ singleton clusters; iteratively merge the two closest clusters |
| **Divisive** | Top-down | DIANA | Start with one cluster containing all points; iteratively split the most heterogeneous cluster |

**Linkage Methods (Critical for KTU 14-Mark Questions):**

Given two clusters $C_i$ and $C_j$, define cluster distance $d(C_i, C_j)$ as:

| Linkage | Formula | Tendency |
|---------|---------|----------|
| **Single Linkage** (MIN) | $d(C_i, C_j) = \min_{x \in C_i, y \in C_j} d(x, y)$ | Chaining effect, elongated clusters |
| **Complete Linkage** (MAX) | $d(C_i, C_j) = \max_{x \in C_i, y \in C_j} d(x, y)$ | Compact, equal-size clusters |
| **Average Linkage** (Group Average) | $d(C_i, C_j) = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert \vert C_j \vert} \sum_{x \in C_i, y \in C_j} d(x, y)$ | Compromise between MIN and MAX |
| **Centroid Linkage** | $d(C_i, C_j) = \lVert \mu_i - \mu_j \rVert$ | Inversions possible |
| **Ward's Method** | $\Delta E = \frac{\vert C_i \vert \cdot \vert C_j \vert}{\vert C_i \vert + \vert C_j \vert} \lVert \mu_i - \mu_j \rVert^2$ | Minimizes within-cluster SSE |

### 2.4.4 DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise)

Two parameters: **Eps** ($\varepsilon$, neighborhood radius) and **MinPts** (minimum points threshold).

**Key Definitions:**
- $\varepsilon$-**neighborhood** of a point $p$: $N_\varepsilon(p) = \{q \in D \mid d(p, q) \leq \varepsilon\}$
- **Core point:** $\vert N_\varepsilon(p) \vert \geq \text{MinPts}$
- **Border point:** Not a core, but within $\varepsilon$ of a core point
- **Noise point:** Neither core nor border
- **Directly density-reachable:** $q \in N_\varepsilon(p)$ and $p$ is a core point
- **Density-reachable:** Chain of directly density-reachable points
- **Density-connected:** Two points $p, q$ are density-connected if there exists a point $o$ such that both are density-reachable from $o$

### 2.4.5 Cluster Validity Measures (KTU Hot Topic)

| Index | Formula | What it measures |
|-------|---------|------------------|
| **Silhouette Coefficient** | $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$ | Per-point cohesion vs. separation; range $[-1, 1]$ |
| **Davies-Bouldin Index (DBI)** | $DB = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} \max_{j \neq i}\left(\frac{S_i + S_j}{d_{ij}}\right)$ | Lower is better |
| **Dunn Index** | $D = \frac{\min_{i \neq j} d(C_i, C_j)}{\max_k \text{diam}(C_k)}$ | Higher is better |

Where $a(i)$ = mean distance to other points in same cluster; $b(i)$ = min mean distance to points in other clusters.
