# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: K-Means Algorithm by Hand

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive step-by-step solution — typical 7-mark KTU Part B question.**

**Given Data Points (2D):**

$$P_1 = (2, 10), \quad P_2 = (2, 5), \quad P_3 = (8, 4), \quad P_4 = (5, 8), \quad P_5 = (7, 5), \quad P_6 = (6, 4), \quad P_7 = (1, 2), \quad P_8 = (4, 9)$$

Choose $k = 3$. Initial centroids: $C_1 = P_1 = (2, 10)$, $C_2 = P_4 = (5, 8)$, $C_3 = P_7 = (1, 2)$.

### Iteration 1: Assignment

Compute Euclidean distance $d(P_i, C_j) = \sqrt{(x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(P_1, C_1) &= \sqrt{0^2 + 0^2} = 0 \quad \rightarrow \text{Cluster 1} \\
d(P_2, C_1) &= \sqrt{0^2 + 5^2} = 5.00 \\
d(P_2, C_2) &= \sqrt{9 + 9} = \sqrt{18} \approx 4.24 \\
d(P_2, C_3) &= \sqrt{1 + 9} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.16 \quad \rightarrow \text{Cluster 3} \\
d(P_3, C_2) &= \sqrt{9 + 16} = 5.00 \\
d(P_3, C_3) &= \sqrt{49 + 4} = \sqrt{53} \approx 7.28 \\
d(P_3, C_1) &= \sqrt{36 + 36} = \sqrt{72} \approx 8.49 \quad \rightarrow \text{Cluster 2} \\
d(P_4, C_2) &= 0 \quad \rightarrow \text{Cluster 2} \\
d(P_5, C_2) &= \sqrt{4 + 9} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.61 \quad \rightarrow \text{Cluster 2} \\
d(P_6, C_2) &= \sqrt{1 + 16} = \sqrt{17} \approx 4.12 \quad \rightarrow \text{Cluster 2} \\
d(P_7, C_3) &= 0 \quad \rightarrow \text{Cluster 3} \\
d(P_8, C_2) &= \sqrt{1 + 1} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41 \quad \rightarrow \text{Cluster 2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 1 Result:**
- Cluster 1: $\{P_1\}$
- Cluster 2: $\{P_3, P_4, P_5, P_6, P_8\}$
- Cluster 3: $\{P_2, P_7\}$

### Iteration 1: Centroid Update

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_1 &= \frac{1}{1}(2, 10) = (2, 10) \\
\mu_2 &= \frac{1}{5}\left[(8+5+7+6+4), (4+8+5+4+9)\right] = \frac{1}{5}(30, 30) = (6, 6) \\
\mu_3 &= \frac{1}{2}\left[(2+1), (5+2)\right] = \frac{1}{2}(3, 7) = (1.5, 3.5)
\end{aligned}
$$

**SSE after Iteration 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SSE_1 &= \lVert P_1 - \mu_1 \rVert^2 + \sum_{x \in C_2} \lVert x - \mu_2 \rVert^2 + \sum_{x \in C_3} \lVert x - \mu_3 \rVert^2 \\
&= 0 + \big[(8-6)^2+(4-6)^2\big] + \big[(5-6)^2+(8-6)^2\big] + \big[(7-6)^2+(5-6)^2\big] \\
&\quad + \big[(6-6)^2+(4-6)^2\big] + \big[(4-6)^2+(9-6)^2\big] + \big[(2-1.5)^2+(5-3.5)^2\big] + \big[(1-1.5)^2+(2-3.5)^2\big] \\
&= 0 + 8 + 5 + 2 + 4 + 13 + 2.5 + 2.5 \\
&= 37
\end{aligned}
$$

### Iteration 2: Re-Assignment (using new centroids)

Compute distance of $P_2 = (2,5)$ from new centroids: $d(P_2, \mu_1)=5$, $d(P_2, \mu_2)=\sqrt{16+1}=\sqrt{17} \approx 4.12$, $d(P_2, \mu_3)=\sqrt{0.25+2.25}=\sqrt{2.5}\approx 1.58$. So $P_2$ stays in Cluster 3. Similarly verify all points — assignments remain the same. **Algorithm converges** with $SSE = 37$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Clusters after K-Means:**
> - $C_1 = \{(2,10)\}$
> - $C_2 = \{(8,4), (5,8), (7,5), (6,4), (4,9)\}$ with centroid $(6, 6)$
> - $C_3 = \{(2,5), (1,2)\}$ with centroid $(1.5, 3.5)$
> - **Total SSE = 37**

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Hierarchical Agglomerative Clustering (Single Linkage)

**Distance Matrix** for 5 points $A, B, C, D, E$:

$$
\Delta = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 1 & 9 & 7 & 5 \\
1 & 0 & 8 & 6 & 4 \\
9 & 8 & 0 & 3 & 2 \\
7 & 6 & 3 & 0 & 5 \\
5 & 4 & 2 & 5 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 1:** Minimum in $\Delta$ is $d(A, B) = 1$. Merge $\{A, B\}$ into cluster $AB$.

**Step 2:** Recompute distances from $AB$ to others using **single linkage** (MIN):

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(AB, C) &= \min\{d(A,C), d(B,C)\} = \min\{9, 8\} = 8 \\
d(AB, D) &= \min\{7, 6\} = 6 \\
d(AB, E) &= \min\{5, 4\} = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** New distance matrix: $\{AB\}, C, D, E$ with $d(AB, C)=8$, $d(AB, D)=6$, $d(AB, E)=4$, $d(C,D)=3$, $d(C,E)=2$, $d(D,E)=5$.

**Step 4:** Minimum is $d(C, E) = 2$. Merge $\{C, E\}$ into $CE$.

**Step 5:** Compute single-linkage distances from $CE$ to others:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(CE, AB) &= \min\{d(C,A), d(C,B), d(E,A), d(E,B)\} = \min\{9, 8, 5, 4\} = 4 \\
d(CE, D) &= \min\{3, 5\} = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Matrix is now $\{AB\}, \{CE\}, D$ with $d(AB, CE) = 4$, $d(AB, D) = 6$, $d(CE, D) = 3$.

**Step 7:** Minimum is $d(CE, D) = 3$. Merge to form $CED$.

**Step 8:** Distance between $AB$ and $CED$: $\min\{d(A,C), d(A,E), d(A,D), d(B,C), d(B,E), d(B,D)\} = \min\{9, 5, 7, 8, 4, 6\} = 4$.

**Step 9:** Merge into single cluster. **Final dendrogram merge order:** $(A,B) \to (C,E) \to (CED, AB) \to$ final.

> [!NOTE]
> **Merge Heights:** 1, 2, 3, 4. Plot on Y-axis of dendrogram.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: DBSCAN Concept Demonstration

Given $\varepsilon = 1.5$ and $\text{MinPts} = 4$ in 2D space:

- **Point $p$ with $|N_\varepsilon(p)| = 6$** → **Core point**
- **Point $q$ with $|N_\varepsilon(q)| = 2$** but $q \in N_\varepsilon(p)$ for some core $p$ → **Border point**
- **Point $r$ with $|N_\varepsilon(r)| = 2$** and not in any core's neighborhood → **Noise/Outlier**

The cluster = all points density-reachable from any core point. Border points attach to the nearest core. Noise is discarded.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Major Algorithms

| Feature | K-Means | K-Medoids (PAM) | Hierarchical (AGNES) | DBSCAN |
|---------|---------|-----------------|----------------------|--------|
| **Cluster Shape** | Spherical | Spherical | Arbitrary | Arbitrary |
| **Outlier Robustness** | Poor | Good | Moderate | Excellent |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(nkt)$ | $O(k(n-k)^2 i)$ | $O(n^2 \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ |
| **Input Parameters** | $k$ | $k$ | None / Linkage | $\varepsilon$, MinPts |
| **Scalability** | High | Low | Low | Moderate |
| **Deterministic?** | No (random init) | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Best For** | Large, clean, numeric | Small, with outliers | Small datasets | Irregular shapes, noise |

## 4.2 K-Means Algorithmic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[START] --> S1[INPUT: Dataset D, Number of Clusters k]
    S1 --> S2[Randomly select k points as initial centroids]
    S2 --> S3[ASSIGN step: For each point assign to nearest centroid using Euclidean distance]
    S3 --> S4[UPDATE step: Recompute centroid of each cluster as mean of assigned points]
    S4 --> S5{Convergence check: Did centroids change OR SSE change less than threshold}
    S5 -->|NO| S3
    S5 -->|YES| S6[OUTPUT: k clusters and their centroids]
    S6 --> S7[END]
```

## 4.3 DBSCAN Algorithmic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[START] --> S1[INPUT: Dataset D, epsilon, MinPts]
    S1 --> S2[Mark all points as UNVISITED]
    S2 --> S3[Pick an unvisited point p]
    S3 --> S4[Retrieve N_epsilon of p]
    S4 --> S5{Is N_epsilon of p at least MinPts}
    S5 -->|NO| S6[Mark p as NOISE temporarily]
    S5 -->|YES| S7[Create new cluster C, add p to C]
    S7 --> S8[For each q in N_epsilon of p]
    S8 --> S9{Is q unvisited}
    S9 -->|YES| S10[Mark q as VISITED, add N_epsilon of q to seed set]
    S9 -->|NO| S11{Is q not yet in any cluster}
    S10 --> S8
    S11 -->|YES| S12[Add q to cluster C]
    S11 -->|NO| S8
    S12 --> S8
    S6 --> S13{Any unvisited points left}
    S13 -->|YES| S3
    S13 -->|NO| S14[OUTPUT: clusters and noise points]
    S14 --> S15[END]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define cluster analysis. How is it different from classification?**

**Model Answer:**

Cluster analysis is the process of grouping a set of physical or abstract objects into classes of similar objects. A cluster is a collection of data objects that are similar to one another within the same cluster and dissimilar to objects in other clusters.

The key differences are:

- **Classification** is a **supervised** learning technique where the class label (the category) is known in advance and a model is trained on labeled data.
- **Clustering** is an **unsupervised** learning technique where class labels are **not known** in advance; the algorithm discovers the groupings automatically from the data's intrinsic structure.

| Aspect | Classification | Clustering |
|--------|---------------|------------|
| Type | Supervised | Unsupervised |
| Labels | Known (training data) | Unknown |
| Goal | Predict label of new point | Discover natural groups |
| Example | Spam detection | Customer segmentation |

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Explain the difference between Manhattan and Euclidean distance with formulas. When would you prefer Manhattan over Euclidean?**

**Model Answer:**

**Manhattan distance** (also called city-block or $L_1$ norm) sums the absolute differences along each axis:

$$d_{Manhattan}(X, Y) = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \lvert x_i - y_i \rvert$$

**Euclidean distance** (or $L_2$ norm) is the straight-line distance in multi-dimensional space:

$$d_{Euclidean}(X, Y) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{p} (x_i - y_i)^2}$$

**When to prefer Manhattan:**
- High-dimensional sparse data (e.g., text features)
- When feature scales differ greatly and we want a less sensitive measure (Manhattan is less affected by outliers since it doesn't square differences)
- When movement is constrained to grid-like paths (e.g., city streets, image processing pixels)
- Computationally cheaper in high dimensions

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **K-Means clustering algorithm** with its steps. Discuss its **advantages** and **limitations**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Given the following 2D data points: $A(2, 10)$, $B(2, 5)$, $C(8, 4)$, $D(5, 8)$, $E(7, 5)$, $F(6, 4)$, $G(1, 2)$, $H(4, 9)$. Apply **K-Means with $k=3$**, taking initial centroids as $A$, $D$, $G$. Show **all iterations** until convergence and compute the final **Sum of Squared Errors (SSE)**.

---

#### Model Solution (a) — K-Means Algorithm, Advantages, Limitations

**Definition:** K-Means is a **partitioning-based** clustering algorithm that divides $n$ objects into $k$ clusters such that each object belongs to the cluster with the **nearest mean (centroid)**.

**Algorithmic Steps:**

1. **Input:** Dataset $D$ of $n$ objects, integer $k$.
2. **Initialization:** Randomly select $k$ objects from $D$ as initial centroids $\mu_1^{(0)}, \mu_2^{(0)}, \ldots, \mu_k^{(0)}$.
3. **Assignment Step:** For each object $x_i \in D$, assign it to the nearest cluster:

$$C_j^{(t)} = \left\{ x_i : \lVert x_i - \mu_j^{(t)} \rVert^2 \leq \lVert x_i - \mu_{j^*}^{(t)} \rVert^2 \; \forall j^* = 1, \ldots, k \right\}$$

4. **Update Step:** Recompute the centroids as the mean of all objects assigned to each cluster:

$$\mu_j^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_j^{(t)} \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_j^{(t)}} x_i$$

5. **Convergence Check:** If $\mu_j^{(t+1)} = \mu_j^{(t)}$ for all $j$, STOP; else, return to Step 3.
6. **Output:** $k$ clusters and their centroids.

**Objective Function (Minimized):**

$$J = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_j} \lVert x - \mu_j \rVert^2$$

**Advantages:**
- Simple, easy to implement, computationally efficient ($O(nkt)$)
- Scales well to large datasets
- Guaranteed convergence (monotonic SSE decrease)
- Produces tight, spherical clusters when data is well-separated

**Limitations:**
- Must specify $k$ in advance
- Sensitive to **initial centroid selection** (may converge to local optima)
- Sensitive to **outliers** and **noise** (squared distance amplifies their effect)
- Only discovers **spherical/convex** clusters of similar size
- Sensitive to **feature scaling** (must normalize data first)
- Result varies with **order of input** (in some implementations)

---

#### Model Solution (b) — K-Means Numerical Computation

**Initial Centroids:** $\mu_1 = A(2, 10)$, $\mu_2 = D(5, 8)$, $\mu_3 = G(1, 2)$.

**Iteration 1: Assignment**

| Point | Coord | $d$ to $A$ | $d$ to $D$ | $d$ to $G$ | Assigned Cluster |
|-------|-------|------------|------------|------------|------------------|
| A | (2,10) | 0.00 | 3.61 | 11.31 | 1 |
| B | (2,5) | 5.00 | 4.24 | 3.16 | 3 |
| C | (8,4) | 8.49 | 5.00 | 7.28 | 2 |
| D | (5,8) | 3.61 | 0.00 | 6.71 | 2 |
| E | (7,5) | 7.07 | 3.61 | 6.71 | 2 |
| F | (6,4) | 7.21 | 4.12 | 6.40 | 2 |
| G | (1,2) | 11.31 | 6.71 | 0.00 | 3 |
| H | (4,9) | 2.83 | 1.41 | 8.54 | 2 |

**Clusters after Iteration 1:** $C_1 = \{A\}$, $C_2 = \{C, D, E, F, H\}$, $C_3 = \{B, G\}$.

**Centroid Update:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_1 &= A = (2, 10) \\
\mu_2 &= \left(\frac{8+5+7+6+4}{5}, \frac{4+8+5+4+9}{5}\right) = (6, 6) \\
\mu_3 &= \left(\frac{2+1}{2}, \frac{5+2}{2}\right) = (1.5, 3.5)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 2: Re-assignment using new centroids**

| Point | $d$ to $(2,10)$ | $d$ to $(6,6)$ | $d$ to $(1.5, 3.5)$ | New Cluster |
|-------|-----------------|----------------|---------------------|-------------|
| A | 0.00 | 5.66 | 6.52 | 1 |
| B | 5.00 | 4.12 | 1.58 | 3 |
| C | 8.49 | 2.83 | 6.52 | 2 |
| D | 3.61 | 1.41 | 4.61 | 2 |
| E | 7.07 | 2.24 | 5.59 | 2 |
| F | 7.21 | 2.00 | 5.22 | 2 |
| G | 11.31 | 6.71 | 1.58 | 3 |
| H | 2.83 | 2.24 | 6.04 | 2 |

**Clusters unchanged. Algorithm converges.**

**Final SSE Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SSE &= \lVert A - (2,10) \rVert^2 \\
&\quad + \lVert (8,4)-(6,6) \rVert^2 + \lVert (5,8)-(6,6) \rVert^2 + \lVert (7,5)-(6,6) \rVert^2 + \lVert (6,4)-(6,6) \rVert^2 + \lVert (4,9)-(6,6) \rVert^2 \\
&\quad + \lVert (2,5)-(1.5, 3.5) \rVert^2 + \lVert (1,2)-(1.5, 3.5) \rVert^2 \\
&= 0 + (4+4) + (1+4) + (1+1) + (0+4) + (4+9) + (0.25+2.25) + (0.25+2.25) \\
&= 0 + 8 + 5 + 2 + 4 + 13 + 2.5 + 2.5 \\
&= \boxed{37}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **Hierarchical Agglomerative Clustering** with a neat diagram. Discuss the various **linkage methods** (single, complete, average) with their characteristics.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Given the distance matrix below for 5 points, perform **agglomerative clustering using single linkage** and draw the **dendrogram**.

$$
\Delta = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 4 & 8 & 6 & 9 \\
4 & 0 & 6 & 5 & 7 \\
8 & 6 & 0 & 3 & 2 \\
6 & 5 & 3 & 0 & 5 \\
9 & 7 & 2 & 5 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

---

#### Model Solution (a) — Hierarchical Agglomerative Clustering

**Definition:** Hierarchical Agglomerative Clustering (HAC) builds a **tree of clusters** (dendrogram) by starting with each object as its own cluster and **successively merging** the two closest clusters until only one cluster remains.

**Algorithm (AGNES):**

1. Start with $n$ singleton clusters $C_1 = \{x_1\}, C_2 = \{x_2\}, \ldots, C_n = \{x_n\}$.
2. Compute the $n \times n$ **proximity matrix** (distance/dissimilarity).
3. Repeat:
   - Find the pair $(C_i, C_j)$ with minimum inter-cluster distance.
   - Merge them into a new cluster $C_{ij} = C_i \cup C_j$.
   - Update the proximity matrix by recomputing distances from the new cluster to all others.
4. Stop when only one cluster remains.

**Diagram:** (Already shown in Section 4.4)

**Linkage Methods:**

| Linkage | Formula | Tendency | Pros | Cons |
|---------|---------|----------|------|------|
| **Single (MIN)** | $\min_{x \in C_i, y \in C_j} d(x, y)$ | Chaining — elongated clusters | Can find non-elliptical shapes | Sensitive to noise |
| **Complete (MAX)** | $\max_{x \in C_i, y \in C_j} d(x, y)$ | Compact, equal-diameter clusters | Less sensitive to noise | Tends to break large clusters |
| **Average (Group Average)** | $\frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert \vert C_j \vert} \sum_{x,y} d(x, y)$ | Compromise | Robust | Computationally expensive |

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical heuristic:** Average linkage is the most commonly used in practice; complete linkage is preferred when noise is moderate; single linkage is reserved for detecting elongated / chain-like structures.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — Single Linkage Agglomerative Clustering

**Initial distance matrix (symmetric):** Rows/columns: P1, P2, P3, P4, P5.

|   | P1 | P2 | P3 | P4 | P5 |
|---|----|----|----|----|----|
| P1 | 0 | 4 | 8 | 6 | 9 |
| P2 | 4 | 0 | 6 | 5 | 7 |
| P3 | 8 | 6 | 0 | 3 | 2 |
| P4 | 6 | 5 | 3 | 0 | 5 |
| P5 | 9 | 7 | 2 | 5 | 0 |

**Step 1:** Smallest distance = $d(P_1, P_2) = 4$. Merge into cluster $C_{12}$.

**Step 2:** Recompute distances (single linkage) from $C_{12}$ to all others:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(C_{12}, P_3) &= \min\{8, 6\} = 6 \\
d(C_{12}, P_4) &= \min\{6, 5\} = 5 \\
d(C_{12}, P_5) &= \min\{9, 7\} = 7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Updated matrix: $\{C_{12}\}, P_3, P_4, P_5$.

|   | $C_{12}$ | P3 | P4 | P5 |
|---|----|----|----|----|
| $C_{12}$ | 0 | 6 | 5 | 7 |
| P3 | 6 | 0 | 3 | 2 |
| P4 | 5 | 3 | 0 | 5 |
| P5 | 7 | 2 | 5 | 0 |

**Step 4:** Smallest = $d(P_3, P_5) = 2$. Merge into $C_{35}$.

**Step 5:** Recompute distances from $C_{35}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(C_{35}, C_{12}) &= \min\{d(P_3, C_{12}), d(P_5, C_{12})\} = \min\{6, 7\} = 6 \\
d(C_{35}, P_4) &= \min\{d(P_3, P_4), d(P_5, P_4)\} = \min\{3, 5\} = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Updated matrix: $\{C_{12}\}, \{C_{35}\}, P_4$.

|   | $C_{12}$ | $C_{35}$ | P4 |
|---|----|----|----|
| $C_{12}$ | 0 | 6 | 5 |
| $C_{35}$ | 6 | 0 | 3 |
| P4 | 5 | 3 | 0 |

**Step 7:** Smallest = $d(C_{35}, P_4) = 3$. Merge into $C_{345}$.

**Step 8:** Final merge: $d(C_{12}, C_{345}) = \min\{6, 5, 7, 6\} = 5$.

**Merge Sequence (for dendrogram):**
1. $(P_1, P_2)$ at height 4
2. $(P_3, P_5)$ at height 2
3. $(C_{35}, P_4)$ at height 3
4. $(C_{12}, C_{345})$ at height 5

**Dendrogram:**

```
Height
  5 |--------[Final Cluster]---------
    |        /                       \
  4 |   [P1,P2]              [P3,P5,P4]
    |    /    \              /    |    \
  3 |  P1   P2      [P3,P5]   ?      P4
    |                  /  \
  2 |               P3    P5
    |
  0 |  P1   P2   P3   P4   P5
```

(Vertical lines indicate merge points at the respective heights.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Centroid and Medoid** — Centroid is the *mean* (often a virtual point); Medoid is the *actual data point* closest to all others. Many students write "centroid = medoid" — this loses full marks.
>
> 2. **Wrong Distance Formula** — Frequently, students write Euclidean with a wrong exponent (e.g., $\sum (x_i - y_i)^2$ without the square root) and lose 2 marks.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to Update Centroids** — In K-Means numericals, students often do Step 1 (assignment) but skip Step 2 (centroid recomputation). Always perform BOTH.
>
> 4. **SSE Calculation Errors** — Apply the formula $SSE = \sum \sum \lVert x_i - \mu_j \rVert^2$ carefully. A single point miscalculated loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 5. **Hierarchical Clustering — Wrong Linkage** — When using complete linkage, students use MIN distances by mistake. **Read the question carefully.**
>
> 6. **DBSCAN: Missing Definitions** — A 7-mark DBSCAN question requires defining core, border, noise points AND density-reachable / density-connected. Partial definitions lose marks.
>
> 7. **Saying "K-Means is optimal"** — K-Means only converges to a *local* optimum, not global. This is a classic 2-mark trap.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary — Read Before Exam:**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- ✅ Clustering = **unsupervised** grouping of data by similarity
- ✅ **Intra-cluster similarity** ↑, **Inter-cluster similarity** ↓
- ✅ Classification is supervised, Clustering is unsupervised
- ✅ Clustering is widely used in customer segmentation, image analysis, anomaly detection, bioinformatics

### 🔢 Distance / Proximity Metrics
- ✅ **Euclidean** (default for K-Means): $d = \sqrt{\sum (x_i - y_i)^2}$
- ✅ **Manhattan / City-Block** ($L_1$): $d = \sum \lvert x_i - y_i \rvert$
- ✅ **Minkowski** (general $L_r$): $d = (\sum \lvert x_i - y_i \rvert^r)^{1/r}$
- ✅ **Mahalanobis**: $d = \sqrt{(X-Y)^T \Sigma^{-1} (X-Y)}$ — scale-invariant
- ✅ **Cosine Similarity**: $\frac{X \cdot Y}{\lVert X \rVert \lVert Y \rVert}$ — used in text mining
- ✅ **Jaccard Coefficient**: $\frac{M_{11}}{M_{11} + M_{10} + M_{01}}$ — for binary/set data

### 🎯 K-Means
- ✅ Partitioning algorithm, $k$ must be specified
- ✅ Objective: minimize **SSE** (Sum of Squared Errors)
- ✅ Steps: **Initialize → Assign → Update → Check Convergence**
- ✅ Converges to **local optimum** (not global)
- ✅ Sensitive to **outliers** and **initial seeds**
- ✅ Only finds **spherical, equal-size** clusters

### 🌳 Hierarchical Clustering
- ✅ Produces a **dendrogram** (tree structure)
- ✅ **AGNES** (agglomerative, bottom-up) vs **DIANA** (divisive, top-down)
- ✅ Linkage: **Single (MIN)**, **Complete (MAX)**, **Average (Group Avg)**, **Ward's**
- ✅ Single linkage → chaining effect, elongated clusters
- ✅ Complete linkage → compact, equal-diameter clusters
- ✅ $O(n^2 \log n)$ time complexity — not scalable to large data

### 🔵 DBSCAN (Density-Based)
- ✅ Parameters: **$\varepsilon$** (radius) and **MinPts** (threshold)
- ✅ **Core point**: $\geq$ MinPts within $\varepsilon$-neighborhood
- ✅ **Border point**: in core's neighborhood but not a core itself
- ✅ **Noise point**: neither core nor border (outlier!)
- ✅ Concepts: density-**reachable**, density-**connected**
- ✅ Can find **arbitrary-shaped** clusters + identifies outliers
- ✅ NOT suitable for varying-density clusters

### 📊 Cluster Validity
- ✅ **Silhouette Coefficient** ∈ [-1, 1]: higher is better
- ✅ **Davies-Bouldin Index**: lower is better
- ✅ **Dunn Index**: higher is better
- ✅ Internal measures (no ground truth) vs External measures (with labels)

### 🧠 Exam-Day Heuristics
- ✅ Always normalize data before K-Means
- ✅ Always state **$k$ value and initial centroids** in numericals
- ✅ Show **distance table** for each iteration
- ✅ Always **recompute centroids** after assignment
- ✅ Compute **SSE** at convergence as proof of optimization
- ✅ For hierarchical, **state the linkage method** clearly
- ✅ Draw dendrogram with **merge heights** on Y-axis
- ✅ Mention **complexity** and **limitations** of any algorithm asked

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 30-Second Mantra:** *Cluster = intra-similar + inter-dissimilar. K-Means = centroid (virtual) + Euclidean. K-Medoids = medoid (real) + any distance. Hierarchical = dendrogram + linkage. DBSCAN = density + core/border/noise. KTU loves numericals on K-Means and hierarchical — practice them with pen and paper.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Data in Cluster Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT604 - DATA MINING AND WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Data in Cluster Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Types of Data in Cluster Analysis — KTU Module 2

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Cluster Analysis** (a core unsupervised data mining technique), the **type of data** refers to the mathematical and statistical nature of the attributes (features) associated with each object (data point) being clustered. The choice of **proximity measure** (similarity or dissimilarity), the **clustering algorithm**, and the **interpretation of results** are all fundamentally governed by the data type.

According to standard KTU-referenced Data Mining literature, data in cluster analysis is broadly classified into the following categories:

1. **Interval-Scaled (Numeric / Continuous) Data**
2. **Binary (Dichotomous) Data** — Symmetric and Asymmetric
3. **Categorical (Nominal) Data**
4. **Ordinal Data**
5. **Ratio-Scaled Data**
6. **Mixed-Type Data** (combinations of the above)
7. **Higher-Dimensional / Complex Data** (Vector, Document, Transaction, Time-Series, Graph)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Understanding data types is a **prerequisite for Module 3 (Clustering Methods)**. Without correctly identifying data type, you cannot choose the correct distance metric — a guaranteed 3-mark question every semester.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (The "Library Analogy")

Imagine a massive **library** where you are asked to group books into shelves (clusters).

- **Numbers (Interval-Scaled)** → "Sort by number of pages." A book of 300 pages is closer to 310 than 800.
- **Yes/No answers (Binary)** → "Is the book hardcover? Is it fiction?" (asymmetric questions like "Is it a best-seller?" carry more weight than "Is it hardcover?").
- **Categories (Nominal)** → "Group by color of the cover."
- **Ordered Ranks (Ordinal)** → "Group by star rating: ★★★ < ★★★★ < ★★★★★."
- **Mixed Data** → A book has pages (numeric), genre (categorical), rating (ordinal), and availability (binary) — all at once.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** You cannot use a "ruler" to measure a "color." Similarly, you cannot use Euclidean distance on categorical data. The data type **dictates** the correct measurement tool.

---

## 1.3 Why Data Types Matter in Clustering

| Data Type | Appropriate Proximity Measure | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Interval-Scaled | Euclidean, Manhattan, Minkowski | Height, Weight, Temperature |
| Binary | Jaccard, Simple Matching Coefficient | Has-Disease (Yes/No) |
| Categorical | Matching Coefficient, One-Hot Encoding + Hamming | Blood Group, City Name |
| Ordinal | Spearman Footrule, Rank-based | Likert Scale (1–5) |
| Mixed-Type | Weighted Combined Distance | Customer Profile (Age, Gender, Income) |

---

## 1.4 Interval-Scaled Variables (The Most Common Type)

**Definition:** Variables that take continuous numeric values on a linear scale, where the difference between any two values is meaningful.

- Mean, standard deviation, and other statistical properties are defined.
- Examples: Height (cm), Weight (kg), Temperature (°C), Income (₹).

**Critical Issue: Standardization**
Because attributes may be measured in different units (e.g., kg vs. cm), raw values must be normalized before clustering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standardization Formula (z-score normalization):**
> $$z_{if} = \frac{x_{if} - \mu_f}{\sigma_f}$$
> where $\mu_f$ is the mean and $\sigma_f$ is the standard deviation of feature $f$.

---

## 1.5 Binary Variables

**Definition:** Variables that take only two values — typically 0 and 1.

**Two Sub-Types:**
- **Symmetric Binary:** Both outcomes carry equal weight.  
  Example: Gender = {Male, Female}
- **Asymmetric Binary:** One outcome (typically the rarer one) is more significant.  
  Example: Has-Disease = {Yes, No} — "Yes" is the critical state.

> [!NOTE]
> For **asymmetric** binary data, **Jaccard Coefficient** is preferred over Simple Matching Coefficient because mismatches on the rare "Yes" carry more information.

---

## 1.6 Categorical (Nominal) Variables

**Definition:** Variables that take values from a finite, unordered set of categories.

- Examples: Eye Color {Blue, Brown, Green}, City {Delhi, Mumbai, Kochi}.
- Cannot define arithmetic operations.
- One-Hot Encoding converts them into binary vectors, but this inflates dimensionality — a problem for distance-based clustering.

---

## 1.7 Ordinal Variables

**Definition:** Categorical variables with a meaningful **order/rank**, but the distance between ranks is unknown.

- Examples: {Low, Medium, High}, Likert Scale (1=Strongly Disagree, 5=Strongly Agree).
- Must be **mapped to ranks** $(1, 2, \dots, M)$ and then **normalized to $[0, 1]$** before applying numeric distance metrics.

---

## 1.8 Ratio-Scaled Variables

**Definition:** Numeric variables with a true, non-arbitrary zero point.

- Examples: Age, Length, Weight (in some contexts), Kelvin temperature.
- Logarithmic transformation is often applied: $y_{if} = \log(x_{if})$ to compress large ranges before applying Minkowski-based distances.

---

## 1.9 Mixed-Type Data

**Definition:** Real-world datasets where each object is described by a combination of numeric, binary, categorical, and ordinal attributes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Combined Proximity Formula (Hellerstein et al.):**
> $$d(i, j) = \frac{\sum_{f=1}^{p} \delta_{ij}^{(f)} d_{ij}^{(f)}}{\sum_{f=1}^{p} \delta_{ij}^{(f)}}$$
> where $\delta_{ij}^{(f)} = 0$ if $x_{if} = x_{jf}$ (matching, contribution zero), and $d_{ij}^{(f)}$ is the per-attribute distance. This is a **high-yield KTU formula**.

---

## 1.10 Complex / Higher-Dimensional Data Types

| Data Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Vector Data** | Each object is a fixed-length feature vector | Customer record with 10 attributes |
| **Document Data** | Each document is a sparse term-frequency vector | News articles, emails |
| **Transaction Data** | Each transaction is a set/basket of items | Market-basket: {Milk, Bread} |
| **Time-Series Data** | Ordered sequence of observations | Stock prices, ECG signals |
| **Graph / Network Data** | Objects are nodes, relationships are edges | Social networks, road maps |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2️⃣ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Interval-Scaled Variables — Detailed Treatment

### 2.1.1 Properties
- Arithmetic operations (+, −, ×, ÷) are meaningful.
- Distance between two points is well-defined.
- Sensitive to **scale** and **outliers** → requires standardization.

### 2.1.2 Dissimilarity (Distance) Measures

For two data objects $i$ and $j$ with $p$ features, let $x_{if}$ and $x_{jf}$ be their values on feature $f$.

**1. Euclidean Distance ($L_2$ norm)**
$$d(i, j) = \sqrt{\sum_{f=1}^{p} (x_{if} - x_{jf})^2}$$

**2. Manhattan / City-Block Distance ($L_1$ norm)**
$$d(i, j) = \sum_{f=1}^{p} \vert x_{if} - x_{jf} \vert$$

**3. Minkowski Distance ($L_p$ generalized)**
$$d(i, j) = \left( \sum_{f=1}^{p} \vert x_{if} - x_{jf} \vert^{\lambda} \right)^{1/\lambda}$$
where $\lambda \geq 1$. $\lambda = 1$ → Manhattan; $\lambda = 2$ → Euclidean; $\lambda \to \infty$ → Supremum (Chebyshev) distance.

**4. Chebyshev Distance ($L_\infty$)**
$$d(i, j) = \max_{f} \vert x_{if} - x_{jf} \vert$$

### 2.1.3 Standardization Steps
1. Compute mean: $\mu_f = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{if}$
2. Compute standard deviation: $\sigma_f = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_{if} - \mu_f)^2}$
3. Compute z-score: $z_{if} = (x_{if} - \mu_f) / \sigma_f$

---

## 2.2 Binary Variables — Detailed Treatment

### 2.2.1 Contingency Table

For two binary objects $i$ and $j$, build the $2 \times 2$ table:

| | $j = 1$ | $j = 0$ | Sum |
|---|---|---|---|
| **$i = 1$** | $a$ | $b$ | $a+b$ |
| **$i = 0$** | $c$ | $d$ | $c+d$ |
| **Sum** | $a+c$ | $b+d$ | $p$ |

where:
- $a$ = number of attributes where both are 1
- $d$ = number of attributes where both are 0
- $b, c$ = number of mismatches

### 2.2.2 Proximity Measures

**1. Simple Matching Coefficient (SMC) — Symmetric Binary**
$$SMC(i, j) = \frac{a + d}{a + b + c + d}$$
$$d(i, j) = 1 - SMC = \frac{b + c}{a + b + c + d}$$

**2. Jaccard Coefficient — Asymmetric Binary**
$$J(i, j) = \frac{a}{a + b + c}$$
$$d(i, j) = 1 - J = \frac{b + c}{a + b + c}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU-favorite trick question):**
> For **asymmetric** attributes, $d$ is ignored because matches on 0–0 are not informative (e.g., both not having a rare disease is uninteresting). Therefore **Jaccard is used**. For **symmetric** attributes, both 0–0 and 1–1 matter, so **SMC is used**.

---

## 2.3 Categorical (Nominal) Variables — Detailed Treatment

- Distance can be defined as **simple matching**:
$$d(i, j) = \frac{p - m}{p}$$
where $m$ = number of matching attributes, $p$ = total attributes.
- For more sophistication, **one-hot encoding** is used, and then any binary distance measure applies — but beware of the **curse of dimensionality** when categories are many.

---

## 2.4 Ordinal Variables — Detailed Treatment

**Step-by-step transformation** (this appears in many KTU problems):

1. Let attribute $f$ have $M_f$ ordered states, ranked $1, 2, \dots, M_f$.
2. Replace $x_{if}$ with its rank $r_{if} \in \{1, \dots, M_f\}$.
3. Map to $[0, 1]$ interval:
$$z_{if} = \frac{r_{if} - 1}{M_f - 1}$$
4. Apply Euclidean (or any numeric) distance on $z_{if}$.

---

## 2.5 Mixed-Type Data — Detailed Treatment

**Combined Dissimilarity Formula (weighted):**
$$d(i, j) = \frac{\sum_{f=1}^{p} w_f \cdot \delta_{ij}^{(f)} \cdot d_{ij}^{(f)}}{\sum_{f=1}^{p} w_f \cdot \delta_{ij}^{(f)}}$$

where:
- $w_f$ = weight of attribute $f$ (often 1)
- $\delta_{ij}^{(f)} = 0$ if $x_{if} = x_{jf}$ (no contribution to dissimilarity)
- $d_{ij}^{(f)}$ = per-attribute distance, computed as:
  - Numeric: $\vert x_{if} - x_{jf} \vert / \max_f - \min_f$ (normalized)
  - Binary: 0 or 1
  - Categorical: 0 or 1
  - Ordinal: normalized rank difference

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most exam-relevant formula** for this topic. Memorize it.

---

## 2.6 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Data Type | Distance / Similarity Formula | Key Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Interval (Euclidean) | $d(i,j) = \sqrt{\sum (x_{if} - x_{jf})^2}$ | Sensitive to scale; standardize first |
| 2 | Interval (Manhattan) | $d(i,j) = \sum \vert x_{if} - x_{jf} \vert$ | Robust to outliers |
| 3 | Interval (Minkowski) | $d(i,j) = (\sum \vert x_{if} - x_{jf} \vert^{\lambda})^{1/\lambda}$ | Generalization; $\lambda \geq 1$ |
| 4 | Binary (SMC) | $SMC = (a+d)/(a+b+c+d)$ | Symmetric binary |
| 5 | Binary (Jaccard) | $J = a/(a+b+c)$ | Asymmetric binary |
| 6 | Categorical | $d(i,j) = (p - m)/p$ | Simple matching |
| 7 | Ordinal | $z_{if} = (r_{if} - 1)/(M_f - 1)$ | Normalize rank to $[0,1]$ |
| 8 | Ratio-Scaled | Apply $\log$ then Euclidean | Compress range |
| 9 | Mixed-Type | $d = \sum w_f \delta_f d_f / \sum w_f \delta_f$ | ⭐ **KTU favorite** |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Customer Segmentation** (Marketing) → Mixed data (age, gender, income, purchase history).
- **Anomaly Detection in Networks** → Time-series + graph data.
- **Bioinformatics** → Gene expression (interval) + presence/absence of mutation (binary).
- **Image Clustering** → High-dimensional vector data.
- **Recommender Systems** → Transaction data.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3️⃣ Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Standardization of Interval-Scaled Data

**Given:** A dataset with feature $f$ having values $x_{1f}, x_{2f}, \dots, x_{nf}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the mean of feature $f$:**
$$\mu_f = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{if}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the variance of feature $f$:**
$$\sigma_f^2 = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_{if} - \mu_f)^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the standard deviation:**
$$\sigma_f = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_{if} - \mu_f)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the z-score for each object $i$:**
$$z_{if} = \frac{x_{if} - \mu_f}{\sigma_f}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Subtracting the mean centers the data at 0; dividing by standard deviation scales it to unit variance. After this, all features are comparable regardless of their original units.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Euclidean Distance from First Principles

**Given:** Two points $i = (x_{i1}, x_{i2}, \dots, x_{ip})$ and $j = (x_{j1}, x_{j2}, \dots, x_{jp})$ in $p$-dimensional space.

**Step 1 — Per-coordinate squared difference:**
$$\Delta_f^2 = (x_{if} - x_{jf})^2, \quad f = 1, 2, \dots, p$$

**Step 2 — Sum all squared differences:**
$$S = \sum_{f=1}^{p} (x_{if} - x_{jf})^2$$

**Step 3 — Take the square root (to reverse the squaring):**
$$d(i, j) = \sqrt{\sum_{f=1}^{p} (x_{if} - x_{jf})^2}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Euclidean distance is the geometric straight-line distance, derived from the Pythagorean theorem generalized to $p$ dimensions. The square root undoes the squaring so that $d$ is in the same unit as the original features.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Jaccard Coefficient from a Contingency Table

**Given:** Two binary objects $i$ and $j$ with contingency table counts $a, b, c$ (assume asymmetric, so $d$ is excluded).

**Step 1 — Define "positive matches" (both 1):**
$$a = \sum_{f=1}^{p} \mathbb{1}(x_{if} = 1 \text{ and } x_{jf} = 1)$$

**Step 2 — Define "negative mismatches" (asymmetric — both 0 ignored):**
$$b = \sum_{f=1}^{p} \mathbb{1}(x_{if} = 1 \text{ and } x_{jf} = 0)$$
$$c = \sum_{f=1}^{p} \mathbb{1}(x_{if} = 0 \text{ and } x_{jf} = 1)$$

**Step 3 — Form the Jaccard ratio (matches / (matches + mismatches)):**
$$J(i, j) = \frac{a}{a + b + c}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to a dissimilarity:**
$$d(i, j) = 1 - J(i, j) = \frac{b + c}{a + b + c}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** $a + b + c$ counts every instance where at least one object has the rare "1." Dividing mismatches $b + c$ by this total gives the proportion of "interesting" features that disagree. This is why $d$ (both 0) is excluded — in asymmetric cases, both being 0 is the **uninteresting default**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Ordinal Attribute Normalization

**Given:** An ordinal attribute with $M_f$ ordered categories. Object $i$ has value $x_{if}$.

**Step 1 — Assign ranks:** $r_{if} \in \{1, 2, \dots, M_f\}$ where $r = 1$ is the lowest and $r = M_f$ is the highest.

**Step 2 — Apply min-max normalization to $[0, 1]$:**
$$z_{if} = \frac{r_{if} - 1}{M_f - 1}$$

**Step 3 — Apply standard Euclidean (or Manhattan) distance using $z_{if}$.**

**Worked Numerical Example:**
Suppose an attribute has $M_f = 5$ categories (1=Very Low → 5=Very High). Object $i$ is ranked 3, Object $j$ is ranked 5.

$$z_{if} = \frac{3 - 1}{5 - 1} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5$$

$$z_{jf} = \frac{5 - 1}{5 - 1} = \frac{4}{4} = 1.0$$

$$d(i, j) = \vert 0.5 - 1.0 \vert = 0.5$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Ranks are inherently non-uniform (the "gap" between rank 1 and 2 may differ from gap between 4 and 5). Mapping to $[0,1]$ produces a uniform numeric scale that distance metrics can handle.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Mixed-Type Combined Dissimilarity

**Given:** Two objects $i$ and $j$ with $p$ mixed-type attributes, with a weight $w_f$ per attribute.

**Step 1 — Define the indicator $\delta_{ij}^{(f)}$:**
$$\delta_{ij}^{(f)} = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } x_{if} = x_{jf} \text{ or } x_{if} \text{ or } x_{jf} \text{ is missing} \\ 1 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Define per-attribute distance $d_{ij}^{(f)}$:**

For **numeric** attributes (normalized to $[0,1]$):
$$d_{ij}^{(f)} = \frac{\vert x_{if} - x_{jf} \vert}{\max_h x_{hf} - \min_h x_{hf}}$$

For **binary or categorical** attributes:
$$d_{ij}^{(f)} = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } x_{if} = x_{jf} \\ 1 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Aggregate via weighted average:**
$$d(i, j) = \frac{\sum_{f=1}^{p} w_f \cdot \delta_{ij}^{(f)} \cdot d_{ij}^{(f)}}{\sum_{f=1}^{p} w_f \cdot \delta_{ij}^{(f)}}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The denominator $\sum w_f \delta_{ij}^{(f)}$ normalizes by the number of attributes that actually contribute (matching and missing attributes are excluded, since they add no discriminative information). The numerator sums the weighted, per-attribute dissimilarities.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Distance

| Data Type | Recommended Distance | Why? | Pitfall to Avoid |
|---|---|---|---|
| Interval (same scale) | Euclidean | Geometrically intuitive | Not using it on different scales |
| Interval (different scales) | Standardized Euclidean | Removes unit bias | Forgetting to standardize |
| Binary Symmetric | SMC | Both 0-0 and 1-1 matter | Using Jaccard unnecessarily |
| Binary Asymmetric | Jaccard | 0-0 is uninformative | Using SMC and inflating similarity |
| Categorical (unordered) | Simple Matching / One-hot + Hamming | No inherent order | Assigning arbitrary numeric codes |
| Ordinal | Normalized rank + Euclidean | Respects order | Skipping rank normalization |
| Ratio-Scaled (wide range) | log-transformed Euclidean | Compresses range | Direct Euclidean on raw data |
| Mixed-Type | Weighted combined formula | Unifies all types | Forgetting $\delta$ indicator |
| Document (text) | Cosine similarity | Ignores magnitude, focuses on direction | Using Euclidean on sparse vectors |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5️⃣ KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What are the major types of data that occur in cluster analysis? Briefly explain any two.*

**Model Answer:**

In cluster analysis, the major types of data are:

1. **Interval-Scaled Variables:** Continuous numeric attributes where the difference between values is meaningful (e.g., height, weight, temperature). Require standardization before applying distance-based clustering.

2. **Binary Variables:** Attributes with only two values (0 or 1). Divided into **symmetric** (both outcomes equally important, e.g., gender) and **asymmetric** (one outcome is rarer and more meaningful, e.g., presence of disease).

3. **Categorical (Nominal) Variables:** Unordered categories (e.g., city, eye color). Distance is computed via simple matching.

4. **Ordinal Variables:** Categorical with intrinsic order (e.g., {Low, Medium, High}). Mapped to ranks and normalized.

5. **Ratio-Scaled Variables:** Numeric with true zero; often log-transformed.

6. **Mixed-Type Data:** Combination of multiple types — handled by weighted combined dissimilarity.

**Any two explained with examples (≈ 1.5 marks each).**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between symmetric and asymmetric binary attributes with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Symmetric Binary | Asymmetric Binary |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Both states (0 and 1) are equally important | One state (usually 1) is more important |
| **Example** | Gender (Male/Female) | Medical test result (Positive/Negative) |
| **Distance Measure** | Simple Matching Coefficient (SMC) | Jaccard Coefficient |
| **Why?** | Both 0-0 and 1-1 matches are informative | 0-0 matches are common and uninformative |

**Example:** In a customer survey, "Is the customer male?" is symmetric. But "Does the customer have a rare disease?" is asymmetric, because the rare "Yes" is critical, and two customers both being disease-free is the **default** and carries no similarity information.

---

## 📝 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the different types of data encountered in cluster analysis. Discuss the importance of standardization for interval-scaled data. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** For two binary objects $A$ and $B$, the contingency table is given below. Compute the Simple Matching Coefficient and the Jaccard Coefficient. Comment on which is more appropriate if the attributes are **asymmetric**. **[7 marks]**

|  | B = 1 | B = 0 | Sum |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A = 1** | 3 | 2 | 5 |
| **A = 0** | 1 | 4 | 5 |
| **Sum** | 4 | 6 | 10 |

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Different types of data in cluster analysis:**

1. **Interval-Scaled Variables** — Numeric, continuous; meaningful differences.
2. **Binary Variables** — Two values 0/1; symmetric or asymmetric.
3. **Categorical (Nominal) Variables** — Unordered categories.
4. **Ordinal Variables** — Ordered categories.
5. **Ratio-Scaled Variables** — Numeric with true zero.
6. **Mixed-Type Variables** — Combinations of the above.

**Standardization (z-score):**
$$z_{if} = \frac{x_{if} - \mu_f}{\sigma_f}$$

**Importance:** When features have different units (e.g., age in years vs. income in ₹), the larger-scale feature will dominate the distance computation. Standardization rescales all features to zero mean and unit variance, ensuring each contributes equally.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

Given: $a = 3, b = 2, c = 1, d = 4, p = 10$.

**Step 1 — Simple Matching Coefficient (SMC):**
$$SMC = \frac{a + d}{a + b + c + d} = \frac{3 + 4}{3 + 2 + 1 + 4} = \frac{7}{10} = 0.7$$

$$d_{SMC} = 1 - SMC = 0.3$$

**Step 2 — Jaccard Coefficient:**
$$J = \frac{a}{a + b + c} = \frac{3}{3 + 2 + 1} = \frac{3}{6} = 0.5$$

$$d_{J} = 1 - J = 0.5$$

**Step 3 — Comment on asymmetric attributes:**

For **asymmetric** attributes, the $d = 4$ matches (both 0) are **uninformative** and should be excluded. Jaccard ignores $d$ and yields a similarity of $0.5$ (dissimilarity $0.5$). SMC inflates similarity to $0.7$ by counting the uninformative 0-0 matches. **Therefore, Jaccard is more appropriate for asymmetric binary data.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain interval-scaled, binary, categorical, ordinal, and ratio-scaled variables in detail. Provide a real-world example for each. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Three customers have the following mixed-type profiles. Compute the combined dissimilarity between Customer 1 and Customer 2 using the weighted mixed-type formula. Assume equal weights. **[7 marks]**

| Attribute | Type | Customer 1 | Customer 2 | Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Age | Numeric | 30 | 45 | 20–60 |
| Gender | Binary (1=M) | 1 | 0 | — |
| Income (₹L) | Numeric | 5 | 7 | 1–10 |
| Rating | Ordinal (1–5) | 4 | 2 | — |

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Interval-Scaled:** Continuous numeric, meaningful differences. *Example:* Height of students in cm.
2. **Binary:** Two values. *Example:* Pass/Fail in an exam.
3. **Categorical:** Unordered categories. *Example:* Blood group A/B/AB/O.
4. **Ordinal:** Ordered categories. *Example:* Hotel star rating (1★ to 5★).
5. **Ratio-Scaled:** Numeric with true zero. *Example:* Age in years, weight in kg.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Normalize numeric distances:**

- **Age:** range = 60 − 20 = 40  
  $d_{12}^{(age)} = \vert 30 - 45 \vert / 40 = 15/40 = 0.375$

- **Income:** range = 10 − 1 = 9  
  $d_{12}^{(income)} = \vert 5 - 7 \vert / 9 = 2/9 \approx 0.222$

**Step 2 — Binary distance (Gender):**
$$d_{12}^{(gender)} = \vert 1 - 0 \vert = 1$$

**Step 3 — Ordinal distance (Rating):**
- Rank range: $M_f = 5$, so divisor = $5 - 1 = 4$
$$d_{12}^{(rating)} = \frac{\vert 4 - 2 \vert}{4} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5$$

**Step 4 — All $\delta$ indicators = 1 (no matches, no missing):**

Sum of $\delta \cdot d$:
$$= 0.375 + 0.222 + 1 + 0.5 = 2.097$$

Sum of $\delta$:
$$= 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 4$$

**Step 5 — Combined dissimilarity:**
$$d(1, 2) = \frac{2.097}{4} \approx 0.524$$

**Interpretation:** Customers 1 and 2 are about 52% dissimilar overall, driven mainly by the gender mismatch (contributing the maximum individual weight of 1.0).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to standardize interval-scaled data** when features are in different units → full 7-mark answer marked down 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing SMC with Jaccard** in asymmetric binary problems. Read the question carefully — "asymmetric" is the trigger for Jaccard.
> 3. **In ordinal problems, students forget to normalize ranks to $[0,1]$** before applying distance. This is a 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **In mixed-type problems, students forget the $\delta$ indicator** — they sum over ALL attributes even when values match, producing incorrect denominators.
> 5. **Using Euclidean distance on categorical data** is a conceptual error worth 3 marks.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **📌 Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ Cluster analysis data types: **Interval, Binary, Categorical, Ordinal, Ratio, Mixed, Complex (Vector/Document/Transaction/Time-Series/Graph)**.
- ✅ Always **standardize** (z-score) interval-scaled data when units/scales differ.
- ✅ **Euclidean**, **Manhattan**, **Minkowski** are the three core interval-data distance measures.
- ✅ **Symmetric binary → SMC** (use $a, b, c, d$). **Asymmetric binary → Jaccard** (use $a, b, c$ only).
- ✅ **Categorical** → simple matching or one-hot + Hamming.
- ✅ **Ordinal** → rank first, then normalize to $[0,1]$, then apply Euclidean.
- ✅ **Ratio-Scaled** → log-transform first if range is wide.
- ✅ **Mixed-type** → weighted combined formula with $\delta$ indicator (the KTU favorite).
- ✅ **Document data** → Cosine similarity, not Euclidean.
- ✅ **Transaction data** → Jaccard on item sets.
- ✅ **Time-series** → DTW or correlation-based.
- ✅ **Graph data** → Graph edit distance or spectral methods.
- ✅ The $\delta$ indicator in the mixed-type formula = 0 when values match OR are missing.
- ✅ **Jaccard is preferred over SMC** whenever 0-0 matches are "default" and uninformative.
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark questions, always **show the formula, substitute values, and interpret the result**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: A Categorization of Major Clustering Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT604 - DATA MINING AND WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** A Categorization of Major Clustering Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of unsupervised learning within Data Mining, **Clustering** is the process of partitioning a set of data objects into multiple groups (clusters) such that objects within the same group are highly similar (high intra-cluster similarity) while objects in different groups are highly dissimilar (low inter-cluster similarity). According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PBADT604, "A Categorization of Major Clustering Methods" refers to the systematic taxonomy that organizes clustering algorithms based on their underlying mathematical principles, computational strategies, and assumptions about data structure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Clustering methods are broadly classified into **5 major categories**:
> 1. **Partitioning Methods** (e.g., K-Means, K-Medoids)
> 2. **Hierarchical Methods** (e.g., AGNES, DIANA)
> 3. **Density-Based Methods** (e.g., DBSCAN, OPTICS)
> 4. **Grid-Based Methods** (e.g., STING, CLIQUE)
> 5. **Model-Based Methods** (e.g., EM, COBWEB, Self-Organizing Maps)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **librarian** with a massive, unsorted pile of 10,000 returned books. You must arrange them back onto shelves so similar books are together. How would you approach this?

- **Partitioning (K-Means):** You decide in advance to create exactly **K = 10 shelves**. You randomly place 10 "seed" books (centroids), and each book goes to the nearest seed. Then you move the seeds to the average position of their assigned books and repeat. (Top-down, iterative)
- **Hierarchical (AGNES):** You start with **every book as its own shelf**, then repeatedly merge the two closest shelves until you are left with a manageable number (or a tree-shaped shelf hierarchy). (Bottom-up, agglomerative)
- **Density-Based (DBSCAN):** You look for **dense neighborhoods**. A shelf exists only if there are at least 5 books within a radius of 1 meter. Books in sparse, isolated regions are marked as "outliers" and not assigned. (Shape-flexible, noise-aware)
- **Grid-Based (STING):** You first **divide the library floor into a grid of cells** (e.g., 2m × 2m squares), count books per cell, and cluster based on grid density. (Fast, summarization-first)
- **Model-Based (EM):** You assume the books were generated from a **mixture of Gaussian distributions** (some genres are more common) and statistically estimate which "latent genre" each book belongs to. (Probabilistic, generative)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The choice of method depends on the **data type**, **cluster shape assumption**, **scalability requirement**, and **presence of noise/outliers**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Clustering

- **Intra-cluster similarity:** Measured by **cohesion** (e.g., Sum of Squared Errors, SSE).
- **Inter-cluster similarity:** Measured by **separation** (e.g., distance between centroids).
- **Cluster validity indices:** **Silhouette Coefficient**, **Davies-Bouldin Index (DBI)**, **Dunn Index**.
- The fundamental objective function: **minimize SSE** while **maximizing inter-cluster distance**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Major Categories — Detailed Breakdown

#### A. Partitioning Methods

- Construct **k** partitions of the data, where each partition represents one cluster.
- Each cluster must contain **at least one object**, and each object belongs to **exactly one cluster** (hard partitioning).
- Use an **iterative relocation heuristic** that tries to improve the partitioning by moving objects between clusters.
- **Representative Algorithms:** K-Means (uses mean/centroid), K-Medoids (uses medoid/most central actual point).
- **Strengths:** Relatively scalable, O(nkt) time complexity, efficient for spherical clusters.
- **Weaknesses:** Sensitive to initial seeds, requires pre-specification of k, poor for non-convex shapes, sensitive to outliers.

#### B. Hierarchical Methods

- Create a **hierarchical decomposition (dendrogram)** of the dataset.
- Two approaches:
  * **Agglomerative (Bottom-Up):** Start with each object as its own cluster; successively merge the closest pair until one cluster remains.
  * **Divisive (Top-Down):** Start with all objects in one cluster; recursively split until each is its own cluster.
- Linkage criteria determine how inter-cluster distance is computed:
  * **Single Linkage:** $\min \{ d(x,y) \mid x \in C_i, y \in C_j \}$
  * **Complete Linkage:** $\max \{ d(x,y) \mid x \in C_i, y \in C_j \}$
  * **Average Linkage:** $\frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert \cdot \vert C_j \vert} \sum_{x \in C_i} \sum_{y \in C_j} d(x,y)$
  * **Ward's Linkage:** Minimizes the increase in SSE when merging.

#### C. Density-Based Methods

- Clusters are defined as **dense regions** separated by low-density regions.
- Can discover clusters of **arbitrary shape** (unlike K-Means which assumes spherical).
- Two key parameters: **Eps (neighborhood radius)** and **MinPts (minimum points to form a dense region)**.
- **Core Point:** A point with at least MinPts neighbors within Eps radius.
- **Border Point:** A point within Eps of a core point but not itself a core point.
- **Noise Point:** Neither a core nor a border point.
- **Representative Algorithms:** DBSCAN, OPTICS, DENCLUE.

#### D. Grid-Based Methods

- Quantize the object space into a **finite number of cells** (grid structure).
- Clustering is performed on the **grid cells**, not the original data points.
- Primary advantage: **Fast processing time** (typically independent of data size, depends only on grid resolution).
- **Representative Algorithms:** STING (Statistical Information Grid), CLIQUE (combines grid + density + subspace clustering), WaveCluster (uses wavelet transform).

#### E. Model-Based Methods

- Hypothesize a **mathematical model** for each cluster and try to fit the data to that model.
- Most commonly use **statistical approaches** (mixture of Gaussians with Expectation-Maximization).
- **Conceptual clustering** methods (e.g., COBWEB) use a hierarchical probabilistic model.
- **Neural network approaches** (e.g., Self-Organizing Maps) learn a low-dimensional representation.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Definition** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|
| Euclidean Distance (L2) | $d(x,y) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - y_i)^2}$ | Most common for K-Means |
| Manhattan Distance (L1) | $d(x,y) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - y_i \vert$ | Robust to outliers, grid-based |
| Minkowski Distance (Lp) | $d(x,y) = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - y_i \vert^p \right)^{1/p}$ | Generalization of L1, L2, L∞ |
| Cosine Similarity | $\cos(\theta) = \frac{x \cdot y}{\Vert x \Vert \cdot \Vert y \Vert}$ | Document/text clustering |
| K-Means Objective (SSE) | $SSE = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^2$ | Minimization target |
| Centroid Update | $\mu_i = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert} \sum_{x \in C_i} x$ | Recomputation in K-Means |
| DBSCAN Eps-Neighborhood | $N_{Eps}(p) = \{ q \in D \mid dist(p,q) \le Eps \}$ | Density reachability base |
| Single Linkage | $d(C_i, C_j) = \min_{x \in C_i, y \in C_j} d(x,y)$ | Sensitive to noise/chaining |
| Complete Linkage | $d(C_i, C_j) = \max_{x \in C_i, y \in C_j} d(x,y)$ | Tends to find compact clusters |
| Silhouette Coefficient | $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$ | Cluster validity, range $[-1, +1]$ |
| Davies-Bouldin Index | $DBI = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \max_{j \neq i} \left( \frac{S_i + S_j}{M_{ij}} \right)$ | Lower is better |
| Jaccard Coefficient | $J(C, C') = \frac{TP}{TP + FP + FN}$ | External cluster validation |
| EM Mixture Model | $P(x) = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \pi_j \cdot \mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu_j, \Sigma_j)$ | Model-based clustering |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder for Tables:** Notice how the absolute value and norm symbols are written using `\vert` and `\Vert` (LaTeX commands) — **never** use the raw pipe character `|`, which would break the markdown table parser.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Partitioning:** Customer segmentation in retail, image compression (vector quantization).
- **Hierarchical:** Phylogenetic tree construction in bioinformatics, taxonomy organization.
- **Density-Based:** Anomaly detection in network traffic, earthquake epicenter identification.
- **Grid-Based:** Spatial data mining in GIS systems, real-time sensor data analysis.
- **Model-Based:** Gene expression analysis, recommender systems, image segmentation with uncertainty.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: K-Means Objective Function (SSE) from First Principles

The K-Means algorithm seeks to partition *n* data points into **k** clusters by minimizing the within-cluster sum of squared errors. Below is the rigorous derivation.

**Step 1 — Define the assignment indicator.**

Let $r_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}$ be a binary indicator variable such that:

$$r_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if point } x_i \text{ is assigned to cluster } j \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Define the centroid of cluster j.**

The centroid $\mu_j$ is the mean of all points assigned to cluster $j$:

$$\mu_j = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} r_{ij} \, x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} r_{ij}}$$

**Step 3 — Define the objective function (distortion / SSE).**

The total sum of squared distances from each point to its assigned centroid is:

$$J = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{k} r_{ij} \cdot \Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^2$$

where $\Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^2$ expands to:

$$\Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^2 = (x_i - \mu_j)^\top (x_i - \mu_j) = \sum_{d=1}^{D} (x_{id} - \mu_{jd})^2$$

**Step 4 — Minimize J with respect to $r_{ij}$ (Expectation-like step).**

To minimize $J$ over $r_{ij}$ while keeping $\mu_j$ fixed, we set $r_{ij} = 1$ for the cluster $j$ nearest to $x_i$:

$$r_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } j = \arg\min_{j'} \Vert x_i - \mu_{j'} \Vert^2 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 5 — Minimize J with respect to $\mu_j$ (Maximization-like step).**

Setting $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mu_j} = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \mu_j} \sum_{i=1}^{n} r_{ij} (x_i - \mu_j)^\top (x_i - \mu_j) = 0$$

$$-2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} r_{ij} (x_i - \mu_j) = 0$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} r_{ij} x_i = \mu_j \sum_{i=1}^{n} r_{ij}$$

$$\mu_j = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} r_{ij} x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} r_{ij}}$$

This is exactly the centroid definition in Step 2. Thus, the two-step EM-like iteration of K-Means is mathematically justified as coordinate descent on $J$.

**Step 6 — Convergence guarantee.**

Each step of K-Means **monotonically decreases** $J$ (or leaves it unchanged). Since $J \ge 0$ and bounded below, the algorithm **converges in finite steps** to a local minimum (not necessarily global).

### 3.2 Derivation: Silhouette Coefficient

For a data point $i$ in cluster $C_I$:

**Step 1 — Compute $a(i)$, the average distance to other points in the same cluster:**

$$a(i) = \frac{1}{\vert C_I \vert - 1} \sum_{j \in C_I, j \neq i} d(i, j)$$

**Step 2 — Compute $b(i)$, the minimum average distance to points in any other cluster:**

$$b(i) = \min_{J \neq I} \frac{1}{\vert C_J \vert} \sum_{j \in C_J} d(i, j)$$

**Step 3 — Combine to form the Silhouette Coefficient:**

$$s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{ a(i), b(i) \}}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation of the range:**
- $s(i) \approx +1$: Point is well-clustered.
- $s(i) \approx 0$: Point lies on the boundary between two clusters.
- $s(i) \approx -1$: Point may have been assigned to the wrong cluster.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Clustering Method

| **Method Category** | **Best For** | **Cluster Shape** | **Handles Noise?** | **Scalability (n)** | **Parameter Sensitivity** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Partitioning (K-Means) | Large, numeric, spherical data | Spherical/globular | No | High (O(nkt)) | High (k, initial seeds) |
| Partitioning (K-Medoids) | Small datasets, robustness to outliers | Spherical | Yes (slightly) | Low (O(k(n-k)^2)) | High (k) |
| Hierarchical (Agglomerative) | Taxonomy creation, small data | Any (single link) | No | Low (O(n^2 log n)) | Low (linkage type) |
| Density (DBSCAN) | Arbitrary shapes, spatial data | Arbitrary | **Yes (excellent)** | Moderate (O(n log n)) | High (Eps, MinPts) |
| Grid (STING) | Spatial data, fast queries | Rectangular/grid | Limited | Very High (O(G) where G = #cells) | Medium (grid resolution) |
| Model-Based (EM) | Data with known distributional form | Ellipsoidal (Gaussian) | No | Moderate | High (# components) |
| Constraint-Based | Domain knowledge integration | Depends on base | Depends | Depends | Depends |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked for comparison questions, structure the answer using the analytical matrix above. Examiners reward **tabular comparison** with explicit metrics rather than vague prose.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
