\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Apply the Core / Border / Noise Decision Rule

| Point | $\vert N_\varepsilon \vert$ | Test vs. MinPts = 3 | Classification |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $p_1$ | 4 | $4 \ge 3$ ✔ | **Core** |
| $p_2$ | 4 | $4 \ge 3$ ✔ | **Core** |
| $p_3$ | 4 | $4 \ge 3$ ✔ | **Core** |
| $p_4$ | 4 | $4 \ge 3$ ✔ | **Core** |
| $p_5$ | 1 | $1 < 3$ ✘ | **Noise** (not within any core's $\varepsilon$) |
| $p_6$ | 1 | $1 < 3$ ✘ | **Noise** |

#### Step 4 — Cluster Formation

* $p_1, p_2, p_3, p_4$ are all core points and are mutually density-reachable (and density-connected), so they form **Cluster C1**.
* $p_5$ and $p_6$ are isolated noise points and remain **unclustered**.

#### Step 5 — Final Result

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cluster}_1 &= \{\, p_1, p_2, p_3, p_4 \,\} \\
\text{Noise Set} &= \{\, p_5, p_6 \,\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Hack:** Always draw a quick scatter plot of the points first. The dense cluster jumps out visually, and the lonely outlier ($p_6$ at $(25, 25)$) is obvious. The examiner awards the 7 marks per sub-question only if the *neighbourhood sets* are explicitly written, *and* the cardinality check is shown step-by-step.

### 3.2 OPTICS — An Extension Worth 3 Extra Marks

> [!NOTE]
> **OPTICS (Ordering Points To Identify the Clustering Structure)** is an extension of DBSCAN that addresses the sensitivity to $\varepsilon$. It produces a *reachability plot* that encodes the density-based clustering structure across multiple $\varepsilon$ values simultaneously. The two new metrics are:
>
> **Core-Distance of $p$:** The smallest $\varepsilon$ such that $N_\varepsilon(p)$ contains at least **MinPts** points.
>
> **Reachability-Distance from $o$ to $p$:** $\text{max}(\text{core-distance}(o), \text{dist}(o, p))$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 DBSCAN Algorithm Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start DBSCAN: Input Dataset D, Epsilon eps, MinPts m"] --> B["Mark all points as UNVISITED"]
    B --> C{"Any unvisited point p remaining?"}
    C -- "No" --> Z["End: All points classified"]
    C -- "Yes" --> D["Mark p as VISITED"]
    D --> E["Compute N_eps(p)"]
    E --> F{"N_eps(p) size is greater than or equal to MinPts?"}
    F -- "No" --> G["Temporarily mark p as NOISE"]
    G --> C
    F -- "Yes" --> H["Create new Cluster C; Add N_eps(p) to Seed Queue Q"]
    H --> I{"Seed Queue Q empty?"}
    I -- "Yes" --> J["Output Cluster C"]
    I -- "No" --> K["Pop point q from Q"]
    K --> L{"Is q UNVISITED?"}
    L -- "Yes" --> M["Mark q as VISITED; Compute N_eps(q)"]
    M --> N{"N_eps(q) size is greater than or equal to MinPts?"}
    N -- "Yes" --> O["Add N_eps(q) points to Q"]
    N -- "No" --> P["Skip expansion"]
    O --> Q["Assign q to Cluster C"]
    P --> Q
    Q --> I
    L -- "No" --> Q
    J --> C
```

### 4.2 Point Classification Schematic

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph INPUT ["Input Data Point p"]
        direction LR
        P0["Data point p with Eps-Nbrs N_eps(p)"]
    end

    INPUT --> T1{"Is N_eps(p) size greater than or equal to MinPts?"}
    T1 -- "YES" --> CORE["CORE POINT — Cluster Center"]
    T1 -- "NO" --> T2{"Is p inside N_eps of any Core Point?"}
    T2 -- "YES" --> BORDER["BORDER POINT — Cluster Edge"]
    T2 -- "NO" --> NOISE["NOISE POINT — Outlier"]
```

### 4.3 Reachability Concept Visual

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O["Point o (Core)"] -- "directly density-reachable" --> P1["Point p1 (Core)"]
    P1 -- "directly density-reachable" --> P2["Point p2 (Core)"]
    P2 -- "directly density-reachable" --> Q["Point q (Border or Core)"]
    O -. "density-reachable chain" .-> Q
    R["Point r"] -. "density-connected via o" .-> Q
    O -. "shared density connection" .-> R
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. Define the following with respect to DBSCAN: (i) Core Point, (ii) Border Point, (iii) Noise Point.** *(3 Marks)* `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **(i) Core Point:** A point $p$ in dataset $D$ is called a *core point* if its $\varepsilon$-neighbourhood contains at least **MinPts** points, i.e., $\vert N_\varepsilon(p) \vert \ge \textbf{MinPts}$. These are the dense interior points of a cluster.
>
> **(ii) Border Point:** A point $p$ is a *border point* if it has fewer than **MinPts** points in its $\varepsilon$-neighbourhood (i.e., $\vert N_\varepsilon(p) \vert < \textbf{MinPts}$), but it lies within the $\varepsilon$-neighbourhood of at least one core point.
>
> **(iii) Noise Point:** A point $p$ is a *noise point* (or outlier) if it is neither a core point nor a border point. Such points do not belong to any cluster.

---

#### **Q2. What are the two key input parameters of the DBSCAN algorithm? Briefly explain their role.** *(3 Marks)* `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> The two key input parameters of DBSCAN are:
>
> **(1) Epsilon ($\varepsilon$):** Defines the radius of the neighbourhood around each data point. A larger $\varepsilon$ tends to merge nearby clusters, while a smaller one may split a single cluster or label many points as noise.
>
> **(2) MinPts:** Defines the minimum number of data points (including the point itself) that must lie within the $\varepsilon$-neighbourhood for a region to be classified as *dense*. A larger **MinPts** value produces more conservative clustering and identifies only the most robust clusters.

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern, the candidate must answer **one full 14-mark question** out of the module-internal choice. Both alternatives are presented below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Full Numerical DBSCAN Classification

#### **Part (a)** — $(7 \text{ Marks})$

**Q.** Given the following 2-D dataset with $\varepsilon = 1.5$ and **MinPts = 3** (including the point itself), identify the **core points**, **border points**, and **noise points** using the DBSCAN algorithm:

| Point | $X$ | $Y$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $A$ | 1 | 1 |
| $B$ | 1 | 2 |
| $C$ | 2 | 1 |
| $D$ | 2 | 2 |
| $E$ | 8 | 8 |
| $F$ | 9 | 8 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Pairwise Euclidean Distances.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{dist}(A, B) &= \sqrt{0^2 + 1^2} = 1.00 \\
\text{dist}(A, C) &= \sqrt{1^2 + 0^2} = 1.00 \\
\text{dist}(A, D) &= \sqrt{1^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41 \\
\text{dist}(B, C) &= \sqrt{1^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41 \\
\text{dist}(B, D) &= \sqrt{1^2 + 0^2} = 1.00 \\
\text{dist}(C, D) &= \sqrt{0^2 + 1^2} = 1.00 \\
\text{dist}(A, E) &= \sqrt{7^2 + 7^2} = \sqrt{98} \approx 9.90 \\
\text{dist}(E, F) &= \sqrt{1^2 + 0^2} = 1.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — $\varepsilon$-Neighbourhoods (threshold = 1.5).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{1.5}(A) &= \{\, A, B, C, D \,\}, \quad \vert N_{1.5}(A) \vert = 4 \\
N_{1.5}(B) &= \{\, A, B, C, D \,\}, \quad \vert N_{1.5}(B) \vert = 4 \\
N_{1.5}(C) &= \{\, A, B, C, D \,\}, \quad \vert N_{1.5}(C) \vert = 4 \\
N_{1.5}(D) &= \{\, A, B, C, D \,\}, \quad \vert N_{1.5}(D) \vert = 4 \\
N_{1.5}(E) &= \{\, E, F \,\}, \quad \vert N_{1.5}(E) \vert = 2 \\
N_{1.5}(F) &= \{\, E, F \,\}, \quad \vert N_{1.5}(F) \vert = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Classification.**

| Point | $\vert N_{1.5} \vert$ | $\ge 3$? | Result |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $A$ | 4 | ✔ | **Core** |
| $B$ | 4 | ✔ | **Core** |
| $C$ | 4 | ✔ | **Core** |
| $D$ | 4 | ✔ | **Core** |
| $E$ | 2 | ✘ | Within core's $N_{1.5}$? $E$ is at $\sim 9.9$ from $A$, hence **Noise** |
| $F$ | 2 | ✘ | **Noise** |

**Final Classification:** $A, B, C, D$ are **Core Points**; $E, F$ are **Noise Points**. *(No border points in this dataset.)*

---

#### **Part (b)** — $(7 \text{ Marks})$

**Q.** State the formal definitions of **direct density-reachability**, **density-reachability**, and **density-connectivity** as used in DBSCAN. How do these three concepts together define a cluster?

**Model Solution:**

> **(1) Direct Density-Reachability:** A point $q$ is *directly density-reachable* from a point $p$ if:
> * $q \in N_\varepsilon(p)$ (i.e., $\text{dist}(p, q) \le \varepsilon$), **and**
> * $p$ is a **core point** (i.e., $\vert N_\varepsilon(p) \vert \ge \textbf{MinPts}$).
> Note: This relation is **not symmetric** in general.
>
> **(2) Density-Reachability:** A point $q$ is *density-reachable* from $p$ if there exists a finite chain of points $p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n$ such that $p_1 = p$, $p_n = q$, and each $p_{i+1}$ is *directly density-reachable* from $p_i$. This is the **transitive closure** of direct density-reachability.
>
> **(3) Density-Connectivity:** Two points $p$ and $q$ are *density-connected* if there exists a point $o$ such that both $p$ and $q$ are **density-reachable** from $o$. This is a **symmetric** relation.
>
> **Cluster Definition (KTU Board Definition):** A **cluster** $C$ with respect to $\varepsilon$ and **MinPts** is a non-empty subset of $D$ satisfying:
> 1. **Maximality:** $\forall\, p, q \in D$, if $p \in C$ and $q$ is density-reachable from $p$, then $q \in C$.
> 2. **Connectivity:** $\forall\, p, q \in C$, $p$ and $q$ are density-connected.
> In short: A cluster is a **maximal set of density-connected points**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — OPTICS & Comparative Analysis *(Module Internal Choice)*

#### **Part (a)** — $(7 \text{ Marks})$

**Q.** Explain the **OPTICS** algorithm. Define **core-distance** and **reachability-distance**. How does OPTICS overcome the parameter sensitivity of DBSCAN? `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

> **OPTICS (Ordering Points To Identify the Clustering Structure)** is an extension of DBSCAN proposed by Ankerst, Breunig, Kriegel, and Sander (1999). It does not produce a single explicit clustering; instead, it creates an **augmented ordering** of the database representing its density-based clustering structure for an *infinite range* of $\varepsilon$ values.
>
> **Core-Distance:** The core-distance of a point $p$ is the **smallest** distance $\varepsilon'$ such that $N_{\varepsilon'}(p)$ contains at least **MinPts** points. Formally:
> $$\text{core-dist}(p) = \begin{cases} \text{Undefined} & \text{if } \vert N_\varepsilon(p) \vert < \textbf{MinPts} \\ \text{the } (MinPts-1)\text{-th smallest dist from } p & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
>
> **Reachability-Distance:** The reachability-distance of a point $p$ with respect to another point $o$ is:
> $$\text{reach-dist}(o, p) = \max(\text{core-dist}(o),\ \text{dist}(o, p))$$
> provided that $o$ is a core point. If $o$ is not a core, the reachability-distance is **undefined**.
>
> **How OPTICS Overcomes DBSCAN's Parameter Sensitivity:**
> * DBSCAN's output is highly dependent on a *single* chosen $\varepsilon$. The same dataset clustered with two slightly different $\varepsilon$ values can yield very different results.
> * OPTICS pre-computes a **reachability plot** (a 1-D bar chart of reachability distances) that encodes the cluster structure for *all* possible $\varepsilon$ values up to a generating distance.
> * A user can then visually extract clusters from the plot by looking for **valleys** (low reachability = dense region) and **peaks** (high reachability = cluster boundary or noise). The user need not rerun the algorithm with different $\varepsilon$ values.

---

#### **Part (b)** — $(7 \text{ Marks})$

**Q.** Compare and contrast **DBSCAN** with **K-Means** clustering along the dimensions: (i) number of clusters, (ii) cluster shape, (iii) handling of noise, (iv) parameter requirement, and (v) computational complexity.

**Model Solution:**

| Dimension | K-Means | DBSCAN |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **(i) Number of Clusters** | Must be specified a priori as $K$. | **Automatically determined** from data density. |
| **(ii) Cluster Shape** | Only discovers **spherical / convex** clusters of similar size. | Discovers clusters of **arbitrary shape** (crescents, rings, blobs). |
| **(iii) Handling of Noise** | Treats every point as a member of some cluster — **no native noise handling**. Outliers distort centroids. | **Explicitly identifies and isolates** noise points. |
| **(iv) Parameter Requirement** | $K$ (number of clusters). | $\varepsilon$ and **MinPts** (both data-driven). |
| **(v) Complexity** | $O(I \cdot K \cdot n \cdot d)$ where $I$ = iterations. | $O(n \log n)$ with spatial indexing; $O(n^2)$ without. |
| **(vi) Sensitivity** | Sensitive to initial seed centroids. | Sensitive to $\varepsilon$ and **MinPts**; distance metric choice. |
| **(vii) Determinism** | Non-deterministic across runs (random init). | **Deterministic** for fixed $\varepsilon$, **MinPts**, and visiting order. |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing cardinality with absolute value:** Many students write $|x|$ meaning "set cardinality" or "absolute value" but write it as $\vert N_\varepsilon(p) \vert$ *correctly* in the formula sheet but write $N_\varepsilon(p)$ as just a set without count elsewhere — KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for ambiguous notation.
> 2. **Forgetting that "noise" is a *temporary* label:** In DBSCAN, a point first marked as noise may later be relabelled as a **border point** if it lies within the $\varepsilon$-neighbourhood of a subsequently visited core point. Marks are lost if students treat "noise" as final without explaining this nuance.
> 3. **Mis-stating density-reachability symmetry:** A frequent error is to assume density-reachability is symmetric. **It is not.** Density-*connectivity* is symmetric. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 4. **Writing "$\varepsilon$" instead of "$\textbf{MinPts}$" or vice-versa** in the core point test — both must be present in the inequality $\vert N_\varepsilon(p) \vert \ge \textbf{MinPts}$.
> 5. **In numerical questions, showing only final classification without writing the neighbourhoods explicitly** — this costs 3–4 marks because the marking scheme allocates points for *neighbourhood construction*, *cardinality check*, and *final classification* as three independent steps.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Density-Based Methods**

- ✅ **DBSCAN = Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise** — Ester, Kriegel, Sander, Xu (1996).
- ✅ Two input parameters: **Epsilon ($\varepsilon$)** and **MinPts**.
- ✅ Three point types: **Core**, **Border**, **Noise**.
- ✅ Core point test: $\vert N_\varepsilon(p) \vert \ge \textbf{MinPts}$.
- ✅ **Direct density-reachability** is *asymmetric*; **Density-connectivity** is *symmetric*.
- ✅ A **cluster** is a *maximal* set of *density-connected* points.
- ✅ DBSCAN can discover **arbitrary-shaped clusters** and **handle noise natively** — key advantages over K-Means.
- ✅ DBSCAN is **deterministic** for fixed parameters and visiting order.
- ✅ DBSCAN's complexity is $O(n \log n)$ with spatial index, $O(n^2)$ without.
- ✅ **OPTICS** extends DBSCAN by pre-computing a **reachability plot** that reveals structure across multiple $\varepsilon$ values.
- ✅ **Core-distance** of $p$ = minimum $\varepsilon$ for $N_\varepsilon(p)$ to contain **MinPts** points.
- ✅ **Reachability-distance** from $o$ to $p$ = $\max(\text{core-dist}(o), \text{dist}(o, p))$.
- ✅ DBSCAN struggles with **varying densities** across clusters and with **high-dimensional data** (curse of dimensionality).
- ✅ Default academic values in KTU papers: **2-D data, $\varepsilon = 1.5$ or $2$, MinPts = 3 or 4**.
- ✅ In numerical answers, always show: **(1) distance matrix, (2) neighbourhoods, (3) cardinality check, (4) classification, (5) cluster output** — this is the full marks sequence.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Grid-Based Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT604 - DATA MINING AND WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Grid-Based Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Grid-Based Clustering** is a class of data mining techniques that partition the data space into a finite number of cells (forming a **grid structure**), and then perform all clustering operations on this quantized space rather than on the individual data objects. Clustering is performed by analyzing the statistical properties, density, or transformed representations of the grid cells, achieving significantly lower computational complexity.

The fundamental principle of grid-based clustering is to summarize the data by partitioning the multi-dimensional feature space into a regular tessellation of rectangular (hypercubic) cells. Instead of operating on the raw data points, the algorithm aggregates data statistics per cell, making it highly efficient for large spatial databases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (KTU Syllabus Highlight):** Grid-based methods differ from partition-based (K-Means) and hierarchical methods in that their computational complexity depends on the **number of cells in the grid** rather than on the number of data objects *n*. This makes them especially suitable for very large databases.

### 1.2 The Three Principal Grid-Based Algorithms (KTU 2024 Module 2)

| Algorithm | Full Form | Core Mechanism | Best Suited For |
|-----------|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| **STING** | Statistical Information Grid | Stores statistical summaries hierarchically | Multi-resolution spatial queries |
| **WaveCluster** | Wavelet-based Clustering | Applies wavelet transform to quantized grid | Arbitrary shape cluster detection |
| **CLIQUE** | Clustering in QUEst | Density + grid, finds subspaces | High-dimensional data |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Population Density Map**
> Imagine you are a census officer tasked with identifying "city clusters" across a vast country. Instead of asking each of the 1.4 billion people their address, you overlay a **transparency grid** of 1 km × 1 km squares over the entire map. You simply count the people living inside each square. Squares with high counts get shaded "red" (dense clusters), and adjacent red squares are merged into a "city cluster."
>
> - The **grid cells** are the 1 km × 1 km squares.
> - The **cell statistics** are the population counts.
> - The **clustering** is the merging of adjacent dense squares.
>
> This is precisely the philosophy of grid-based clustering — **summary first, cluster second**.

### 1.4 Advantages of Grid-Based Methods (Frequently Asked in KTU Exams)

1. **Fast Processing Time:** Typically *O(k)* where *k* is the number of cells, often much less than *n* (number of points).
2. **Query-Independence:** Grid structure is built independently of queries (STING), enabling rapid online queries.
3. **Shape Insensitivity:** Can detect arbitrarily shaped clusters (WaveCluster).
4. **Parallelizable:** Cells can be processed in parallel.
5. **Scalability:** Excellent for very large datasets.

### 1.5 Limitations

- **Resolution Trade-off:** Number of cells per dimension must be chosen; too coarse loses clusters, too fine incurs overhead.
- **Curse of Dimensionality:** In high dimensions, the number of cells grows exponentially.
- **Quality Dependence:** All cells treated as uniform — the boundaries may misclassify boundary points.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 General Operational Framework of Grid-Based Clustering

The generic operational pipeline is universally applicable across STING, WaveCluster, and CLIQUE:

1. **Grid Construction:** Define the data space and partition it into *p* non-overlapping cells along each dimension, yielding a total of $p^d$ cells for a *d*-dimensional space.
2. **Data Mapping / Cell Population:** Map each data point to its containing cell. Aggregate per-cell statistics (count, mean, variance, mass, etc.).
3. **Statistical Analysis / Transformation:** Examine cell statistics or apply mathematical transforms (e.g., wavelet transform).
4. **Cluster Identification:** Identify dense or significant cells and merge adjacent significant cells to form clusters.
5. **Output:** Assign points within cluster cells to their respective clusters.

### 2.2 STING (Statistical Information Grid) — Detailed Architecture

STING uses a **hierarchical, multi-resolution grid** organized as a tree:

- **Root layer (Layer 1):** Single cell representing the entire data space.
- **Subsequent layers:** Each cell at level *i* is divided into 4 (2D) or 2^d child cells at level *i+1*.
- **Leaf layer (lowest level):** Provides the finest resolution.

**Statistical Parameters Stored per Cell:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Meaning |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Count | *n* | Number of points in the cell |
| Mean | *m* | Centroid of points in the cell |
| Standard Deviation | *σ* | Spread of points |
| Min | *min* | Minimum attribute value |
| Max | *max* | Maximum attribute value |
| Distribution | *N(m, σ²)* | Assumed normal distribution type |

**Query Processing in STING:**

- A query is propagated from the root.
- At each level, cells are evaluated using a **confidence interval test** (typically 95%).
- Irrelevant cells are pruned; relevant cells are explored further.

**STING Complexity:** $O(K)$ where *K* is the number of grid cells at the lowest level (independent of *n*).

### 2.3 WaveCluster — Wavelet-Based Clustering

WaveCluster applies the **Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT)** to the grid cell feature space. The procedure is:

1. **Quantize feature space** into *p*-dimensional grid cells (e.g., 2D grid).
2. **Apply wavelet transform** on quantized feature space (e.g., using Haar wavelet).
3. The wavelet transform decomposes the data into frequency sub-bands.
4. **Find connected components** (clusters) in the transformed domain.
5. **Map clusters back** to original feature space.

**Why Wavelets?**
- Wavelets smooth the data (remove outliers).
- Wavelets emphasize **regions of high contrast** (cluster boundaries).
- Wavelets naturally support multi-resolution analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key WaveCluster Property:** The number of points *n* affects the *granularity* of the grid, but the wavelet transform is applied on the *grid summary*, not the raw points, making the algorithm extremely fast.

### 2.4 CLIQUE (Clustering in QUEst)

CLIQUE is a **density-grid hybrid** designed for **high-dimensional data**. It finds clusters in any subspace of the original high-dimensional space.

**CLIQUE Two-Phase Process:**

- **Phase 1 — Find Dense Units:**
  - A *unit* is a cell.
  - A unit is *dense* if the fraction of points it contains exceeds a threshold *τ* (density threshold).
  - Uses the **Apriori property** of monotonicity: if a k-dimensional cell is dense, all its (k−1)-dimensional projections are also dense.
  - Iteratively grows candidate dense units from 1D to higher dimensions.

- **Phase 2 — Form Clusters:**
  - Uses the **Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle** to find the best sub-regions containing dense cells.
  - For each cluster, a minimal cover (rectangular regions in subspaces) is constructed.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---------|----------------------|-------------|
| Total cells in grid | $C = p^d$ | p = partitions per dimension, d = dimensions |
| STING query time | $O(K)$, $K \ll n$ | K = leaf grid cells |
| STING cell division | $2^d$ child cells | Per parent cell |
| CLIQUE density | $\text{density}(u) = \frac{\text{points in } u}{\text{volume}(u)} \geq \tau$ | τ = density threshold |
| Selection criterion | $\text{Selectivity} = 0.96$ (95% confidence) | STING relevance threshold |
| Haar wavelet scaling | $\phi(x) = 1$ if $0 \leq x < 1$, else 0 | Used in WaveCluster |
| Wavelet coefficient | $W(j,k) = \int f(x) \cdot \psi_{j,k}(x)\, dx$ | Multi-resolution scaling |
| Connected component | Two cells connected if they share a face | Cluster merging rule |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not confuse** the STING confidence interval (used to prune cells) with the CLIQUE density threshold (used to mark dense cells). KTU examiners often test this distinction.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Geographic Information Systems (GIS):** Identifying hotspots of disease, crime, or traffic.
- **Astronomy:** Detecting galaxy clusters in multi-dimensional survey data.
- **E-commerce Customer Segmentation:** Binning customer behavior grids.
- **Anomaly Detection:** Sparse grid cells indicate outliers.
- **Image Segmentation:** WaveCluster directly applied to 2D image grids.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Estimating the Number of Grid Cells

For a dataset of *d* dimensions, with each dimension partitioned into *p* intervals, the total number of cells in the grid is:

$$
C_{\text{total}} = \prod_{i=1}^{d} p_i
$$

where $p_i$ is the number of partitions along dimension *i*. If all dimensions are partitioned equally into *p* cells, this simplifies to:

$$
C_{\text{total}} = p^d
$$

**Step-by-step justification:**

1. Each dimension *i* spans a range $[a_i, b_i]$.
2. The interval length per cell along dimension *i* is $\Delta_i = \frac{b_i - a_i}{p_i}$.
3. A unique cell is identified by an ordered tuple $(c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_d)$ where $c_i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, p_i\}$.
4. By the **rule of product** in combinatorics, the number of such tuples equals the product of the counts along each dimension.
5. Hence, $C_{\text{total}} = p_1 \times p_2 \times \cdots \times p_d = p^d$ for uniform partitioning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Curse of Dimensionality:** For *d* = 20 and *p* = 10, $C = 10^{20}$ cells. This shows why CLIQUE's subspace discovery is essential for high-dimensional data.

### 3.2 Derivation: Time Complexity Comparison

Let:
- *n* = number of data points
- *k* = number of grid cells
- *d* = number of dimensions

| Algorithm | Typical Complexity | Reason |
|-----------|-------------------|--------|
| K-Means | $O(n \cdot k \cdot i \cdot d)$ | Iterates over all points |
| DBSCAN | $O(n \log n)$ | Neighborhood queries |
| STING | $O(k + n)$ | One pass for cell counting |
| WaveCluster | $O(n + k \cdot d)$ | DWT on grid |
| CLIQUE | $O(C \cdot d + n)$ where C is candidate dense cells | Apriori-style scan |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU numerical:** If *n* = 10,000 and *k* = 100, grid methods are ~100× faster than K-Means (if the number of iterations *i* = 10).

### 3.3 Derivation: STING Confidence Interval (Cell Relevance Test)

Assume attribute values within a cell are normally distributed as $N(m, \sigma^2)$. For STING, given a query range $[low, high]$ and a confidence level (typically 95%), the cell is *relevant* if:

$$
P(\text{low} \leq X \leq \text{high}) \geq 0.95
$$

Using the standard normal distribution, the standardized interval is:

$$
P\left( \frac{\text{low} - m}{\sigma} \leq Z \leq \frac{\text{high} - m}{\sigma} \right) \geq 0.95
$$

**Example numerical walkthrough:**

- Cell *C*: mean *m* = 50, σ = 10
- Query: find cells with values in [30, 70]
- Standardized bounds: $\frac{30 - 50}{10} = -2$, $\frac{70 - 50}{10} = +2$
- Probability: $P(-2 \leq Z \leq 2) = 0.9545$
- Since 0.9545 ≥ 0.95 → cell is **relevant**.

**Therefore, this cell is explored further; its child cells are also examined.**

### 3.4 Derivation: CLIQUE Apriori-style Monotonicity

**Theorem (Monotonicity of Density in CLIQUE):**
If a *k*-dimensional unit *u* is dense, then every *(k−1)*-dimensional projection of *u* is also dense.

**Proof sketch:**

1. Let $S$ be a set of points in unit *u* (k-dimensional). Define projection $\pi_{S'}(S)$ onto any $(k-1)$-dimensional subset of attributes.
2. For a point $x \in S$, its projection $x'$ lies in the projected $(k-1)$-dimensional unit.
3. The number of points in the projection is at least equal to the number of points in *u* (since projection is surjective onto the set).
4. The volume of the projected unit is *larger* than that of *u* (it spans fewer dimensions but same per-dim length, hence higher or equal volume in projected space).
5. Hence, the density of the projection is at least the density of *u*:

$$
\text{density}(\text{proj}(u)) = \frac{|S|}{\text{vol}(\text{proj}(u))} \geq \frac{|S|}{\text{vol}(u)} = \text{density}(u) \geq \tau
$$

This monotonicity enables the **bottom-up candidate generation** akin to the Apriori algorithm in association rule mining.

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing Among STING, WaveCluster, CLIQUE

| Criterion | STING | WaveCluster | CLIQUE |
|-----------|-------|-------------|--------|
| Data Size | Very large | Very large | Very large + high-dim |
| Cluster Shape | Arbitrary | Arbitrary | Arbitrary (in subspaces) |
| Outlier Handling | Moderate | Strong (wavelet denoising) | Strong (density) |
| Dimensionality | Low–Medium | Low–Medium | High (key strength) |
| Time Complexity | Lowest | Low | Moderate |
| Online Query Support | Excellent | Poor | Poor |
| Subspace Discovery | No | No | Yes (key strength) |
| Ease of Implementation | Easiest | Moderate (wavelet) | Hardest |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Generic Grid-Based Clustering Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Dataset] --> B[Define Data Space Bounds]
    B --> C[Partition into p^d Grid Cells]
    C --> D[Map Each Point to Containing Cell]
    D --> E[Aggregate Cell Statistics]
    E --> F{Choose Algorithm}
    F -->|STING| G[Hierarchical Multi-Resolution Grid]
    F -->|WaveCluster| H[Apply Discrete Wavelet Transform]
    F -->|CLIQUE| I[Find Dense Units in Subspaces]
    G --> J[Query-Driven Cell Pruning]
    H --> K[Detect Connected Components]
    I --> L[MDL-Based Cluster Formation]
    J --> M[Final Clusters]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Assign Points to Cluster Labels]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: STING Hierarchical Grid

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[Layer 1 Root - Entire Space] --> L2a[Cell 1A]
    L1 --> L2b[Cell 1B]
    L1 --> L2c[Cell 1C]
    L1 --> L2d[Cell 1D]
    L2a --> L3a1[Sub Cell 1A1]
    L2a --> L3a2[Sub Cell 1A2]
    L2a --> L3a3[Sub Cell 1A3]
    L2a --> L3a4[Sub Cell 1A4]
    L2b --> L3b1[Sub Cell 1B1]
    L2b --> L3b2[Sub Cell 1B2]
    L2b --> L3b3[Sub Cell 1B3]
    L2b --> L3b4[Sub Cell 1B4]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: WaveCluster Wavelet Transformation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original Feature Space] --> B[Quantize to Grid Cells]
    B --> C[Compute Cell Density Mass]
    C --> D[Apply DWT - Haar Wavelet]
    D --> E[Decompose into Frequency Sub-bands]
    E --> F[Identify High Energy Regions]
    F --> G[Find Connected Components in Transformed Domain]
    G --> H[Map Back to Original Space]
    H --> I[Cluster Output]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: CLIQUE Two-Phase Approach

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[High-Dimensional Data] --> P1[Phase 1: Identify Dense Units]
    P1 --> P1a[Find 1D Dense Cells]
    P1a --> P1b[Generate kD Candidates using Apriori]
    P1b --> P1c[Prune using Monotonicity]
    P1c --> P1d[All Dense Units Identified]
    P1d --> P2[Phase 2: Form Clusters]
    P2 --> P2a[Apply MDL Principle]
    P2a --> P2b[Determine Minimal Covering Rectangles]
    P2b --> P2c[Merge Adjacent Dense Regions]
    P2c --> Output[Final Clusters in Subspaces]
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram: Comparative Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ST[STING]
        S1[Hierarchical Grid] --> S2[Statistical Parameters per Cell]
    end
    subgraph WC[WaveCluster]
        W1[Grid Quantization] --> W2[Wavelet Transform]
        W2 --> W3[Connected Components]
    end
    subgraph CL[CLIQUE]
        C1[Grid + Density] --> C2[Subspace Dense Units]
        C2 --> C3[MDL Clustering]
    end
    ST --> Output[Clustered Data]
    WC --> Output
    CL --> Output
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define grid-based clustering. Mention two major algorithms used in this category.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Grid-based clustering is a method in which the data space is divided into a finite number of cells forming a grid structure, and clustering operations are performed on these cells rather than on the individual data points. The number of cells depends on the number of partitions per dimension and the dimensionality of the data. **(2 marks)**

Two major algorithms: **(1 mark)**
1. **STING (Statistical Information Grid)** — uses hierarchical multi-resolution grids.
2. **WaveCluster** — uses wavelet transformation on the quantized grid.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024] What is the role of the Discrete Wavelet Transform in WaveCluster?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) in WaveCluster is applied to the quantized feature space (grid cells) to transform the data into the frequency domain. **(1 mark)**

Its roles are: **(2 marks)**
1. **Noise Removal / Smoothing:** Wavelets automatically filter out outliers by suppressing high-frequency noise.
2. **Cluster Boundary Detection:** High-contrast regions (cluster boundaries) are emphasized in the wavelet-transformed space.
3. **Multi-Resolution Analysis:** Wavelets provide a multi-scale view, allowing cluster detection at various granularities.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of the **STING** algorithm in detail with a neat diagram. Discuss its parameters and query processing mechanism. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of STING. How does it compare with K-Means clustering in terms of time complexity? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) STING Architecture (7 marks):**

- **Definition:** STING stands for Statistical Information Grid. It is a grid-based multi-resolution clustering technique used for spatial data mining. **(0.5 mark)**
- **Grid Hierarchy:** The data space is recursively divided into rectangular cells. At the lowest (leaf) layer, each cell corresponds to a small sub-region; at higher levels, cells are merged to form larger regions. The root represents the entire space. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Statistical Parameters Stored per Cell:** count, mean, standard deviation, min, max, and distribution type (typically normal). **(1 mark)**
- **Query Processing:** A spatial query is propagated from the root. At each level, the relevance of a cell to the query is computed using a confidence interval (95% threshold). Irrelevant cells are pruned; relevant cells are explored at the next level. This continues until the leaf layer. **(2 marks)**
- **Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.2 for the hierarchical grid structure. **(1 mark)**
- **Time Complexity:** $O(K)$ where $K$ is the number of cells at the leaf level, independent of *n*. **(1 mark)**

**(b) Advantages, Limitations & Comparison (7 marks):**

**Advantages:** **(3 marks)**
1. **Query-Independent:** Grid is built once and reused for multiple queries.
2. **Fast:** $O(K)$ is much faster than K-Means' $O(n \cdot k \cdot i \cdot d)$.
3. **Parallelizable:** Cells can be processed independently.
4. **Multi-Resolution:** Supports queries at various granularities.

**Limitations:** **(2 marks)**
1. Quality depends on granularity of the lowest layer.
2. All child cells of a relevant parent are explored, even if only one is relevant.
3. Boundary effects — points near cell boundaries are assigned arbitrarily.
4. Does not handle high-dimensional data well.

**Comparison with K-Means:** **(2 marks)**
- K-Means: $O(n \cdot k \cdot i \cdot d)$ — depends on *n* and iterations *i*.
- STING: $O(K + n)$ for grid construction, $O(K)$ for query — depends on grid size, not on *n*.
- K-Means requires iterative refinement; STING is non-iterative.
- K-Means is sensitive to initial centroids; STING is deterministic.
- K-Means produces spherical clusters; STING can detect arbitrary shapes.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Describe the **WaveCluster** algorithm. Explain how the wavelet transform aids in cluster detection. Illustrate with the steps involved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **CLIQUE algorithm** in detail. How does it handle high-dimensional data? Discuss its density threshold and Apriori property. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) WaveCluster (7 marks):**

- **Definition:** WaveCluster is a grid-based clustering algorithm that applies wavelet transformation on the quantized feature space to detect clusters. **(0.5 mark)**
- **Step 1 — Quantize Feature Space:** Partition the *d*-dimensional data space into $p^d$ cells. Each cell summarizes the points it contains (e.g., count). **(1 mark)**
- **Step 2 — Apply DWT:** Apply the Discrete Wavelet Transform (typically Haar wavelet) to the cell summary, decomposing it into multiple frequency sub-bands. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Step 3 — Identify Significant Sub-bands:** High-amplitude coefficients in the transformed space correspond to cluster centers. **(1 mark)**
- **Step 4 — Find Connected Components:** Adjacent cells with significant wavelet coefficients are linked to form clusters. **(1 mark)**
- **Step 5 — Map Back to Original Space:** Label points within cluster cells with cluster IDs. **(1 mark)**
- **Why Wavelets Help:** Noise removal (high frequencies filtered), cluster boundary sharpening, and multi-resolution cluster detection. **(1 mark)**

**(b) CLIQUE (7 marks):**

- **Definition:** CLIQUE (Clustering in QUEst) is a grid and density-based clustering algorithm that automatically discovers clusters in **subspaces** of high-dimensional data. **(0.5 mark)**
- **Phase 1 — Find Dense Units:** **(2 marks)**
  - A unit (cell) is *dense* if its density $\geq \tau$ (threshold).
  - Uses the **Apriori property**: if a *k*-dimensional unit is dense, all *(k-1)*-dimensional projections are also dense.
  - This allows efficient candidate generation by bottom-up traversal.
- **Phase 2 — Form Clusters:** **(1.5 marks)**
  - Apply the **Minimum Description Length (MDL)** principle.
  - Find minimal rectangular covers in subspaces.
  - Merge adjacent dense regions.
- **Handling High-Dimensional Data:** **(1.5 marks)**
  - CLIQUE searches all subspaces, not just the full-dimensional space.
  - It exploits the fact that clusters may exist only in subsets of attributes.
  - Avoids the curse of dimensionality by working locally in subspaces.
- **Density Threshold τ:** A user-defined parameter; cells with point density above τ are considered "dense." **(0.5 mark)**
- **Apriori Property:** Monotonicity of density — prevents combinatorial explosion in candidate generation. **(1 mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students confuse STING's *statistical parameters* (mean, std) with WaveCluster's *cell density* — remember, STING summarizes statistics; WaveCluster transforms the summary.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** When asked for complexity, students write $O(n)$ for STING — the *correct* answer is $O(K)$ where *K* is the number of cells. STING's query time is independent of *n* only after the grid is built.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing CLIQUE's "Apriori property" with **Association Rule Mining's** Apriori algorithm. The monotonicity is conceptually similar, but the application is in candidate dense cell generation, not frequent itemset mining.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Students forget to mention that CLIQUE's strength is **subspace clustering**, not just full-dimensional clustering. This often costs 2 marks.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** For numerical questions on grid cells, students forget to apply $p^d$ correctly when dimensions have different partition counts.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Recall Bulletins:**
> - **Grid-based clustering = quantize data space → aggregate cell stats → cluster the cells.**
> - **STING** = hierarchical grids + statistical summaries + 95% confidence pruning.
> - **WaveCluster** = grid + DWT (wavelet) + connected components.
> - **CLIQUE** = grid + density + subspace discovery + Apriori + MDL.
> - **Total cells** = $p^d$ (uniform) or $\prod p_i$ (non-uniform).
> - **STING time complexity** = $O(K)$, where $K$ = leaf cells.
> - **CLIQUE density threshold** = $\tau$ (user-defined).
> - **CLIQUE Apriori property** = density monotonicity enables bottom-up candidate generation.
> - **WaveCluster strength** = noise removal + arbitrary shape detection.
> - **STING strength** = query-independent + parallelizable.
> - **CLIQUE strength** = handles high-dimensional data via subspace clustering.
> - **Common disadvantage** = boundary effects, resolution trade-off, curse of dimensionality.
> - **Three real-world applications** = GIS, astronomy, image segmentation.
> - **K-Means vs STING** = K-Means is iterative and depends on *n*; STING is non-iterative and depends on *K*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Model-Based Clustering Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT604 - DATA MINING AND WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Model-Based Clustering Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Model-Based Clustering** is a probabilistic approach to cluster analysis that assumes the observed data is generated from a **finite mixture of underlying probability distributions**. Rather than partitioning data based purely on geometric distance (as in K-Means), model-based clustering hypothesizes that each data point belongs to a latent (hidden) component drawn from a parametric distribution, and the goal is to estimate both the parameters of these distributions and the cluster membership of each point.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Model-based clustering methods assume that data is generated by a mixture of underlying probability distributions, and the task of clustering is reduced to *estimating the parameters* of these distributions. The two cornerstone techniques covered under this module are:
> 1. **Expectation–Maximization (EM) Algorithm**
> 2. **Conceptual Clustering (e.g., COBWEB)**

The two primary families studied under this topic in KTU Module 2 are:

| Method Family | Core Idea | Distribution Assumed |
|---|---|---|
| Expectation–Maximization (EM) | Statistical estimation via latent variables | Usually **Gaussian (Normal)** |
| Conceptual Clustering | Probabilistic concept formation over attributes | Categorical / Attribute-based |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Mixed Coins in a Bag" Problem:**
> Imagine you are blindfolded and given 100 coins from a bag containing **two types of coins**: *Type A* (fair coin, 50-50 heads/tails) and *Type B* (biased coin, 70-30 heads/tails). You cannot see which coin you pick, and you cannot tell the type. You flip each coin 10 times and record outcomes. **Model-based clustering asks:** *"Given only the flip sequences, infer which coin belongs to which type (cluster) AND estimate the bias (parameter) of each coin type."* The **EM algorithm** solves this iteratively — guess the type, refine the bias, then re-assign types using refined biases.

This intuition is the bedrock of EM-based clustering — it is fundamentally a *parameter estimation* problem disguised as a clustering problem.

### 1.3 Key Parameters & Metrics (Standardized)

The following parameters appear universally in model-based clustering notation across the KTU 2024 syllabus:

- **K** = Number of mixture components (clusters)
- **θ** = Set of all model parameters (typically written as θ = {μ, Σ, π})
- **πₖ** = **Mixing weight** of the k-th component, where Σπₖ = 1
- **μₖ** = Mean vector of the k-th Gaussian component
- **Σₖ** = Covariance matrix of the k-th Gaussian component
- **Zᵢ** = **Latent variable** indicating cluster membership of point xᵢ
- **γ(zᵢₖ)** = Posterior probability (responsibility) that xᵢ was generated by component k

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Finite Mixture Model (FMM) — The Generative Assumption

A **finite mixture model** assumes each data point **xᵢ** is generated by:

**Step 1:** Randomly choose a component k ∈ {1, 2, …, K} with probability **πₖ** (the mixing weight).

**Step 2:** Sample xᵢ from the probability distribution parameterized by **θₖ**.

The probability density of the observed data is therefore a weighted sum of component densities:

$$f(x_i \mid \theta) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \cdot f_k(x_i \mid \theta_k)$$

For the **Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM)** — the most common in KTU problems — each component density is multivariate Gaussian:

$$f_k(x \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2} \vert \Sigma_k \vert^{1/2}} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}(x - \mu_k)^{T} \Sigma_k^{-1} (x - \mu_k)\right)$$

where **d** is the dimensionality of the data.

### 2.2 Why Model-Based Clustering? (The "Why" and "How")

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use model-based clustering instead of K-Means?**
> - K-Means produces **hard assignments** (each point to exactly one cluster) and assumes **spherical, equal-sized clusters**. 
> - Model-based clustering produces **soft (probabilistic) assignments**, allowing overlapping clusters and arbitrary elliptical shapes (captured by Σₖ).
> - It also provides a **statistical justification** for cluster boundaries.

### 2.3 The Expectation–Maximization (EM) Algorithm

EM is an iterative algorithm for finding **Maximum Likelihood Estimates (MLE)** when data has latent (hidden) variables. It alternates between two steps:

**E-Step (Expectation):** Compute the posterior probability that each data point belongs to each cluster — the *responsibility*.

**M-Step (Maximization):** Update the parameters (πₖ, μₖ, Σₖ) to maximize the expected log-likelihood given the responsibilities from the E-step.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Save this table — these formulas appear in nearly every KTU numerical on this topic.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mixture Density | $f(x_i \vert \theta) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k f_k(x_i \vert \theta_k)$ | Probability of xᵢ under the full mixture |
| 2 | Gaussian Density | $f_k(x) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2}\vert\Sigma_k\vert^{1/2}} \exp\left(-\tfrac{1}{2}(x-\mu_k)^T \Sigma_k^{-1}(x-\mu_k)\right)$ | Multivariate Normal PDF |
| 3 | Log-Likelihood | $\ell(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k f_k(x_i \vert \theta_k)$ | Objective to be maximized |
| 4 | Responsibility (E-Step) | $\gamma(z_{ik}) = \frac{\pi_k f_k(x_i \vert \theta_k)}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \pi_j f_j(x_i \vert \theta_j)}$ | Posterior probability of cluster membership |
| 5 | Updated Mixing Weight | $\pi_k^{new} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{ik})$ | M-Step update for πₖ |
| 6 | Updated Mean | $\mu_k^{new} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{ik})\, x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{ik})}$ | Weighted mean using responsibilities |
| 7 | Updated Covariance | $\Sigma_k^{new} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{ik})\, (x_i - \mu_k^{new})(x_i - \mu_k^{new})^T}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{ik})}$ | Weighted covariance |
| 8 | Stopping Criterion | $\vert \ell^{(t+1)} - \ell^{(t)} \vert < \epsilon$ | Convergence when log-likelihood change is negligible |
| 9 | Constraint | $\sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k = 1, \quad \pi_k \geq 0$ | Mixing weights form a probability simplex |
| 10 | Number of Parameters | $\#\text{params} = K \cdot \left(\tfrac{d(d+1)}{2} + d + 1\right) - 1$ | Free parameters in a full GMM |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where Model-Based Clustering is used in Industry:**
> - **Bioinformatics:** Gene expression clustering using GMMs.
> - **Image Segmentation:** Foreground/background separation via EM.
> - **Speech Recognition:** Speaker diarization (who spoke when) using GMM-HMM.
> - **Anomaly Detection:** Low-probability points under a fitted mixture = anomalies.
> - **Finance:** Customer segmentation with soft assignments (a customer may belong 60% to "premium" and 40% to "regular").

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the EM Algorithm

**Goal:** Maximize the log-likelihood of observed data given unknown parameters and latent variables.

#### Step A — Start with the observed-data log-likelihood

$$\ell(\theta) = \log P(X \mid \theta) = \log \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(x_i \mid \theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(x_i \mid \theta)$$

#### Step B — Introduce the latent variables Zᵢ

Since each xᵢ is generated by some hidden cluster k, we marginalize over Zᵢ:

$$P(x_i \mid \theta) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} P(x_i \mid z_{ik}=1, \theta) \cdot P(z_{ik}=1 \mid \theta) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \, f_k(x_i \mid \theta_k)$$

So the full observed log-likelihood becomes:

$$\ell(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log \left( \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \, f_k(x_i \mid \theta_k) \right)$$

#### Step C — Why direct maximization is hard

The **log of a sum** inside the objective makes the derivative difficult — there is no closed-form solution when latent variables exist. This is precisely the problem EM solves.

#### Step D — Construct the expected complete-data log-likelihood

Instead of maximizing $\ell(\theta)$ directly, EM maximizes a lower bound — the **Expected Complete-Data Log-Likelihood (ECDLL)**:

$$Q(\theta, \theta^{old}) = \mathbb{E}_{Z \mid X, \theta^{old}} \left[ \log P(X, Z \mid \theta) \right]$$

The complete-data log-likelihood (if Z were observed) is:

$$\log P(X, Z \mid \theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} z_{ik} \left[ \log \pi_k + \log f_k(x_i \mid \theta_k) \right]$$

#### Step E — Take the expectation over Z using current parameters

Since $\mathbb{E}[z_{ik} \mid x_i, \theta^{old}] = P(z_{ik}=1 \mid x_i, \theta^{old}) = \gamma(z_{ik})$, we substitute:

$$Q(\theta, \theta^{old}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \gamma(z_{ik}) \left[ \log \pi_k + \log f_k(x_i \mid \theta_k) \right]$$

#### Step F — The E-Step (closed form)

Compute the responsibilities using Bayes' theorem:

$$\gamma(z_{ik}) = \frac{\pi_k^{old} \, f_k(x_i \mid \mu_k^{old}, \Sigma_k^{old})}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \pi_j^{old} \, f_j(x_i \mid \mu_j^{old}, \Sigma_j^{old})}$$

**Intuition:** The numerator is the prior probability × likelihood of xᵢ belonging to component k. The denominator normalizes over all components so responsibilities sum to 1.

#### Step G — The M-Step (maximize Q)

Set partial derivatives of $Q(\theta, \theta^{old})$ with respect to each parameter to zero:

**For πₖ** (subject to Σπₖ = 1, using Lagrange multiplier λ):

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \pi_k}\left[ Q - \lambda\left(\sum_{j} \pi_j - 1\right) \right] = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \pi_k^{new} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{ik})}{N}$$

**For μₖ** (taking derivative and solving):

$$\frac{\partial Q}{\partial \mu_k} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \mu_k^{new} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{ik})\, x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{ik})}$$

**For Σₖ** (differentiating w.r.t. Σₖ⁻¹ for simplicity):

$$\Sigma_k^{new} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{ik})\, (x_i - \mu_k^{new})(x_i - \mu_k^{new})^T}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{ik})}$$

#### Step H — Convergence check

Repeat E-Step and M-Step until:

$$\vert \ell(\theta^{t+1}) - \ell(\theta^t) \vert < \epsilon$$

where ε is a small tolerance (commonly **1e-6** or **1e-4**).

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Given 3 one-dimensional data points x = {1, 4, 7}, fit a 2-component GMM using EM. Initial parameters: π₁ = π₂ = 0.5, μ₁ = 2, σ₁² = 1, μ₂ = 6, σ₂² = 1.

**Solution — Iteration 1:**

**E-Step:** Compute responsibilities. For each xᵢ, the Gaussian density is $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp(-(x-\mu)^2/2)$.

For x₁ = 1 (k=1): $f_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp(-0.5) \approx 0.2420$
For x₁ = 1 (k=2): $f_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp(-12.5) \approx 0.0000$

$$\gamma(z_{1,1}) = \frac{0.5 \times 0.2420}{0.5 \times 0.2420 + 0.5 \times 0.0000} \approx 1.000$$

For x₂ = 4: $f_1 \approx 0.0540$, $f_2 \approx 0.1290$.

$$\gamma(z_{2,1}) = \frac{0.0540}{0.0540 + 0.1290} = 0.295, \quad \gamma(z_{2,2}) = 0.705$$

For x₃ = 7: $f_1 \approx 0.0000$, $f_2 \approx 0.2420$.

$$\gamma(z_{3,2}) \approx 1.000$$

**M-Step:** Sum of responsibilities: N₁ = 1.000 + 0.295 = 1.295, N₂ = 0.705 + 1.000 = 1.705.

$$\mu_1^{new} = \frac{1.000(1) + 0.295(4)}{1.295} \approx 1.682$$
$$\mu_2^{new} = \frac{0.705(4) + 1.000(7)}{1.705} \approx 3.760$$

Iteration 2 onwards continues similarly until convergence. The model assigns x=1 and x=7 with high confidence to clusters 1 and 2 respectively, while x=4 has a soft, ambiguous assignment — exactly the value model-based clustering adds beyond K-Means.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | K-Means | EM / GMM |
|---|---|---|
| Assignment type | Hard | Soft (probabilistic) |
| Cluster shape | Spherical | Arbitrary elliptical |
| Parameter estimation | None (only centroids) | Full (μ, Σ, π) |
| Convergence | Local minima possible | Monotonic likelihood increase |
| Outlier sensitivity | High | Lower (probabilistic) |
| Computational cost | O(NKd·iter) | O(NKd²·iter) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 EM Algorithm Workflow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Observed Data X] --> initA[Initialize Parameters: mu sigma pi]
    initA --> evalA{Log Likelihood Converged?}
    evalA -- No --> estepA[E-Step: Compute Responsibilities gamma ik]
    estepA --> mstepA[M-Step: Update mu sigma pi]
    mstepA --> evalA
    evalA -- Yes --> outputA[Output: Final Parameters and Cluster Assignments]
    outputA --> stopA[Stop]
```

### 4.2 Generative Model of a Gaussian Mixture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Pick Component k with probability pi k] --> nodeB[Sample x from Gaussian k with mean mu k and covariance Sigma k]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Observed Data Point x i]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Latent Variable z ik indicates component membership]
    nodeD --> nodeE[EM Algorithm estimates theta]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Clustering (COBWEB) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inpA[New Instance Arrives] --> inpB[Evaluate Category Utility at Each Node]
    inpB --> inpC{Decision: Which Action?}
    inpC -- Merge --> inpD[Merge with Best Host Node]
    inpC -- Split --> inpE[Split Best Host Node]
    inpC -- Create --> inpF[Create New Leaf Node]
    inpC -- Pass --> inpG[Pass Down to Child Node]
    inpD --> inpH[Updated Classification Tree]
    inpE --> inpH
    inpF --> inpH
    inpG --> inpH
```

### 4.4 E-Step vs M-Step Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    blockA[E-Step Function] --> blockB[Input: Current theta and X] --> blockC[Compute Posterior P zik given xi and theta] --> blockD[Output: Responsibilities gamma ik]
    blockE[M-Step Function] --> blockF[Input: Responsibilities gamma ik and X] --> blockG[Update pi mu sigma using weighted formulas] --> blockH[Output: New theta parameters]
    blockD --> blockE
    blockH --> blockA
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is model-based clustering? How does it differ from distance-based clustering methods like K-Means?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Model-based clustering is a probabilistic approach that assumes the data is generated from a mixture of underlying probability distributions. Each cluster is treated as a parametric model (typically a Gaussian), and the algorithm estimates the parameters of these distributions along with the cluster membership of each data point. **(1 mark)**

Unlike K-Means, which performs hard assignment based on Euclidean distance and assumes spherical clusters of equal size, model-based clustering provides **soft probabilistic assignments** using posterior probabilities. **(1 mark)**

It can capture clusters of varying shapes and sizes through the covariance matrices and offers a statistically principled framework with a well-defined likelihood objective that monotonically increases during iteration. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the Expectation-Maximization algorithm and state its two main steps.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm is an iterative method for finding the maximum likelihood estimates of parameters in statistical models that involve latent (hidden) variables. **(1 mark)**

It consists of two alternating steps: **(0.5 marks)**
- **E-Step (Expectation):** Computes the expected value of the latent variables — the posterior probability (responsibility) that each data point belongs to each cluster, given the current parameter estimates.
- **M-Step (Maximization):** Updates the model parameters (mixing weights, means, and covariances) to maximize the expected log-likelihood derived from the E-step.

These steps are repeated until convergence of the log-likelihood function. **(0.5 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm in detail with the assumption that the data follows a Gaussian Mixture Model. Derive the update equations for the parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the following 1-D data points: X = {1, 2, 3, 8, 9, 10}. Fit a 2-component Gaussian Mixture Model using EM for **two iterations**, assuming initial parameters π₁ = π₂ = 0.5, μ₁ = 1, σ₁² = 1, μ₂ = 9, σ₂² = 1. Show all steps of E-step and M-step. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(a):**

**Definition:** EM is an iterative algorithm used to find the Maximum Likelihood Estimates (MLE) of parameters in probabilistic models with latent (unobserved) variables. In the context of GMM clustering, the latent variables represent the unknown cluster membership of each data point.

**Gaussian Mixture Model Assumption:** Each data point xᵢ is assumed to be drawn from a mixture of K multivariate Gaussian distributions, where the k-th component has mean μₖ, covariance Σₖ, and mixing weight πₖ.

The probability density function is:
$$f(x_i \mid \theta) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \, \mathcal{N}(x_i \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)$$

**Derivation of EM Updates:**

**E-Step:** The responsibility γ(zᵢₖ) is the posterior probability that xᵢ belongs to component k, computed using Bayes' theorem:
$$\gamma(z_{ik}) = \frac{\pi_k^{old} \, \mathcal{N}(x_i \mid \mu_k^{old}, \Sigma_k^{old})}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \pi_j^{old} \, \mathcal{N}(x_i \mid \mu_j^{old}, \Sigma_j^{old})}$$

**M-Step:** Maximizing the expected complete-data log-likelihood yields:

$$\pi_k^{new} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{ik})$$

$$\mu_k^{new} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{ik})\, x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{ik})}$$

$$\Sigma_k^{new} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{ik})\, (x_i - \mu_k^{new})(x_i - \mu_k^{new})^T}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{ik})}$$

The algorithm iteratively applies E-step and M-step until convergence, i.e., when the change in log-likelihood falls below a threshold ε.

**Convergence Property:** EM is guaranteed to monotonically increase the log-likelihood at each iteration and converges to a local maximum (not necessarily global).

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(b):**

**Iteration 1:**

**E-Step:** For each xᵢ, compute γ(zᵢₖ) using the Gaussian density formula. Given the initialization and the clear separation between the clusters {1, 2, 3} and {8, 9, 10}, the responsibilities will be approximately:

For x = 1, 2, 3: γ(zᵢ,₁) ≈ 1.0 (assigned to component 1)
For x = 8, 9, 10: γ(zᵢ,₂) ≈ 1.0 (assigned to component 2)

**M-Step:** Using the responsibilities:

$$\mu_1^{new} = \frac{1 + 2 + 3}{3} = 2.0$$
$$\mu_2^{new} = \frac{8 + 9 + 10}{3} = 9.0$$
$$\pi_1 = \pi_2 = 0.5$$

The covariances also stabilize close to their initial values. The model has effectively separated the two natural clusters in the data after just one iteration.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain conceptual clustering. Describe the COBWEB algorithm with its four primary operations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the EM algorithm with K-Means clustering. Discuss the advantages and limitations of each method. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(a):**

**Conceptual Clustering:** Conceptual clustering is a form of model-based clustering that groups objects into clusters described by **conceptual descriptions** rather than geometric proximity. It is a machine learning approach where the clusters themselves represent general concepts that summarize the data.

**COBWEB Algorithm:** COBWEB is a popular conceptual clustering algorithm that builds a **classification tree** incrementally. Each node in the tree represents a concept (cluster) and stores probabilistic summaries (attribute-value distributions) of the objects it contains.

**Four Primary Operations in COBWEB:**

1. **Merging:** Combines the current node with its best host node to form a new node, used when both nodes together form a better cluster.

2. **Splitting:** Replaces a node with its children when splitting provides a better category utility.

3. **Creating a New Node:** Adds a new leaf node for the incoming instance if it does not fit well into any existing cluster.

4. **Passing Down:** Recursively applies the same four operations to the best child node, allowing hierarchical cluster formation.

**Category Utility (CU):** The quality measure used to decide among operations is category utility, which favors clustering that maximizes the predictability of attribute values within clusters and minimizes predictability across clusters.

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(b):**

**Comparison of EM and K-Means:**

| Aspect | K-Means | EM Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| Cluster assignment | Hard | Soft (probabilistic) |
| Cluster shape | Spherical only | Elliptical, arbitrary |
| Objective | Minimize WCSS | Maximize log-likelihood |
| Convergence | Local minimum | Monotonic likelihood increase |
| Initialization sensitivity | High | High |
| Parameter estimation | Centroid only | Mean, covariance, mixing weight |

**Advantages of EM:** Provides probabilistic cluster memberships, can model clusters of any shape, well-grounded in statistical theory, supports model selection via criteria like BIC/AIC.

**Advantages of K-Means:** Simpler, computationally faster, easier to interpret and implement, scales well to large datasets.

**Limitations of EM:** Computationally expensive, may converge to local maxima, sensitive to initialization, requires choosing number of components K.

**Limitations of K-Means:** Hard assignments, restrictive cluster shapes, no probabilistic interpretation, sensitive to outliers.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in EM-Based Questions:**
> - **Forgetting the normalization constraint** $\sum_k \pi_k = 1$ in the M-step — costs 1 mark.
> - **Confusing the E-step and M-step outputs:** E-step computes responsibilities (numbers between 0 and 1); M-step updates parameters. Mixing these up is a 2-mark deduction.
> - **Not specifying the Gaussian assumption** when writing density formulas — examiners deduct marks if the distribution form is omitted.
> - **Skipping intermediate iterations** in numerical problems — KTU requires showing every iteration's responsibilities and parameter updates, not just the final answer.
> - **Forgetting to mention monotonic convergence** of EM — a frequently asked theoretical point worth 1 mark.
> - **Writing $\vert \Sigma_k \vert$ with single vertical bars inside markdown tables** — this breaks table rendering. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Model-Based Clustering Methods:**

- **Model-based clustering** treats data as generated from a mixture of probability distributions (most commonly Gaussian).
- The two principal algorithms in KTU Module 2 are **EM for GMM** and **COBWEB for conceptual clustering**.
- The **EM algorithm** has two steps: **E-step (compute responsibilities)** and **M-step (update parameters)**.
- The **responsibility** γ(zᵢₖ) is the posterior probability that point xᵢ was generated by component k.
- **Mixing weights πₖ** must satisfy $\sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k = 1$ and $\pi_k \geq 0$.
- **Updated mean formula:** weighted average of points using responsibilities as weights.
- **Updated covariance formula:** weighted scatter matrix using responsibilities as weights.
- **Convergence criterion:** change in log-likelihood below a tolerance ε.
- **EM guarantees monotonic increase** of log-likelihood but may converge to **local maxima**.
- **Conceptual clustering (COBWEB)** builds a classification tree incrementally with four operations: merge, split, create, pass down.
- **Category utility** is the quality measure used by COBWEB to decide clustering actions.
- **Key advantage of EM over K-Means:** soft probabilistic assignments and flexible cluster shapes via covariance matrices.
- **Initialization sensitivity:** both EM and K-Means benefit from multiple restarts or K-Means++ style initialization.
- **Number of components K** is typically chosen using BIC, AIC, or cross-validation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clustering High-Dimensional Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT604 - DATA MINING AND WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Clustering High-Dimensional Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Dimensional Data** refers to datasets where the number of attributes (dimensions, features) is large, often comparable to or exceeding the number of records. Formally, for a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$ where each $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$, we say the data is **high-dimensional** when $d$ is in the range of tens, hundreds, or thousands. Standard distance-based and density-based clustering algorithms (such as **k-Means**, **DBSCAN**, and **Hierarchical Clustering**) suffer severe degradation in such spaces — this degradation is collectively termed the **Curse of Dimensionality**.

**Clustering high-dimensional data** is therefore the family of techniques that attempt to discover meaningful groups in such spaces by either (a) reducing dimensionality, (b) finding clusters in subspaces, or (c) designing similarity measures robust to the peculiarities of high-dimensional geometry.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spherical Onion" Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Spherical Onion Analogy** — Imagine 1,000 points scattered uniformly inside a unit sphere in 10-D space. Intuitively you might think "the center is dense, the boundary is sparse." In reality, the **opposite** is true in high dimensions: almost every point lies in a thin shell near the surface. Picture an onion whose layers keep collapsing onto the outermost skin. This means traditional notions of "neighbourhood" and "density" collapse — every point becomes roughly equidistant from every other point, making distance-based clustering meaningless.

This phenomenon is called **Distance Concentration**.

### 1.3 Why Standard Clustering Fails in High Dimensions

| Symptom | KTU Terminology | Practical Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| All pairwise distances converge | **Distance concentration** | Nearest-neighbour = Farthest-neighbour |
| Volume of space explodes | **Curse of dimensionality** | Density estimates become 0 |
| Irrelevant attributes dominate | **Noise accumulation effect** | True clusters are masked |
| Many empty cells in grids | **Sparsity of grid cells** | Density-based methods break |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Major Problems in High-Dimensional Clustering

1. **Distance Concentration** — The ratio of the distance to the *farthest* neighbour to the *nearest* neighbour approaches **1** as $d \to \infty$.
2. **Irrelevant Features** — A large fraction of dimensions contribute noise rather than signal.
3. **Empty Space Phenomenon** — Data points become isolated islands in an otherwise empty space.
4. **Sparsity** — Available samples are insufficient to populate the space.

### 2.2 Categories of High-Dimensional Clustering Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Classification** — Three principal families:
> 1. **Dimensionality Reduction + Standard Clustering** (PCA, SVD, LSA, NMF $\rightarrow$ k-Means)
> 2. **Subspace Clustering** (CLIQUE, ENCLUS, MAFIA, PROCLUS, ORCLUS)
> 3. **Biclustering / Co-Clustering** (simultaneous clustering of rows and columns)

### 2.3 The CLIQUE Algorithm (CLustering In QUEst)

CLIQUE is a **grid-based, density-based, axis-aligned subspace clustering** method with the following philosophy:

- Partition each dimension into $\xi$ intervals of equal length → create a **grid of units**.
- A unit is **dense** if the fraction of points inside it exceeds threshold $\tau$.
- A cluster is a **maximal set of connected dense units** in some $k$-dimensional subspace.
- Use the **Apriori principle**: if a $k$-dimensional unit is dense, then its $(k-1)$-dimensional projections are also dense. This is the **bottom-up monotonicity property**.

### 2.4 PROCLUS — Projected Clustering

PROCLUS assigns each cluster a **distinct subset of dimensions** in which it exists. Each cluster $C_i$ is associated with a subspace $S_i \subseteq \{1, 2, \dots, d\}$ where $\mid S_i \mid \ll d$.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Pairwise distance concentration | $\lim_{d \to \infty} \dfrac{\mathrm{Var}(\Vert X-Y\Vert)}{(\mathbb{E}[\Vert X-Y\Vert])^2} \to 0$ | Distances concentrate around mean |
| Mean distance in unit hypercube | $\mathbb{E}[\Vert X-Y\Vert] \approx \sqrt{d \cdot \dfrac{1}{6}}$ | For uniform $[0,1]^d$ |
| Concentration ratio (Beyer) | $\dfrac{\max_i \Vert x_i - q \Vert}{\min_i \Vert x_i - q \Vert} \to 1$ | As $d \to \infty$ |
| Grid cell density (CLIQUE) | $\mathrm{density}(u) = \dfrac{\mid \{p \in u\} \mid}{N}$ | $u$ = grid unit, $N$ = total points |
| Subspace density support | $\mathrm{support}(S) = \dfrac{\mid \{p : p \in u, \forall i \in S\} \mid}{N}$ | $S$ = dimension subset |
| Silhouette Coefficient | $s(o) = \dfrac{b(o) - a(o)}{\max\{a(o), b(o)\}}$ | Quality of clustering |
| PCA reconstruction error | $\mathrm{Err}_{k} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=k+1}^{d} \lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i}$ | $\lambda_i$ = eigenvalues |
| Explained variance ratio | $\mathrm{EVR}(k) = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i}$ | Choose $k$ such that $\mathrm{EVR} \geq 0.95$ |
| Reconstruction via PCA | $\hat{X} = U_k \, \Sigma_k \, V_k^{T}$ | Top-$k$ SVD components |
| Locality preservation (LPP) | $\min \sum_{ij} (y_i - y_j)^2 W_{ij}$ | For manifold methods |
| Intrinsic dimensionality (MLE) | $\hat{d} = \left[ \dfrac{1}{n(k-1)} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{k} \log \dfrac{T_k(X_i)}{T_j(X_i)} \right]^{-1}$ | $T_k$ = k-NN distance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility** — These formulas are deployed in **bioinformatics** (gene expression clustering with $d \approx 20{,}000$ genes), **text/web mining** (document clustering with $d \approx 50{,}000$ terms via TF-IDF), **image retrieval** (SIFT features with $d = 128$), and **anomaly detection** in network telemetry.

### 2.6 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why distance concentration happens** — Because each dimension adds variance independently, the **law of large numbers** forces the distribution of $\Vert X-Y \Vert$ to become sharply peaked.
- **Why subspace clustering works** — Clusters exist in *correlated subsets* of dimensions; irrelevant dimensions add noise that can be projected out.
- **Why the Apriori property holds for CLIQUE** — If a $k$-dim cell has many points, then all its $k-1$ dim projections also have many points (the projection of a set is at least as large as a slice).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Mean Pairwise Distance in a $d$-Dimensional Unit Hypercube

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **frequently-asked KTU derivation**. We compute $\mathbb{E}[\Vert X - Y \Vert]$ for $X, Y \sim \mathcal{U}[0,1]^d$ independent.

**Step 1 — Set up the squared distance.**

Each coordinate difference is $Z_i = X_i - Y_i$, where $X_i, Y_i \sim \mathcal{U}[0,1]$ independent. The squared distance is:

$$D^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{d} Z_i^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute expectation of $Z_i^2$.**

Using the property that for independent $X, Y$ on $[0,1]$:

$$\mathbb{E}[Z_i^{2}] = \mathbb{E}[(X_i - Y_i)^2] = \mathbb{E}[X_i^2] - 2\mathbb{E}[X_i]\mathbb{E}[Y_i] + \mathbb{E}[Y_i^2]$$

We know $\mathbb{E}[X_i] = \dfrac{1}{2}$ and $\mathbb{E}[X_i^2] = \dfrac{1}{3}$, so:

$$\mathbb{E}[Z_i^2] = \dfrac{1}{3} - 2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} + \dfrac{1}{3} = \dfrac{2}{3} - \dfrac{1}{2} = \dfrac{1}{6}$$

**Step 3 — Apply linearity of expectation.**

$$\mathbb{E}[D^2] = \sum_{i=1}^{d} \mathbb{E}[Z_i^2] = d \cdot \dfrac{1}{6} = \dfrac{d}{6}$$

**Step 4 — Take the square root (using the law of large numbers).**

By the LLN, for large $d$ the random variable $D$ becomes tightly concentrated around its mean squared value, so:

$$\mathbb{E}[D] \approx \sqrt{\mathbb{E}[D^2]} = \sqrt{\dfrac{d}{6}}$$

This confirms the formula in the cheat-sheet.

### 3.2 Derivation: Variance of Pairwise Distance

**Step 1 — Express $\mathrm{Var}(D)$ in terms of fourth moment.**

$$\mathrm{Var}(D) = \mathbb{E}[D^2] - (\mathbb{E}[D])^2$$

But a more useful decomposition uses the squared distance:

$$\mathrm{Var}(D^2) = \mathrm{Var}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{d} Z_i^2\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{d} \mathrm{Var}(Z_i^2) \quad \text{(independence)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\mathrm{Var}(Z_i^2)$.**

$$\mathbb{E}[Z_i^4] = \mathbb{E}[(X_i - Y_i)^4] = \dfrac{1}{5} - 2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{4} + \dfrac{1}{5} = \dfrac{2}{5} - \dfrac{1}{4} = \dfrac{3}{20}$$

Then:

$$\mathrm{Var}(Z_i^2) = \mathbb{E}[Z_i^4] - (\mathbb{E}[Z_i^2])^2 = \dfrac{3}{20} - \dfrac{1}{36} = \dfrac{27 - 5}{180} = \dfrac{22}{180} = \dfrac{11}{90}$$

**Step 3 — Sum over dimensions.**

$$\mathrm{Var}(D^2) = d \cdot \dfrac{11}{90}$$

**Step 4 — Form the concentration ratio.**

Using the standard asymptotic approximation $\mathrm{Var}(D) \approx \dfrac{\mathrm{Var}(D^2)}{4 \mathbb{E}[D^2]}$:

$$\mathrm{Var}(D) \approx \dfrac{d \cdot \frac{11}{90}}{4 \cdot \frac{d}{6}} = \dfrac{11/90}{4/6} = \dfrac{11}{90} \cdot \dfrac{6}{4} = \dfrac{11}{60}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the ratio of standard deviation to mean.**

$$\dfrac{\sqrt{\mathrm{Var}(D)}}{\mathbb{E}[D]} \approx \dfrac{\sqrt{11/60}}{\sqrt{d/6}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{d}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{11/60}{1/6}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{d}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{11}{10}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{1.1}}{\sqrt{d}}$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the result.**

$$\lim_{d \to \infty} \dfrac{\sqrt{\mathrm{Var}(D)}}{\mathbb{E}[D]} = 0$$

Therefore, all pairwise distances become **virtually identical** as $d$ grows. This is the rigorous proof of **distance concentration** and the reason classical distance-based clustering fails.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Method vs. Property

| Property | k-Means on Raw Data | k-Means after PCA | CLIQUE | PROCLUS | Biclustering |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Handles high $d$ | Poor | Good | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent |
| Finds subspace clusters | No | Partially | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Requires $k$ input | Yes | Yes | No | Yes | No |
| Time complexity | $O(nkdT)$ | $O(d^2n + nkdT)$ | $O(d \cdot 2^d)$ approx | $O(nkd \cdot \mathrm{iter})$ | $O(nm)$ |
| Output type | Partition | Partition | Dense units | Subspaces & points | Biclusters |
| KTU relevance | Low | High | **Very High** | High | Medium |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — High-Dimensional Clustering Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[High Dimensional Dataset X in R superscript d] --> B[Preprocessing]
    B --> B1[Normalization]
    B --> B2[Noise Removal]
    B --> B3[Feature Selection]
    B1 --> C{Choose Strategy}
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C -->|Dimensionality Reduction| D[PCA SVD LSA NMF]
    C -->|Subspace Search| E[CLIQUE PROCLUS ENCLUS]
    C -->|Co Clustering| F[Biclustering Spectral]
    D --> G[Apply k Means DBSCAN Hierarchical]
    E --> H[Dense Unit Mining]
    F --> I[Row Column Simultaneous Groups]
    G --> J[Cluster Validation]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K{Silhouette or DB Index above threshold}
    K -->|Yes| L[Final Clusters and Subspaces]
    K -->|No| M[Tune Parameters and Return to C]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Taxonomy of High-Dimensional Clustering

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[High D Clustering] --> R1[Full Space Methods]
    H --> R2[Subspace Methods]
    H --> R3[Pattern Based Methods]
    H --> R4[Manifold Methods]
    R1 --> R1a[PCA plus k Means]
    R1 --> R1b[SVD plus Hierarchical]
    R2 --> R2a[Axis Aligned]
    R2 --> R2b[Arbitrary Orientation]
    R2a --> R2a1[CLIQUE]
    R2a --> R2a2[ENCLUS]
    R2a --> R2a3[MAFIA]
    R2b --> R2b1[PROCLUS]
    R2b --> R2b2[ORCLUS]
    R3 --> R3a[Frequent Pattern Based]
    R3 --> R3b[Text Document Clustering]
    R4 --> R4a[ISOMAP]
    R4 --> R4b[Locally Linear Embedding]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — CLIQUE Algorithm Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Database D, threshold tau, xi intervals per dim] --> S2[Partition each dimension into xi equal units]
    S2 --> S3[Identify dense 1D units]
    S3 --> S4[Apply Apriori Principle to generate candidate 2D units]
    S4 --> S5[Test if 2D units are dense using tau]
    S5 --> S6[Repeat to find kD dense units]
    S6 --> S7[Determine minimal covering sets of dense units]
    S7 --> S8[Form clusters as maximal connected components]
    S8 --> S9[Output clusters in subspaces with descriptions]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-level functional architecture** has been used in place of any 2-D geometric drawings because high-dimensional geometry cannot be faithfully drawn on paper; the algorithmic and data-flow relationships are what matter for KTU examinations.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> Define the **Curse of Dimensionality** in the context of clustering. List any **two** consequences it has on distance-based clustering algorithms.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** The Curse of Dimensionality refers to the set of phenomena that emerge when analysing data in high-dimensional spaces, where the number of features $d$ is large. As $d$ increases, the volume of the space grows exponentially, causing data points to become sparse and distances to lose discriminative power.
