- **Two consequences (1 mark):**
  1. **Distance concentration** — The ratio of maximum to minimum pairwise distances approaches 1, making nearest-neighbour queries meaningless.
  2. **Density estimation failure** — The number of cells required grows as $d^k$, so density estimates become unreliable and most grid cells are empty.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> What is **distance concentration**? State the limiting expression for the ratio of farthest to nearest neighbour distance.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Distance concentration (2 marks):** It is the phenomenon where, in high-dimensional spaces, all pairwise distances between data points become approximately equal; the distribution of distances becomes sharply peaked around its mean.
- **Limiting expression (1 mark):**
$$\lim_{d \to \infty} \dfrac{\max_{i} \Vert x_i - q \Vert}{\min_{i} \Vert x_i - q \Vert} = 1$$

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** For Part B, you MUST show the **Apriori property derivation** explicitly, label every figure/diagram, and present the **cluster formation step** separately from the **subspace identification step**. Marks are routinely lost when students merge these two phases. Always end with a quality-measure equation.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **CLIQUE algorithm** in detail. Describe the **dense unit identification**, the **Apriori principle used for candidate generation**, and the **cluster formation** step.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a clear labelled diagram, explain the **PROCLUS algorithm** for projected clustering. Compare PROCLUS and CLIQUE on at least **three** parameters.

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Overview (1 mark):** CLIQUE is a **grid-based, density-based, axis-aligned subspace clustering algorithm** that finds clusters in any $k$-dimensional subspace, where $k$ may be less than $d$. Input: dataset $\mathcal{D}$, number of intervals $\xi$ per dimension, density threshold $\tau$.

2. **Step 1 — Grid Construction (1 mark):** Partition each of the $d$ dimensions into $\xi$ equal-length intervals. The space is divided into a grid of at most $\xi^d$ units. Each unit $u$ is described by an interval $[\ell_i, h_i)$ on each selected dimension.

3. **Step 2 — Identify 1-D Dense Units (1 mark):** A unit is **dense** if
$$\mathrm{density}(u) = \dfrac{\mid \{ p \in \mathcal{D} : p \in u \} \mid}{N} \geq \tau$$
   Mark all such units as **dense**.

4. **Step 3 — Apriori Candidate Generation (2 marks):** Use the **downward-closure property**: if a $k$-dimensional unit is dense, then every $(k-1)$-dimensional projection is also dense. Therefore:
   - Generate candidate $k$-dimensional units by joining pairs of $(k-1)$-dimensional dense units that share a $(k-2)$-dimensional face.
   - **Prune** any candidate whose projection fails to be dense.
   - Test density of remaining candidates.

5. **Step 4 — Cluster Formation (1 mark):** Find **Maximal Regions** — connected components of dense units. Two units are connected if they share a face. The cluster is the set of points lying in the union of these connected dense units.

6. **Step 5 — Minimal Covering Description (1 mark):** For each discovered cluster, greedily cover it with the smallest number of dense units to provide a minimal DNF-style description; this is the cluster's "signature."

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

1. **PROCLUS Overview (1 mark):** PROCLUS = **PRO**jected **CLUS**tering. It is a **k-medoid-based** algorithm that produces an **arbitrary-orientation** projected cluster for each of $k$ clusters. Each cluster has its own subspace $S_i$ with $\mid S_i \mid = s$ (chosen by the user or by algorithm).

2. **Three Phases (2 marks):**
   - **Phase 1 — Initialization:** Randomly select a small superset of $k \cdot a$ candidate medoids ($a$ typically 2–3).
   - **Phase 2 — Subspace Identification & Medoid Refinement:** Iteratively choose one medoid per cluster, evaluate the average distance along each dimension in a small neighbourhood, and retain the $s$ dimensions with the **smallest average distance** (lowest spread = highest locality). This defines $S_i$.
   - **Phase 3 — Point Assignment:** Assign every non-medoid point to the cluster whose medoid is closest in subspace $S_i$.

3. **Diagram (1 mark):** A labelled flow diagram of the three phases is essential.

4. **Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | CLIQUE | PROCLUS |
|---|---|---|
| Cluster orientation | **Axis-aligned** | **Arbitrary** (rotated) |
| Subspace shape | Rectangular grid cells | Voronoi-like in chosen dims |
| Input $k$ required | No (auto-discovers) | Yes (number of clusters) |
| Complexity | Exponential in $k$ | $O(n \cdot k \cdot d \cdot \mathrm{iter})$ |
| Scalability | High $d$ | Moderate $d$ |
| Cluster description | DNF of grid units | Centroid + subspace mask |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **mean pairwise Euclidean distance** $\mathbb{E}[\Vert X - Y \Vert]$ for two independent random points $X, Y$ drawn uniformly from the unit hypercube $[0,1]^d$. Show every step.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss **three major problems** of clustering in high-dimensional spaces. Explain with suitable illustrations how **subspace clustering** addresses each problem.

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

(Same derivation as Section 3.1, reproduced and elaborated for full marks.)

1. Set up $D^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{d} (X_i - Y_i)^2$ with $X_i, Y_i \stackrel{\text{iid}}{\sim} \mathcal{U}[0,1]$. (1 mark)
2. Compute $\mathbb{E}[(X_i - Y_i)^2] = \mathbb{E}[X_i^2] - 2\mathbb{E}[X_i]\mathbb{E}[Y_i] + \mathbb{E}[Y_i^2] = \dfrac{1}{3} - \dfrac{1}{2} + \dfrac{1}{3} = \dfrac{1}{6}$. (2 marks)
3. Sum over $d$ dimensions: $\mathbb{E}[D^2] = \dfrac{d}{6}$. (1 mark)
4. Apply the **law of large numbers** to justify $D \approx \sqrt{D^2}$ in expectation, giving:
$$\mathbb{E}[D] \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{d}{6}} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$
5. **Concluding remark (1 mark):** Hence for $d = 100$, $\mathbb{E}[D] \approx \sqrt{100/6} \approx 4.08$, and the standard deviation scales as $1/\sqrt{d}$, illustrating that distances concentrate.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

1. **Problem 1 — Distance concentration (2 marks):** All pairwise distances become nearly equal; $k$-NN loses meaning. **Subspace fix:** Search for clusters in low-dimensional subspaces where the data is no longer concentrated. A cluster that is invisible in $d$ dimensions may be a tight, well-separated cluster in a 2-D subspace.
2. **Problem 2 — Irrelevant features (2 marks):** Many dimensions are noise. **Subspace fix:** Identify the relevant subset $S \subseteq \{1, \dots, d\}$ where a cluster is dense; project the data onto $S$ before measuring distance.
3. **Problem 3 — Sparsity and empty cells (2 marks):** The fraction of empty grid cells grows as $\tau^d$ for a uniform grid. **Subspace fix:** Search low-dimensional subspaces where the data is dense — fewer cells to populate, denser coverage.
4. **Illustration (1 mark):** A simple 2-D picture showing that two diagonal clusters are masked in full 2-D but are revealed in their respective 1-D projections.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Curse of Dimensionality** = exponential growth of space as $d$ increases.
- [ ] **Distance concentration** formula: ratio of max to min distance $\to 1$ as $d \to \infty$.
- [ ] **Mean pairwise distance** in $[0,1]^d$ is $\sqrt{d/6}$.
- [ ] **CLIQUE** = grid + density + Apriori; **axis-aligned subspace clusters**.
- [ ] **PROCLUS** = k-medoid in projected subspaces; **arbitrary orientation**.
- [ ] **Biclustering** clusters rows and columns simultaneously; used in gene-expression analysis.
- [ ] **PCA + k-Means** is a common dimensionality-reduction pipeline.
- [ ] **Apriori property of CLIQUE**: density is **monotone downward** in dimensionality.
- [ ] **Silhouette Coefficient** is the most common validation metric in KTU papers.
- [ ] Always specify **three components** in any high-D clustering answer: (i) subspace identification, (ii) cluster formation, (iii) validation.
- [ ] Avoid confusing "high-dimensional clustering" with "clustering many clusters" — they are unrelated.
- [ ] For $d > 50$, **never** apply raw $k$-Means; always reduce or use subspace methods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When asked "why does $k$-Means fail in high dimensions?", structure your answer as: **(1) Distance concentration → equal distances → centroid distortion**, **(2) Irrelevant features dominate variance → wrong axis for SSE minimization**, **(3) Empty space → cluster boundary is meaningless**. This three-point structure consistently scores full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constraint-Based Cluster Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT604 - DATA MINING AND WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Constraint-Based Cluster Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
**Constraint-Based Cluster Analysis** is a sub-field of data clustering in which the clustering process is driven by *user-specified constraints* in addition to the inherent similarity of data objects. Formally, given a dataset $D = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$ and a set of constraints $C = \{c_1, c_2, \dots, c_k\}$, the goal is to find a partition $P = \{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_m\}$ such that:
1. Every cluster $C_j$ satisfies the constraints in $C$.
2. The quality objective (e.g., intra-cluster similarity, separation) is optimized.

This transforms classical unsupervised clustering into a **semi-supervised** task where domain knowledge guides the discovery process.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition: The "Filtered Job Recruitment" Analogy**
> Imagine recruiting interns from a pool of 10,000 students. If you simply group "similar" students using any clustering algorithm, you may end up with clusters based on hair color or height. But as a real recruiter, you *do not want* that — you have hard rules: *"Minimum CGPA = 7"*, *"Must be from Kerala"*, *"At most 5 interns per company"*. These rules are **constraints**. Constraint-based clustering is exactly this: clustering *with rules*. The data is grouped by similarity, **but** only clusters that pass your filters are accepted.

### KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlights
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Takeaways (Module 2):**
> - Distinguish between *instance-level* and *cluster-level* constraints.
> - Recognize *obstinate (stubborn) clustering* as the classic baseline approach.
> - Understand the **COP-KMeans** formulation (Constrained Optimization Problem).
> - Know the difference between *hard* and *soft* constraints.
> - Be aware of real-world applications (location-based services, marketing segmentation, biology).

### Mathematical Entities (Bold Constants)
- **Hard Constraint**: A condition that *must* be satisfied. A violation makes the clustering invalid.
- **Soft Constraint**: A condition that *should* be satisfied. Violations are penalized but not forbidden.
- **Constraint Violation Penalty** ($\lambda$): The weight given to a soft-constraint breach in the objective function.
- **Cluster Diameter** ($\delta$): The maximum pairwise distance among objects in a cluster.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Constraint-Based Clustering is Needed
Classical algorithms (k-Means, DBSCAN, hierarchical) ignore domain knowledge. This causes practical problems:
- A store wants clusters of customers spending more than ₹5000, but k-Means groups low spenders together.
- A biologist wants clusters of genes with similar expression *and* similar function — pure similarity misses function.
- A city planner wants clusters of regions that are spatially contiguous but also have a minimum population.

### 2.2 Categories of Constraints (The KTU Must-Know Taxonomy)

| Category | Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **By Object** | Instance-level | Apply to individual points | "Salary $\geq$ ₹50,000" |
| **By Cluster** | Cluster-level | Apply to all points in a cluster | "Diameter $\leq$ 10 km" |
| **By Coupling** | Must-link | Two points **must** be in the same cluster | $x_i$ and $x_j$ are siblings |
| | Cannot-link | Two points **must not** be in the same cluster | $x_i$ and $x_j$ are competitors |
| **By Hardness** | Hard | Must be satisfied | "Minimum size = 5" |
| | Soft | Should be satisfied; penalty applies | "Average age $\approx$ 30" |
| **By Geography** | Spatial / Obstacle | Physical barriers exist | River, mountain between houses |

### 2.3 The Obstinate (Stubborn) Approach
This is the **naive baseline** taught in KTU. The logic is brutally simple:
1. Run standard clustering (say, k-Means).
2. Discard any cluster that violates a hard constraint.
3. Re-cluster the discarded points.

**Limitation**: Information is lost because the initial clustering had no idea about constraints. The clusters are formed in a "blind" first pass.

### 2.4 The Constrained Optimization Problem (COP) Approach
This is the *mathematically superior* technique. Instead of two passes, the constraint is *embedded* into the objective function.

The classical unconstrained k-Means minimizes:

$$ J = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{k} w_{ij} \, \lVert x_i - \mu_j \rVert^2 $$

where $w_{ij} = 1$ if $x_i$ belongs to cluster $j$, else $0$, and $\mu_j$ is the centroid.

In COP-KMeans, we add a **constraint violation penalty term**:

$$ J_{\text{con}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{k} w_{ij} \, \lVert x_i - \mu_j \rVert^2 + \lambda \cdot P(\text{constraint violation}) $$

where $\lambda$ is the penalty weight and $P(\cdot)$ counts violations.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Meaning | Constraints Used |
|---|---|---|
| $J = \sum_{i,j} w_{ij} \lVert x_i - \mu_j \rVert^2$ | Standard k-Means cost | None |
| $J_{\text{con}} = J + \lambda \cdot P$ | Constrained objective | Soft |
| $\lVert x_i - x_j \rVert \leq \delta$ | Diameter constraint | Cluster-level |
| $\lvert C_j \rvert \geq s_{\min}$ | Minimum cluster size | Cluster-level |
| $w_{ij} = 1$ if must-link$(i,j)$ | Must-link enforcement | Pairwise |
| $w_{ij} + w_{il} \leq 1$ | Cannot-link enforcement | Pairwise |
| $\text{SSQ} = \sum_j \sum_{x \in C_j} \lVert x - \mu_j \rVert^2$ | Sum of Squared Errors | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL NOTATION RULE FOR KTU EXAMS**: Always use $\lVert \cdot \rVert$ for the Euclidean norm. Never write $\vert x \vert$ because in data mining, $\vert \cdot \vert$ usually denotes set cardinality. For absolute distance, prefer $\lVert \cdot \rVert$ or $\text{dist}(\cdot,\cdot)$.

### 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility
- **Telecom (Kerala context)**: Tower placement respecting population and coverage diameter constraints.
- **Healthcare**: Patient cohort discovery with must-link (same genetic profile) and cannot-link (different demographics) constraints.
- **Retail Analytics (e.g., LuLu Mall)**: Customer segmentation constrained by spending power and visit frequency.
- **Urban Planning (Smart City Kerala Mission)**: Spatial clustering of zones with population and contiguity constraints.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From k-Means to COP-KMeans

We start from the unconstrained objective:

$$ J(W, \mu) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{k} w_{ij} \, \lVert x_i - \mu_j \rVert^2 $$

**Step 1 — Acknowledge the constraints.**

Suppose we have a *must-link* set $M = \{(i_1, j_1), (i_2, j_2), \dots\}$ and a *cannot-link* set $N$. We define a violation function $P$:

$$ P = \underbrace{\sum_{(p,q) \in M} \mathbf{1}\big[w_{p,:} \neq w_{q,:}\big]}_{\text{must-link violations}} + \underbrace{\sum_{(p,q) \in N} \mathbf{1}\big[w_{p,:} = w_{q,:}\big]}_{\text{cannot-link violations}} $$

where $w_{p,:}$ is the cluster assignment vector for $x_p$, and $\mathbf{1}[\cdot]$ is the indicator function (returns $1$ if true, $0$ otherwise).

**Step 2 — Construct the constrained objective.**

$$ J_{\text{con}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{k} w_{ij} \, \lVert x_i - \mu_j \rVert^2 + \lambda \cdot P $$

**Step 3 — Soft-constraint case: $\lambda$ as a penalty weight.**

When $\lambda \to \infty$, soft constraints effectively become hard.

**Step 4 — Hard-constraint case: feasibility projection.**

For hard constraints, we modify the iterative k-Means update:

- During the **assignment step**, an assignment $w_{ij} = 1$ is *rejected* if it would violate any hard constraint.
- This may force a *sub-optimal* distance match (a "blocked" assignment).

$$ w_{ij}^{*} = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } j = \arg\min_{j' : \text{valid}(i,j')} \lVert x_i - \mu_{j'} \rVert^2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$

**Step 5 — Centroid update remains identical.**

$$ \mu_j = \frac{\sum_{i : w_{ij}=1} x_i}{\sum_{i} w_{ij}} $$

This is valid because the centroids are computed *after* a valid assignment has been enforced.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (K-Means with Minimum Size Constraint)

**Given**: Dataset $D = \{2, 4, 10, 12, 15, 20\}$, $k = 2$, constraint $\lvert C_j \rvert \geq 2$ for every $j$.

**Step 1 — Initial centroids**: $\mu_1 = 4$, $\mu_2 = 15$ (random selection).

**Step 2 — Distance computation**:

$$ d(2, \mu_1) = \lVert 2 - 4 \rVert = 2 $$
$$ d(4, \mu_1) = \lVert 4 - 4 \rVert = 0 $$
$$ d(10, \mu_1) = \lVert 10 - 4 \rVert = 6 $$
$$ d(12, \mu_1) = \lVert 12 - 4 \rVert = 8 $$

And for $\mu_2$:

$$ d(2, \mu_2) = \lVert 2 - 15 \rVert = 13 $$
$$ d(4, \mu_2) = \lVert 4 - 15 \rVert = 11 $$
$$ d(10, \mu_2) = \lVert 10 - 15 \rVert = 5 $$
$$ d(12, \mu_2) = \lVert 12 - 15 \rVert = 3 $$

**Step 3 — Assignment (assuming no constraint yet)**:

$$ C_1 = \{2, 4, 10\}, \quad C_2 = \{12, 15, 20\} $$

**Step 4 — Apply hard constraint**: Both clusters satisfy $\lvert C_j \rvert \geq 2$. Accepted.

**Step 5 — Recompute centroids**:

$$ \mu_1 = \frac{2+4+10}{3} = \frac{16}{3} \approx 5.33 $$
$$ \mu_2 = \frac{12+15+20}{3} = \frac{47}{3} \approx 15.67 $$

**Step 6 — Convergence check**: Reassign points; if no change, STOP.

> [!NOTE]
> **Counter-example (Constraint Violation)**: If $k = 3$ were used with $\lvert C_j \rvert \geq 2$, we could not form three valid clusters. The COP-KMeans would *block* the third cluster and effectively re-route the orphaned points to clusters 1 or 2.

### 3.3 Analytic Comparison Matrix (Frequently Asked in KTU)

| Property | Obstinate (Stubborn) | COP (Constraint-Optimized) |
|---|---|---|
| Stages | 2 (cluster, then filter) | 1 (single integrated optimization) |
| Quality of result | Sub-optimal | Near-optimal |
| Time complexity | $O(nkt)$ | $O(nkt \cdot \text{validity-check})$ |
| Hard constraint handling | Discard & re-cluster | In-line blocking |
| Soft constraint handling | Ignored | Weighted penalty |
| Robustness | Low | High |
| KTU Exam Weightage | High (definition-only) | High (derivation + numerical) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Constraint-Based Clustering Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Dataset D] --> B[User Domain Knowledge]
    B --> C[Constraint Set C]
    A --> D[Constraint Processing Unit]
    C --> D
    D --> E{Constraint Type Classifier}
    E -->|Must-link| F[Pairwise Coupling Matrix]
    E -->|Cannot-link| F
    E -->|Min Size| G[Cluster-Level Validator]
    E -->|Diameter| G
    E -->|Spatial| H[Obstacle Map Module]
    F --> I[COP K-Means Engine]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Assignment Step with Validity Check]
    J --> K{All Hard Constraints Met?}
    K -->|No| L[Reassign or Block]
    L --> J
    K -->|Yes| M[Update Centroids]
    M --> N{Convergence Reached?}
    N -->|No| J
    N -->|Yes| O[Final Valid Clusters]
    O --> P[Quality Metric Computation]
    P --> Q[Output: Constrained Clustering]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Flow: Obstinate vs. COP Approach

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Traditional
        T1[Input Data] --> T2[Unconstrained k-Means]
        T2 --> T3[All Clusters Formed]
        T3 --> T4{Filter by Constraints}
        T4 -->|Pass| T5[Accept]
        T4 -->|Fail| T6[Discard and Re-cluster]
    end
    subgraph COP
        C1[Input Data] --> C2[Constraints Embedded in Objective]
        C2 --> C3[Single Integrated k-Means Loop]
        C3 --> C4[Valid Clusters Only]
    end
```

### 4.3 Constraint Taxonomy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Clustering Constraints] --> Y[By Scope]
    Y --> Y1[Instance Level: Object Properties]
    Y --> Y2[Cluster Level: Cluster Properties]
    X --> Z[By Hardness]
    Z --> Z1[Hard: Must Satisfy]
    Z --> Z2[Soft: Penalty if Violated]
    X --> W[By Coupling]
    W --> W1[Must-Link: Same Cluster]
    W --> W2[Cannot-Link: Different Cluster]
    X --> V[By Domain]
    V --> V1[Spatial Obstacles]
    V --> V2[Domain Rules Business Logic]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip**: When asked to "explain with a diagram", a Mermaid-rendered block diagram (as above) is accepted. If hand-drawing, a labeled flowchart with the same logical blocks suffices.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define constraint-based cluster analysis. Differentiate between must-link and cannot-link constraints with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Constraint-based cluster analysis is a clustering approach that incorporates user-specified constraints, in addition to similarity, to guide the discovery of meaningful groups (1 mark).
- **Must-link constraint**: Two objects *must* belong to the same cluster. Example: Two patients with the same genetic marker in a medical study (1 mark).
- **Cannot-link constraint**: Two objects *must not* be in the same cluster. Example: A direct competitor and a customer's record being kept in different segments (1 mark).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is an obstacle-based constraint? How does it affect clustering?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An obstacle-based constraint arises from physical barriers (rivers, mountains, walls) in spatial data that prevent direct reachability between points (1 mark).
- It forces clustering algorithms to compute *shortest-path distances around obstacles* instead of straight-line Euclidean distances (1 mark).
- This can cause two geographically close points to be considered far, splitting a natural cluster (1 mark).

---

### Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Question Paper Pattern Note (KTU 2024 Scheme)**: Module 2 ESE questions typically carry 14 marks with internal choice. Two sub-parts (a) and (b), each 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the *obstinate (stubborn)* approach to constraint-based clustering with its limitations. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider the following 1-D data: $D = \{1, 2, 3, 8, 9, 10, 25\}$ with $k = 2$. Apply k-Means with the hard constraint $\lvert C_j \rvert \geq 3$ for each cluster. Show two iterations. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Obstinate Approach — 7 marks**

1. **Definition** (1 mark): The obstinate approach is a two-stage method where classical clustering (e.g., k-Means) is run first *without* any awareness of constraints, and then any cluster violating the constraints is discarded and re-clustered.

2. **Algorithm steps** (3 marks):
   - Step 1: Run standard k-Means to obtain $k$ clusters.
   - Step 2: Validate each cluster against hard constraints.
   - Step 3: For violating clusters, remove their points and re-cluster *those points only*.
   - Step 4: Repeat until all clusters satisfy constraints.

3. **Limitations** (3 marks):
   - *Information loss*: Initial clustering had no idea of constraints, so the discarded points may have been correctly placed originally.
   - *Order sensitivity*: Which cluster is re-clustered first affects the final result.
   - *Convergence issues*: Re-clustering loops can become unstable; no guarantee of global optimum.
   - *Scalability*: Repeated k-Means runs are computationally expensive.

**(b) Numerical — 7 marks**

**Initial**: $\mu_1 = 1$, $\mu_2 = 10$ (chosen from the data).

**Iteration 1 — Assignment**:

| Point | $d(x, \mu_1)$ | $d(x, \mu_2)$ | Cluster |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 9 | 1 |
| 2 | 1 | 8 | 1 |
| 3 | 2 | 7 | 1 |
| 8 | 7 | 2 | 2 |
| 9 | 8 | 1 | 2 |
| 10 | 9 | 0 | 2 |
| 25 | 24 | 15 | 2 |

Result: $C_1 = \{1,2,3\}$ (size 3 ✓), $C_2 = \{8,9,10,25\}$ (size 4 ✓). Both satisfy $\lvert C_j \rvert \geq 3$.

**Centroid Update**:
$$ \mu_1 = \frac{1+2+3}{3} = 2, \quad \mu_2 = \frac{8+9+10+25}{4} = 13 $$

**Iteration 2 — Reassignment**:

| Point | $d(x, 2)$ | $d(x, 13)$ | Cluster |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 12 | 1 |
| 2 | 0 | 11 | 1 |
| 3 | 1 | 10 | 1 |
| 8 | 6 | 5 | 2 |
| 9 | 7 | 4 | 2 |
| 10 | 8 | 3 | 2 |
| 25 | 23 | 12 | 2 |

No change in assignments. **Converged** (1 mark for clusters, 1 mark for centroids, 1 mark for validation, 1 mark for conclusion).

**Final Clusters**: $C_1 = \{1,2,3\}$, $C_2 = \{8,9,10,25\}$ — both satisfy the constraint.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** What is a *COP-KMeans* algorithm? Derive the constrained objective function from the standard k-Means objective. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Given must-link pairs $M = \{(1,2), (3,4)\}$ and cannot-link pair $N = \{(1,3)\}$ over 4 data points, explain how the constraint violation penalty $P$ is computed in the soft-constraint formulation. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) COP-KMeans — 7 marks**

COP-KMeans stands for *Constrained Optimization Problem K-Means* (1 mark). It is a unified algorithm that integrates user constraints into the standard k-Means objective function so that the iterative search process *respects* constraints from the start (1 mark).

**Derivation**:
Start with standard k-Means:

$$ J(W, \mu) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{k} w_{ij} \, \lVert x_i - \mu_j \rVert^2 $$

Introduce constraint violation penalty $P$ and weight $\lambda$:

$$ J_{\text{con}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{k} w_{ij} \, \lVert x_i - \mu_j \rVert^2 + \lambda \cdot P $$

For hard constraints, the validity check ensures that *no* assignment violating a constraint is ever made. For soft constraints, the penalty $\lambda \cdot P$ is added (3 marks).

**Update equations** (2 marks):
- *Assignment step*: Only consider valid assignments, i.e., $j$ such that constraint set $C$ is satisfied.
- *Centroid step*: Unchanged, $\mu_j = \frac{\sum_{i : w_{ij}=1} x_i}{\sum_i w_{ij}}$.

**(b) Penalty Computation — 7 marks**

Given: $M = \{(1,2),(3,4)\}$ (must-link), $N = \{(1,3)\}$ (cannot-link). For 4 points.

**Possible cluster assignments** (2 marks): A valid clustering of 4 points into, say, 2 clusters: $C_1 = \{1,2\}$, $C_2 = \{3,4\}$.

**Constraint check**:
- $(1,2) \in M$: Both in $C_1$. ✓ No violation.
- $(3,4) \in M$: Both in $C_2$. ✓ No violation.
- $(1,3) \in N$: 1 in $C_1$, 3 in $C_2$. ✓ No violation.

**Total penalty**: $P = 0$.

**Counter-example — violating clustering**: $C_1 = \{1,3\}$, $C_2 = \{2,4\}$.

- $(1,2) \in M$: 1 in $C_1$, 2 in $C_2$. ✗ Violation.
- $(3,4) \in M$: 3 in $C_1$, 4 in $C_2$. ✗ Violation.
- $(1,3) \in N$: 1 and 3 in $C_1$. ✗ Violation.

$$ P = \mathbf{1}[(1,2)] + \mathbf{1}[(3,4)] + \mathbf{1}[(1,3)] = 1 + 1 + 1 = 3 $$

So the **constrained objective** becomes $J_{\text{con}} = J + 3\lambda$. If $\lambda \to \infty$, this clustering is rejected (3 marks for computation, 1 mark for the conclusion).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing $\lVert x \rVert$ with $\vert x \vert$**: Marks deducted for sloppy notation. Use $\lVert \cdot \rVert$ for distance.
> 2. **Forgetting the indicator function $\mathbf{1}[\cdot]$**: Many students write $P$ as a plain sum. Always specify that $P$ uses an indicator (0 or 1).
> 3. **Mixing up soft and hard constraints**: Soft uses $\lambda \cdot P$ added; Hard uses feasibility filter. Examiners test this distinction.
> 4. **Not validating constraint satisfaction in numericals**: Always end with a "Both clusters satisfy the size constraint" line.
> 5. **Skipping the centroid recomputation**: In iterative k-Means questions, missing the second centroid update costs 2–3 marks.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition**: Constraint-based clustering = *similarity + user-specified rules*.
- **Three Constraint Categories**: Must-link, Cannot-link, Cluster-level properties.
- **Two Approaches**: Obstinate (filter-after) and COP (integrated).
- **Soft Constraints**: Embedded as penalty term $\lambda \cdot P$ in objective.
- **Hard Constraints**: Feasibility check during assignment step.
- **Obstacle-Based**: For spatial data with physical barriers.
- **Key Formula**: $J_{\text{con}} = \sum_{i,j} w_{ij} \lVert x_i - \mu_j \rVert^2 + \lambda \cdot P$.
- **Numerical Tip**: Always show *distance matrix*, *assignment*, *centroid update*, *constraint validation*.
- **Notation Rule**: $\lVert \cdot \rVert$ for norm, $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ for set cardinality, $\mathbf{1}[\cdot]$ for indicator.
- **Real-World**: Telecom, healthcare, retail, urban planning all use this technique.
- **Common KTU Trap**: Writing $w_{ij} \in \{0,1\}$ without the *constraint-feasibility condition*.
- **Memory Aid (KTU-friendly acronym)**: **C-L-A-S-S** — *Constraints Lead to Aware Semi-Supervised Segmentation*.
- **Past Year Frequency**: Dec 2023, June 2024, Dec 2024 — appears in *every* Module 2 paper.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Outlier Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT604 - DATA MINING AND WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Outlier Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OUTLIER (Definition per Han, Kamber & Pei — KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> An **outlier** is a data object that deviates significantly from the rest of the dataset — essentially, a data point whose behavior is "far different" from the expected norm, pattern, or distribution exhibited by the majority of the data.

In rigorous statistical and data mining terminology, outliers are also referred to as:
- **Anomalies** (in anomaly detection literature)
- **Deviants** (statistical literature)
- **Exceptions** (business analytics literature)

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight for KTU Exams:** Although outliers are often considered "noise" to be discarded in traditional statistical analysis, in Data Mining they are treated as **valuable informational entities** because they frequently represent:
> 1. **Critical incidents** (e.g., credit card fraud)
> 2. **Rare events** (e.g., equipment failure)
> 3. **Evolving patterns** (e.g., novel disease outbreaks)
> 4. **Data quality issues** (e.g., measurement errors)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy: The "Odd One Out" Game**

Imagine a classroom of 50 students, where 49 students are aged between 18–22 years. Suddenly, a 65-year-old part-time learner walks in.

That 65-year-old student is an **outlier** — not because they are "wrong" or "bad," but because their value (age = 65) is statistically **far from the central tendency** of the rest of the group.

> **Engineering Domain Example:** In a sensor network monitoring the temperature of a chemical reactor, if 99% of sensors report temperatures between **80°C and 90°C**, but a single sensor suddenly reports **150°C**, that reading is an outlier. It may indicate either a sensor malfunction OR a critical safety event (e.g., reactor runaway) that requires immediate intervention.

### 1.3 Why Outlier Analysis Matters in KTU 2024 Perspective

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:**
> Module 2 of PBADT604 explicitly treats Outlier Analysis as a **standalone data mining task**, alongside classification, clustering, and association analysis. This reflects the modern data science paradigm where **outliers are informative, not noise**.

### 1.4 Three Fundamental Types of Outliers

| # | Type | Definition | Example |
|---|------|------------|---------|
| 1 | **Global Outlier (Point Anomaly)** | A data point that deviates significantly from the **entire dataset** | A salary of ₹10 crore in a dataset of average salaries ₹5–10 LPA |
| 2 | **Contextual Outlier (Conditional Anomaly)** | A data object considered abnormal in a **specific context** but normal otherwise | 30°C in **winter in Delhi** is an outlier, but 30°C in **summer** is normal |
| 3 | **Collective Outlier (Group Anomaly)** | A **collection** of related data points anomalous w.r.t. the entire dataset | A sudden pattern of multiple simultaneous network requests indicating a DDoS attack |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Global outliers are **point-level** anomalies, contextual outliers are **context-dependent**, and collective outliers are **group-level** phenomena requiring the joint behavior of multiple points.

### 1.5 Common Causes of Outliers

- **Data Quality Issues:** Measurement errors, data entry mistakes, sensor malfunctions
- **Sampling / Recording Errors:** Improper data collection, transcription errors
- **Genuine Variability / Rare Events:** Natural deviations in population (e.g., genius IQ scores)
- **Fraud or Malicious Activity:** Credit card fraud, network intrusions
- **Distribution Mis-specification:** Outliers that reveal the underlying distribution is non-Gaussian

## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Categorization of Outlier Detection Methods

Outlier detection methods are broadly classified into four families:

```
OUTLIER DETECTION METHODS
│
├── 1. STATISTICAL (Model-Based) METHODS
│   ├── Parametric (Z-Score, Grubbs' Test, T² Test)
│   └── Non-Parametric (Histogram, Kernel Density)
│
├── 2. DISTANCE-BASED METHODS
│   └── k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN) Approach
│
├── 3. DENSITY-BASED METHODS
│   └── Local Outlier Factor (LOF)
│
└── 4. CLUSTERING-BASED METHODS
    ├── DBSCAN-style residual analysis
    └── k-Means distance-based scoring
```

### 2.2 Method 1: Statistical (Model-Based) Approaches

**Core Idea:** Fit a statistical model to the data; points with **low probability** under the model are declared outliers.

#### 2.2.1 Z-Score (Standard Score) Method

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption:** The underlying data follows (or approximately follows) a **Gaussian (Normal) distribution**.

The Z-score measures how many standard deviations a data point $x$ is from the mean $\mu$:

$$Z(x_i) = \frac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}$$

**Decision Rule:**
- If $\vert Z(x_i) \vert > 3$ → **Outlier** (using 3-$\sigma$ rule, covers 99.7% of normal data)
- If $\vert Z(x_i) \vert > 2$ → **Possible Outlier** (using 2-$\sigma$ rule, covers 95%)

#### 2.2.2 Modified Z-Score (Robust to Outliers)

For datasets that may already contain outliers (which distort the mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$), the **Median Absolute Deviation (MAD)** is used:

$$\text{MAD} = \text{median}(\vert x_i - \text{median}(X) \vert)$$

$$\text{Modified Z-Score} = \frac{0.6745 \cdot (x_i - \tilde{x})}{\text{MAD}}$$

where $\tilde{x}$ is the median, and **0.6745** is the 75th percentile of the standard normal distribution.

#### 2.2.3 IQR (Interquartile Range) Method — Non-Parametric

$$\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1$$

**Outlier Bounds (Tukey's Fences):**
$$\text{Lower Fence} = Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}$$
$$\text{Upper Fence} = Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}$$

**Extreme Outliers:** Beyond $Q_3 + 3 \cdot \text{IQR}$ or below $Q_1 - 3 \cdot \text{IQR}$.

#### 2.2.4 Grubbs' Test (Statistical Hypothesis Testing)

$$G = \frac{\max \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert}{s}$$

where $\bar{x}$ is the sample mean and $s$ is the sample standard deviation.

> **Decision:** If $G > G_{\text{critical}}$ (from Grubbs' table) → reject null hypothesis → **outlier present**.

### 2.3 Method 2: Distance-Based Outlier Detection (Knorr & Ng, 1998)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (Distance-Based Outlier):**  
> An object $o$ in a dataset $D$ is a **DB(p, D)-outlier** if at least a fraction $p$ of the objects in $D$ lie at a distance **greater than D** from $o$.

**Algorithm Logic (k-NN based):**
1. For each data point $o$, compute the distance to its $k$-th nearest neighbor: $d_k(o)$
2. Compute the **outlier score** as: $\text{score}(o) = d_k(o)$
3. Rank points by score; top-ranked points are outliers

**Limitation:** Suffers from the **"curse of dimensionality"** and assumes uniform density.

### 2.4 Method 3: Density-Based Methods — LOF (Local Outlier Factor)

> [!NOTE]
> **Proposed by:** Markus M. Breunig, Hans-Peter Kriegel, Raymond T. Ng, Jörg Sander (SIGMOD 2000).  
> **Key Advantage:** Handles **varying density regions** of the dataset.

**Step 1: k-Distance of object $p$** = distance from $p$ to its $k$-th nearest neighbor, denoted as $d_k(p)$.

**Step 2: Reachability Distance of $p$ w.r.t. object $o$:**

$$\text{reach-dist}_k(p, o) = \max\{ d_k(o), \text{dist}(p, o) \}$$

**Step 3: Local Reachability Density (LRD) of $p$:**

$$\text{LRD}_k(p) = \frac{\vert N_k(p) \vert}{\sum_{o \in N_k(p)} \text{reach-dist}_k(p, o)}$$

where $N_k(p)$ is the set of $k$-nearest neighbors of $p$.

**Step 4: Local Outlier Factor of $p$:**

$$\text{LOF}_k(p) = \frac{1}{\vert N_k(p) \vert} \sum_{o \in N_k(p)} \frac{\text{LRD}_k(o)}{\text{LRD}_k(p)}$$

> **Decision Rule:**
> - $\text{LOF}_k(p) \approx 1$ → $p$ is **inlier** (similar density to neighbors)
> - $\text{LOF}_k(p) \gg 1$ → $p$ is **outlier** (much lower density than neighbors)
> - $\text{LOF}_k(p) \ll 1$ → $p$ is in a **dense region**

### 2.5 Method 4: Clustering-Based Outlier Detection

**Core Idea:** Points that **do not belong strongly** to any cluster are considered outliers.

**Approaches:**
- **DBSCAN-based:** Points labeled as "noise" by DBSCAN are outliers.
- **k-Means-based:** Compute the distance from each point to its nearest cluster centroid. Points with **large distances** are outliers.
- **Cluster-based local outlier factor (CBLOF):** Identifies small clusters far from large clusters as outliers.

### 2.6 KTU 2024 HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET

| # | Method | Key Formula | Decision Threshold | Use Case |
|---|--------|-------------|--------------------|----------|
| 1 | Z-Score | $Z = (x - \mu)/\sigma$ | $\vert Z \vert > 3$ | Univariate Gaussian data |
| 2 | Modified Z-Score | $0.6745(x - \tilde{x})/\text{MAD}$ | $\vert M_i \vert > 3.5$ | Robust to outliers |
| 3 | IQR Method | $Q_3 - Q_1$ | Beyond $Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}$ | Non-parametric |
| 4 | Grubbs' Test | $G = \max \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert / s$ | $G > G_{\text{critical}}$ | Single outlier detection |
| 5 | k-NN Distance | $d_k(o)$ | $d_k(o) > D$ threshold | Distance-based |
| 6 | LOF | $\text{LRD}(o) / \text{LRD}(p)$ average | $\text{LOF} \gg 1$ | Variable density regions |
| 7 | DBSCAN Noise | Border vs. core points | "Noise" label | Spatial outlier detection |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

> **Domain-wise Use Cases (KTU-Relevant):**
> 1. **Cybersecurity:** Intrusion detection systems (IDS) identify anomalous network packets.
> 2. **Healthcare:** ECG/EEG anomaly detection for cardiac arrest prediction.
> 3. **Finance:** Credit card fraud, stock market manipulation detection.
> 4. **IoT / Smart Manufacturing:** Sensor fault detection in Industry 4.0 environments.
> 5. **Telecommunications:** Detecting unusual call patterns (fraudulent SIM usage).

## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Comprehensive Worked Example: Z-Score Outlier Detection

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** Consider a univariate dataset of 7 exam scores: $\{ 72, 85, 90, 78, 88, 92, 145 \}$. Detect the outlier using the Z-Score method.

#### Step 1: Compute the Arithmetic Mean ($\mu$)

$$\mu = \frac{72 + 85 + 90 + 78 + 88 + 92 + 145}{7}$$

$$\mu = \frac{650}{7}$$

$$\mu \approx 92.857$$

#### Step 2: Compute the Standard Deviation ($\sigma$)

First, compute squared deviations:

$$\begin{aligned}
(72 - 92.857)^2 &= (-20.857)^2 \approx 435.020 \\
(85 - 92.857)^2 &= (-7.857)^2 \approx 61.737 \\
(90 - 92.857)^2 &= (-2.857)^2 \approx 8.163 \\
(78 - 92.857)^2 &= (-14.857)^2 \approx 220.735 \\
(88 - 92.857)^2 &= (-4.857)^2 \approx 23.592 \\
(92 - 92.857)^2 &= (-0.857)^2 \approx 0.735 \\
(145 - 92.857)^2 &= (52.143)^2 \approx 2718.892
\end{aligned}$$

Sum of squared deviations:

$$SS = 435.020 + 61.737 + 8.163 + 220.735 + 23.592 + 0.735 + 2718.892 \approx 3468.874$$

Variance (using $n$ for population std, as is common in Z-score):

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{SS}{n} = \frac{3468.874}{7} \approx 495.553$$

Standard deviation:

$$\sigma = \sqrt{495.553} \approx 22.262$$

#### Step 3: Compute Z-Scores for Each Data Point

$$\begin{aligned}
Z(72) &= (72 - 92.857) / 22.262 \approx -0.937 \\
Z(85) &= (85 - 92.857) / 22.262 \approx -0.353 \\
Z(90) &= (90 - 92.857) / 22.262 \approx -0.128 \\
Z(78) &= (78 - 92.857) / 22.262 \approx -0.667 \\
Z(88) &= (88 - 92.857) / 22.262 \approx -0.218 \\
Z(92) &= (92 - 92.857) / 22.262 \approx -0.039 \\
Z(145) &= (145 - 92.857) / 22.262 \approx +2.343
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 4: Apply Decision Rule

Using the **2-σ rule** (threshold = 2) and **3-σ rule** (threshold = 3):

| Score | Z-Value | $\vert Z \vert > 2$? | $\vert Z \vert > 3$? | Verdict |
|-------|---------|----------------------|----------------------|---------|
| 72 | -0.937 | No | No | Inlier |
| 85 | -0.353 | No | No | Inlier |
| 90 | -0.128 | No | No | Inlier |
| 78 | -0.667 | No | No | Inlier |
| 88 | -0.218 | No | No | Inlier |
| 92 | -0.039 | No | No | Inlier |
| **145** | **+2.343** | **Yes (2-σ rule)** | No (3-σ rule) | **Probable Outlier** |

> **Conclusion:** Score **145** is flagged as a **probable outlier** under the 2-σ rule, as it deviates more than 2 standard deviations above the mean.

---

### 3.2 Comprehensive Worked Example: LOF Computation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** Compute the LOF for a 1-D dataset (1D = simple distance = absolute difference). Let $X = \{1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3, 4, 4, 5, 100\}$ and $k = 3$.

**Step 1: Identify 3-Nearest Neighbors and 3-Distances**

For each point, find the 3 closest points and the 3rd-nearest distance $d_3$:

| Point $p$ | 3-NN | $d_3(p)$ |
|-----------|------|----------|
| 1 | {2, 2, 3} | 2 |
| 2 | {1, 2, 3} | 1 |
| 3 | {2, 3, 4} | 1 |
| 4 | {3, 4, 5} | 1 |
| 5 | {4, 4, 3} | 1 |
| **100** | {5, 4, 4} | 95 |

**Step 2: Compute Reachability Distances**

For a point $p$ and neighbor $o$: $\text{reach-dist}_k(p,o) = \max\{ d_k(o), \vert p - o \vert \}$

**Step 3: Compute LRD**

$$\text{LRD}(p) = \frac{k}{\sum_{o \in N_k(p)} \text{reach-dist}_k(p, o)}$$

**Step 4: Compute LOF for $p = 100$**

For $p = 100$, $N_3(100) = \{5, 4, 4\}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{reach-dist}_3(100, 5) &= \max\{d_3(5), \vert 100 - 5 \vert\} = \max\{1, 95\} = 95 \\
\text{reach-dist}_3(100, 4) &= \max\{d_3(4), \vert 100 - 4 \vert\} = \max\{1, 96\} = 96 \\
\text{reach-dist}_3(100, 4) &= \max\{d_3(4), \vert 100 - 4 \vert\} = \max\{1, 96\} = 96
\end{aligned}$$

Sum of reachability distances = $95 + 96 + 96 = 287$.

$$\text{LRD}_3(100) = \frac{3}{287} \approx 0.01045$$

For each neighbor (using similar LRD calculation for points 4 and 5):

$$\text{LRD}_3(4) \approx \frac{3}{\text{sum reach-dists}} \approx \frac{3}{3} = 1.0$$
$$\text{LRD}_3(5) \approx \frac{3}{3} = 1.0$$

**Final LOF Calculation for $p = 100$:**

$$\text{LOF}_3(100) = \frac{1}{3} \left[ \frac{\text{LRD}(5)}{\text{LRD}(100)} + \frac{\text{LRD}(4)}{\text{LRD}(100)} + \frac{\text{LRD}(4)}{\text{LRD}(100)} \right]$$

$$\text{LOF}_3(100) = \frac{1}{3} \left[ \frac{1.0}{0.01045} + \frac{1.0}{0.01045} + \frac{1.0}{0.01045} \right]$$

$$\text{LOF}_3(100) = \frac{1}{3} \times 3 \times 95.69 \approx 95.69$$

> **Verdict:** $\text{LOF}_3(100) = 95.69 \gg 1$ → **Point 100 is a strong outlier**, as expected.

---

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix of Methods

| Property | Statistical | Distance-Based | Density-Based (LOF) | Clustering-Based |
|----------|-------------|----------------|---------------------|------------------|
| **Assumes Distribution** | Yes (often Gaussian) | No | No | No |
| **Handles High Dimensionality** | Poor | Poor | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Handles Variable Density** | No | No | **Yes (Strength)** | Limited |
| **Computational Cost** | Low | High ($O(n^2)$) | High ($O(n \log n)$) | Moderate |
| **Global vs. Local** | Global | Global | **Local** | Global |
| **Univariate Support** | Excellent | Limited | Limited | Limited |
| **Multivariate Support** | Limited | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| **Interpretability** | High | Medium | Low | Medium |
