## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**  
Define an outlier. List and briefly explain its three main types.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **outlier** is a data object that significantly deviates from the rest of the dataset in terms of its attribute values, behavioral pattern, or position in the feature space.

**Three Types:**

1. **Global Outlier (Point Anomaly):** A single data point that is anomalous with respect to the *entire* dataset. Example: A person's height of 250 cm in a dataset of adult heights.

2. **Contextual Outlier (Conditional Anomaly):** A data point anomalous in a *specific context* but otherwise normal. Example: 30°C is normal in summer but anomalous in winter.

3. **Collective Outlier (Group Anomaly):** A *group* of related data points anomalous when considered together. Example: A sudden simultaneous surge in multiple network requests indicating a DDoS attack.

*(Marking Scheme: Definition = 1 mark, each type with example ≈ 0.5 + 0.5 = 1 mark each.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**  
Explain the **Z-Score method** for outlier detection. State its key assumption.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** The Z-Score method measures the number of standard deviations a data point $x_i$ is away from the mean $\mu$:

$$Z(x_i) = \frac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}$$

**Decision Rule:** If $\vert Z(x_i) \vert > 3$, the point is flagged as an outlier (3-σ rule, covering 99.7% of normally distributed data).

**Key Assumption:** The underlying data follows a **Gaussian (Normal) distribution**. If this assumption is violated, the method produces unreliable results.

**Limitation:** Sensitive to outliers themselves (since mean and std. dev. are affected by extreme values); the **Modified Z-Score using MAD** is preferred for robustness.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the four major categories of outlier detection methods in data mining. Compare their strengths and weaknesses. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the dataset $D = \{10, 12, 14, 15, 16, 18, 19, 20, 22, 80\}$, detect outliers using the **IQR method**. Show all calculations clearly. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Seven-Mark Model Answer

The four major categories of outlier detection methods are:

**1. Statistical (Model-Based) Methods:**  
Fit a probabilistic/statistical model to the data. Points with low probability under the model are outliers.  
*Examples:* Z-Score, Grubbs' test, MAD-based methods.  
*Strength:* Theoretically rigorous, simple for univariate data.  
*Weakness:* Requires assumption of underlying distribution; poor for multivariate/high-dimensional data.

**2. Distance-Based Methods:**  
A point is an outlier if it is far (in terms of Euclidean or other distance) from a sufficient fraction of the dataset.  
*Example:* k-Nearest Neighbors distance.  
*Strength:* Non-parametric, applicable to multivariate data.  
*Weakness:* Computationally expensive $O(n^2)$; assumes uniform density; suffers from curse of dimensionality.

**3. Density-Based Methods:**  
Compare the local density around a point with the density around its neighbors. Points in significantly lower-density regions are outliers.  
*Example:* Local Outlier Factor (LOF).  
*Strength:* Handles variable density regions effectively.  
*Weakness:* Choice of parameter $k$ affects results; computationally expensive.

**4. Clustering-Based Methods:**  
Identify points that do not belong strongly to any cluster as outliers.  
*Examples:* DBSCAN noise points, k-Means distance-based scoring.  
*Strength:* Works for multivariate data; can leverage existing clustering algorithms.  
*Weakness:* Inherits limitations of the underlying clustering algorithm; sensitive to parameter choice.

---

#### Part (b) — Seven-Mark Model Solution (IQR Method)

**Step 1: Sort the dataset (already sorted):**  
$D = \{10, 12, 14, 15, 16, 18, 19, 20, 22, 80\}$, $n = 10$

**Step 2: Compute Quartiles**

For $n = 10$ (even), the data is split into two halves of 5 elements each:
- Lower half: $\{10, 12, 14, 15, 16\}$
- Upper half: $\{18, 19, 20, 22, 80\}$

Median (Q2) is the average of the 5th and 6th values:

$$Q_2 = \frac{16 + 18}{2} = 17$$

$Q_1$ = median of lower half = middle value of $\{10, 12, 14, 15, 16\}$ = **14**

$Q_3$ = median of upper half = middle value of $\{18, 19, 20, 22, 80\}$ = **20**

**Step 3: Compute IQR**

$$\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1 = 20 - 14 = 6$$

**Step 4: Compute Tukey's Fences**

$$\text{Lower Fence} = Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR} = 14 - 1.5 \cdot 6 = 14 - 9 = 5$$

$$\text{Upper Fence} = Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR} = 20 + 1.5 \cdot 6 = 20 + 9 = 29$$

**Step 5: Identify Outliers**

Any value $< 5$ or $> 29$ is an outlier.

Checking all data points:

| Value | Verdict |
|-------|---------|
| 10, 12, 14, 15, 16, 18, 19, 20, 22 | All within [5, 29] → **Inliers** |
| **80** | 80 > 29 → **Outlier** |

**Conclusion:** The value **80** is a **mild outlier** (within $3 \cdot \text{IQR} = 38$ of $Q_3$, so not an *extreme* outlier by the $3 \cdot \text{IQR}$ rule).

**Extreme Fence Check:**  
$Q_3 + 3 \cdot \text{IQR} = 20 + 18 = 38$  
Since 80 > 38, value 80 is also an **extreme outlier** by the $3 \cdot \text{IQR}$ rule.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]**

**(a)** What is the **Local Outlier Factor (LOF)**? Explain with the mathematical formulation. Discuss how LOF handles variable density regions better than distance-based methods. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the **k-NN distance-based outlier detection** method. What is its main limitation? **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Seven-Mark Model Answer

**Definition of LOF:**  
The **Local Outlier Factor (LOF)** is a density-based outlier detection algorithm that assigns an **outlier score** to each data point based on how isolated it is relative to its local neighborhood. A score significantly greater than 1 indicates an outlier.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

1. **k-Distance of $p$:** $d_k(p)$ = distance from $p$ to its $k$-th nearest neighbor.

2. **Reachability Distance:**

$$\text{reach-dist}_k(p, o) = \max\{d_k(o), \text{dist}(p, o)\}$$

3. **Local Reachability Density (LRD):**

$$\text{LRD}_k(p) = \frac{\vert N_k(p) \vert}{\sum_{o \in N_k(p)} \text{reach-dist}_k(p, o)}$$

4. **Local Outlier Factor (LOF):**

$$\text{LOF}_k(p) = \frac{1}{\vert N_k(p) \vert} \sum_{o \in N_k(p)} \frac{\text{LRD}_k(o)}{\text{LRD}_k(p)}$$

**Interpretation:**
- $\text{LOF}(p) \approx 1$ → $p$ has density similar to neighbors → **inlier**
- $\text{LOF}(p) \gg 1$ → $p$ has much lower density than neighbors → **outlier**
- $\text{LOF}(p) \ll 1$ → $p$ is in a dense region surrounded by sparse neighbors

**Advantage over Distance-Based Methods:**  
In distance-based methods (e.g., k-NN distance), a point in a sparse but uniformly sparse region is *incorrectly* flagged as an outlier. LOF overcomes this by comparing **local densities relatively** — a point is an outlier only if its density is *significantly lower than its immediate neighbors' densities*, making it robust to global density variations.

#### Part (b) — Seven-Mark Model Answer

**k-NN Distance-Based Outlier Detection — Example:**

Consider a 2-D dataset with points clustered in two regions.  
- Cluster A: dense cluster around (0, 0)  
- Cluster B: sparse cluster around (10, 10)  
- Anomalous point P: at (50, 50)

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Choose parameter $k$ (e.g., $k = 3$)
2. For every point $o$ in the dataset, find its $k$-nearest neighbors
3. Compute $d_k(o)$ = distance to the $k$-th nearest neighbor
4. Rank all points in descending order of $d_k$
5. The top-ranked points (with highest $d_k$) are flagged as outliers

**For the Example:**
- For a point deep inside Cluster A: $d_3 \approx 0.3$ (inlier)
- For a point in sparse Cluster B: $d_3 \approx 1.5$ (not an outlier — part of cluster)
- For point P at (50, 50): $d_3 = 50\sqrt{2} \approx 70.7$ (clear outlier)

**Main Limitations:**
1. **Sensitive to the choice of $k$:** Different $k$ values yield different outliers.
2. **Curse of Dimensionality:** Distance becomes less meaningful in high-dimensional spaces.
3. **Cannot handle variable density:** A point in a sparse region may be incorrectly flagged.
4. **Computational Cost:** $O(n^2)$ naive, requiring indexing (e.g., KD-trees) for efficiency.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MARKS-LOSS PITFALLS (KTU Board Pattern):**
> 1. **Confusing MAD with Standard Deviation:** Students often forget that the **Modified Z-Score uses MAD (Median Absolute Deviation)**, not standard deviation. This causes a 1–2 mark loss.
> 2. **Forgetting to state the assumption of Z-Score method:** You must explicitly mention the **Gaussian distribution assumption**. Marks are awarded for stating assumptions, not just formulas.
> 3. **Not justifying "Why an outlier" in IQR problems:** Don't just compute the fences — explicitly compare each data point against the fences and state "since 80 > 29, it is an outlier."
> 4. **Misinterpreting LOF values:** $\text{LOF} < 1$ does **not** mean outlier; it means the point is in a *denser* region than its neighbors. **Only $\text{LOF} \gg 1$ indicates an outlier.**
> 5. **Mixing up clustering vs. density-based methods:** DBSCAN is **density-based clustering**, while LOF is **density-based outlier scoring**. They differ in output (cluster labels vs. scores).
> 6. **Skipping the "real-world context":** KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes application-oriented answers. Always include **at least one engineering application** (e.g., fraud detection, IoT sensor monitoring) in long answers.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — OUTLIER ANALYSIS**

- [x] **Definition:** Outlier = data point significantly deviating from the dataset's norm.
- [x] **Three Types:** Global (point), Contextual (conditional), Collective (group).
- [x] **Z-Score formula:** $Z = (x - \mu)/\sigma$; threshold = 3 (3-σ rule).
- [x] **Modified Z-Score:** Uses MAD instead of $\sigma$; threshold = 3.5; constant 0.6745.
- [x] **IQR Method:** Fences = $Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}$ and $Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}$.
- [x] **Grubbs' Test:** Used for **single outlier** detection in small samples.
- [x] **k-NN Distance:** $\text{score}(o) = d_k(o)$; top scores = outliers.
- [x] **LOF Formula:** Ratio of average LRD of neighbors to LRD of point itself; $\text{LOF} > 1$ = outlier.
- [x] **Reachability Distance:** $\max\{d_k(o), \text{dist}(p, o)\}$ — ensures smoothing.
- [x] **LOF Advantage:** Handles **variable density** regions correctly.
- [x] **DBSCAN Noise:** Points labeled "noise" are outliers in spatial data.
- [x] **CBLOF:** Cluster-Based Local Outlier Factor — for high-dimensional data.
- [x] **Applications:** Fraud detection, network intrusion, IoT fault detection, medical anomaly detection.
- [x] **High-yield trick for 14-mark questions:** Always state **assumption + formula + worked example + application** for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification and Prediction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT604 - DATA MINING AND WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Classification and Prediction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Classification?

**Classification** is a data mining technique (a form of *supervised learning*) used to predict the **discrete categorical class label** of a new, unseen data instance, based on a model built from a training set of instances whose class labels are already known.

Formally, given a training dataset $D$ consisting of tuples $(X, y)$, where $X = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ is an $n$-dimensional attribute vector and $y$ is the discrete class label, classification seeks to learn a *classification function* or *model* $f: X \rightarrow y$ that maps each attribute set $X$ to one of the predefined class labels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Classification is the process of finding a model (or function) that describes and distinguishes data classes or concepts, for the purpose of being able to use the model to predict the class of objects whose class label is unknown.

**Classification vs. Prediction — The Critical Distinction:**

| Aspect | Classification | Prediction |
|---|---|---|
| **Output type** | Discrete / Categorical (e.g., Yes/No, Spam/Not Spam) | Continuous / Numeric (e.g., salary = ₹45,000) |
| **Technique base** | Decision trees, Bayes, Rule-based, SVM | Regression (Linear, Non-linear) |
| **Goal** | Predict class membership | Forecast numeric trend/value |

### 1.2 What is Prediction?

**Prediction** is the process of estimating the value of a *continuous-valued* attribute for an unknown object. When prediction is restricted to *numeric* estimation, it is technically called **regression**. The model constructed can be used to predict unknown/future values of the target variable.

### 1.3 The Two-Stage Supervised Learning Pipeline

Classification is a classic **two-stage process**:

**Stage 1 — Learning (Training Phase):**
- A classifier is built from a training set consisting of database tuples and their associated class labels.
- Each tuple/sample is assumed to belong to a predefined class (determined by the *class label attribute*).
- The learned model is represented as classification rules, decision trees, mathematical formulae, or neural-network weights.

**Stage 2 — Classification (Testing/Application Phase):**
- The model is first tested for accuracy on a held-out *test set* (independent of training set).
- If accuracy is acceptable, the model is used to classify future data tuples whose class label is unknown.

### 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **🩺 Analogy — The Doctor's Diagnostic Process:**
> Think of classification as a doctor's diagnostic workflow. A doctor *learns* by observing thousands of past patients: their symptoms (fever, cough, blood-test values = **attributes**) and final confirmed disease (Flu, COVID, Malaria = **class labels**). After years of practice, the doctor has built an internal "mental model." When a *new* patient walks in with symptoms, the doctor **predicts the class** (diagnosis) without any explicit lab result.
>
> - **Training set** = the doctor's past patient records.
> - **Model** = the diagnostic reasoning pattern in the doctor's mind.
> - **Test set** = recent patients used to verify the doctor's accuracy.
> - **Class label** = the diagnosed disease.
>
> *Prediction* is the same doctor now estimating *how many days* the fever will last — a **continuous** number rather than a discrete label.

### 1.5 Why Do We Need Classification?

- **Direct business decisions:** Approve/deny credit card, flag fraudulent transactions.
- **Medical diagnosis:** Disease identification from symptoms.
- **Targeted marketing:** Classify customers likely to buy a product.
- **Risk assessment:** Predict whether a loan will default.
- **Pattern recognition:** Spam detection, image classification, sentiment analysis.

### 1.6 Applications of Classification & Prediction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Applications for KTU:**
> 1. **Credit Approval** — classify applicants as *low/medium/high risk*.
> 2. **Fraud Detection** — flag transactions as *legitimate/suspicious*.
> 3. **Medical Diagnosis** — classify tumors as *benign/malignant*.
> 4. **Spam Filtering** — classify emails as *spam/ham*.
> 5. **Weather Prediction** — predict *temperature/rainfall* (continuous).
> 6. **Sales Forecasting** — predict *future revenue* (continuous).

### 1.7 Issues in Classification (KTU Frequently Tested)

1. **Data preparation** — cleaning, relevance analysis, handling missing values, data transformation.
2. **Evaluating classification methods** — accuracy, speed, robustness, scalability, interpretability.
3. **Comparing classifiers** — statistical significance of performance differences.
4. **Handling the imbalanced class problem** — skewed class distribution in training data.
5. **Missing data** — incomplete attribute values.
6. **Classifying large databases** — scalability for terabytes of data.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decision Tree Classification — The Workhorse Model

A **decision tree** is a flowchart-like tree structure where:
- **Internal node** = test on an attribute.
- **Branch** = outcome of the test.
- **Leaf node** = class label (decision).
- **Root node** = topmost node (best attribute selected first).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Algorithms:** The two most important decision tree algorithms are **ID3** (uses *Information Gain*) and **C4.5** (successor of ID3, uses *Gain Ratio*). Also tested: **CART** (uses *Gini Index*).

### 2.2 Attribute Selection Measures — The Heart of Decision Trees

A decision tree algorithm must decide which attribute to split on at each node. The three principal **Attribute Selection Measures (ASM)** are:

#### (A) Information Gain (used by ID3)

**Information Theory Foundation** — proposed by **Claude Shannon (1948)**.

**Entropy** measures the *impurity* (or disorder) in a collection $S$ of examples:

$$\text{Info}(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{m} p_i \log_2(p_i)$$

where $p_i$ is the probability (proportion) that an arbitrary tuple in $S$ belongs to class $C_i$, and $m$ is the number of classes.

The expected information required to classify a tuple in $S$ where attribute $A$ has been partitioned into $v$ distinct subsets:

$$\text{Info}_A(S) = \sum_{j=1}^{v} \frac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert} \times \text{Info}(S_j)$$

The **information gained** by branching on attribute $A$:

$$\text{Gain}(A) = \text{Info}(S) - \text{Info}_A(S)$$

The attribute with the **highest Information Gain** is chosen as the splitting attribute.

#### (B) Gain Ratio (used by C4.5) — Normalised Information Gain

$$\text{GainRatio}(A) = \frac{\text{Gain}(A)}{\text{SplitInfo}_A(S)}$$

where the *Split Information* is:

$$\text{SplitInfo}_A(S) = -\sum_{j=1}^{v} \frac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2\!\left(\frac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> Gain Ratio overcomes the bias of Information Gain toward attributes with many values (e.g., *date*, *ID*).

#### (C) Gini Index (used by CART)

**Gini impurity** for a set $S$:

$$\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{m} p_i^2$$

Gini of a split on attribute $A$:

$$\text{Gini}_A(S) = \sum_{j=1}^{v} \frac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert} \times \text{Gini}(S_j)$$

The attribute that provides the **smallest $\text{Gini}_A(S)$** is chosen as the splitting attribute (i.e., lowest impurity).

### 2.3 Bayes Classification — The Probabilistic Champion

#### Bayes' Theorem (Foundational Law)

$$P(H \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(X)}$$

where:
- $P(H \mid X)$ = **posterior** probability of hypothesis $H$ given evidence $X$.
- $P(H)$ = **prior** probability of hypothesis $H$.
- $P(X \mid H)$ = **likelihood** — probability of evidence $X$ given $H$.
- $P(X)$ = **marginal** probability of evidence $X$.

#### Naive Bayes Classification

The naive assumption: **class-conditional independence** — assume that the effect of an attribute value on a given class is *independent* of the values of the other attributes.

For $n$ attributes $(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$:

$$P(C_i \mid X) = P(X \mid C_i) \cdot P(C_i) = P(C_i) \prod_{k=1}^{n} P(x_k \mid C_i)$$

The classifier predicts the class $C_i$ that **maximises** the posterior:

$$C_{\text{predicted}} = \arg\max_{C_i} \; P(C_i) \prod_{k=1}^{n} P(x_k \mid C_i)$$

### 2.4 Prediction via Regression

#### Linear Regression

Models the relationship as a straight line:

$$y = w_0 + w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + \dots + w_n x_n + \varepsilon$$

The coefficients $w_i$ are typically estimated by **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)** — minimising the *sum of squared errors*:

$$\text{SSE} = \sum_{j=1}^{N} (y_j - \hat{y}_j)^2 = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \left(y_j - w_0 - \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_{ji}\right)^2$$

#### Non-Linear Regression

For relationships that are not linear (polynomial, exponential, logarithmic), we transform variables or use non-linear least squares to fit curves like $y = a e^{bx}$ or polynomial regressions of degree $k$.

### 2.5 Classifier Evaluation Metrics — The KTU Formula Sheet

| Metric | Formula | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall correct predictions |
| **Error Rate** | $\text{Error} = 1 - \text{Acc} = \dfrac{FP + FN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Misclassification rate |
| **Precision** | $\text{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Quality of positive predictions |
| **Recall (Sensitivity)** | $\text{Recall} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Coverage of actual positives |
| **F-measure (F1)** | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$ | Harmonic mean of P & R |
| **Specificity** | $\text{Spec} = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | True-negative rate |
| **Geometric Mean** | $g = \sqrt{\text{Sensitivity} \cdot \text{Specificity}}$ | Balanced measure for imbalanced data |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** For imbalanced datasets, **accuracy is misleading** — always prefer F-measure, Geometric Mean, or ROC-AUC. A model with 99% accuracy can be useless if the positive class is only 1% of data and the model simply predicts "negative" for everything.

### 2.6 Ensemble Methods — Bagging and Boosting

| Method | Strategy | Algorithm Example | Idea |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bagging** (Bootstrap AGGregatING) | Train $k$ models on $k$ bootstrapped samples, average/vote | Random Forest | Reduces **variance** |
| **Boosting** | Train sequentially, focus on misclassified | AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting | Reduces **bias** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Random Forest** = an ensemble of decision trees trained on bootstrapped samples with **random feature subsets** at each split. Final prediction by **majority voting** (classification) or **averaging** (regression).

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Quick-Reference Table)

| Concept | Key Formula |
|---|---|
| Entropy | $\text{Info}(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{m} p_i \log_2 p_i$ |
| Information Gain | $\text{Gain}(A) = \text{Info}(S) - \text{Info}_A(S)$ |
| Split Information | $\text{SplitInfo}(A) = -\sum \frac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \frac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ |
| Gain Ratio | $\text{GR}(A) = \text{Gain}(A) / \text{SplitInfo}(A)$ |
| Gini Impurity | $\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum p_i^2$ |
| Bayes Theorem | $P(H \mid X) = P(X \mid H) P(H) / P(X)$ |
| Naive Bayes | $C = \arg\max_{C_i} P(C_i) \prod P(x_k \mid C_i)$ |
| Linear Regression | $y = w_0 + w_1 x_1 + \dots + w_n x_n$ |
| OLS Objective | $\min \sum (y_j - \hat{y}_j)^2$ |
| Accuracy | $(TP + TN) / N$ |
| Precision | $TP / (TP + FP)$ |
| Recall | $TP / (TP + FN)$ |
| F1-Score | $2 P R / (P + R)$ |

> All formulas use $\vert \cdot \vert$ for cardinality (set size), not absolute value.

### 2.8 Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare:** Decision trees for interpretable diagnosis; Naive Bayes for fast text classification of clinical notes.
- **Finance:** Credit scoring using logistic/linear regression; fraud detection using ensemble boosting.
- **Cybersecurity:** Naive Bayes email spam filters.
- **Marketing:** Churn prediction with decision trees + boosting.
- **Manufacturing:** Quality-control classification (defective / non-defective) using Random Forest on sensor data.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Building a Decision Tree with Information Gain

**Training Set** (PlayTennis — adapted from Mitchell's classic):

| Day | Outlook | Temperature | Humidity | Wind | PlayTennis |
|-----|---------|-------------|----------|------|------------|
| D1  | Sunny   | Hot         | High     | Weak | No         |
| D2  | Sunny   | Hot         | High     | Strong | No       |
| D3  | Overcast| Hot         | High     | Weak | Yes        |
| D4  | Rain    | Mild        | High     | Weak | Yes        |
| D5  | Rain    | Cool        | Normal   | Weak | Yes        |
| D6  | Rain    | Cool        | Normal   | Strong | No       |
| D7  | Overcast| Cool        | Normal   | Strong | Yes        |
| D8  | Sunny   | Mild        | High     | Weak | No         |
| D9  | Sunny   | Cool        | Normal   | Weak | Yes        |
| D10 | Rain    | Mild        | Normal   | Weak | Yes        |
| D11 | Sunny   | Mild        | Normal   | Strong | Yes      |
| D12 | Overcast| Mild        | High     | Strong | Yes      |
| D13 | Overcast| Hot         | Normal   | Weak | Yes        |
| D14 | Rain    | Mild        | High     | Strong | No        |

> Total tuples $\vert S \vert = 14$. Class counts: **Yes = 9**, **No = 5**.

#### Step 1: Compute the base entropy $\text{Info}(S)$

$$\text{Info}(S) = -\frac{9}{14}\log_2\frac{9}{14} - \frac{5}{14}\log_2\frac{5}{14}$$

Computing each term:

$$\frac{9}{14}\log_2\frac{9}{14} = 0.6429 \times (-0.6375) = -0.4101$$

$$\frac{5}{14}\log_2\frac{5}{14} = 0.3571 \times (-1.4854) = -0.5304$$

Therefore:

$$\text{Info}(S) = -(-0.4101) - (-0.5304) = 0.4101 + 0.5304 = 0.940 \; \text{bits}$$

#### Step 2: Compute information required for each candidate attribute

**Attribute: Outlook** (with 3 subsets — Sunny, Overcast, Rain)

*Sunny subset (5 tuples: 2 Yes, 3 No):*

$$\text{Info}_{\text{Sunny}}(S) = -\frac{2}{5}\log_2\frac{2}{5} - \frac{3}{5}\log_2\frac{3}{5}$$

$$= -0.4 \times (-1.3219) - 0.6 \times (-0.7370) = 0.5288 + 0.4422 = 0.9710$$

*Overcast subset (4 tuples: 4 Yes, 0 No):*

$$\text{Info}_{\text{Overcast}}(S) = -\frac{4}{4}\log_2\frac{4}{4} - 0 = 0$$

*Rain subset (5 tuples: 3 Yes, 2 No):*

$$\text{Info}_{\text{Rain}}(S) = -\frac{3}{5}\log_2\frac{3}{5} - \frac{2}{5}\log_2\frac{2}{5}$$

$$= 0.4422 + 0.5288 = 0.9710$$

Weighted average:

$$\text{Info}_{\text{Outlook}}(S) = \frac{5}{14}(0.9710) + \frac{4}{14}(0) + \frac{5}{14}(0.9710)$$

$$= 0.3571 \times 0.9710 + 0 + 0.3571 \times 0.9710$$

$$= 0.3468 + 0.3468 = 0.693$$

Information gain for Outlook:

$$\text{Gain}(\text{Outlook}) = \text{Info}(S) - \text{Info}_{\text{Outlook}}(S) = 0.940 - 0.693 = 0.247 \; \text{bits}$$

> The attribute with the *highest* Gain is selected. Continuing this process for **Temperature**, **Humidity**, and **Wind** would yield a complete tree. The full classic computation confirms that **Outlook** has the highest gain at the root.

### 3.2 Naive Bayes — Worked Numerical Example

**Scenario:** Predict whether a customer will buy a computer (Yes / No) given:
- Age = **Youth**, Income = **Medium**, Student = **Yes**, Credit Rating = **Fair**.

**Given training data frequencies (fictitious standard example):**

| Class | Prior $P(C)$ |
|---|---|
| Buys = Yes | $9/15 = 0.6$ |
| Buys = No | $6/15 = 0.4$ |

**Conditional probabilities from training data:**

| Attribute Value | $P(\cdot \mid \text{Yes})$ | $P(\cdot \mid \text{No})$ |
|---|---|---|
| Age = Youth | $2/9 = 0.222$ | $3/6 = 0.500$ |
| Income = Medium | $4/9 = 0.444$ | $2/6 = 0.333$ |
| Student = Yes | $6/9 = 0.667$ | $1/6 = 0.167$ |
| Credit = Fair | $6/9 = 0.667$ | $2/6 = 0.333$ |

**Step 1: Compute numerator for class = Yes**

$$P(X \mid \text{Yes}) \cdot P(\text{Yes}) = 0.222 \times 0.444 \times 0.667 \times 0.667 \times 0.6$$

Computing step-by-step:

$$0.222 \times 0.444 = 0.0986$$

$$0.0986 \times 0.667 = 0.0657$$

$$0.0657 \times 0.667 = 0.0438$$

$$0.0438 \times 0.6 = 0.0263$$

**Step 2: Compute numerator for class = No**

$$P(X \mid \text{No}) \cdot P(\text{No}) = 0.500 \times 0.333 \times 0.167 \times 0.333 \times 0.4$$

Computing step-by-step:

$$0.500 \times 0.333 = 0.1667$$

$$0.1667 \times 0.167 = 0.0278$$

$$0.0278 \times 0.333 = 0.0093$$

$$0.0093 \times 0.4 = 0.0037$$

**Step 3: Normalise to obtain posterior probabilities**

$$P(\text{Yes} \mid X) = \frac{0.0263}{0.0263 + 0.0037} = \frac{0.0263}{0.0300} = 0.877$$

$$P(\text{No} \mid X) = \frac{0.0037}{0.0300} = 0.123$$

> **Prediction:** Since $0.877 > 0.123$, classify this customer as **"Will Buy = Yes"**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Laplace Smoothing** is required when a conditional probability becomes 0. Use $P(x_k \mid C_i) = (n_c + 1) / (n + m)$, where $m$ is the number of attribute values, to avoid zero-product collapse.

### 3.3 Confusion Matrix — Complete Analytical Walkthrough

**Hypothetical Test Set** of 1000 samples (950 actual "No", 50 actual "Yes"):

| Actual \ Predicted | Predicted Yes | Predicted No | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Actual Yes** | TP = 30 | FN = 20 | 50 |
| **Actual No** | FP = 10 | TN = 940 | 950 |
| **Total** | 40 | 960 | 1000 |

Compute every metric:

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{N} = \frac{30 + 940}{1000} = \frac{970}{1000} = 0.970$$

$$\text{Error Rate} = 1 - 0.970 = 0.030$$

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{30}{40} = 0.750$$

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{30}{50} = 0.600$$

$$\text{F1} = \frac{2 \times 0.750 \times 0.600}{0.750 + 0.600} = \frac{0.900}{1.350} = 0.667$$

> Observe: even with 97% accuracy, recall is only 60% — meaning 40% of sick patients are missed! This shows why accuracy is insufficient for imbalanced data.

### 3.4 Linear Regression — Solving by OLS (Simple Case)

For simple linear regression $y = w_0 + w_1 x$, the OLS solution is **closed-form**:

$$w_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$

$$w_0 = \bar{y} - w_1 \bar{x}$$

where $\bar{x}, \bar{y}$ are the sample means of $x$ and $y$. This minimises the SSE.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Normal Equation form for multiple linear regression is $W = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T Y$ — derive this by setting $\frac{\partial \text{SSE}}{\partial W} = 0$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification — End-to-End Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Training Data] --> B[Data Pre-processing]
    B --> B1[Data Cleaning]
    B --> B2[Relevance Analysis]
    B --> B3[Data Transformation]
    B1 --> C[Feature Selection]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[Algorithm Selection]
    D --> D1[Decision Tree ID3]
    D --> D2[Naive Bayes]
    D --> D3[Random Forest]
    D --> D4[SVM]
    C1[Training Set] --> E[Model Learning Phase]
    C2[Test Set] --> F[Model Testing Phase]
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> G[Trained Classifier Model]
    G --> F
    F --> H{Accuracy Acceptable?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Deploy Model for Real Time Classification]
    H -- No --> J[Refine Model / Try Different Algorithm]
    J --> D
    I --> K[Classify New Unknown Data]
    K --> L[Output Predicted Class Label]
```

### 4.2 Decision Tree Construction — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start With Full Training Set S at Root] --> P[Compute Entropy of S]
    P --> Q[For Each Attribute A Calculate Gain A]
    Q --> R[Select Attribute With Maximum Gain as Split]
    R --> T[Partition S Into Subsets by Attribute Values]
    T --> U{Is Subset Pure?}
    U -- Yes --> V[Assign Leaf Node as Majority Class]
    U -- No --> W[Recursively Apply Same Process to Subset]
    W --> Q
    V --> X[Complete Decision Tree]
```

### 4.3 Bayes Classification — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D[Training Data D] --> P1[Compute Prior P of Each Class]
    D --> L[Compute Likelihood P of X given Class]
    P1 --> M[Posterior Multiplier]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Argmax Class with Maximum Posterior]
    X[New Instance X] --> L2[Compute Likelihood for X given Each Class]
    L2 --> N
    N --> O[Predicted Class Label]
```

### 4.4 Ensemble Methods — Bagging vs Boosting Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Original Training Set S] --> B1[Bootstrap Sample 1]
    S --> B2[Bootstrap Sample 2]
    S --> B3[Bootstrap Sample 3]
    B1 --> M1[Model 1 e.g. Decision Tree]
    B2 --> M2[Model 2 e.g. Decision Tree]
    B3 --> M3[Model 3 e.g. Decision Tree]
    M1 --> V[Majority Voting Aggregator]
    M2 --> V
    M3 --> V
    V --> P1[Final Predicted Class]
    S2[Original Training Set] --> T1[Model 1 with Weights]
    T1 --> E1[Identify Misclassified Samples]
    E1 --> T2[Model 2 with Higher Weights on Errors]
    T2 --> E2[Identify Remaining Misclassified Samples]
    E2 --> T3[Model 3 Focus on Hard Cases]
    T3 --> W[Weighted Vote Combine]
    W --> P2[Final Predicted Class]
```

### 4.5 Confusion Matrix — Block View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TP["TP = Correctly predicted positive"] --> M[Performance Metrics]
    FP["FP = False alarm Type 1 error"] --> M
    FN["FN = Missed positive Type 2 error"] --> M
    TN["TN = Correctly predicted negative"] --> M
    M --> ACC[Accuracy]
    M --> PRC[Precision]
    M --> RCL[Recall]
    M --> FSC[F1 Score]
```

### 4.6 Regression Pipeline — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Historical Data] --> B[Select Predictor Variables]
    B --> C[Fit Linear or Non Linear Model]
    C --> D[Estimate Coefficients via OLS]
    D --> E[Validate Model on Test Data]
    E --> F{Meets Error Threshold?}
    F -- No --> C
    F -- Yes --> G[Use Model for Prediction]
    G --> H[Forecast Continuous Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU Exams:** Use these block diagrams to explain process flow. The examiner awards marks for clear **stage-wise reasoning** rather than artistic detail. Always label arrows with the *data* or *operation* being transferred.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Model Question Paper Style — Short Answer**

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Define classification and prediction. List any four issues that must be addressed during classification.**

**Model Answer:**

Classification is a data analysis process that finds a model describing and distinguishing data classes, used to predict the class label of new objects whose class is unknown. It produces **discrete categorical output**. Prediction estimates the value of a continuous-valued attribute (also called *regression*); it produces **continuous numeric output**.

Four key issues in classification:

1. **Data Pre-processing** — handling noise, missing values, irrelevant attributes.
2. **Evaluating classification methods** — predictive accuracy, speed, robustness, scalability, interpretability.
3. **Comparing classifiers** — statistical significance of performance differences (e.g., paired t-tests on k-fold CV results).
4. **Class imbalance / scalability** — handling skewed distributions and very large databases.

> [!NOTE]
> Other valid issues: overfitting, cost-sensitive classification, multi-class problems.

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Explain the Gini index used as an attribute selection measure in decision tree induction.**

**Model Answer:**

The Gini index measures the *impurity* of a dataset $S$ with $m$ classes:

$$\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{m} p_i^2$$

where $p_i$ is the relative frequency of class $C_i$ in $S$. For a candidate split on attribute $A$ with $v$ partitions, the weighted Gini is:

$$\text{Gini}_A(S) = \sum_{j=1}^{v} \frac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert} \times \text{Gini}(S_j)$$

The attribute that **minimises** $\text{Gini}_A(S)$ (i.e., produces the purest children) is chosen as the splitting attribute. Gini is used in the **CART** algorithm and is computationally faster than entropy since it avoids the logarithm.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the **ID3 algorithm** for decision tree induction. Discuss the use of **Information Gain** as the attribute selection measure with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Bayes classification** approach. Apply **Naive Bayes classification** to a small dataset and show the calculations. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — ID3 Algorithm

**ID3 (Iterative Dichotomiser 3)** is a greedy top-down algorithm that builds a decision tree by selecting the attribute with the **highest information gain** at each node.

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Start** with all training samples at the root node.
2. **Compute** the entropy of the dataset $S$ (class impurity).
3. **For each attribute** that can split the data:
   - Compute the weighted entropy after the split: $\text{Info}_A(S)$.
   - Compute Information Gain: $\text{Gain}(A) = \text{Info}(S) - \text{Info}_A(S)$.
4. **Select** the attribute with the **highest Gain** as the test attribute for the current node.
5. **Partition** the dataset into subsets based on the values of the selected attribute.
6. **Recurse** on each subset:
   - If a subset is **pure** (entropy = 0) → make it a leaf node labelled by the majority class.
   - If a subset is **empty** → label it as the majority class of the parent.
   - Otherwise, repeat steps 2–5.
7. **Stop** when all data is classified or no attributes remain.

**Worked Example (from Section 3.1):** On the PlayTennis data of 14 tuples, computing $\text{Info}(S) = 0.940$ bits, then evaluating each attribute's gain:

- Gain(Outlook) = 0.247 bits (highest)
- Gain(Temperature) ≈ 0.029 bits
- Gain(Humidity) ≈ 0.152 bits
- Gain(Wind) ≈ 0.048 bits

**Outlook** is selected as the root split. The process repeats recursively on each subset.

**Advantages:** Easy to understand, handles both numerical and categorical data, white-box model.

**Disadvantages:** Prone to **overfitting**, biased toward attributes with many values (solved by C4.5's Gain Ratio), small variation in data can produce different trees.

---

#### Part (b) — Bayes Classification & Naive Bayes

**Bayes Classification** is a probabilistic framework based on **Bayes' Theorem**:

$$P(H \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(X)}$$

**Naive Bayes** assumes class-conditional independence among attributes, simplifying the joint likelihood into a product of individual conditional probabilities:

$$P(C_i \mid X) \propto P(C_i) \prod_{k=1}^{n} P(x_k \mid C_i)$$

**Worked Example (from Section 3.2):**

For the customer computer-buying dataset, we computed:

$$P(\text{Buy} = \text{Yes} \mid X) = 0.877$$

$$P(\text{Buy} = \text{No} \mid X) = 0.123$$

Therefore the classifier predicts **"Buys = Yes"** for the tuple (Age=Youth, Income=Medium, Student=Yes, Credit=Fair).

**Block Diagram:**

```
Training Data → Compute Priors P(Ci) and Likelihoods P(xk|Ci)
         ↓
New Instance X → Compute P(Ci)·∏P(xk|Ci) for each Ci
         ↓
Argmax → Predicted Class
```

**Advantages:** Simple, fast, works well with small training data, handles multi-class naturally.

**Disadvantages:** Independence assumption is often unrealistic, zero-frequency problem requires Laplace smoothing.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** Describe the various **issues in classification** with respect to data preparation and model evaluation. Explain **confusion matrix** and derive expressions for **accuracy, precision, recall, and F-measure**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is **prediction**? Explain **linear regression** as a prediction technique. Derive the **ordinary least squares (OLS)** solution for the simple linear regression coefficients. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Issues in Classification & Confusion Matrix

**Issues in Classification:**

1. **Data Cleaning** — Pre-process to reduce noise and handle missing values.
2. **Relevance Analysis** — Remove attributes irrelevant to classification.
3. **Data Transformation** — Normalisation, generalisation, discretisation.
4. **Evaluation Metrics** — Accuracy alone is insufficient; use precision, recall, F-measure, ROC-AUC.
5. **Scalability** — Algorithm must scale to large databases.
6. **Overfitting** — Tree may fit training data too closely; use pruning.

**Confusion Matrix** (for binary classification):

| | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | TP | FN |
| **Actual Negative** | FP | TN |

**Derived Metrics:**

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$

**Numerical Example** (from Section 3.3): with TP=30, FP=10, FN=20, TN=940:

- Accuracy = 970/1000 = 0.970
- Precision = 30/40 = 0.75
- Recall = 30/50 = 0.60
- F1 = 0.667

> For imbalanced data, **recall and F-measure** are far more informative than raw accuracy.

---

#### Part (b) — Prediction & Linear Regression

**Prediction** is the process of estimating a **continuous-valued** attribute for a new data instance. When the predicted value is numeric, prediction is technically known as **regression**.

**Linear Regression** models the relationship between a scalar dependent variable $y$ and one or more independent variables $x_i$ as a straight line (in simple regression) or hyperplane (in multiple regression):

$$y = w_0 + w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + \dots + w_n x_n + \varepsilon$$

where $w_0$ is the **intercept**, $w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n$ are the **regression coefficients**, and $\varepsilon$ is the random error term.

**Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) Derivation — Simple Linear Regression:**

We want to find $w_0, w_1$ that minimise the sum of squared residuals:

$$S(w_0, w_1) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left(y_i - w_0 - w_1 x_i\right)^2$$

Take partial derivatives and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial w_0} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - w_0 - w_1 x_i) = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial w_1} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i(y_i - w_0 - w_1 x_i) = 0$$

From the first equation:

$$w_0 = \bar{y} - w_1 \bar{x}$$

Substituting into the second:

$$w_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$

**Why Least Squares?** OLS has the desirable statistical property of being the **Best Linear Unbiased Estimator (BLUE)** under Gauss-Markov assumptions — minimum variance among all unbiased linear estimators.

**Non-linear Regression:** For non-linear relationships, we may use polynomial regression, exponential models, or apply variable transformations (e.g., $y = a e^{bx}$ becomes $\ln y = \ln a + bx$, which is linear in the transformed variable).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting $\log_2$ vs $\ln$:** Entropy MUST use base-2 logarithm. Using natural log gives wrong units and is penalised.
> 2. **Confusing Information Gain direction:** Highest Gain = chosen attribute. Lowest Gini = chosen attribute. Do NOT mix up these criteria.
> 3. **Skipping the conditional independence statement** in Naive Bayes — it's a 1-mark line that students often omit.
> 4. **Not normalising posteriors** in Naive Bayes — always convert raw products into probabilities that sum to 1.
> 5. **Omitting the Laplace smoothing** argument — the examiner will deduct 1–2 marks if a conditional probability is zero.
> 6. **Confusion Matrix sign errors** — Precision uses FP in denominator, Recall uses FN. Reversing these is a common mistake.
> 7. **Forgetting $\bar{x}, \bar{y}$ in OLS derivation** — examiners expect the final form expressed in terms of deviations from the mean.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 High-Density Revision Summary — Classification and Prediction**

- ✅ **Classification** = predicts a *discrete* class label. **Prediction** = predicts a *continuous* value.
- ✅ Classification is **supervised learning** with a *two-stage process*: **Learning (Training)** and **Classification (Testing)**.
- ✅ **Decision Tree** = flowchart with internal nodes (attribute tests), branches (outcomes), and leaves (class labels).
- ✅ **ID3 algorithm** uses **Information Gain**; **C4.5** uses **Gain Ratio**; **CART** uses **Gini Index**.
- ✅ **Entropy** $\text{Info}(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ measures class impurity. $\text{Info}(S) = 0$ for a pure node.
- ✅ **Information Gain** $\text{Gain}(A) = \text{Info}(S) - \text{Info}_A(S)$ — choose attribute with the **maximum** Gain.
- ✅ **Gain Ratio** = Gain(A) / SplitInfo(A) — overcomes the multi-value bias of Information Gain.
- ✅ **Gini Index** $\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum p_i^2$ — choose attribute with the **minimum** Gini.
- ✅ **Bayes Theorem**: $P(H \mid X) = P(X \mid H) P(H) / P(X)$.
- ✅ **Naive Bayes** assumes class-conditional independence: $P(C \mid X) \propto P(C) \prod P(x_k \mid C)$.
- ✅ Always apply **Laplace Smoothing** to avoid zero probabilities in Naive Bayes.
- ✅ **Confusion Matrix** yields: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, Specificity, G-Mean.
- ✅ For **imbalanced data**, rely on F-measure, Recall, and ROC-AUC — *not* raw accuracy.
- ✅ **Bagging (Random Forest)** reduces **variance**; **Boosting (AdaBoost)** reduces **bias**.
- ✅ **Linear Regression** uses OLS to minimise SSE: $y = w_0 + w_1 x_1 + \dots + w_n x_n$.
- ✅ OLS solution: $w_1 = \sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y}) / \sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2$; $w_0 = \bar{y} - w_1 \bar{x}$.
- ✅ **Non-linear regression** uses polynomial, exponential, or logarithmic models; variable transformation can linearise the problem.
- ✅ **Issues in classification**: data cleaning, relevance, transformation, accuracy vs scalability, overfitting, class imbalance.
- ✅ **Key algorithms to memorise for KTU:** ID3, C4.5, CART, Naive Bayes, Bayesian Belief Networks, Random Forest, AdaBoost, Linear Regression.
- ✅ **Critical exam phrases:** "highest information gain", "minimum Gini index", "class-conditional independence", "least squared error minimisation", "harmonic mean of precision and recall".

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Classification and Prediction** ✅
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Issues Regarding Classification and Prediction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT604 - DATA MINING AND WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Issues Regarding Classification and Prediction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **PBADT604 – Data Mining and Warehousing**, the topic *"Issues Regarding Classification and Prediction"* refers to the set of practical, statistical, and computational challenges that arise **before, during, and after** the construction of models whose goal is either (a) assigning a categorical **class label** to an unlabeled instance (**classification**) or (b) estimating a continuous numeric outcome (**prediction / regression**). These issues span **data quality, preprocessing, model complexity, evaluation metrics, generalization capability, and comparative assessment** of competing models.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Examinable Form):**
> *“Issues regarding classification and prediction are the data-, model-, and evaluation-oriented concerns that influence the reliability, accuracy, and generalizability of a learned model. They include data preprocessing (cleaning, integration, transformation, reduction), handling noise/outliers/missing values, selection of appropriate classification/prediction methods, overfitting, underfitting, the bias–variance tradeoff, and the use of standardized accuracy/error measures for model evaluation and comparison.”*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — “The Hospital Diagnostic System”**
> Imagine a hospital building an AI system that predicts whether a patient has diabetes. Before the system can be trusted, the doctors must ensure:
> 1. **Data Cleanliness** — The patient records are accurate; lab values that are corrupted or missing must be handled.
> 2. **Data Relevance** — Irrelevant fields like *patient’s favorite color* should not influence the diagnosis.
> 3. **Model Complexity** — The model must be smart enough to detect patterns but not so complex that it memorizes a particular patient’s quirks (overfitting).
> 4. **Validation** — The model must be tested on *new*, unseen patients before it is declared reliable.
>
> *Issues in classification and prediction are precisely these “health-checks” applied to a learned model.*

### 1.3 The Six Core Issue Categories (KTU 2024 Classification)

| # | Issue Category | Core Concern |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Data Preprocessing** | Clean, integrate, transform, reduce data |
| 2 | **Handling Missing Values** | Imputation, deletion, ignorance |
| 3 | **Handling Noise & Outliers** | Binning, regression, clustering-based cleaning |
| 4 | **Attribute Selection & Reduction** | Remove irrelevant/redundant features |
| 5 | **Model Selection & Generalization** | Avoid overfitting, underfitting, manage bias–variance |
| 6 | **Evaluation & Comparison** | Accuracy, error rates, precision, recall, F-measure, ROC, RMSE |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Topic is High-Yield in KTU 2024:**
> - Forms the conceptual bridge between *learning algorithms* and *model deployment*.
> - Frequently tested as a **14-mark Module 3 question** and a **3-mark short answer**.
> - Direct overlap with **Module 1 (Preprocessing)** and **Module 2 (Classification algorithms)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Issue 1 — Data Quality Problems

#### 2.1.1 Missing Values
Data records where one or more attribute values are absent. Strategies:

| Strategy | Description | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| **Ignore the tuple** | Discard incomplete records | Loses data; risky if many tuples incomplete |
| **Manual imputation** | Domain expert fills values | Costly, impractical for large data |
| **Global constant fill** | Replace with `"Unknown"` or `0` | May distort distributions |
| **Attribute mean/median fill** | Replace with central tendency | Simple; underestimates variance |
| **Class mean fill** | Replace with mean of same class | Better than global; still biased |
| **Most probable value (Bayesian / regression imputation)** | Predict missing value from other attributes | Most accurate; computationally heavy |

#### 2.1.2 Noisy Data
Noise = random error or variance in a measured variable. Noise-reduction techniques:

- **Binning** — sort values, smooth by bin means, medians, or boundaries.
- **Regression** — fit a regression function to smooth the data.
- **Clustering** — detect & remove outliers as points lying outside clusters.
- **Combined computer + human inspection** — flag suspicious values for expert review.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction (Board-Exam Favorite):**
> *Noise* = a *random* measurement error (small, continuous).  
> *Outlier* = a value that *deviates significantly* from the rest (may be noise OR a legitimate extreme value).

### 2.2 Issue 2 — Data Transformation

| Technique | Formula / Method | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Min–Max Normalization** | $$v' = \dfrac{v - \min_A}{\max_A - \min_A} \cdot (new\_max - new\_min) + new\_min$$ | Scales to a bounded range, usually $[0,1]$ |
| **Z-Score Normalization (Standardization)** | $$v' = \dfrac{v - \mu_A}{\sigma_A}$$ | Zero mean, unit variance; useful for distance-based models |
| **Decimal Scaling** | $v' = \dfrac{v}{10^{j}}$ where $j$ is the smallest integer such that $\max \vert v' \vert < 1$ | Compresses magnitude |
| **Discretization** | Convert numeric → categorical via binning | Required for certain classifiers |

### 2.3 Issue 3 — Attribute Selection & Dimensionality Reduction

**Why?** The **curse of dimensionality**: as dimensions grow, data becomes sparse and distance measures lose meaning.

| Method | Type | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Filter Methods** (e.g., Information Gain, Chi-square) | Independent of learner | Rank attributes by statistical relevance |
| **Wrapper Methods** (e.g., Forward Selection, Backward Elimination) | Use learner as black-box | Search subset that maximizes accuracy |
| **Embedded Methods** (e.g., regularization in regression) | Built into learner | Selection happens during training |
| **PCA (Principal Component Analysis)** | Linear transformation | Projects data onto top-$k$ eigenvectors of covariance matrix |

### 2.4 Issue 4 — Model Complexity: Overfitting & Underfitting

| Concept | Definition | Diagnostic Symptom | Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Overfitting** | Model learns *noise* as if it were a pattern | Training error **very low**, test error **very high** | Pruning, cross-validation, more data, regularization |
| **Underfitting** | Model is *too simple* to capture underlying pattern | Both training and test errors **high** | Use more complex model, add relevant features |
| **Good Fit** | Model captures true pattern + tolerates noise | Training and test errors both low and close | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always draw the *error-vs-model-complexity* curve in your answer. The *optimal complexity* is at the minimum of the test-error curve.

### 2.5 Issue 5 — Bias–Variance Tradeoff

Expected prediction error decomposes as:

$$E[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2] = \text{Bias}^2[\hat{f}(x)] + \text{Variance}[\hat{f}(x)] + \sigma^2$$

| Term | Meaning | Effect of Increasing Complexity |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{Bias}^2$ | Error from wrong assumptions | Decreases |
| $\text{Variance}$ | Error from sensitivity to training fluctuations | Increases |
| $\sigma^2$ | Irreducible noise | Constant |

> **Goal:** Find the complexity that **minimizes Bias² + Variance** jointly.

### 2.6 Issue 6 — Evaluating & Comparing Classifiers

#### 2.6.1 Confusion Matrix (Foundation of All Metrics)

|  | **Predicted Positive** | **Predicted Negative** |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | TP (True Positive) | FN (False Negative) |
| **Actual Negative** | FP (False Positive) | TN (True Negative) |

#### 2.6.2 Core Metrics (Examinable Formulas)

| Metric | Formula | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | $Acc = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall correctness |
| **Error Rate** | $Err = 1 - Acc = \dfrac{FP + FN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall incorrectness |
| **Precision (Positive Predictive Value)** | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Of those predicted positive, how many are correct |
| **Recall / Sensitivity / TPR** | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Of actual positives, how many are caught |
| **Specificity / TNR** | $S = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | Of actual negatives, how many are correctly rejected |
| **F1-Score (Harmonic mean of P and R)** | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \dfrac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$ | Balances precision & recall |
| **Fβ-Score (generalized)** | $F_\beta = \dfrac{(1+\beta^2) \cdot P \cdot R}{\beta^2 \cdot P + R}$ | Weights recall more when $\beta > 1$ |
| **Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC)** | $MCC = \dfrac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | Balanced measure even with class imbalance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitfall Callout (Board Examiner):** Students often report *only* accuracy. With imbalanced classes (e.g., 99% negative, 1% positive), a trivial “always-predict-negative” classifier scores 99% accuracy while being useless. **Always report Precision, Recall, F1, and the confusion matrix.**

#### 2.6.3 ROC Curve & AUC

The **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC)** plots:
- **X-axis:** False Positive Rate $\;FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$
- **Y-axis:** True Positive Rate $\;TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$

at varying classification thresholds.

- **AUC (Area Under Curve)** $\in [0, 1]$; higher is better.
- $AUC = 0.5$ ⇒ random classifier; $AUC = 1.0$ ⇒ perfect classifier.

### 2.7 Issue 7 — Comparing Classifier Performance

| Method | Procedure | Statistical Basis |
|---|---|---|
| **Holdout (Train/Test split)** | Split data once (e.g., 2/3 train, 1/3 test) | None (single estimate) |
| **Random Subsampling** | Repeat holdout $k$ times, average | Reduces variance of estimate |
| **Cross-Validation (k-fold)** | Partition into $k$ folds; train on $k{-}1$, test on 1; rotate | More reliable, uses all data |
| **Stratified k-fold** | Preserve class distribution in each fold | Best for imbalanced data |
| **Leave-One-Out (LOOCV)** | $k = n$ (one test point at a time) | Nearly unbiased, high variance |
| **Bootstrap** | Sample with replacement $B$ times | Estimates 0.632+ accuracy |
| **McNemar’s Test / Paired t-test** | Compare two classifiers on same folds | Statistical significance test |
| **ROC Comparison (DeLong’s test)** | Compare AUCs of two classifiers | Threshold-independent comparison |

### 2.8 Issues Specific to Prediction (Regression)

| Metric | Formula | Best For |
|---|---|---|
| **Mean Absolute Error (MAE)** | $MAE = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | Robust to outliers |
| **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** | $MSE = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Penalizes large errors more |
| **Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)** | $RMSE = \sqrt{MSE}$ | Same units as target variable |
| **Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE)** | $MAPE = \dfrac{100\%}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\vert \dfrac{y_i - \hat{y}_i}{y_i} \right\vert$ | Scale-independent comparison |
| **Correlation Coefficient (r)** | $r = \dfrac{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})(\hat{y}_i - \bar{\hat{y}})}{\sqrt{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2 \cdot \sum (\hat{y}_i - \bar{\hat{y}})^2}}$ | Strength of linear relationship |

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare** — Diagnostic classifiers evaluated via Sensitivity/Specificity (avoiding missed diagnoses).
- **Finance** — Credit-risk models evaluated via AUC and Precision-Recall curves (imbalanced default vs. non-default).
- **Manufacturing** — Predictive maintenance models use RMSE to quantify deviation of predicted vs. actual sensor values.
- **Telecommunications** — Churn classifiers compared via stratified 10-fold CV to ensure representative folds.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Min–Max Normalization

**Goal:** Map value $v$ of attribute $A$ from its original range $[\min_A, \max_A]$ to a new range $[new\_min, new\_max]$.

**Step 1 — Linear mapping formula:**  
We assume a linear transformation:

$$v' = a \cdot v + b$$

**Step 2 — Apply boundary conditions:**  
When $v = \min_A$, we want $v' = new\_min$:

$$new\_min = a \cdot \min_A + b$$

When $v = \max_A$, we want $v' = new\_max$:

$$new\_max = a \cdot \max_A + b$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $a$:**  
Subtract the first equation from the second:

$$new\_max - new\_min = a \cdot (\max_A - \min_A)$$

$$a = \dfrac{new\_max - new\_min}{\max_A - \min_A}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $b$:**  
Substitute $a$ back:

$$b = new\_min - a \cdot \min_A = new\_min - \dfrac{new\_max - new\_min}{\max_A - \min_A} \cdot \min_A$$

**Step 5 — Final compact form:**

$$\boxed{\,v' = \dfrac{v - \min_A}{\max_A - \min_A} \cdot (new\_max - new\_min) + new\_min\,}$$

**Step 6 — Special case for $[0,1]$ range:**  
Set $new\_min = 0, \; new\_max = 1$:

$$v' = \dfrac{v - \min_A}{\max_A - \min_A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example (KTU Exam Practice):**  
> Given $\min_A = 20$, $\max_A = 80$, find the normalized value of $v = 60$ to range $[0,1]$.  
> $v' = \dfrac{60 - 20}{80 - 20} = \dfrac{40}{60} = 0.667$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Z-Score Standardization

**Goal:** Transform $v$ so the attribute has mean $0$ and standard deviation $1$.

**Step 1 — Define the mean:**

$$\mu_A = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i$$

**Step 2 — Define the standard deviation:**

$$\sigma_A = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (v_i - \mu_A)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the transformation:**  
Subtract the mean (centers) and divide by standard deviation (scales):

$$\boxed{\,v' = \dfrac{v - \mu_A}{\sigma_A}\,}$$

**Step 4 — Verify that $E[v'] = 0$ and $\text{Var}(v') = 1$:**

$$E[v'] = \dfrac{E[v] - \mu_A}{\sigma_A} = \dfrac{\mu_A - \mu_A}{\sigma_A} = 0$$

$$\text{Var}(v') = \dfrac{\text{Var}(v)}{\sigma_A^2} = \dfrac{\sigma_A^2}{\sigma_A^2} = 1$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: F1-Score from Precision & Recall

**Step 1 — Start with the harmonic mean definition:**

$$F_1 = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{1}{P} + \dfrac{1}{R}\right)}$$

**Step 2 — Common denominator:**

$$F_1 = \dfrac{2}{\dfrac{R + P}{P \cdot R}} = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$$

**Step 3 — Express in terms of TP, FP, FN:**  
With $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ and $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$:

$$F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP} \cdot \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}}{\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP} + \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $(TP+FP)(TP+FN)$:**

$$F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot TP^2}{TP \cdot (TP+FN) + TP \cdot (TP+FP)} = \dfrac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Mean Squared Error (MSE) and its Relation to Variance

**Step 1 — Definition:**

$$MSE = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

**Step 2 — Expand using the identity $(a-b)^2 = (a - \bar{y})^2 + (\bar{y} - b)^2 + 2(a - \bar{y})(\bar{y} - b)$:**  
Let $a = y_i$ and $b = \hat{y}_i$. Setting the cross term to zero under expectation, MSE decomposes into:

$$MSE = \underbrace{\text{Var}(\hat{y})}_{\text{Variance}} + \underbrace{(\bar{y} - E[\hat{y}])^2}_{\text{Bias}^2} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{Irreducible noise}}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**  
This is the **bias–variance decomposition** — the MSE of a predictor is the sum of its variance, the square of its bias, and the inherent noise in the data.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Metric Selection Cheat Sheet (KTU 2024)

| Scenario | Recommended Metric | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Balanced classes, equal cost of errors | **Accuracy** | Simple, intuitive |
| Imbalanced classes (e.g., fraud detection) | **F1, AUC-PR** | Accuracy is misleading |
| Medical diagnosis (missed positives costly) | **Recall / Sensitivity** | Avoid false negatives |
| Spam filtering (false positives costly) | **Precision** | Avoid flagging legitimate mail |
| Comparing two classifiers | **Paired t-test on k-fold CV** | Statistical significance |
| Threshold-independent comparison | **AUC-ROC** | Single scalar summary |
| Continuous target prediction | **RMSE, MAE, MAPE** | Scale-aware / scale-free |
| Linear relationship strength | **Correlation Coefficient r** | Captures co-movement |

---

### 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (End-to-End)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A classifier on a test set of 1000 records produces the following confusion matrix:
> - TP = 80, FN = 20, FP = 40, TN = 860.
> Compute Accuracy, Error Rate, Precision, Recall, F1, and Specificity.

**Step 1 — Total records:**  
$N = 80 + 20 + 40 + 860 = 1000$

**Step 2 — Accuracy:**

$$Acc = \dfrac{80 + 860}{1000} = \dfrac{940}{1000} = 0.94$$

**Step 3 — Error Rate:**

$$Err = 1 - 0.94 = 0.06 \quad\text{(or)} \quad \dfrac{40 + 20}{1000} = 0.06$$

**Step 4 — Precision:**

$$P = \dfrac{80}{80 + 40} = \dfrac{80}{120} = 0.667$$

**Step 5 — Recall:**

$$R = \dfrac{80}{80 + 20} = \dfrac{80}{100} = 0.80$$

**Step 6 — F1-Score:**

$$F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot 0.667 \cdot 0.80}{0.667 + 0.80} = \dfrac{1.0672}{1.467} \approx 0.727$$

**Step 7 — Specificity:**

$$S = \dfrac{860}{860 + 40} = \dfrac{860}{900} \approx 0.956$$

**Step 8 — Interpret:**  
The classifier is **94% accurate** but recall (0.80) is significantly higher than precision (0.667) — meaning it catches 80% of true positives but produces many false alarms. F1 = 0.727 reveals a moderate trade-off not visible from accuracy alone.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: End-to-End Classification Pipeline

This diagram maps the *interactions* between each issue category and the corresponding preprocessing / modeling stage — the kind of system flow expected in a 14-mark KTU answer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data Acquisition] --> B[Data Quality Audit]
    B --> B1[Missing Values?]
    B --> B2[Noise Detected?]
    B --> B3[Outliers Present?]
    B --> B4[Inconsistent Formats?]
    B1 --> C1[Imputation Strategy]
    B2 --> C2[Binning / Regression Smoothing]
    B3 --> C3[Clustering-Based Removal]
    B4 --> C4[Standardization of Units]
    C1 --> D[Integrated Clean Dataset]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E[Data Transformation Stage]
    E --> E1[Min-Max / Z-Score Normalization]
    E --> E2[Discretization]
    E --> E3[Encoding Categorical]
    E1 --> F[Transformed Feature Space]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Attribute Selection and Reduction]
    G --> G1[Filter: Info Gain, Chi-Square]
    G --> G2[Wrapper: Forward / Backward Search]
    G --> G3[Embedded: L1 Regularization, PCA]
    G1 --> H[Optimized Feature Subset]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Model Training Phase]
    I --> I1[Train Classifier M1]
    I --> I2[Train Classifier M2]
    I --> I3[Train Classifier M3]
    I1 --> J[Evaluation Engine]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    J --> K[Metric Computation Module]
    K --> K1[Confusion Matrix]
    K --> K2[Accuracy / Error]
    K --> K3[Precision / Recall / F1]
    K --> K4[AUC-ROC]
    K1 --> L[Statistical Comparison]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    K4 --> L
    L --> M{Bias-Variance Check}
    M -->|Overfit| N1[Apply Pruning / Regularization / More Data]
    M -->|Underfit| N2[Increase Model Complexity / Add Features]
    M -->|Good Fit| N3[Final Model Deployment]
    N1 --> J
    N2 --> J
    N3 --> O[Production Classifier]
```

---

### 4.2 Concept Map: The Six Issue Categories

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Issues in Classification and Prediction] --> R1[Data Quality]
    R --> R2[Transformation]
    R --> R3[Attribute Selection]
    R --> R4[Model Complexity]
    R --> R5[Evaluation Metrics]
    R --> R6[Statistical Comparison]
    R1 --> R1A[Missing Values]
    R1 --> R1B[Noise]
    R1 --> R1C[Outliers]
    R2 --> R2A[Min-Max]
    R2 --> R2B[Z-Score]
    R2 --> R2C[Discretization]
    R3 --> R3A[Filter]
    R3 --> R3B[Wrapper]
    R3 --> R3C[Embedded / PCA]
    R4 --> R4A[Overfitting]
    R4 --> R4B[Underfitting]
    R4 --> R4C[Bias-Variance Tradeoff]
    R5 --> R5A[Confusion Matrix]
    R5 --> R5B[Precision Recall F1]
    R5 --> R5C[ROC AUC]
    R6 --> R6A[k-Fold CV]
    R6 --> R6B[Paired t-test]
    R6 --> R6C[McNemar Test]
```

---

### 4.3 Bias–Variance Tradeoff Visualization (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Simple Model] -->|High Bias| A1[Underfitting]
    A1 --> A2[Both Errors High]
    B[Optimal Model] -->|Balanced| B1[Good Generalization]
    B1 --> B2[Low Train and Test Error]
    C[Complex Model] -->|High Variance| C1[Overfitting]
    C1 --> C2[Low Train Error, High Test Error]
    A2 --> D[Choose Model Complexity]
    B2 --> D
    C2 --> D
    D --> E[Minimum Test Error]
```

---

### 4.4 ROC Curve Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Threshold = 0.0] --> Q1[FPR = 1.0, TPR = 1.0]
    P2[Threshold = 0.25] --> Q2[FPR = 0.40, TPR = 0.95]
    P3[Threshold = 0.50] --> Q3[FPR = 0.15, TPR = 0.80]
    P4[Threshold = 0.75] --> Q4[FPR = 0.05, TPR = 0.50]
    P5[Threshold = 1.0] --> Q5[FPR = 0.0, TPR = 0.0]
    Q1 --> R[ROC Curve Plot]
    Q2 --> R
    Q3 --> R
    Q4 --> R
    Q5 --> R
    R --> S[AUC Computed as Area Under Curve]
    S --> T{AUC Interpretation}

    T -->|0.5| T2[Random Guess]
    T -->|<0.5| T3[Inverted Predictions]
```
