# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Define Bayes' Theorem and explain the significance of each term in the context of classification.**

**Model Answer:**

Bayes' Theorem is a fundamental probability identity used in Bayesian classification to compute the posterior probability of a class given observed evidence. It is stated as:

$$P(C_i \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid C_i) \cdot P(C_i)}{P(X)}$$

Significance of each term:

- $P(C_i \mid X)$ — **Posterior Probability**: Probability of class $C_i$ after observing the attribute vector $X$; this is the value used for the final classification decision.
- $P(X \mid C_i)$ — **Likelihood**: Probability of observing evidence $X$ given that the tuple belongs to class $C_i$; estimated from training data.
- $P(C_i)$ — **Prior Probability**: Initial probability of class $C_i$ before observing evidence; computed as the fraction of training tuples belonging to $C_i$.
- $P(X)$ — **Evidence**: Overall probability of observing $X$ across all classes; acts as a normalizing constant and is identical for all class computations.

---

> **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **What is the "zero-frequency problem" in Naive Bayes classification? How is it resolved?**

**Model Answer:**

The **zero-frequency problem** occurs when a categorical attribute value does not co-occur with any training tuple of a particular class. In such cases, the estimated conditional probability becomes:

$$P(x_k \mid C_i) = 0$$

Since the Naive Bayes decision rule involves a product over all attributes, a single zero value annihilates the entire product, making the posterior probability of that class equal to zero, regardless of the evidence from other attributes. This leads to systematically biased predictions.

**Resolution — Laplace Smoothing (Add-One Estimation):**

$$P(x_k \mid C_i) = \frac{n_{ik} + 1}{n_i + v}$$

Where $n_{ik}$ is the count of class-$C_i$ tuples with attribute value $x_k$, $n_i$ is the total count of class-$C_i$ tuples, and $v$ is the number of distinct values of the attribute. Adding 1 in the numerator and $v$ in the denominator ensures that no probability is ever zero while keeping the total probability distribution normalized.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **Naive Bayesian Classification** approach in detail. State the underlying assumption and derive the classification rule from Bayes' Theorem. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Given the following training data for a medical diagnosis system predicting whether a patient has **Flu** ($C_1$) or **Allergy** ($C_2$) based on three symptoms: **Sore Throat** (ST), **Fever** (F), and **Runny Nose** (RN). Classify a new patient presenting with ST = Yes, F = Yes, RN = No. Use Laplace smoothing where applicable. **(7 Marks)**

| ID | Sore Throat | Fever | Runny Nose | Diagnosis |
|----|-------------|-------|------------|-----------|
| 1  | Yes         | Yes   | No         | Flu       |
| 2  | Yes         | No    | Yes        | Allergy   |
| 3  | Yes         | Yes   | No         | Flu       |
| 4  | No          | Yes   | Yes        | Flu       |
| 5  | No          | No    | Yes        | Allergy   |
| 6  | Yes         | No    | No         | Allergy   |
| 7  | Yes         | Yes   | Yes        | Flu       |
| 8  | No          | No    | No         | Allergy   |
| 9  | Yes         | Yes   | No         | Flu       |
| 10 | No          | No    | Yes        | Allergy   |

### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Detailed Explanation of Naive Bayesian Classification:**

Naive Bayesian Classification is a probabilistic supervised learning method that applies Bayes' Theorem with a simplifying assumption. The procedure is:

1. Each data tuple is represented as an $n$-dimensional attribute vector $X = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$.
2. There are $m$ classes $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_m$. The classifier predicts the most probable class given the observed attributes.
3. Bayes' Theorem gives: $P(C_i \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid C_i) \cdot P(C_i)}{P(X)}$.
4. **Naive Assumption (Class-Conditional Independence):** Given the class label, all attributes are assumed to be mutually independent. Therefore:

$$P(X \mid C_i) = \prod_{k=1}^{n} P(x_k \mid C_i)$$

5. Substituting back:

$$P(C_i \mid X) = \frac{P(C_i) \cdot \prod_{k=1}^{n} P(x_k \mid C_i)}{P(X)}$$

6. Since $P(X)$ is constant for all classes, the Naive Bayes decision rule simplifies to the MAP estimate:

$$C_{NB} = \arg\max_{C_i \in C} P(C_i) \cdot \prod_{k=1}^{n} P(x_k \mid C_i)$$

7. The priors are estimated as $P(C_i) = \vert C_i \vert / \vert D \vert$, and the likelihoods are estimated by counting co-occurrences in the training set. Laplace smoothing is applied to handle zero-frequency cases.

**Advantages:** fast training and prediction, handles high-dimensional data well, robust to noise, works with categorical and continuous attributes.

**Disadvantages:** strong independence assumption rarely holds in practice; can be outperformed by more sophisticated models like SVMs or Random Forests when the independence assumption is strongly violated.

---

**Part (b) — Step-by-Step Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Priors**

Total tuples: 10; Flu tuples: 5; Allergy tuples: 5.

$$P(\text{Flu}) = \frac{5}{10} = 0.5$$

$$P(\text{Allergy}) = \frac{5}{10} = 0.5$$

**Step 2: Compute Likelihoods for Flu ($C_1$)**

From the 5 Flu tuples (IDs: 1, 3, 4, 7, 9):

$$P(\text{ST} = \text{Yes} \mid \text{Flu}) = \frac{4}{5} = 0.800$$

$$P(\text{F} = \text{Yes} \mid \text{Flu}) = \frac{4}{5} = 0.800$$

$$P(\text{RN} = \text{No} \mid \text{Flu}) = \frac{3}{5} = 0.600$$

**Step 3: Compute Likelihoods for Allergy ($C_2$)**

From the 5 Allergy tuples (IDs: 2, 5, 6, 8, 10):

$$P(\text{ST} = \text{Yes} \mid \text{Allergy}) = \frac{2}{5} = 0.400$$

$$P(\text{F} = \text{Yes} \mid \text{Allergy}) = \frac{0}{5} = 0$$

$$P(\text{RN} = \text{No} \mid \text{Allergy}) = \frac{2}{5} = 0.400$$

**Step 4: Observe the Zero-Frequency Problem**

$P(\text{F} = \text{Yes} \mid \text{Allergy}) = 0$ — none of the 5 Allergy patients had fever. Without smoothing, the Allergy posterior would be zero.

**Step 5: Apply Laplace Smoothing**

For binary attributes, $v = 2$.

For Flu class (using $n_i = 5$):

$$P(\text{F} = \text{Yes} \mid \text{Flu}) = \frac{4 + 1}{5 + 2} = \frac{5}{7} = 0.7143$$

For Allergy class (using $n_i = 5$):

$$P(\text{F} = \text{Yes} \mid \text{Allergy}) = \frac{0 + 1}{5 + 2} = \frac{1}{7} = 0.1429$$

**Step 6: Compute Naive Bayes Products**

For Flu:

$$P(\text{Flu} \mid X) \propto 0.5 \times 0.800 \times 0.7143 \times 0.600 = 0.5 \times 0.3429 = 0.1714$$

For Allergy:

$$P(\text{Allergy} \mid X) \propto 0.5 \times 0.400 \times 0.1429 \times 0.400 = 0.5 \times 0.0229 = 0.01143$$

**Step 7: Decision**

$$C_{NB} = \arg\max(0.1714, \ 0.01143) = \text{Flu}$$

> **Conclusion:** The new patient is classified as having **Flu**. The Laplace smoothing prevented the zero-frequency issue from eliminating the Allergy class entirely, allowing a fair comparison. However, the strong evidence for Flu (fever, sore throat, no runny nose) decisively outweighs the Allergy hypothesis.

---

> **Question B (Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
>
> **(a)** What is a **Bayesian Belief Network (BBN)**? Explain its structure, components, and how the joint probability distribution is factorized. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare and contrast **Naive Bayes Classifier** and **Bayesian Belief Networks** with respect to independence assumptions, computational complexity, training data requirements, and real-world applicability. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B (Outline)

**Part (a) — Bayesian Belief Network (7 Marks):**

A Bayesian Belief Network is a probabilistic graphical model that represents a set of random variables and their conditional dependencies via a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)**. Key components:

- **Nodes** = random variables (discrete or continuous).
- **Directed Edges** = direct causal/probabilistic dependencies.
- **CPT (Conditional Probability Table)** at each node quantifying $P(\text{Node} \mid \text{Parents})$.
- **Joint Distribution Factorization:**

$$P(X_1, \ldots, X_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i))$$

**Part (b) — Comparison (7 Marks):**

Refer to the **Analytical Matrix** in Section 3.4 of these notes for a structured comparison. Key contrast points:

- **Independence:** Naive Bayes assumes all attributes are independent given the class; BBN allows arbitrary conditional dependencies as encoded by the DAG.
- **Complexity:** Naive Bayes has $O(n \cdot m \cdot v)$ parameters; BBN scales with graph structure but generally requires more parameters and structure learning.
- **Data Requirements:** Naive Bayes works well with less data; BBNs benefit from more data to learn reliable CPTs.
- **Applicability:** Naive Bayes for high-dimensional, text-like data; BBN for causal reasoning, medical diagnosis, and domains with known dependency structures.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the denominator $P(X)$:** Many students drop the $P(X)$ term and still get full credit for the MAP decision, but examiners expect students to *mention* that $P(X)$ is constant for all classes and is therefore omitted in the argmax step.
> 2. **Confusing prior and likelihood:** Examiners frequently test whether students can clearly distinguish $P(C_i)$ (prior) from $P(X \mid C_i)$ (likelihood) and $P(C_i \mid X)$ (posterior).
> 3. **Ignoring Laplace Smoothing:** If the data contains any zero-frequency entry, the exam answer is considered incomplete without applying Laplace smoothing.
> 4. **Missing the independence assumption:** Naive Bayes problems that don't explicitly state the class-conditional independence assumption will lose **2 to 3 marks** automatically — this is a hallmark concept of the topic.
> 5. **Unit normalization errors:** When normalizing posteriors, students often forget that $P(X)$ must equal the sum of all unnormalized scores across *every* class. Show the normalization step explicitly.
> 6. **BBN Joint Distribution:** When writing the BBN factorization, students commonly miss one or more parent dependencies. Cross-check by ensuring *every* node (except roots) has its parents in the conditional term.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔹 **Bayes' Theorem** transforms an intractable posterior estimation problem into a tractable product of a likelihood and a prior.
- 🔹 **Naive Bayes** assumes **class-conditional independence** of attributes: $P(X \mid C_i) = \prod_{k} P(x_k \mid C_i)$.
- 🔹 The **MAP decision rule** is $C_{NB} = \arg\max_{C_i} P(C_i) \cdot \prod_{k=1}^{n} P(x_k \mid C_i)$ — the denominator $P(X)$ is dropped because it is constant across all classes.
- 🔹 **Priors** are estimated as $P(C_i) = \vert C_i \vert / \vert D \vert$ — a simple count ratio from training data.
- 🔹 **Likelihoods** for categorical attributes are estimated by counting co-occurrences: $P(x_k \mid C_i) = n_{ik} / n_i$.
- 🔹 **Laplace Smoothing** formula is $P(x_k \mid C_i) = (n_{ik} + 1) / (n_i + v)$ — always apply this when zero counts are possible.
- 🔹 **Naive Bayes is robust** despite its unrealistic independence assumption — it works especially well for high-dimensional text data.
- 🔹 **Bayesian Belief Networks** generalize Naive Bayes by allowing arbitrary conditional dependencies encoded in a DAG with CPTs.
- 🔹 The **BBN joint distribution** factors as $P(X_1, \ldots, X_n) = \prod_{i} P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i))$.
- 🔹 Naive Bayes is the **special case** of a BBN where the class is the *only* parent of all attribute nodes (star topology).
- 🔹 **For continuous attributes**, Naive Bayes uses a Gaussian density model: $P(x_k \mid C_i) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_{C_i}^2}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x_k - \mu_{C_i})^2}{2\sigma_{C_i}^2}\right)$.
- 🔹 KTU 2024 examiners test both **conceptual understanding** (definitions, assumptions) and **numerical application** (computing posteriors from training tables).
- 🔹 **Always show the MAP step explicitly** in numerical answers; even when the question asks only for class assignment, showing the argmax step fetches full marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Rule-Based Classification:**
> Rule-based classification is a supervised learning technique that constructs a predictive model in the form of a set of **IF–THEN rules**. Each rule has the form `R: (Condition) → y`, where the **antecedent (Condition)** is a logical conjunction of attribute tests, and the **consequent (y)** is a class label. An instance is classified by triggering the rule(s) whose antecedents it satisfies, and assigning the predicted class.

A general rule has the form:

$$R : (A_1 \odot v_1) \;\wedge\; (A_2 \odot v_2) \;\wedge\; \dots \;\wedge\; (A_k \odot v_k) \;\rightarrow\; y$$

where each $A_i$ is an **attribute**, each $v_i$ is a value from the domain of $A_i$, the operator $\odot \in \{=, \neq, <, \le, >, \ge\}$ is the test, and $y$ is the **class label**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Traffic Police Officer"**
> Imagine a traffic police officer at a junction who decides whether a car should **GO** or **STOP**. The officer mentally runs through a checklist:
> - IF (light = red) **OR** (officer signals) → **STOP**
> - ELSE IF (light = green) **AND** (no pedestrian) → **GO**
> - ELSE → **STOP**
>
> Each "IF–THEN" line is a **rule**. Together, all the rules form the officer's **decision policy** (the classifier). A new car arriving is matched against these rules top-to-bottom (or by best fit), and the matching rule's conclusion is applied. Rule-based classification works exactly this way — it learns such IF–THEN rules directly from training data, then applies them to unseen records.

## 1.3 Why Rule-Based Classification? (Motivation)

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Mutually exclusive rules** | If every instance is covered by **at most one rule**, the rule set behaves like a decision tree. |
| **Exhaustive rules** | If every instance is covered by **at least one rule**, no default class is needed. |
| **Easy interpretation** | An IF–THEN rule reads like natural English; non-technical stakeholders can audit it. |
| **Easier to combine** | Multiple classifiers can be merged by simply appending their rule sets. |
| **Handles multi-class** | Rules can predict any of the $k$ classes natively. |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners often award extra marks if you explicitly state that rules provide **mutually exclusive + exhaustive** coverage, similar to a decision tree's path structure.

## 1.4 Anatomy of a Rule

Every rule has two parts:

**1. Antecedent (LHS / Condition Part):**
A conjunction of one or more attribute tests. The more conditions, the **more specific** the rule, but the **fewer records** it will cover.

**2. Consequent (RHS / Conclusion Part):**
A class label $y \in \{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_k\}$.

**Worked example (training set):**

| Tid | Refund | Marital Status | Taxable Income | Cheat |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Yes | Single | 125K | No |
| 2 | No | Married | 100K | No |
| 3 | No | Single | 70K | No |
| 4 | Yes | Married | 120K | No |
| 5 | No | Divorced | 95K | Yes |
| 6 | No | Married | 60K | No |
| 7 | Yes | Divorced | 220K | No |
| 8 | No | Single | 85K | Yes |
| 9 | No | Married | 75K | No |
| 10 | No | Single | 90K | Yes |

From this, two reasonable rules might be:

$$R_1 : (\text{Refund} = \text{No}) \;\wedge\; (\text{Marital Status} = \text{Married}) \;\rightarrow\; \text{Cheat} = \text{No}$$

$$R_2 : (\text{Refund} = \text{No}) \;\wedge\; (\text{Taxable Income} > 80K) \;\rightarrow\; \text{Cheat} = \text{Yes}$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Rule Quality Metrics — Coverage & Accuracy

For a rule $R : A \rightarrow y$, let:
- $|D|$ = total number of records in the training set
- $|A|$ = number of records that satisfy the antecedent (the **cover** of the rule)
- $|A \cap C|$ = number of records satisfying the antecedent **and** belonging to class $y$

> [!NOTE]
> **Coverage (or Support)** — fraction of records in $D$ that satisfy the antecedent $A$.

$$\text{Coverage}(R) = \frac{\vert A \cap C \vert}{\vert D \vert}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Accuracy (or Confidence)** — fraction of records satisfying the antecedent that also belong to the predicted class $y$.

$$\text{Accuracy}(R) = \frac{\vert A \cap C \vert}{\vert A \vert}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students confuse coverage and accuracy. Coverage refers to how **broadly** the rule fires in $D$; accuracy refers to how **correctly** it predicts when it fires. Memorize the denominators: Coverage → $|D|$; Accuracy → $\vert A \vert$.

## 2.2 Rule Ordering Schemes

When a rule set is **not mutually exclusive** (a record fires multiple rules), a conflict-resolution strategy is required.

### Scheme A — Rule-Based Ordering (Priority / Decision List)

* Rules are ranked in descending order of a chosen priority (often **highest accuracy**, or **most specific**).
* A record is classified by the **first rule** whose antecedent matches.
* The list may be terminated with a **default class** rule.

> **Example ordering:** $R_1 \succ R_2 \succ R_3 \succ \dots \succ R_n \succ \text{default}$

### Scheme B — Class-Based Ordering (Unordered Rule Set)

* Rules are partitioned **by class**.
* All rules of class $C_1$ are grouped, then all of class $C_2$, and so on.
* For a new record, **all** matching rules vote; the **majority class** wins.
* Ties are broken by **rule strength** (e.g., accuracy × coverage, or Laplace-corrected confidence).

| Property | Rule-Ordered | Class-Ordered |
|---|---|---|
| Determinism | Deterministic (first match wins) | Probabilistic (voting) |
| Robustness to ordering | Low — sensitive to rule order | High — order within a class is irrelevant |
| Interpretability | Reads like a decision list | Reads like a knowledge base |
| Default class needed? | Recommended | Not strictly needed |

## 2.3 Sequential Covering — Learning Rules One at a Time

Rule induction is most often performed by a **sequential covering algorithm** (a.k.a. **separate-and-conquer**). The intuition:

> Learn **one rule** that covers a subset of the training data → **remove** the records it covers → repeat on the remainder until almost all positive records of a class are covered.

**Generic Sequential-Covering Algorithm (pseudocode in plain English — no code):**

1. Let $R \leftarrow \emptyset$ and $D_c$ be the working data set.
2. For each class $y$, find a rule that covers many records of class $y$ and few of other classes.
3. Add the rule $r$ to $R$.
4. Remove from $D_c$ all records covered by $r$.
5. Repeat steps 2–4 until $D_c$ is empty (or stopping criterion met).
6. Return the ordered rule set $R$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symmetry Trick — One-vs-Rest:** Because the algorithm grows rules for **one class at a time**, it is conceptually equivalent to training $k$ binary classifiers (class $C_i$ vs. **not** $C_i$) and concatenating their rules.

## 2.4 Learn-One-Rule — The Inner Loop

The hardest sub-problem is: *"Given a current working set $D_c$, find the single best rule."* Two classic strategies:

| Strategy | Description | Example Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| **General-to-specific** | Start with the empty rule `{ } → y`; greedily add attribute tests that improve quality the most. | FOIL, CN2 |
| **Specific-to-general** | Start with a rule that covers a single random positive example; greedily remove conditions that hurt quality. | Some AQ-family variants |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The general-to-specific strategy is **overwhelmingly** the one tested, because it leads naturally to the **FOIL gain** formula (see §2.5). Memorize it cold.

## 2.5 FOIL (First-Order Inductive Learner) Gain — The KTU High-Yield Formula

When deciding which attribute test to add next, FOIL uses an **information-style gain**:

$$\text{FOIL\_Gain} = p_t \times \left( \log_2 \dfrac{p_t}{p_t + n_t} \;-\; \log_2 \dfrac{p}{p + n} \right)$$

where:
- $p, n$ = number of **positive** and **negative** records covered by the rule **before** adding the new candidate condition
- $p_t, n_t$ = number of positive and negative records covered by the rule **after** adding the new condition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** The term $\log_2\!\dfrac{p_t}{p_t+n_t}$ is the **purity** of the rule after the new condition is added (analogous to entropy). The product with $p_t$ ensures we don't just pick a condition that *isolates* one or two examples — we need it to cover **many** positives.

## 2.6 Rule Pruning — Avoiding Overfitting

A fully grown rule may **overfit** training noise. Two pruning philosophies:

| Approach | Pruning Logic |
|---|---|
| **Pre-pruning** (a.k.a. **direct rule simplification**) | Stop adding conditions as soon as accuracy stops improving on a hold-out set, or once $\text{FOIL\_Gain}$ falls below a threshold. |
| **Post-pruning** | Grow a full rule, then iteratively **remove** the most weakly-supported antecedent (the one whose removal **decreases accuracy the least**, or even **increases** it on a pruning set). |

The **posterior pruning rule** is:

$$\text{Keep rule } R \text{ if } \text{Accuracy}_{\text{prune}}(R) \;\geq\; \text{Accuracy}_{\text{train}}(R)$$

If pruning a conjunct improves or maintains accuracy, the conjunct is removed.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Coverage}(R) = \dfrac{\vert A \cap C \vert}{\vert D \vert}$ | Fraction of $D$ covered by $R$ | Computing rule's reach |
| 2 | $\text{Accuracy}(R) = \dfrac{\vert A \cap C \vert}{\vert A \vert}$ | Fraction of covered records that are correctly classified | Computing rule's correctness |
| 3 | $\text{FOIL\_Gain} = p_t \left( \log_2 \dfrac{p_t}{p_t+n_t} - \log_2 \dfrac{p}{p+n} \right)$ | Information-style quality gain of a candidate condition | Choosing the next conjunct in general-to-specific search |
| 4 | $\text{Laplace Accuracy} = \dfrac{\vert A \cap C \vert + 1}{\vert A \vert + k}$ | Accuracy corrected for small-sample bias (k = number of classes) | When a rule covers only a few records |
| 5 | $\text{Lift} = \dfrac{\text{Accuracy}(R)}{\pi_y}$ | Lift of $R$ over base rate $\pi_y$ of class $y$ | Measuring rule's interestingness |
| 6 | $\text{F1} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{precision} \cdot \text{recall}}{\text{precision} + \text{recall}}$ | Harmonic mean of precision & recall | Comparing rules of different coverages |
| 7 | $\text{Support} = \dfrac{\vert A \cap C \vert}{\vert D \vert}$ | Synonym for coverage in market-basket style | Rule-mining context |

> [!WARNING]
> **Notation Hazard:** All "absolute value" / "cardinality" symbols use the vertical bar. In plain ASCII you may see `|A|`, but inside LaTeX always use $\vert A \vert$ or $\mid A \mid$ to avoid table-rendering errors in markdown.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use of Rule-Based Classification |
|---|---|
| **Banking / Credit Risk** | Loan approval: `IF (income > X) AND (debt_ratio < Y) THEN approve` |
| **Network Intrusion Detection (IDS)** | Snort / Suricata signatures: `IF (packet.flags = SYN) AND (count > 100/min) THEN alert` |
| **Medical Diagnosis** | Symptom-based triage: `IF (fever AND cough AND low_SpO2) THEN isolate` |
| **Industrial Quality Control** | Defect rules on sensor thresholds |
| **Fraud Detection** | Transaction rules (unusual amount, unusual geo, unusual time → fraud) |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Walk-Through

## 3.1 Worked Example — Coverage & Accuracy from First Principles

Consider the rule $R : (\text{Refund} = \text{No}) \wedge (\text{Marital Status} = \text{Married}) \rightarrow \text{Cheat} = \text{No}$, evaluated against the 10-row training table from §1.4.

**Step 1 — Identify records satisfying the antecedent $A$.**

We need `Refund = No` AND `Marital Status = Married`. Scanning the table:

- Tid 1: Refund = Yes → ✗
- Tid 2: Refund = No, Married → ✓
- Tid 3: Refund = No, Single → ✗
- Tid 4: Refund = Yes → ✗
- Tid 5: Refund = No, Divorced → ✗
- Tid 6: Refund = No, Married → ✓
- Tid 7: Refund = Yes → ✗
- Tid 8: Refund = No, Single → ✗
- Tid 9: Refund = No, Married → ✓
- Tid 10: Refund = No, Single → ✗

So $|A| = 3$ (Tids 2, 6, 9).

**Step 2 — Identify records that also have class $y =$ Cheat = No.**

- Tid 2: Cheat = No → ✓
- Tid 6: Cheat = No → ✓
- Tid 9: Cheat = No → ✓

All three are correctly classified, so $|A \cap C| = 3$.

**Step 3 — Compute Coverage and Accuracy.**

$$\text{Coverage}(R) = \dfrac{\vert A \cap C \vert}{\vert D \vert} = \dfrac{3}{10} = 0.30$$

$$\text{Accuracy}(R) = \dfrac{\vert A \cap C \vert}{\vert A \vert} = \dfrac{3}{3} = 1.00$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

> The rule covers **30%** of the training set and is **100% accurate** on its cover. A perfect rule from the KTU valuation perspective — full marks for definition + arithmetic.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Computing FOIL Gain Step-by-Step

Suppose we are learning the rule for class `Cheat = Yes`. We currently have the partial rule $r_0 : \{\;\} \rightarrow \text{Cheat} = \text{Yes}$ and are considering adding the condition `Refund = No`.

**Step 1 — Read counts before adding the condition.**

- Positives (`Cheat = Yes`): Tids 5, 8, 10 → $p = 3$
- Negatives (`Cheat = No`): Tids 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 9 → $n = 7$
- Total covered: $p + n = 10$

**Step 2 — Add the candidate condition `Refund = No`.**

- Positives still covered (Tid 5: Yes, Tid 8: Yes, Tid 10: Yes) → $p_t = 3$
- Negatives still covered (Tid 2, 3, 6, 9) → $n_t = 4$

**Step 3 — Apply the FOIL gain formula.**

$$\text{FOIL\_Gain} = p_t \times \left( \log_2 \dfrac{p_t}{p_t + n_t} - \log_2 \dfrac{p}{p + n} \right)$$

$$= 3 \times \left( \log_2 \dfrac{3}{3 + 4} - \log_2 \dfrac{3}{3 + 7} \right)$$

$$= 3 \times \left( \log_2 \dfrac{3}{7} - \log_2 \dfrac{3}{10} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate each logarithm.**

$$\log_2 \dfrac{3}{7} = \log_2 3 - \log_2 7 = 1.585 - 2.807 = -1.222$$

$$\log_2 \dfrac{3}{10} = \log_2 3 - \log_2 10 = 1.585 - 3.322 = -1.737$$

**Step 5 — Subtract and multiply.**

$$\text{FOIL\_Gain} = 3 \times \bigl(-1.222 - (-1.737)\bigr) = 3 \times 0.515 = 1.545$$

**Step 6 — Decision rule.**

> Since the gain is **positive** ($1.545 > 0$), the condition `Refund = No` **increases** the rule's quality. FOIL will add it. A negative gain would mean the condition makes the rule *less* pure and should be skipped.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Sequential Covering on a Toy Dataset

Suppose we have a 2-D dataset with attributes `A` and `B`, both binary, predicting class `+` or `−`:

| Example | A | B | Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | T | T | + |
| 2 | T | F | + |
| 3 | F | T | − |
| 4 | F | F | − |
| 5 | T | T | + |
| 6 | F | F | − |

**Step 1 — Target class = `+`. Currently positive records = {1, 2, 5}.**

**Step 2 — Candidate rules for `+`:**

- $R_1 : (A = T) \rightarrow +$ → covers {1, 2, 5} all `+` ⇒ accuracy 1.00
- $R_2 : (B = T) \rightarrow +$ → covers {1, 3, 5}, classes {+, −, +} ⇒ accuracy 0.67

**Step 3 — Choose $R_1$ (highest accuracy).** Remove {1, 2, 5} from working set. Remaining data: {3, 4, 6} (all `−`).

**Step 4 — No positives left.** Stop rule induction for class `+`. Add a default rule for class `−`.

**Final rule set:**

$$R_1 : (A = T) \rightarrow +$$

$$R_2 : \text{default} \rightarrow -$$

> This is exactly the "separate-and-conquer" behaviour examined in KTU theory questions.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison: Rule-Based vs Decision Tree

| Aspect | Decision Tree | Rule-Based Classifier |
|---|---|---|
| **Representation** | Tree of nodes & leaves | Set of IF-THEN rules |
| **Mutual exclusion** | Guaranteed (one path) | Guaranteed **only** if rules are mutually exclusive |
| **Default class** | Implicit (majority at root or leaf) | Often explicit (added at end) |
| **Combing models** | Hard (must re-build forest) | Easy (concatenate rule lists) |
| **Readability** | Requires tree walk | Reads as English |
| **Greedy bias** | Recursive partitioning | Sequential covering (also greedy) |
| **Famous algorithm** | ID3, C4.5, CART | FOIL, CN2, AQ, RIPPER |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Every IF-THEN rule corresponds to a path in a decision tree from the root to a leaf. Therefore, a rule-based classifier with **mutually exclusive + exhaustive** rules is **logically equivalent** to a decision tree. This is the most tested one-liner.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Flow of Rule-Based Classification (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Training Dataset D] --> B[Select Target Class y]
    B --> C[Working Set Dc Initialized to D]
    C --> D[Learn One Rule from Dc]
    D --> E{Stopping Criterion Met?}
    E -- No --> F[Add Rule to Rule Set R]
    F --> G[Remove Records Covered by Rule from Dc]
    G --> C
    E -- Yes --> H{More Classes Remaining?}
    H -- Yes --> B
    H -- No --> I[Final Rule Set R1, R2, R3... Rk]
    I --> J[Rule Ordering: Rule-Based OR Class-Based]
    J --> K[Trained Classifier]
    K --> L[New Instance x]
    L --> M[Match x against Rules]
    M --> N[Assign Class y]

    style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#0d47a1
    style K fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#e65100
    style N fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#1b5e20
```

## 4.2 Learn-One-Rule: General-to-Specific Search

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Start: Empty Rule {} -> y] --> S2[Generate Candidate Conditions]
    S2 --> S3[Evaluate Each via FOIL Gain]
    S3 --> S4[Pick Best Condition]
    S4 --> S5{Stop: Pruning Threshold Met?}
    S5 -- No --> S6[Add Best Condition to Rule]
    S6 --> S2
    S5 -- Yes --> S7[Final One-Rule Output]

    style S1 fill:#ffecb3,stroke:#ef6c00,color:#e65100
    style S7 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#1b5e20
```

## 4.3 Rule Ordering Schemes — Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O1[New Instance x] --> O2{Multiple Rules Match?}
    O2 -- No, Exactly One --> O3[Use That Rule's Class]
    O2 -- Yes, Multiple --> O4{Rule-Ordering Scheme Used?}
    O4 -- Yes, Rule-Based --> O5[Pick Highest-Priority Rule]
    O4 -- No, Class-Based --> O6[Group Rules by Predicted Class]
    O6 --> O7[Majority Voting Within Class]
    O6 --> O8[Tie-Break by Rule Strength]
    O7 --> O9[Final Class Label y]
    O8 --> O9
    O5 --> O9
    O3 --> O9

    style O9 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#1b5e20

```

## 4.4 Coverage vs Accuracy — Venn Conceptual View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D[Dataset D] --> A[Records Satisfying Antecedent A]
    A --> AC[Records in A and Class y]
    A -.accuracy.-> AC
    D --> AC
    D -.coverage.-> AC

    style D fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#0d47a1
    style A fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#e65100
    style AC fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#1b5e20
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **denominator** for accuracy is $|A|$ (the antecedent set); the **denominator** for coverage is $\vert D \vert$ (the entire dataset). The numerator is the **same** intersection $|A \cap C|$ in both cases.
